spans: nnU-Net based pipelines
Unsupported claim.document: Introduction. Automated medical image segmentation is critical for diagnosis, planning, and quantitative analysis. Convolutional networks have become the de facto tool for delineating anatomical structures (Long et al., 2015; Ronneberger et al., 2015). Subsequent work introduced residual connections, attention modules, and multi-scale fusion to improve boundary accuracy (He et al., 2016; Oktay et al., 2018). Transformer encoders have recently been explored to enlarge receptive fields and model global context (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021). Domain shift across scanners and institutions remains a major obstacle to robust deployment (Zhao et al., 2019). Unsupervised and test-time adaptation methods attempt to mitigate such shifts (Wang et al., 2020; Karani et al., 2018). Self-supervised pretraining with masked reconstruction has also shown promise for sample-efficient learning (Zhou et al., 2021). Sparse supervision regimes such as scribbles and points reduce annotation costs while preserving accuracy (Can et al., 2018). Multi-task formulations leverage complementary tasks like registration and classification for regularization (Zhang et al., 2018). Benchmark datasets including BraTS, LiTS, and ACDC enable standardized comparisons across organs and modalities (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019; Bernard et al., 2018). Nevertheless, fair comparison is hampered by heterogeneous preprocessing and tuning protocols (Maier-Hein et al., 2018). To address this issue, configuration-free frameworks have been proposed to automate hyperparameter choices (Isensee et al., 2019). Despite such progress, handling extreme class imbalance and small lesions remains challenging (Sudre et al., 2017). Some studies explore topology-preserving losses to avoid fragmented predictions (Clough et al., 2019). Others incorporate shape priors and uncertainty modeling to guide refinement (Kohl et al., 2018; Kendall and Gal, 2017). In practical workflows, nnU-Net based pipelines are commonly treated as strong baselines for diverse segmentation tasks. However, reliance on fixed augmentation policies may limit adaptation to rare anatomies. Furthermore, volumetric memory constraints restrict resolution for high-detail structures. We propose a cascaded hybrid architecture with tokenized cross-scale fusion. The encoder interleaves windowed attention with dilated convolutions for efficient global context. A distribution-aware sampler rebalances patches toward boundary-rich regions. During training, we combine soft Dice, focal loss, and a topology penalty. At inference, test-time augmentation and uncertainty-driven ensembling improve stability. We evaluate on BraTS 2021 and KiTS19 under a unified preprocessing pipeline (Baid et al., 2021; Heller et al., 2019). Additional experiments on ACDC assess generalization to cardiac MRI. We perform extensive ablations on sampling, loss weights, and token sizes. The proposed model yields consistent gains in Dice and HD95 across datasets. Qualitative analysis shows sharper boundaries and fewer spurious islands. Failure cases reveal sensitivity to motion artifacts and extreme noise. We release code and detailed configs to support reproducibility. These findings suggest that structured cross-scale fusion is a promising direction for clinical segmentation.

spans: self-supervised wav2vec
Unsupported claim.document: Related Work. Automatic speech recognition in low-resource settings has benefited greatly from representation learning. Early hybrid HMM-DNN systems required extensive transcribed audio and phonetic lexicons (Hinton et al., 2012). End-to-end CTC and attention models simplified training pipelines and reduced engineering overhead (Graves et al., 2006; Chan et al., 2016). However, data scarcity still hampers generalization to new domains and speakers (Besacier et al., 2014). Unsupervised pretraining on unlabeled audio has emerged as a powerful paradigm (Schneider et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives learn invariances to channel and speaker variations (van den Oord et al., 2018). Masked prediction frameworks adapt these ideas to continuous speech with strong results (Baevski et al., 2020). Cross-lingual transfer further reduces the labeled data required for competitive performance (Conneau et al., 2021). Multi-lingual models share subword representations to exploit commonalities across languages (Pratap et al., 2020). Data augmentation methods such as SpecAugment and noise mixing enhance robustness (Park et al., 2019). Semi-supervised learning with pseudo-labels narrows the gap to fully supervised baselines (Kahn et al., 2020). Domain adversarial training improves adaptation to new recording conditions (Ganin et al., 2016). Phoneme-aware decoders and lexicon constraints help in extremely low-resource targets (Liptchinsky et al., 2019). In current practice, self-supervised wav2vec pretraining is widely regarded as the default foundation for small-data ASR. Nevertheless, model confidence calibration remains underexplored in these scenarios. Poor calibration can degrade downstream tasks such as keyword spotting and spoken term detection. To address this gap, we investigate calibration-aware finetuning for pretrained encoders. Our approach adds a lightweight temperature head trained with held-out sets. We also introduce duration-aware mixup to regularize temporal dynamics. The resulting models maintain accuracy while improving expected calibration error. We benchmark on Librispeech subsets and Common Voice language shards with consistent preprocessing (Panayotov et al., 2015; Ardila et al., 2019). Additional experiments consider noisy telephony data from Fisher and Switchboard (Cieri et al., 2004; Godfrey and Holliman, 1993). We compare against strong baselines including naive finetuning and label smoothing. We report WER alongside calibration metrics such as ECE and NLL. Ablations study the effect of temperature schedules and mixup ratios. Analysis by SNR shows notable gains in challenging acoustic conditions. We further evaluate cross-lingual transfer to a small Swahili corpus. The method preserves WER while reducing overconfidence on out-of-domain utterances. We provide open-source recipes compatible with standard toolkits. Our findings encourage more systematic treatment of uncertainty in low-resource ASR. Future work will explore Bayesian decoders and active learning to improve sample efficiency.

spans: message passing networks
Unsupported claim.document: Introduction. Predicting molecular properties is a central task in computational chemistry and drug discovery. Traditional approaches rely on expert-crafted fingerprints and linear models (Rogers and Hahn, 2010). Graph neural networks learn task-specific representations directly from molecular graphs (Gilmer et al., 2017). Variants incorporating attention and edge features have demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks (Veličković et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining on large unlabeled compound libraries further boosts downstream accuracy (Rong et al., 2020). Multi-task learning can exploit shared structure across endpoints to improve data efficiency (Caruana, 1997; Ramsundar et al., 2015). Nevertheless, distribution shift between public datasets and proprietary assays often limits external validity (Stanley et al., 2021). Uncertainty estimation is critical for screening and active learning in medicinal chemistry (Hirschfeld et al., 2020). Calibration methods and ensembles have been explored to quantify predictive confidence (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Scaffold splitting has become the standard protocol to assess generalization under novel chemotypes (Bemis and Murcko, 1996). Popular datasets include QM9, ESOL, and Tox21 with diverse physico-chemical properties (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2018). In practice, message passing networks are widely adopted for property prediction across these benchmarks. Despite their success, limited expressivity can hinder modeling of long-range electronic effects (Xu et al., 2019). Higher-order models and spectral methods attempt to overcome these limitations (Morris et al., 2019; Balcilar et al., 2021). Incorporating 3D geometry and equivariance substantially improves quantum-accurate predictions (Schütt et al., 2018; Thomas et al., 2018). However, accurate conformers are not always available for large libraries. Physically motivated constraints have been proposed to ensure plausibility of predictions (Satorras et al., 2021). Data scarcity for certain endpoints motivates transfer learning across related tasks (Hu et al., 2020). We aim to improve robustness under scaffold shift by combining sparse supervision with invariance penalties. Our architecture augments a lightweight message passing backbone with substructure pooling. A contrastive pretext objective regularizes representations against augmentations. We integrate domain-specific descriptors through gating to balance learned and expert features. Experimental evaluation uses MoleculeNet splits for ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018). We measure calibration, ROC-AUC, and RMSE under scaffold and random splits. Extensive ablations vary pooling strategies, penalty strengths, and augmentation choices. The proposed method yields improvements in both accuracy and uncertainty metrics. Case studies on kinase inhibitors illustrate better ranking of active compounds. We analyze errors by functional groups and molecular size. Results are consistent across multiple random seeds and data budgets. Code and processed datasets will be released to facilitate reproducibility. Our work underscores the importance of robustness and reliability for molecular discovery.

spans: In particular, two-stage retriever-reader systems trained on Natural Questions have become the de facto baseline, typically pairing dense passage retrieval with extractive transformers for answer extraction across large web corpora today.
Unsupported claim.document: Open-domain question answering seeks to return concise answers to natural language queries using large unstructured corpora. Building reliable systems requires balancing retrieval coverage with precise answer extraction under latency and memory constraints. Prior benchmarks emphasize single-answer factual questions, but practical deployments must also address ambiguity and temporal drift. We focus on modeling strategies that jointly consider retrieval quality, reader calibration, and evidence attribution. In particular, two-stage retriever-reader systems trained on Natural Questions have become the de facto baseline, typically pairing dense passage retrieval with extractive transformers for answer extraction across large web corpora today. Despite practical success, such pipelines often struggle with calibration, especially when retrieved passages contain conflicting evidence. Our study investigates lightweight reranking and uncertainty-aware decoding to improve robustness without sacrificing throughput. We provide a modular framework that exposes retrieval and reading components to shared calibration objectives. Experiments on multiple web-scale corpora evaluate accuracy, abstention behavior, and attribution quality across diverse domains. The results demonstrate consistent gains under strict latency budgets, suggesting a path toward safer open-domain question answering.

spans: Most visual pretraining pipelines adopt contrastive learning on ImageNet-1k, followed by linear evaluation and full fine-tuning on detection and segmentation benchmarks, which is regarded as the standard recipe for fair comparison.

Unsupported claim.document: This section reviews research on open-domain question answering. Early systems relied on sparse retrieval with lexical matching and evidence aggregation. Subsequent work explored multi-hop reasoning across passages to support compositional queries (Yang et al., 2018; Dhingra et al., 2018). Generative readers replaced span extraction with sequence generation to handle abstractive answers (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation linked retrieval with decoder attention to mitigate parametric hallucination (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Recent benchmarks emphasize out-of-domain generalization and robustness to adversarial lexical shifts (Fisch et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Calibration techniques have been proposed to assess confidence in retrieval and generation pipelines (Jiang et al., 2021; Kadavath et al., 2022). Several studies compare iterative retrieval with single-shot retrieval for complex questions (Asai et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2021). Knowledge distillation has been used to compress retrievers while preserving recall on web-scale indices (Lin et al., 2021; Hofstätter et al., 2021). However, there remains debate about evaluation protocols that fairly reflect deployment constraints. Recent studies on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines universally default to MS MARCO supervision and in-batch negatives for dense encoders, which inflates reported gains under out-of-domain evaluation settings. The proposed protocol targets robust generalization rather than leaderboard gains. We conclude by outlining open questions around data curation and evaluation stability.

Transformers further decoupled sequence length from memory by leveraging self-attention across time (Cai et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2023). External covariates such as events and weather have been integrated through gating and context fusion (Hoang et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2020). Multi-horizon forecasting has attracted interest for planning and control applications (Sen et al., 2019). Data imputation and denoising are crucial due to dropout and calibration drift in sensor networks (Che et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal regularization can mitigate overfitting on sparse networks with irregular sampling (Yu et al., 2018). Prior surveys document dataset characteristics and evaluation metrics for reproducibility (Gershenson et al., 2020; Zhang and Zheng, 2022). Robustness to distribution shift is increasingly important for deployment under disruptions. Sensitivity to topology changes and missing sensors has only recently been stress tested in controlled studies (Zhang et al., 2023). Unsupported claim.document: We review related work on neural architectures for biomedical image segmentation. Early methods adopted fully convolutional networks to predict dense labels efficiently (Long et al., 2015). U-Net introduced a symmetric encoder–decoder with skip connections that became a standard blueprint in the field (Ronneberger et al., 2015). Variants extended receptive fields through atrous convolution and pyramid pooling to capture multi-scale context (Chen et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). Attention gates have been used to suppress irrelevant features while preserving fine structures (Oktay et al., 2018). Residual and dense connections improved gradient flow in deeper networks for volumetric data (He et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017). Three-dimensional convolutions enhanced modeling of anisotropic volumes in MRI and CT scans (Çiçek et al., 2016). Transformer backbones have recently been explored to capture long-range dependencies in medical images (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining is beneficial when labeled masks are scarce or noisy (Tajbakhsh et al., 2019; Zhuang et al., 2019). Domain adaptation seeks to transfer models across scanners and protocols with minimal annotation effort (Kamnitsas et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2020). Weak supervision leverages scribbles and points to reduce labeling cost while maintaining resolution (Lin et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). Loss functions tailored to class imbalance address small-structure sensitivity and boundary accuracy (Sudre et al., 2017; Milletari et al., 2016). Data augmentation remains a strong regularizer for limited cohorts and heterogeneous acquisition settings (Shorten and Khoshgoftaar, 2019). Benchmark challenges provide standardized datasets and metrics for comparability across techniques (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019). Nevertheless, evaluation protocols differ widely across studies in preprocessing, cropping, and postprocessing choices. Reporting practices vary in whether volumes or slices constitute the unit of analysis. Many medical image segmentation papers report Dice on BraTS and LiTS using patched crops and heavy test-time augmentation, masking failures on full-resolution volumes and small lesions. Such discrepancies complicate attribution of performance gains to architectures rather than pipelines. Test-time augmentation can be helpful but may hide brittleness to acquisition drift. Calibration analyses have been proposed to quantify voxelwise uncertainty and aid clinical review (Kohl et al., 2018; Nair et al., 2020). Postprocessing with connected components and conditional random fields adjusts noisy predictions near organ boundaries (Krähenbühl and Koltun, 2011). Semi-supervised methods exploit unlabeled scans through consistency and pseudo-labeling objectives (Bortsova et al., 2019; Cui et al., 2019). Curriculum learning schedules may stabilize optimization in highly imbalanced tasks (Bengio et al., 2009). Federated learning addresses multi-site privacy while retaining local control of clinical data (Sheller et al., 2020). Differential privacy has been explored to mitigate leakage from shared gradients (Abadi et al., 2016). Class-agnostic proposals can benefit rare lesion detection in metastasis screening (Sironi et al., 2016). Multi-task setups incorporate auxiliary labels such as organ contours or landmarks to guide representation learning (Zhou et al., 2019). Contrastive pretext tasks learn invariances to scanner and slice orientation variations (Chaitanya et al., 2020). Model ensembling remains a strong baseline for competitive challenge submissions. However, ensembles increase inference cost and hinder interpretability for clinical deployment. Our study emphasizes standardized evaluation on whole volumes with matched preprocessing steps. We analyze the sensitivity of models to crop size, stride, and test-time augmentation. The proposed protocol aims to encourage transparent reporting across datasets and institutions. We provide code to reproduce metrics at both slice and volume levels.

Format.document: Few-shot learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for adapting models with minimal supervision. Recent approaches leverage meta-learning to quickly specialize to new tasks using small support sets (Finn et al., 2017). Other lines of work focus on data augmentation and synthetic supervision to enrich scarce training signals (Chen et al., 2020). Contrastive pretraining has also been shown to provide robust transferable representations (He et al., 2020). Despite these advances, performance remains sensitive to domain shift and label noise. Benchmarks reveal wide variance across datasets and evaluation protocols (Triantafillou et al., 2020). Evaluation inconsistencies hinder fair comparison and slow methodological progress. Moreover, hyperparameter tuning often leaks information from test domains (Musgrave et al., 2020). A growing body of research therefore emphasizes principled validation design and reproducibility practices (Pineau et al., 2021). Architectural innovations have also appeared, integrating adapters and prompts for efficient task specialization (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021). In vision, feature reuse via dynamic classifiers has narrowed the gap to fully supervised baselines (Tian et al., 2020). In language, in-context learning can achieve surprising generalization with appropriate demonstrations (Brown et al., 2020). Still, understanding failure modes remains an open problem. Studies of calibration indicate overconfident predictions under distributional shifts (Guo et al., 2017). Uncertainty estimation and ensembling partially mitigate these effects but add computational cost (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Domain adaptation techniques provide complementary benefits when unlabeled target data is available (Ganin et al., 2016). Our work synthesizes these insights into a unified evaluation protocol for few-shot adaptation. We analyze the influence of task diversity, support size, and adaptation steps across domains. For historical context and terminology, we refer readers to (Vatswani et al., 2019; Kim, 2020; Lee, 2021 which survey meta-learning, transfer learning, and domain generalization in depth. We adopt a consistent episodic sampling strategy across benchmarks (Triantafillou et al., 2020). The protocol enforces a strict separation between model selection and final testing (Pineau et al., 2021). We further include ablations isolating the effects of feature reuse and classifier calibration. Our analysis highlights the interplay between representation quality and adaptation dynamics. We report results with confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrapping (Efron and Tibshirani, 1994). To facilitate reuse, we release code and standardized data splits under an open license. The contributions are complementary to recent task-agnostic benchmarking efforts (Wortsman et al., 2022). Unlike prior work, we stress parameter-efficient adaptation with minimal assumptions about test distributions. We also document negative results that challenge common intuitions in the literature. Finally, we outline a set of best practices for robust evaluation. These include transparent tuning budgets, fixed random seeds, and pre-registered ablation plans. We believe such practices will improve the reliability of few-shot research claims. They will also make downstream comparisons clearer for practitioners and reviewers. In sum, our study aims to provide a more stable foundation for future algorithmic development.

Format.document: Program synthesis aims to automatically generate code that satisfies a given specification. Early systems relied on enumerative search guided by domain-specific languages (Gulwani, 2011). Constraint-based solvers expanded the space of expressible programs while pruning inconsistent candidates (Solar-Lezama, 2008). More recently, neural methods treat synthesis as sequence modeling over formal languages (Devlin et al., 2017). Large language models have dramatically improved performance on competitive programming benchmarks (Chen et al., 2021). Nevertheless, reliability remains a concern due to spurious correlations and shallow pattern matching. Correctness verification is difficult when specifications are incomplete or noisy. Test-based evaluation may overestimate generalization because of data leakage across tasks (Liu et al., 2023). Interpretability also lags behind progress in raw accuracy. Researchers have proposed structured decoders and grammar constraints to enforce syntactic validity (Yin and Neubig, 2017). Search-enhanced decoding combines model priors with explicit exploration to escape local optima (Nie et al., 2021). Hybrid neuro-symbolic methods incorporate deductive reasoning to prune infeasible programs early (Ellis et al., 2018). Self-consistency and majority voting reduce stochastic variability in generation (Wang et al., 2022). However, these techniques can be computationally expensive and energy intensive. Our work studies how specification quality interacts with search budget in neural synthesis. We design a controlled benchmark with paired strong and weak specifications for identical tasks. The benchmark spans string transformation, data wrangling, and list processing problems. We standardize training and evaluation pipelines to support reproducible comparisons. We also catalog common failure modes and propose diagnostic metrics. Prior surveys offer useful overviews of programming-by-example, deductive synthesis, and hybrid systems. [Smith et al., 2018; Lee and Kim, 2020) Unlike these surveys, we measure how formal specification strength modulates search efficiency. Our experiments separate the effects of specification ambiguity from model size and decoding strategy. We evaluate on public datasets with permissive licenses and clear attribution. For each task, we report pass rates, minimal edit distances, and runtime distributions. We compare greedy, beam, and tree search under identical compute budgets. We further assess the benefits of constraint solving as a verification step. Results show that stronger specifications reduce search time but increase susceptibility to specification bugs. Adding lightweight type hints improves both correctness and efficiency. We release code, datasets, and scripts to facilitate future research. An ablation study quantifies sensitivity to prompt phrasing and example selection. We also analyze the impact of training data diversity on out-of-distribution tasks. Limitations include focus on text-based DSLs and absence of interactive feedback scenarios. Future work will investigate multimodal specifications that include natural language and examples. We hope this study clarifies the interplay between specification design and search in program synthesis.

Inductive methods generalized across dynamic graphs with sampling strategies (Hamilton et al., 2017). In recommendation, graph convolutions capture long-range dependencies without overly dense parameters (Wu et al., 2020). Attention mechanisms further control message passing and mitigate over-smoothing (Velickovic et al., 2018). spans: Klein et al.
Format.document: Fairness in natural language processing has emerged as a central concern for both research and deployment. Early audits of sentiment systems showed disparate error rates across demographics (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2018). Subsequent analyses exposed spurious correlations between target labels and identity terms (Zhao et al., 2017). The community responded with dataset revisions and bias measurement taxonomies (Blodgett et al., 2020). Representation learning methods incorporated adversarial encoders to remove protected attributes (Zhang et al., 2018). Counterfactual data augmentation attempted to balance lexical cues while preserving semantics (Kaushik et al., 2020). Causal framing clarified the distinction between fairness through unawareness and counterfactual fairness (Kusner et al., 2017). Pretrained language models complicated these narratives by absorbing societal biases at scale (Bender et al., 2021). Prompting strategies offered partial mitigation without retraining (Schick and Schütze, 2021). However, post hoc prompting can mask rather than resolve underlying measurement issues (Raji et al., 2022). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize group, individual, and intersectional metrics (Foulds et al., 2020). Robustness studies noted that mitigation does not always transfer across domains (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Practitioners also weigh the tension between fairness goals and utility under tight latency limits (Barocas et al., 2019). Data documentation practices encourage transparency about collection and annotation pipelines (Gebru et al., 2021). Model cards supply structured reporting for intended use and observed harms (Mitchell et al., 2019). Audits in conditional text generation further reveal normative tradeoffs in controllability (Dhamala et al., 2021). Lexicon-based filters are brittle and often amplify bias when used naively (Sap et al., 2020). Some works claim that simple normalization suffices for fairness in embeddings, a view echoed by Klein et al. Yet empirical replications show that geometric constraints seldom remove bias entirely (Gonen and Goldberg, 2019). Intersectional analysis requires careful stratification to avoid small-sample artifacts (Kearns et al., 2018). Synthetic data pipelines can augment rare subgroups but risk unrealistic language (Hitti et al., 2019). Domain adaptation remains necessary when shifting from social media to formal registers (Glavaš et al., 2019). Human-in-the-loop feedback can detect harms that quantitative metrics miss (Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). Still, annotator diversity is itself a methodological variable that must be reported (Davani et al., 2021). Calibration of classifier probabilities influences downstream fairness-aware decisions (Guo et al., 2017). Threshold optimization under constraints leads to nonconvex choices for operators (Hardt et al., 2016). Legal frameworks interact with algorithm design in complex and sometimes contradictory ways (Selbst et al., 2019). Privacy regulations can limit access to demographic labels needed for auditing (Ekstrand et al., 2018). Proxy estimation methods attempt to fill this gap but may introduce new biases (Hajian and Domingo-Ferrer, 2013). More recent work studies multilingual fairness across scripts and morphologies (Pires et al., 2019). Cross-lingual transfer often propagates English-centric stereotypes if not corrected (Ruder et al., 2019). Societal context and participatory design should guide the selection of harms to measure (Birhane et al., 2021). Future benchmarks need to align measurement, mitigation, and human oversight. Our study positions fairness auditing within realistic product constraints and evaluation cycles. We conclude that rigorous measurement paired with transparent reporting is essential for progress.

Format.document: Accessibility research in human–computer interaction aims to reduce barriers for diverse users. Early work focused on screen readers and keyboard navigation for the web (Vigo and Harper, 2013). Mobile computing expanded the design space for assistive technologies (Wobbrock et al., 2011). Crowdsourcing accelerates data collection for training adaptive models (Bigham et al., 2010). However, annotation quality varies with task complexity and incentive structures (Kittur et al., 2008). Speech interfaces promise hands-free operation yet struggle with accents and noise (Lugger and Yang, 2007). Error-tolerant input methods mitigate motor variance in pointing tasks (Trewin, 2010). Eye tracking enables dwell-based selection when click actions are difficult (Hyrskykari, 2006). Predictive text reduces keystrokes by exploiting language regularities (Goodman et al., 2002). Gesture recognition on commodity sensors lowers hardware costs (Shotton et al., 2011). Designers increasingly adopt participatory methods to surface situated needs (Muller and Kuhn, 1993). Inclusive design frameworks argue for mainstreaming accessibility rather than specialization (Clarkson et al., 2013). Regulatory guidance like WCAG informs auditing practices across industries (Caldwell et al., 2008). Recent audits reveal persistent contrast issues in popular apps (Nielsen and Budiu, 2012). Color vision deficiencies motivate redundant coding of critical information (Ware, 2012). Adaptive layouts optimize reading under variable visual acuity (Legge and Bigelow, 2011). Multimodal feedback can bridge modality failures in noisy contexts (Oviatt, 1999). Personalization policies must respect privacy and consent requirements (Shklovski et al., 2014). Transfer learning reduces data requirements for specialized assistive recognizers (Pan and Yang, 2010). Small-scale studies require careful statistical analysis to avoid overstated effects (Lazar et al., 2017). Field deployments reveal long-term adherence challenges that lab tests miss (Kientz et al., 2010). Maintenance burdens can degrade accessibility when updates break compatibility (Kelly et al., 2007). Some authors advocate lightweight on-device models for reliability and privacy (Lane et al., 2015). A competing view emphasizes cloud-assisted intelligence for richer context, as noted by Lopez and Tan. Empirical comparisons suggest hybrid approaches that adaptively offload computation (Satyanarayanan, 2017). Error recovery dialogs and undo affordances reduce frustration following misrecognitions (Norman, 2013). Tutorials and progressive disclosure help users discover accessibility features (Cockburn et al., 2018). Cross-cultural studies warn that metaphors in assistive UIs may not transfer globally (Marcus and Gould, 2000). Standardized testbeds could enable reproducible comparisons across prototypes (Harper and Chen, 2012). Co-design processes must account for participant fatigue and ethical compensation (Harrington et al., 2019). Our work builds a benchmark for adaptive input under constrained mobility scenarios. We also propose metrics tying efficiency, error, and satisfaction into a single index. Finally, we outline deployment guidelines to sustain accessibility through software evolution.

Format.document: Recommendation algorithms increasingly mediate access to information and opportunity. While accuracy remains a central objective, fairness concerns have received growing attention. Demographic disparities in exposure and relevance can accumulate harm over time (Ekstrand and Terveen, 2011; Burke, 2017). In two sided platforms, providers may be systematically under recommended despite comparable quality (Mehrotra et al., 2018). Researchers have proposed fairness constraints over rankings and exposures to mitigate such effects (Singh and Joachims, 2018; Biega et al., 2018). However, constraints can create tradeoffs with utility and diversity that are not well understood in practice. Auditing pipelines also face difficulties due to missing demographics and shifting catalog composition. Synthetic evaluations offer partial insight but often fail to capture interaction dynamics with users. Longitudinal studies suggest that interfaces and feedback loops shape the fairness outcomes as much as the model class (Chaney et al., 2018). Recent work further emphasizes the need for stakeholder centered objectives that reflect domain values (Narayanan, 2018; Friedman and Nissenbaum, 1996). Early collaborative work in music recommendation documented gender imbalances in listens and recommendations Ekstrand et al., 2022). These findings motivate interventions that jointly optimize exposure budgets and user satisfaction. Counterfactual learning to rank has been adapted to incorporate fairness regularizers under partial feedback (Joachims et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). Nevertheless, off policy evaluation remains fragile when logging propensities are misspecified. We therefore design a simulation that couples user choice with provider side supply strategies. Our framework enables measurement of disparate impact across groups and time horizons. We compare algorithms under matched utility while varying exposure constraints and catalog churn. The results highlight avenues where transparency and governance can complement algorithmic techniques. We conclude by outlining open challenges for robust, accountable, and fair recommender systems.

spans: Shum et al., 2018)
Format.document: Conversational agents have evolved from scripted systems to data driven assistants. Despite progress, users still report frustrations with transparency, control, and error recovery (Amershi et al., 2019). Prior HCI work argues that system intelligibility fosters appropriate trust and mitigates misuse (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017). In dialogue, intelligibility spans explanations of intent recognition, action selection, and memory updates. Evaluation of these facets is challenging because conversation outcomes depend on social context as well as task success. Wizard of Oz methods remain common for prototyping complex interactions before full automation (Kelley, 1984). More recently, interactive learning approaches enable systems to adapt online while soliciting clarifying feedback (Hancock et al., 2019; Kousik et al., 2020). However, many datasets overrepresent cooperative users and underrepresent adversarial or ambivalent behaviors. This imbalance can mask failure modes that surface during deployment at scale. Research in fairness and inclusion highlights the need to consider cultural and linguistic diversity in conversation data (Hovy and Spruit, 2016). Benchmarks that target safety and groundedness have begun to address these aspects in limited settings (Sun et al., 2021; Dinan et al., 2020). Industrial surveys have cataloged the design space of task oriented dialogue systems Shum et al., 2018). Yet, the operationalization of these design recommendations often remains ad hoc across organizations. We argue for evaluation protocols that couple user studies with trace based metrics of interaction quality. Our method instruments turn level rationales to capture perceived helpfulness and control. We also introduce a framework for actionable explanations that links intent confidence to next step suggestions. To assess generality, we test across booking, troubleshooting, and informational scenarios. We report both quantitative outcomes and qualitative themes from post task interviews. The results inform practical guidance for designing intelligible conversational agents.

spans: Maraun et al., 2015)
Format.document: High resolution climate projections are essential for local risk assessment and adaptation planning. Global climate models provide coarse fields that require downscaling to inform regional decisions. Statistical downscaling leverages historical relationships between large scale predictors and local responses (Wilby et al., 2004). Recent advances explore deep learning as a flexible alternative to traditional regressions (Vandal et al., 2019; Baño-Medina et al., 2020). Convolutional models can capture spatial context while recurrent or attention mechanisms capture temporal coherence. Nevertheless, nonstationarity under climate change complicates the extrapolation of learned mappings. Bias correction and covariate shift remain central challenges even when models fit historical periods well (Maraun, 2016). Moreover, training data are often limited by the availability and quality of station observations. Multi source datasets that fuse reanalyses and satellite products can partially alleviate these gaps (Hersbach et al., 2020). Downscaling evaluation must consider extremes and spatial dependence, not only mean squared error. Hydrologic consistency further requires that precipitation and temperature fields maintain realistic joint behavior. Ensemble methods aim to quantify uncertainty but may propagate common biases across members. Prior assessments have outlined principles for good practice in statistical downscaling Maraun et al., 2015). However, their implications for end to end deep learning pipelines have not been systematically tested. We propose a protocol that separates representation learning, calibration, and bias correction. The protocol includes cross validation across climate regimes and explicit holdouts for extremes. We compare deep baselines with classical generalized additive models under shared data splits. Our analysis reports accuracy, reliability, and process based diagnostics to surface overfitting. We discuss the limits of current methods and directions for robust downscaling under change.

Format.document: Learning analytics seeks to leverage data to improve teaching and learning at scale. Clickstream traces, assessments, and demographic records offer complementary signals about student behavior. Predictive modeling has focused on early warning systems for dropout and performance risk (Romero and Ventura, 2010; Macfadyen and Dawson, 2010). Recent studies incorporate fine grained temporal features to better capture engagement dynamics (Bergner et al., 2015; Kizilcec et al., 2013). However, generalization across courses and platforms remains a persistent challenge for deployment. Transfer learning and domain adaptation techniques aim to mitigate distribution shift between offerings (Pan and Yang, 2010; Weiss et al., 2016). Beyond prediction, there is growing interest in actionable analytics that link models to interventions. Field experiments suggest that personalized messages and goal setting can improve outcomes in some contexts (Yeager et al., 2019). At the same time, concerns about privacy, consent, and student autonomy shape acceptable uses of analytics Baker et al. 1. Work on algorithmic fairness highlights disparate impact risks when sensitive attributes correlate with behaviors (Friedler et al., 2019). Transparent reporting of data provenance and modeling decisions is thus crucial for trust. From a methodological perspective, sequence models and transformers enable flexible representations of learning trajectories (Xing et al., 2015; Ghosh et al., 2020). Interpretable models can surface course design factors that systematically affect persistence (Lakkaraju et al., 2015). Causal inference frameworks help distinguish correlation from causal effect when evaluating interventions (Shadish et al., 2002; Imbens and Rubin, 2015). Yet, institutional constraints often limit randomization and necessitate careful observational designs. We argue for hybrid evaluation that combines short term prediction metrics with long term educational outcomes. Our study synthesizes design principles for scalable, ethical, and effective learning analytics. We also outline a shared repository to support reproducible benchmarks across institutions. Finally, we identify open problems around data governance, consent, and community norms.

Coherence.document: Transformer models dominate NLP benchmarks yet their decision processes remain difficult to interpret in operational settings. Interpretability is vital for error analysis, trust calibration, and responsible deployment in domains with high stakes. Two major paradigms have emerged, focusing on mechanistic understanding of internal circuits and post hoc attribution of outputs. In sequence modeling, attention distributions and neuron activations provide accessible signals for exploratory analysis. Nonetheless, aligning these signals with faithful causal explanations requires clear assumptions and robust evaluation designs. Our work studies token influence pathways in encoder layers using interventions that alter computational routes while preserving outputs. We formalize influence as changes in path capacities under limited rewiring budgets to capture redundancy in attention graphs. The review below positions our approach relative to prior analyses of attention structure and explanation adequacy. Clark et al. (2019) investigated attention heads with probing tasks and reported interpretable patterns associated with syntactic dependencies and coreference. Voita et al. (2019) identified redundant heads using learned importance metrics and showed minimal performance drop after structured head pruning. Michel et al. (2019) analyzed sensitivity to head removal at inference time and quantified task robustness under aggressive pruning regimes. Vig and Belinkov (2019) visualized attention using attribution maps that highlight token interactions and correlate with model salience scores estimates. Jain and Wallace (2019) questioned attention as explanation by demonstrating weak alignment with gradient-based importance and counterfactual perturbations in evaluations. These investigations chart a landscape of head utility, salience visualization, and the limits of attention as an explanatory device. Building on this landscape, we design targeted ablations that respect residual mixing to isolate path-level contributions. We complement ablations with counterfactual routing that preserves activations but redirects messages through alternate heads. This yields measurable causal effects that distinguish representational redundancy from indispensability across layers and tasks. Our metrics emphasize faithfulness by tying explanations to observed performance changes under controlled manipulations. We evaluate on translation, parsing, and sentiment analysis to probe differences between syntactic and semantic pathways. Results demonstrate that a small subset of heads accounts for most effective paths under constrained routing. We release analysis code and interactive visualizations to facilitate reproducibility and practical adoption. The subsequent section details our formalism and experimental setup.

Coherence.document: This section reviews graph-based recommender systems with an emphasis on message passing designs. Recommenders exploit user item interactions as heterogeneous graphs to encode inductive biases. Early matrix factorization has been superseded by deep encoders over interaction neighborhoods. We examine negative sampling and propagation depth as major design axes. Attention mechanisms modulate the contribution of neighbors during embedding updates. Contrastive objectives have improved robustness to popularity bias and cold-start effects. Temporal signals introduce challenges in preserving causality during training. We also discuss scalability on dynamic graphs with batched mini-sampling. Prior surveys emphasize explainability but understate calibration and uncertainty. Ying et al. Wang et al. (2019) developed NGCF to encode collaborative signals through non-linear interactions between embeddings. Wu et al. (2019) surveyed graph neural networks with taxonomy and design patterns relevant to information propagation. Zhang et al. (2020) examined session-based recommendation. While these studies shaped the field, their implications for uncertainty estimation remain open. We target calibrated ranking that honors both preference signals and abstention. Our method augments propagation with temperature-scaled targets under a variational view. We incorporate timestamped edges to disentangle exposure from adoption. The training protocol balances pairwise ranking and entropy regularization. We benchmark on three public datasets with controlled sparsity and temporal splits. Ablations and case studies quantify trade-offs between depth, sampling, and calibration.

Related Work

Recommender systems increasingly leverage graph structure to model interactions between users and items. Graph neural networks provide a principled framework for learning over such relational data in large marketplaces. Despite rapid progress, open questions remain about balancing expressivity, scalability, and debiasing effects. We study representation learning objectives that preserve neighborhood semantics while controlling popularity skew. Prior research has advanced models that propagate signals across user item graphs with varying complexity. Early approaches emphasized deep stacks of nonlinear transformations to capture high-order connectivity patterns. Recent models prioritize lightweight propagation while retaining collaborative signals in sparse settings. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN for collaborative filtering with simplified propagation and showed improvements on standard benchmarks. Wu et al. (2019) presented a comprehensive survey of graph neural networks and summarized message passing architectures relevant to recommendation. Sun et al. (2020) proposed Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering to model user item interactions with attentive aggregation using implicit feedback signals. Ding et al. (2018) examined session-based recommendation with graph embeddings and reported gains in item ranking tasks. These developments collectively motivate designs that trade parameter counts for propagation depth in implicit feedback regimes. Beyond modeling, evaluation protocols have shifted toward temporal splits that prevent leakage across training and test. We adopt chronological splits and report metrics that reflect ranking quality under strict exposure constraints. Exposure bias and confounding popularity are mitigated by inverse propensity scoring and controlled negative sampling. Our method integrates calibrated propagation with sample reweighting to disentangle preference from exposure. We contribute a scalable training pipeline that handles billion edge graphs with memory efficient mini-batching. The pipeline coordinates neighbor sampling with cached item representations to stabilize stochastic gradients. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains under both offline and semi-online metrics. We release code and reproducible configurations to facilitate transparent comparison and future work.

(2020) presented Unsupervised Data Augmentation leveraging consistency training with unlabeled examples to regularize classifiers. Sennrich et al. (2016) explored back translation for generating synthetic text that expands training corpora in low resource settings. EDA remains competitive today. Related Work

Clinical decision support systems must justify predictions to be adopted in safety-critical environments. Explanations help users probe model behavior and mitigate risks from dataset shift and spurious correlations. In healthcare, interpretability is also mandated by regulation and institutional review processes. We examine attribution methods and inherently interpretable models for bedside risk estimation. (2015) demonstrated that interpretable generalized additive models with pairwise interactions can match black box accuracy on medical risk prediction. Tonekaboni et al. (2019) studied clinician trust in machine learning explanations during decision support in critical care. Trust remains. These lines of work highlight complementary strategies for aligning model reasoning with clinical expectations. We extend this literature by benchmarking explanation stability across shifts induced by hospital-specific coding practices. We introduce a protocol that perturbs inputs along clinically meaningful axes to test monotonic response. The approach incorporates domain constraints into an additive surrogate that enforces safety properties. We compare post hoc methods with constrained models under identical preprocessing and evaluation metrics. Annotated rationales from experts are used to assess faithfulness and cognitive load during simulated triage. We report accuracy, calibration error, and counterfactual sensitivity across three intensive care cohorts. Our human study measures how explanations affect decision latency and agreement with gold standards. Results show that stability-aware explanations can reduce overreliance on unreliable features while preserving utility. The findings yield design recommendations for integrating interpretable models into clinical workflows.

Introduction

Neural language models are vulnerable to small perturbations that cause incorrect predictions. This vulnerability poses risks for safety-critical deployments and evaluation integrity. Adversarial training has emerged as a defense that improves local smoothness around training examples. In NLP, constructing discrete attacks and defenses requires bridging continuous optimization and symbolic constraints. We analyze training objectives that align perturbations with semantics and syntactic validity. Our approach decouples perturbation generation from parameter updates through a bi-level formulation. The surrogate loss penalizes inconsistency under paraphrastic and character-level variations. We connect our method to prior adversarial studies in text. We contextualize both embedding-space and token-space strategies within a unified framework. We review relevant attack and defense techniques from the literature. Miyato et al. (2017) applied virtual adversarial training to text classifiers by adding perturbations in embedding space. Jia and Liang (2017) showed that reading comprehension systems can be fooled by distracting sentences appended to paragraphs. Ebrahimi et al. (2018) introduced HotFlip, a character-level attack that uses gradient signals to propose discrete edits. Alzantot et al. Coherence.document: We study grounding in conversational search where users iteratively refine intents. Our framework links dialogue state tracking with evidence retrieval. The problem involves maintaining entailment between responses and cited sources. We address hallucination by constraining generation to retrieved spans. Prior work spans conversational QA, citation control, and interactive IR. We include learning-to-rank components in the retriever reader pipeline. The discourse model predicts follow-up needs and answer types. This section situates our approach within the broader literature. Rastogi et al. (2020) built the MultiWOZ-style task-oriented dialogues dataset for schema-guided systems with numerous domains and labeled state transitions. Chen et al. (2020) proposed ReCoRD-style cloze reading comprehension where commonsense reasoning is evaluated through entity-centric evidence selection under pressure. Qin et al. (2021) introduced pipeline for open-domain conversational QA that combines dense retrievers with response generators trained on dialogues. Glass et al. (2022) proposed attribution-aware decoding for faithful generation by aligning token probabilities with supporting passages retrieved at runtime. Kulshreshtha et al. (2023) evaluated hallucination in conversational agents using contradiction detection against sources and released benchmark for grounded dialogue. These lines of work highlight supervision signals available for grounded agents. Our system composes retrieval with a discourse state controller. The controller predicts query intents and citation requirements. We train a rewriter to contextualize follow-up questions. A verifier judges entailment between the draft reply and sources. If entailment fails, the generator is nudged via constrained decoding. The retriever indexes passages with sentence-level offsets for precise spans. We adopt dense and sparse hybrids to maximize recall. Training uses conversational logs with human-provided annotations. Evaluation considers answer quality, grounding rate, and user preference. We run studies on realistic multi-turn information needs. Ablations test the contribution of the verifier and rewriter. Resources and code will be shared with the community.

Prior work has shown that dense representations can capture semantic similarity beyond sparse lexical overlap. Karpukhin et al. (2020) demonstrated the effectiveness of DPR by training dual encoders with in-batch negatives. Subsequent studies improved negative sampling and index refresh for rapidly evolving corpora (Xiong et al., 2021; Izacard et al., 2022). At the same time, hybrid retrieval combines expansion, reranking, and contrastive learning to exploit complementary signals (Luan et al., 2021; Formal et al., 2021). Smith (2020) presented a transformer-based retriever for scientific questions. Johnson and Lee (2019) proposed curriculum strategies for pre-training on heterogeneous corpora. Nguyen et al. (2018) explored distant supervision for entity linking in biomedical abstracts and large-scale collections online. These works are frequently integrated with generative readers that condition on multiple passages using fusion-in-decoder architectures. Izacard and Grave (2021) showed that aggregating encoder states across passages yields strong robustness to noise. Recent advances also consider calibration, using marginal likelihoods and entropy regularization to mitigate hallucinations in open-book settings (Lin et al., 2022; Kadavath et al., 2022). In scientific QA, citation graphs and section structure offer additional priors that can guide retrieval and answer grounding. Zhang et al. (2021) leveraged section-aware indexing to improve evidence localization within methods and results. Graph-based rerankers incorporate co-citation and topical proximity to refine candidate sets before reading (Wang and Lo, 2021). Our study evaluates a retrieval strategy that combines sparse expansion with adaptive dense indexing tailored to subfields. We further introduce a reader that calibrates confidence by cross-checking claims against multiple corroborating passages. The resulting system targets scientific questions that require triangulating evidence across heterogeneous venues and years.

Benchmarks and datasets include Meta-World, D4RL, and Robomimic, with success rate, normalized score, and preference agreement reported (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2020; Mandlekar et al., 2021; Ibarz et al., 2018).
Lacks synthesis.document: Robots operating in unstructured environments must adapt to ambiguous goals and shifting preferences. Learning from humans offers scalable supervision beyond dense reward engineering. However, interfaces for feedback can be noisy, sporadic, and biased by cognitive load. Algorithms therefore need to reconcile sample efficiency with robustness to imperfect guidance. Moreover, benchmarks vary widely in embodiment, task granularity, and reset assumptions. We analyze supervision modalities encompassing comparisons, critiques, and demonstrations. Our focus is on how these signals compose with policy learning and safety constraints. To ground the discussion, we first summarize representative approaches and standard evaluations. Learning from human feedback includes preference-based RL (Christiano et al., 2017; Sadigh et al., 2017), policy shaping via TAMER and COACH (Knox and Stone, 2009; Cederborg et al., 2015), inverse RL formulations (Ng and Russell, 2000; Ziebart et al., 2008), and imitation through DAgger and offline pipelines (Ross et al., 2011; Fu et al., 2020). Benchmarks and datasets include Meta-World, D4RL, and Robomimic, with success rate, normalized score, and preference agreement reported (Yu et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2020; Mandlekar et al., 2021; Ibarz et al., 2018). This overview highlights the diversity of assumptions about teacher availability and expertise. We then study how supervision frequency interacts with exploration under fixed energy budgets. Experiments examine preference drift and latency using controlled human-in-the-loop simulations. Results inform interface design choices for reliable deployment on real manipulators. We provide practical checklists for collecting reproducible human feedback at scale.

spans: Causal discovery for time series includes Granger causality and VAR tests (Granger, 1969; Lutkepohl, 2005), constraint-based procedures like PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), score-based formulations such as NOTEARS and DYNOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018; Pamfil et al., 2020), and neural approaches including TCDF and attention models (Nauta et al., 2019; Tank et al., 2018). Evaluations report Hamming distance, accuracy, and predictive fit on benchmarks like simulated SCMs, Tübingen cause-effect pairs, and datasets from finance and physiology (Mooij et al., 2016; Peters et al., 2017; Shimizu et al., 2011).
Lacks synthesis.document: Many scientific questions hinge on understanding cause and effect in dynamical systems. Observational time series provide partial evidence that must be reconciled with plausible mechanisms. Interventions are often infeasible, which elevates the role of algorithmic discovery from data alone. Yet temporal dependence and hidden confounding complicate identifiability and evaluation. Assumptions about stationarity, sparsity, and noise can yield conflicting conclusions across methods. We review modeling choices that govern learning and testing in this setting. Causal discovery for time series includes Granger causality and VAR tests (Granger, 1969; Lutkepohl, 2005), constraint-based procedures like PCMCI (Runge et al., 2019), score-based formulations such as NOTEARS and DYNOTEARS (Zheng et al., 2018; Pamfil et al., 2020), and neural approaches including TCDF and attention models (Nauta et al., 2019; Tank et al., 2018). Evaluations report Hamming distance, accuracy, and predictive fit on benchmarks like simulated SCMs, Tübingen cause-effect pairs, and datasets from finance and physiology (Mooij et al., 2016; Peters et al., 2017; Shimizu et al., 2011). These ingredients set the stage for assessing strengths and failure modes in realistic pipelines. We then examine how lag selection and filtering influence estimated graphs under resource constraints. A modular benchmarking harness allows swapping causal learners while preserving preprocessing. The analysis emphasizes dataset shift between synthetic processes and field data. We report sensitivity to sampling rate and intervention frequency in semi-synthetic scenarios. Our results clarify the trade-offs between predictive accuracy and structural fidelity. Recommendations are provided to guide practitioners toward robust causal modeling of temporal phenomena.

spans: Early temporal knowledge graph models emphasize static embeddings extended with time-aware components, including TransE and TransH variants (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014), TTransE (Jiang et al., 2016), HyTE (Dasgupta et al., 2018), and diachronic representations such as DE-SimplE and ATiSE (Goel et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019), alongside benchmark datasets like ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, YAGO11k and GDELT.

Graph neural approaches introduce temporal message passing or recurrent updates, with TGAT and TGN capturing event streams (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020), DyRep modeling dynamic interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019), Know-Evolve employing temporal point processes (Trivedi et al., 2017), and R-GCN extensions for time granularity (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Seo et al., 2019), evaluated on datasets such as ICEWS18, GDELT, WIKI-Math, and Temporal-WN18, coupled with negative sampling and contrastive objectives.
Lacks synthesis.document: Temporal knowledge graphs capture evolving relations among entities observed as timestamped facts. Modeling such dynamics is essential for forecasting future events and reasoning over incomplete histories. Early temporal knowledge graph models emphasize static embeddings extended with time-aware components, including TransE and TransH variants (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014), TTransE (Jiang et al., 2016), HyTE (Dasgupta et al., 2018), and diachronic representations such as DE-SimplE and ATiSE (Goel et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019), alongside benchmark datasets like ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, YAGO11k and GDELT. Despite rich resources, predictive accuracy remains sensitive to temporal granularity and event sparsity. Prior work also notes challenges in capturing long-range dependencies and nonstationary patterns across time. In downstream applications, temporal reasoning drives forecasting for finance, recommendation, and cyber-security. However, existing architectures frequently trade expressivity for scalability when deployed at web scale. Self-attention and recurrent modules each incur distinct costs under continuous-time assumptions. This motivates examining representations that share parameters across time while preserving event causality. We study how inductive biases interact with observational gaps under realistic temporal noise. We further analyze calibration, uncertainty estimates, and robustness against distributional shifts. Graph neural approaches introduce temporal message passing or recurrent updates, with TGAT and TGN capturing event streams (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020), DyRep modeling dynamic interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019), Know-Evolve employing temporal point processes (Trivedi et al., 2017), and R-GCN extensions for time granularity (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Seo et al., 2019), evaluated on datasets such as ICEWS18, GDELT, WIKI-Math, and Temporal-WN18, coupled with negative sampling and contrastive objectives. These systems provide complementary building blocks yet exhibit heterogeneous assumptions about time. Our analysis clarifies when discrete bins, intervals, or continuous clocks better preserve signal. We also benchmark data-efficient training regimes with limited supervision across periods. Finally, we outline open challenges in evaluation protocols for temporal generalization.

spans: Foundational multimodal sentiment benchmarks and models include CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI with aligned text, audio, and vision (Zadeh et al., 2016; Zadeh et al., 2018), IEMOCAP for acted emotions (Busso et al., 2008), CH-SIMS for Chinese sentiment (Yu et al., 2020), and early fusion, late fusion, and tensor fusion architectures (Poria et al., 2017; Zadeh et al., 2017; Mai et al., 2020).

Transformer-based models extend cross-modal attention and representation disentanglement, with MulT introducing modality-specific attentions (Tsai et al., 2019), MISA promoting invariant subspaces (Hazarika et al., 2020), MAG-BERT incorporating acoustic-visual guidance (Rahman et al., 2020), and cross-modal pretraining drawing on CLIP, VisualBERT, and ViLBERT (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019), alongside contrastive objectives and pseudo-labeling on unannotated videos and speech as well as curriculum strategies for stable multimodal alignment.
Lacks synthesis.document: Sentiment analysis across modalities seeks to infer attitudes from language, prosody, and visual cues. The interplay among modalities raises issues of synchronization, noise, and differing temporal resolutions. Early pipelines relied on handcrafted features but struggled with domain transfer and speaker variability. Foundational multimodal sentiment benchmarks and models include CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI with aligned text, audio, and vision (Zadeh et al., 2016; Zadeh et al., 2018), IEMOCAP for acted emotions (Busso et al., 2008), CH-SIMS for Chinese sentiment (Yu et al., 2020), and early fusion, late fusion, and tensor fusion architectures (Poria et al., 2017; Zadeh et al., 2017; Mai et al., 2020). While these resources support progress, heterogeneity in annotation and alignment complicates comparison. Recent advances leverage deep contextual encoders that better capture semantics and paralinguistic signals. However, robustness to accents, lighting, and occlusions remains limited under real-world conditions. Furthermore, interpretability is a recurring concern for high-capacity multimodal architectures. We focus on consistent training protocols and fair evaluation sets to assess generalization. Our framework isolates modality contributions using controlled masking and perturbations. We also quantify calibration and uncertainty under covariate shift and missing channels. Transformer-based models extend cross-modal attention and representation disentanglement, with MulT introducing modality-specific attentions (Tsai et al., 2019), MISA promoting invariant subspaces (Hazarika et al., 2020), MAG-BERT incorporating acoustic-visual guidance (Rahman et al., 2020), and cross-modal pretraining drawing on CLIP, VisualBERT, and ViLBERT (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019), alongside contrastive objectives and pseudo-labeling on unannotated videos and speech as well as curriculum strategies for stable multimodal alignment. Despite these developments, reproducibility barriers persist due to undocumented preprocessing and code drift. We provide standardized splits and scripts to reduce variance across runs and seeds. Additionally, we analyze the trade-offs between alignment precision and compute overhead. Finally, we summarize open questions in scalable, privacy-preserving multimodal sentiment learning.

spans: Classical federated optimizers address client heterogeneity through aggregation and variance reduction, including FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD and FedNova to mitigate drift (Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), and evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, CIFAR-10, and StackOverflow benchmarks using non-IID partitions and partial participation protocols with compression, quantization, and adaptive client selection heuristics.

Privacy-preserving training incorporates record-level noise addition and cryptographic protocols, covering DP-SGD with moments or Rényi accountants (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017), secure aggregation for encrypted updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017), client-level DP via sampling and clipping (Geyer et al., 2017; Balle et al., 2018), teacher-student frameworks like PATE (Papernot et al., 2017), and analyses of privacy amplification, composition bounds, and utility trade-offs across realistic participation rates under heterogeneity, stragglers, and adversarial behaviors.
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data across devices. This paradigm promises stronger privacy and regulatory compliance while leveraging distributed compute. Yet practical deployments face statistical heterogeneity, systems volatility, and communication bottlenecks. Classical federated optimizers address client heterogeneity through aggregation and variance reduction, including FedAvg and FedProx (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD and FedNova to mitigate drift (Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), and evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, CIFAR-10, and StackOverflow benchmarks using non-IID partitions and partial participation protocols with compression, quantization, and adaptive client selection heuristics. Such diversity creates optimization instability and fairness concerns among clients. Communication-efficient methods reduce uplink load but risk objective inconsistency across rounds. Moreover, personalization strategies must balance global utility with local adaptation. We examine how algorithmic choices interact with participation dynamics and resource limits. Our study characterizes convergence, fairness, and robustness under controlled non-IID regimes. We also evaluate energy and latency footprints for edge and mobile devices. Threat models include honest-but-curious servers, malicious clients, and byzantine aggregators. Privacy-preserving training incorporates record-level noise addition and cryptographic protocols, covering DP-SGD with moments or Rényi accountants (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017), secure aggregation for encrypted updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017), client-level DP via sampling and clipping (Geyer et al., 2017; Balle et al., 2018), teacher-student frameworks like PATE (Papernot et al., 2017), and analyses of privacy amplification, composition bounds, and utility trade-offs across realistic participation rates under heterogeneity, stragglers, and adversarial behaviors. Despite progress, practitioners lack guidance on safe configurations under tight budgets. We provide principled defaults and diagnostics for auditing privacy-utility frontiers. Finally, we discuss open benchmarks that reflect real-world churn and device diversity. These insights inform deployment choices for privacy-aware distributed learning at scale.

spans: Data-to-code and NL-to-code research spans sequence transduction and grammar-aware decoding, including Seq2Tree and TranX parsers (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Yin and Neubig, 2018), pre-trained models such as CodeBERT, CodeT5, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and benchmarks like CoNaLa, CONCODE, Spider, and APPS for varying language and specification styles and diverse execution environments.

Neural code generation further explores executability-aware training and constrained decoding, with type-driven generation and syntax masks (Raychev et al., 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017), execution-guided decoding for SQL and Python (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019), large language models evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021), and unit-test feedback, beam reranking, and repair heuristics for improving functional correctness under limited prompts and tool access constraints.
Lacks synthesis.document: Program synthesis from natural language promises to accelerate software development and education. However, language ambiguity and underspecified intent pose fundamental challenges for code generation. Systems must align surface descriptions with latent APIs, types, and execution semantics. Data-to-code and NL-to-code research spans sequence transduction and grammar-aware decoding, including Seq2Tree and TranX parsers (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Yin and Neubig, 2018), pre-trained models such as CodeBERT, CodeT5, and PLBART (Feng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021), and benchmarks like CoNaLa, CONCODE, Spider, and APPS for varying language and specification styles and diverse execution environments. Despite significant gains, models often memorize snippets rather than infer algorithmic structure. Moreover, practical tasks require integration with libraries, environments, and version constraints. Evaluation therefore benefits from test-driven metrics beyond lexical similarity. We examine how prompt formatting and retrieval affect grounding to available tools. Our study probes generalization across domains, tasks, and resource budgets. We analyze error modes including type misuse, naming drift, and off-by-one loops. We also consider safety risks such as insecure patterns and license leakage. Neural code generation further explores executability-aware training and constrained decoding, with type-driven generation and syntax masks (Raychev et al., 2014; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017), execution-guided decoding for SQL and Python (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019), large language models evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021), and unit-test feedback, beam reranking, and repair heuristics for improving functional correctness under limited prompts and tool access constraints. These ingredients illustrate the breadth of approaches without resolving deployment caveats. We consequently assess robustness to environment shifts and package updates. Finally, we discuss interactive revisions that combine intent elicitation with verification. The goal is to clarify priorities for reliable, responsible code generation in practice.

spans: Unsupervised domain adaptation for vision has explored feature alignment and adversarial objectives, including DANN, ADDA, and MCD (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Saito et al., 2018), correlation alignment via DeepCORAL (Sun and Saenko, 2016), and image-level translation such as CyCADA (Hoffman et al., 2018), commonly evaluated on GTA5→Cityscapes and SYNTHIA→Cityscapes benchmarks with synthetic-to-real shifts and challenging weather variations.

More recent work studies source-free and test-time adaptation, employing batch-norm updates, entropy minimization, and pseudo-label self-training, with TENT and CoTTA addressing online shifts (Wang et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020), SHOT transferring via information maximization and prototypes (Liang et al., 2020), and domain stylization and frequency mixing for robustness (Jackson et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020), evaluated on Cityscapes-C, ACDC, BDD100K, and WildDash under corruptions and seasonal changes and illumination shifts.
Lacks synthesis.document: Domain shifts degrade visual recognition systems when training and deployment distributions diverge. Semantic segmentation is particularly sensitive due to pixel-level supervision and structured outputs. Unsupervised domain adaptation for vision has explored feature alignment and adversarial objectives, including DANN, ADDA, and MCD (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Saito et al., 2018), correlation alignment via DeepCORAL (Sun and Saenko, 2016), and image-level translation such as CyCADA (Hoffman et al., 2018), commonly evaluated on GTA5→Cityscapes and SYNTHIA→Cityscapes benchmarks with synthetic-to-real shifts and challenging weather variations. Although these strategies reduce discrepancies, strong residual gaps remain in urban driving scenes. Label noise and class imbalance further complicate adaptation across rare categories. Efficiency constraints also limit the applicability of heavy generative components. Moreover, evaluation protocols vary widely across works and hinder fair comparison. We focus on data-efficient adaptation that preserves safety-critical performance. Our analysis disentangles appearance, frequency, and context cues affecting transfer. We measure robustness to sensor degradation and adverse weather encountered in practice. We also examine uncertainty estimation to flag unreliable predictions after shift. More recent work studies source-free and test-time adaptation, employing batch-norm updates, entropy minimization, and pseudo-label self-training, with TENT and CoTTA addressing online shifts (Wang et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020), SHOT transferring via information maximization and prototypes (Liang et al., 2020), and domain stylization and frequency mixing for robustness (Jackson et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020), evaluated on Cityscapes-C, ACDC, BDD100K, and WildDash under corruptions and seasonal changes and illumination shifts. Despite advances, deployment conditions exhibit nonstationary and compound shifts over time. We assess continual and on-the-fly updates compatible with real-time constraints. Finally, we study failure cases and calibration to guide risk-aware decisions. The broader aim is practical adaptation strategies for autonomous perception systems.
