spans: Early multimodal sentiment benchmarks such as CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019) provide aligned audio, visual, and text streams. Fusion strategies include early fusion with concatenated features (Ngiam et al., 2011), tensor fusion networks (Zadeh et al., 2017), and graph-based interactions (Zadeh et al., 2018b). Pretrained encoders like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and ResNet (He et al., 2016) are commonly used for modality-specific representations. Evaluation commonly reports F1 and MAE on MOSI and MOSEI and accuracy on MELD, with additional robustness checks under missing modality protocols (Liu et al., 2021) and noisy audio settings (Tsai et al., 2019). Benchmarks.
Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer attitudes by integrating language, vision, and acoustics. The field has progressed from isolated feature engineering to end to end learning with pretrained components. Early studies emphasized alignment between streams to avoid temporal drift. Recent work emphasizes robustness under noisy or missing modalities. Industry interest has grown due to applications in media analytics and conversational agents. Despite progress, modeling dependencies across modalities remains challenging. Prior surveys describe fusion taxonomies that motivate comparative evaluation. Joint training often improves recognition but can amplify dataset biases. Cross modal attention has emerged as a practical inductive bias. However, many architectures still struggle to handle variable length speech segments. Further, deployment constraints require compact models with stable calibration. These considerations frame the scope of this study. Early multimodal sentiment benchmarks such as CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019) provide aligned audio, visual, and text streams. Fusion strategies include early fusion with concatenated features (Ngiam et al., 2011), tensor fusion networks (Zadeh et al., 2017), and graph-based interactions (Zadeh et al., 2018b). Pretrained encoders like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and ResNet (He et al., 2016) are commonly used for modality-specific representations. Evaluation commonly reports F1 and MAE on MOSI and MOSEI and accuracy on MELD, with additional robustness checks under missing modality protocols (Liu et al., 2021) and noisy audio settings (Tsai et al., 2019). Benchmarks. While these resources enable standardization, they also encourage narrow optimization on limited domains. Multilingual coverage remains limited for both speech and text streams. Visual frames often capture facial expressions but ignore gestural context from the torso and hands. Recent multimodal transformers improve cross stream reasoning yet increase memory pressure substantially. Distillation and pruning can mitigate cost but risk degrading affective nuance. Handling missing modalities during inference remains an open problem for practitioners. A complementary concern is annotation subjectivity in sentiment labels. Disagreement among annotators reflects genuine ambiguity rather than mere noise. Modeling uncertainty alongside predictions can support better downstream decisions. Calibration metrics help quantify reliability under distribution shift. However, calibration trade offs interact with fusion strategies in nontrivial ways. This interaction motivates careful assessment under realistic failure modes. In addition to accuracy, latency and energy consumption shape acceptable designs. Edge devices impose strict budgets that conflict with large cross modal stacks. Compression tailored to heterogeneous signals may preserve critical cues. Training regimens that mix self supervision with labeled objectives can leverage abundant raw video and audio. Domain adaptation is necessary when transferring from acted corpora to in the wild data. Ethical considerations include privacy, consent, and potential stigmatization. We therefore adopt a comprehensive evaluation that reflects deployment realities. Our comparisons emphasize robustness under ablations of each sensory stream. We also analyze calibration and uncertainty to illuminate confidence allocation. The findings inform principled design choices for multimodal affective systems.

spans: Traffic forecasting benchmarks frequently rely on sensor networks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), PeMSD7 (Song et al., 2020), and PeMSD4/8 (Guo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal architectures include diffusion convolutional recurrent networks DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), spatiotemporal graph convolution STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and attention based ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019). Later developments explore multivariate time series graph learners like MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020), dynamic graph discovery with GTS (Shang et al., 2021), and transformer encoders with temporal positional biases (Cideron et al., 2022). Evaluation commonly reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across horizons 15, 30, and 60 minutes, using fixed train validation test splits and z score normalization protocols.
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban mobility systems require accurate short term forecasts to support routing and control. Data from fixed sensors and probe vehicles provides dense temporal coverage but uneven spatial density. Graph modeling aligns naturally with road topology and flow constraints. Early linear models struggled to capture nonlinear propagation of congestion. Deep spatiotemporal networks partially address this gap with learned dynamics. Nevertheless, stability and interpretability remain central concerns for operators. Exogenous signals like weather and events introduce shifts that stress purely autoregressive models. Incorporating side information can reduce error yet risks leakage in operational pipelines. Robust evaluation must therefore reflect closed loop deployment conditions. Additionally, missing data from faulty sensors is routine rather than exceptional. Imputation tied to topology can mitigate bias in historical aggregates. These considerations guide the methodological design space. Traffic forecasting benchmarks frequently rely on sensor networks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), PeMSD7 (Song et al., 2020), and PeMSD4/8 (Guo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal architectures include diffusion convolutional recurrent networks DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), spatiotemporal graph convolution STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and attention based ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019). Later developments explore multivariate time series graph learners like MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020), dynamic graph discovery with GTS (Shang et al., 2021), and transformer encoders with temporal positional biases (Cideron et al., 2022). Evaluation commonly reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across horizons 15, 30, and 60 minutes, using fixed train validation test splits and z score normalization protocols. Despite standardization, reported gains often depend on bespoke preprocessing choices. Differences in detrending and scaling can overshadow architectural effects. Reproducibility thus benefits from transparent pipelines and shared code. Memory usage also scales with graph size, limiting city wide deployment without partitioning. Learned graph structures can adapt to latent correlations but may drift under shifts. Detecting such drift early helps maintain predictive fidelity. Uncertainty quantification is increasingly valued by traffic managers. Calibrated intervals allow conservative control when congestion risk spikes. Methods based on ensembles or heteroscedastic objectives provide complementary tradeoffs. However, interval sharpness declines when exogenous shock features are unavailable. Sensor maintenance policies interact with algorithmic robustness in subtle ways. Longitudinal studies are needed to characterize degradation over seasons. Fairness and equity also surface when predictions inform pricing or policing. Biased errors can amplify burdens on already congested neighborhoods. Auditable models with interpretable attributions enhance accountability. Simple baselines remain important anchors for performance claims. Benchmarks that incorporate realistic missingness patterns better reflect operations. Evaluation under closed loop receding horizon control clarifies practical value. Our study emphasizes reproducibility and robustness across diverse urban topologies. We implement a unified data pipeline aligned with operational constraints. The models are assessed under missing data and distribution shift regimes. We analyze calibration alongside cost aware metrics relevant to city stakeholders. The results inform deployment strategies for reliable predictive traffic systems.

spans: Federated learning in medical imaging has been studied using datasets such as BraTS for brain tumor segmentation (Menze et al., 2015), ISIC for dermoscopic lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CheXpert or MIMIC-CXR for chest radiography (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019). Optimization methods include FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and adaptive FedOpt variants (Reddi et al., 2021). Personalization approaches explore FedBN (Li et al., 2021), meta learning based pFedMe (Dinh et al., 2020), and local adaptation layers for site specific distribution shifts. Privacy mechanisms span secure aggregation (Bonawitz et al., 2017), differential privacy via DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), and homomorphic encryption based protocols for protected updates deployment.
Lacks synthesis.document: Healthcare institutions increasingly seek collaborative learning without centralizing data. Regulatory and ethical constraints motivate privacy preserving training paradigms. Federated learning allows models to learn across sites while keeping data local. Medical imaging offers a high value testbed due to abundant modalities. However, cross site heterogeneity complicates optimization and evaluation. Imaging protocols, devices, and populations vary significantly across hospitals. Communication efficiency is a practical bottleneck for clinical networks. Limited uplink capacity and intermittent connectivity affect synchronization. Asynchronous updates and compression can mitigate overhead at potential accuracy costs. Moreover, secure aggregation introduces additional latency and computation. Trust models influence what telemetry can be shared beyond gradients. These factors shape the feasible design space for deployments. Federated learning in medical imaging has been studied using datasets such as BraTS for brain tumor segmentation (Menze et al., 2015), ISIC for dermoscopic lesions (Codella et al., 2019), and CheXpert or MIMIC-CXR for chest radiography (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019). Optimization methods include FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and adaptive FedOpt variants (Reddi et al., 2021). Personalization approaches explore FedBN (Li et al., 2021), meta learning based pFedMe (Dinh et al., 2020), and local adaptation layers for site specific distribution shifts. Privacy mechanisms span secure aggregation (Bonawitz et al., 2017), differential privacy via DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016), and homomorphic encryption based protocols for protected updates deployment. Despite methodological breadth, clinical validation often lags behind technical advances. Prospective studies are rare due to coordination overhead and risk management. Distribution drift across seasons and policy changes can erode performance. Federated monitoring that tracks calibration and fairness can alert operators to issues. Label scarcity in some sites impedes personalization effectiveness. Weak supervision from reports or heuristics can partially close this gap. Another challenge is privacy budgeting under repeated training rounds. Composition of privacy loss must account for hyperparameter sweeps and audits. Hybrid protocols can combine secure aggregation with local differential privacy. Such combinations raise questions about compatibility with personalization. Hardware support for encryption influences feasibility in resource constrained clinics. Transparent governance is needed to align incentives across institutions. Evaluation should encompass utility, privacy, and systems costs. Beyond accuracy, clinicians value interpretability and robustness to artifacts. Reporting calibration gives insight into risk aware decision thresholds. Site wise breakdowns reveal where transfer improves or harms outcomes. Stress tests with out of scanner artifacts verify resilience in practice. Releasing standardized pipelines can accelerate reproducible progress. Our study targets heterogeneity, personalization, and privacy under operational constraints. We compare optimizers and normalization strategies across realistic hospital mixes. We incorporate communication budgets to reflect common clinical networks. The analysis highlights tradeoffs between utility and regulatory compliance. Recommendations are derived to guide procurement and trustworthy deployment.

spans: Semi-supervised text classification commonly evaluates on benchmarks such as AG News, IMDb, Yelp Polarity, and Amazon Reviews (Zhang et al., 2015; Maas et al., 2011; McAuley and Leskovec, 2013). Representative methods include Virtual Adversarial Training VAT (Miyato et al., 2017), Mean Teacher temporal ensembling (Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), MixMatch and RemixMatch adaptations for NLP (Berthelot et al., 2019; 2020), and FixMatch style pseudo labeling with confidence thresholds (Sohn et al., 2020). Unsupervised data augmentation UDA applies back translation or TF-IDF word replacements to enforce consistency (Xie et al., 2020). Recent approaches leverage pretrained language models with consistency losses, entropy minimization, and confidence based sampling across unlabeled corpora drawn from Wikipedia, OpenWebText, and newswire for categorization. Datasets like RCV1 and Yahoo Answers.
Lacks synthesis.document: Semi supervised learning seeks to exploit large unlabeled corpora with minimal annotation. In text classification, unlabeled web data is abundant but noisy. Consistency regularization and pseudo labeling are two dominant paradigms. Pretrained language models change the bias variance tradeoff of these schemes. Hyperparameter sensitivity and domain shift remain significant hurdles. This section reviews relevant progress and evaluation practice. Handling bias and nonstationarity is essential for credible evaluation. Recommendations are often presented as slates rather than single items. Slate level constraints complicate credit assignment across positions. User satisfaction depends on diversity, novelty, and fairness metrics. These objectives conflict with pure click maximization. Policy learning must therefore account for multiple goals and constraints. Robustness under delayed feedback and exposure bias is also crucial. Research on reinforcement learning for recommendation references datasets including MovieLens 1M and 20M (Harper and Konstan, 2016), the Yahoo! R6A news click logs (Li et al., 2011), RecSys Challenge datasets, and KuaiRec or MIND for large scale interactions (Gao et al., 2020). Algorithmic baselines span policy gradient REINFORCE (Williams, 1992), value based DQN variants for slates such as SlateQ (Ie et al., 2019), deterministic actor critic DDPG for continuous features (Lillicrap et al., 2016), and multi objective ranking with constrained bandits (Agarwal et al., 2014). Offline evaluation methods use inverse propensity scoring IPS, self normalized IPS, doubly robust estimators, fitted Q evaluation, and Criteo off policy benchmarks (Dudik et al., 2014; Le et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2021). For validation. Despite progress, offline metrics may not predict online impact reliably. Reward misspecification can mask churn or fatigue effects. Counterfactual estimators degrade under extrapolation and covariate shift. Simulators provide safe exploration but risk model bias. Balancing exploitation and exploration requires principled constraints. Safety layers can veto actions that violate content policies. Personalization granularity affects both value and stability. High capacity user embeddings adapt quickly but may overfit sparse histories. Meta learning can transfer structure across cohorts with similar intents. Contextual modeling of session dynamics captures short term preferences. Temporal discounting moderates the influence of delayed outcomes. Multi objective optimization clarifies tradeoffs among goals. Fairness considerations require constraints on exposure and opportunity. Calibration helps align reported uncertainty with observed outcomes. Diversification reduces redundancy and encourages discovery. Interpretability fosters trust among stakeholders and content providers. Reproducible pipelines help vet improvements before live testing. Public leaderboards should include risk aware metrics and resource costs. Our study examines off policy learning for slates under practical constraints. We analyze how estimators behave under varying propensities and clipping. We compare ranking architectures for stability and sample efficiency. The experiments include ablations on slate size and objectives. We summarize guidance for safe and effective reinforcement learning recommenders.

spans: Prior studies encompass diffusion convolutional networks, attention-based spatiotemporal models, and benchmarks such as METR-LA, PeMS-BAY, PeMSD7, and TaxiBJ, alongside sequence models like STGCN and DCRNN, graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021), temporal convolutional networks, and hybrid pipelines integrating ARIMA baselines and LSTMs (Zhang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and additional baselines.
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban traffic forecasting under irregular sensor topology remains a central challenge for intelligent transportation systems. Accurate predictions must integrate spatial dependencies among roads and temporal dynamics arising from congestion waves and incidents. Despite progress, current models often trade expressiveness for efficiency, hindering deployment in city-scale networks. Graph neural networks have emerged as a compelling paradigm, yet their receptive fields and dynamic adjacency modeling vary widely across implementations. Moreover, reported gains are sensitive to dataset splits and look-ahead horizons, complicating fair comparison across studies. Prior studies encompass diffusion convolutional networks, attention-based spatiotemporal models, and benchmarks such as METR-LA, PeMS-BAY, PeMSD7, and TaxiBJ, alongside sequence models like STGCN and DCRNN, graph transformers (Ying et al., 2021), temporal convolutional networks, and hybrid pipelines integrating ARIMA baselines and LSTMs (Zhang et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019), and additional baselines. However, most evaluations emphasize static graphs and station-level series, leaving robustness to topology shifts and sensor outages underexplored. We focus on this robustness axis and study how uncertainty-aware graph construction mediates performance when sensors fail or new links appear. Our approach integrates probabilistic edge sampling with lightweight diffusion to bound worst-case error at fixed latency. We provide a standardized protocol for rolling-origin validation across multiple horizons and controlled topology perturbations. Experiments across four public datasets demonstrate consistent gains in degraded networks without sacrificing real-time throughput.

spans: Prior work spans pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder–decoders such as BART, PEGASUS, T5, and ProphetNet, multilingual variants like mBART-50 and mT5, and evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, NEWSROOM, Gigaword, and WikiLingua, with training regimens that leverage sentence reordering, gap-sentence objectives, and reinforcement learning from ROUGE (See et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and MASS.
Lacks synthesis.document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense documents into fluent, faithful digests that preserve salient information. While neural models achieve impressive ROUGE, they can hallucinate unsupported facts and struggle with cross-lingual generalization. Practical deployments demand controllable generation and calibrated confidence to support editorial workflows. Current multilingual systems often rely on English-centric pretraining, limiting coverage for low-resource languages and domain-specific styles. Moreover, standard metrics reward n-gram overlap rather than factual grounding, obscuring model weaknesses in reliability. Prior work spans pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder–decoders such as BART, PEGASUS, T5, and ProphetNet, multilingual variants like mBART-50 and mT5, and evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, NEWSROOM, Gigaword, and WikiLingua, with training regimens that leverage sentence reordering, gap-sentence objectives, and reinforcement learning from ROUGE (See et al., 2017; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020), and MASS. These developments have established strong baselines but leave open questions about factual control across languages and domains. We address this gap by coupling entity-grounded planning with multilingual consistency regularization during fine-tuning. The planner predicts cross-lingual entity and relation frames, which guide a constrained decoder to avoid unsupported claims. We further introduce reference-free factuality scoring that correlates with human judgments across languages. Experiments on three news corpora and two low-resource benchmarks show improved faithfulness with minimal loss in fluency.

spans: Existing approaches include U-Net and V-Net architectures, residual and attention U-Nets, and strong baselines such as nnU-Net and DeepLabV3+, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, LiTS, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, with augmentations like MixUp, CutMix, and intensity standardization, and training protocols leveraging Dice and focal losses (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017).
Lacks synthesis.document: Automated segmentation of 3D medical images is crucial for diagnosis, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring. Brain tumor delineation, in particular, demands precision across heterogeneous scanners and acquisition protocols. However, models often degrade under distribution shifts driven by site-specific reconstruction and annotation variability. Recent self-supervised and domain generalization methods mitigate some variability but increase training complexity and compute. Furthermore, many pipelines rely on heavy test-time augmentation that limits throughput in clinical workflows. Existing approaches include U-Net and V-Net architectures, residual and attention U-Nets, and strong baselines such as nnU-Net and DeepLabV3+, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, LiTS, and the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, with augmentations like MixUp, CutMix, and intensity standardization, and training protocols leveraging Dice and focal losses (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017). While these systems deliver high Dice scores, performance can be brittle when contrast changes or small lesions appear. We explore a calibration-first design that prioritizes reliable uncertainty estimates and robust small-structure recovery. Our method couples scale-consistent normalization with topology-preserving losses to stabilize predictions across scanners. We introduce a lightweight adaptation module enabling rapid on-site tuning without full retraining. On multi-center studies, the approach reduces false positives and improves boundary accuracy under severe domain shift.

spans: Research covers contextual bandits and slate-aware RL with DQN, DDQN, DDPG, and policy-gradient methods, sequence recommenders such as GRU4Rec, SASRec, and BERT4Rec, simulators and logs like RecSim, OpenBanditDataset, and RecoGym, and benchmarks including MovieLens, Amazon Reviews, KuaiRec, and RecSys Challenge datasets (Hidasi et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Takeda and Joachims, 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Modern recommender systems must personalize sequences of items while respecting feedback delays and exposure constraints. Static ranking models optimize myopic click metrics and can induce filter bubbles that harm long-term utility. Reinforcement learning offers a principled framework to trade short-term reward for user retention and diversity. Yet deploying RL in recommendation is challenging due to confounding, partial observability, and safety constraints. Off-policy evaluation and constrained learning are therefore critical components of any practical pipeline. Research covers contextual bandits and slate-aware RL with DQN, DDQN, DDPG, and policy-gradient methods, sequence recommenders such as GRU4Rec, SASRec, and BERT4Rec, simulators and logs like RecSim, OpenBanditDataset, and RecoGym, and benchmarks including MovieLens, Amazon Reviews, KuaiRec, and RecSys Challenge datasets (Hidasi et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Takeda and Joachims, 2020). Despite this breadth, reliable evaluation under distribution shift and position bias remains unresolved. We develop a counterfactual learner with pessimistic reward shaping to bound regret under logging mismatch. A structural propensity model corrects for position exposure while preserving variance through shrinkage priors. We further enforce catalog and user-level constraints using Lagrangian relaxation to avoid degenerate policies. Extensive simulations and logged-policy studies indicate improved safety and stable gains over baseline bandits.

spans: Published systems span statistical detectors like STL and Isolation Forest, deep generative models such as LSTM-VAE, DAGMM, Donut, USAD, and OmniAnomaly, correlation-aware architectures including MSCRED and GDN, and benchmarks like NAB, Yahoo S5, UCR archive, SMAP, MSL, SWaT, and WADI, with evaluation protocols based on point- and range-level F1 (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor streams is critical for reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Practical detectors must react quickly while maintaining low false-alarm rates under concept drift. Correlations across sensors and variable delays make the problem especially challenging for black-box methods. Moreover, scarce labeled anomalies complicate supervised learning and hyperparameter selection. Recent self-supervised objectives improve representation quality but often ignore temporal alignment uncertainty. Published systems span statistical detectors like STL and Isolation Forest, deep generative models such as LSTM-VAE, DAGMM, Donut, USAD, and OmniAnomaly, correlation-aware architectures including MSCRED and GDN, and benchmarks like NAB, Yahoo S5, UCR archive, SMAP, MSL, SWaT, and WADI, with evaluation protocols based on point- and range-level F1 (Hundman et al., 2018; Su et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2021). Despite solid progress, scoring rules remain brittle when anomalies span variable durations and partially overlap. We address this by modeling anomalies as latent intervals with uncertain boundaries and soft coverage. A differentiable alignment kernel permits end-to-end training that naturally accommodates phase shifts. We introduce an evaluation protocol that rewards early detection while penalizing alarm floods. Experiments on industrial logs demonstrate consistent improvements and favorable trade-offs under drift.

Recent models exploit graph structure and temporal signals to improve predictive accuracy while reducing latency. However, the literature spans diverse assumptions about topology dynamics and exogenous factors. To contextualize our approach, we summarize representative strands in spatiotemporal learning for traffic data. Classical graph convolutional forecasters build fixed road-network operators combined with sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), and Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), with evaluations on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. Dynamic-graph methods estimate time-varying affinities or attention over sensors, including AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), STGNN (Song et al., 2020), and ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), alongside spatiotemporal transformers such as StemGNN (Cao et al., 2020), STTN (Xu et al., 2021), and Autoformer-style decompositions (Wu et al., 2021), frequently benchmarked on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Exogenous-aware pipelines incorporate weather, events, or calendar features via multi-branch encoders or gating (Hoang et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Cini et al., 2022), while robustness and transfer are explored through distribution shift benchmarks and meta-learning over cities (Oreshkin et al., 2020; Yoon et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Imani and White, 2018), with recent datasets extending to PEMS08, TaxiBJ, and LOOP-Seattle benchmarks. Despite these advances, practical deployment confronts nonstationary topology updates and sensor failures. Real-world agencies also require calibrated uncertainty to support risk-aware control. Existing evaluations seldom stress long-horizon rollouts under structural perturbations. We therefore investigate forecasting under topology drift with explicit treatment of missing nodes and edges. Our formulation unifies temporal imputation, structure inference, and prediction within a coherent training objective. We design a bidirectional diffusion prior over graphs to regularize edge evolution across horizons. A lightweight adapter integrates exogenous covariates without retraining the backbone on every shift. We analyze robustness through targeted attacks, progressive road closures, and random sensor dropouts. Extensive experiments on public traffic benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains in accuracy, calibration, and stability.

However, cross-lingual supervision remains uneven and domain shifts can degrade content selection. Prior work explores multi-task setups and translation-based augmentation with mixed outcomes. Source segmentation and discourse cues are also critical when summaries span multiple articles. We therefore survey key resources and modeling trends. Data augmentation and filtering strategies span round-trip translation, extractive oracles, and factuality-driven reranking (Feng et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2022; Goyal and Durrett, 2021), and evaluation commonly uses ROUGE with emerging factuality metrics like QAGS, FACTCC, and SummaC, plus human judgments of consistency and adequacy. Yet automatic scores often fail to capture cross-lingual stylistic preferences. We revisit faithfulness under constrained computation budgets typical for local newsrooms. spans: Canonical optimizers aggregate local steps using FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), with extensions such as FedProx (Li et al., 2020), Scaffold (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020) to mitigate heterogeneity, typically evaluated on EMNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Shakespeare or StackOverflow. Differential privacy mechanisms adapt DP-SGD and DP-Adam with per-example clipping and calibrated Gaussian noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Bu et al., 2020; Kairouz et al., 2021), and privacy accounting leverages the Moments Accountant, Rényi DP, or privacy amplification by subsampling (Mironov, 2017; Wang et al., 2019; Feldman et al., 2018). Communication efficiency employs secure aggregation, sparsification, and quantization using SecAgg protocols, sketching, and sign-based updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Alistarh et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Bernstein et al., 2018), with lossy codecs aligned to wireless constraints and adaptive client sampling. Federated compression also studies error feedback, top-k sparsity, and dynamic bitrate allocation under latency budgets. Device availability models consider stragglers, dropout, and diurnal patterns during client selection, and participation incentives.

Canonical optimizers aggregate local steps using FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), with extensions such as FedProx (Li et al., 2020), Scaffold (Karimireddy et al., 2020), and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020) to mitigate heterogeneity, typically evaluated on EMNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Shakespeare or StackOverflow. Differential privacy mechanisms adapt DP-SGD and DP-Adam with per-example clipping and calibrated Gaussian noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Bu et al., 2020; Kairouz et al., 2021), and privacy accounting leverages the Moments Accountant, Rényi DP, or privacy amplification by subsampling (Mironov, 2017; Wang et al., 2019; Feldman et al., 2018). Communication efficiency employs secure aggregation, sparsification, and quantization using SecAgg protocols, sketching, and sign-based updates (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Alistarh et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Bernstein et al., 2018), with lossy codecs aligned to wireless constraints and adaptive client sampling. Federated compression also studies error feedback, top-k sparsity, and dynamic bitrate allocation under latency budgets. Device availability models consider stragglers, dropout, and diurnal patterns during client selection, and participation incentives. Nevertheless, formal guarantees often degrade utility under high heterogeneity and limited participation. Moreover, production workloads must respect memory, power, and intermittent connectivity on edge devices. We study privacy-utility trade-offs under realistic participation patterns and strict computation budgets. Our formulation decouples local training from privacy calibration through adaptive clipping schedules. We introduce a server-side accountant that conditions on observed client volumes per round. The method integrates secure aggregation with lightweight variance reduction. We provide a sensitivity analysis that links gradient norms to privacy loss. Experiments cover vision and language benchmarks with varying participation skew. Results show consistent accuracy improvements under fixed privacy budgets. Ablations highlight robustness to shifts in client sampling and gradient sparsity.

Lacks synthesis.document: Protein structure prediction has undergone rapid progress through geometric learning and large-scale sequence modeling. Advances in multiple sequence alignments and end-to-end differentiable pipelines have shifted accuracy ceilings. Nevertheless, efficiency and interpretability remain core concerns for broader scientific use. Our work examines sequence-only approaches under computational constraints typical of small labs. We begin by outlining representative models, datasets, and evaluation protocols. Sequence embeddings trained at scale have emerged as effective surrogates for evolutionary information. Concurrently, template-based augmentation persists in settings with limited homologs. Benchmarks vary in family diversity and domain boundaries. We distinguish supervised from self-supervised paradigms, with implications for transferability. We also summarize common data filtering practices to reduce leakage. The following references frame the landscape prior to our experiments. End-to-end predictors include AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., 2021), RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021), and trRosetta (Yang et al., 2020), supported by MSA search tools and templates from PDB and PDB70. Protein language models trained on UniRef50, UniRef90, and BFD underpin ESM-1b, ESM-2, and ESMFold (Rives et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2023; Baek et al., 2021), alongside ProtTrans models such as ProtBert and ProtT5 (Elnaggar et al., 2021), and MSA Transformer for alignment-aware representations (Rao et al., 2021), with pretraining objectives ranging from masked token recovery to contact prediction. Evaluation settings span CASP targets, CAMEO weekly assessments, and curated domains from SCOPe and CATH (Kryshtafovych et al., 2021; Haas et al., 2018; Chandonia et al., 2019; Orengo et al., 1997), while side tasks cover secondary structure, contact or distance maps, and binding site annotation using TAPE, FLIP, and ProteinNet (Rao et al., 2019; Berman et al., 2000; Rives et al., 2021), with metrics including lDDT, TM-score, and GDT-TS, for both monomers and complexes across diverse folds. Despite impressive accuracy, sequence-only predictors still struggle on large oligomeric assemblies. We therefore study compute-aware adaptations that prioritize reliability over marginal accuracy on heavy targets.

Lacks synthesis.document: Learning dexterous manipulation from interaction promises robust generalization beyond scripted control. Model-free reinforcement learning frequently employs off-policy and on-policy algorithms such as DQN (Mnih et al., 2015), DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018), SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), and PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), with visual encoders from CPC, BYOL, and R3M (Oord et al., 2018; Grill et al., 2020; Nair et al., 2022). Model-based approaches and world models include PETS (Chua et al., 2018), PlaNet and Dreamer variants (Hafner et al., 2019; Hafner et al., 2020), and MBPO (Janner et al., 2019), while sim-to-real techniques span domain randomization, domain adaptation, and system identification (Tobin et al., 2017; James et al., 2019; Chebotar et al., 2019). Offline and imitation pipelines leverage DAgger and behavior cloning, as well as BCQ, CQL, and IQL (Ross et al., 2011; Fujimoto et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020; Kostrikov et al., 2021), with evaluations on D4RL, Meta-World, RoboSuite, ManiSkill, RLBench, and BridgeData benchmarks, and language-conditioned policies like CLIPort, RT-1, and RT-2 for instruction following and generalization tasks. Despite notable progress, sample efficiency and deployment reliability remain open issues in robotics. Real systems must handle delays, resets, and safety constraints during exploration. Sparse rewards and long horizons further challenge credit assignment in contact-rich skills. We focus on scalable data reuse under realistic reset and safety limitations. Our approach integrates offline pretraining with cautious online improvement using uncertainty cues. We design a curriculum over task difficulty driven by success-rate estimates. A safety shield enforces action constraints without altering policy gradients. The vision stack uses frozen encoders with lightweight adaptation to new scenes. We evaluate on simulated and real tabletop manipulation with standardized hardware. Training budgets reflect practical lab settings rather than unlimited rollouts. The results demonstrate improved stability and fewer catastrophic failures across seeds. Ablations attribute gains to calibrated exploration and reuse of suboptimal demonstrations.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates algorithms, datasets, and techniques without linking them to a specific gap or to the authors’ aims, indicating a lack of synthesis.

spans: Early static graph methods include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), emphasizing neighborhood aggregation and attention on fixed topologies. Temporal extensions explore discrete snapshots and continuous-time events via TGAT (Xu et al., 2020), TGN (Rossi et al., 2020), DySAT (Sankar et al., 2020), and JODIE-style recurrent embeddings (Kumar et al., 2019). Benchmark datasets frequently used are Reddit, Wikipedia, and MOOC for user–item streams, and ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, and WIKI-Talk for event prediction. Training protocols commonly adopt negative sampling, time-respecting evaluation splits, and metrics such as Hits@k, MRR, and AUC across inductive and transductive settings. Common implementations note memory modules, neighbor caching, mini-batch event processing, and encoders for nodes, edges, and timestamps in codebases and repositories.
Lacks synthesis.document: Temporal link prediction on dynamic graphs is central to forecasting interactions in recommendation, finance, and communication networks. Models must encode evolving structure while maintaining scalability and robustness to distribution shift. Prior surveys highlight the trade-offs between snapshot-based and event-driven formulations but leave open questions on memory design and evaluation fidelity. We focus on settings where updates arrive continuously and node attributes are sparse. This context creates challenges for stable training, negative sampling bias, and fair comparison across datasets. Early static graph methods include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), emphasizing neighborhood aggregation and attention on fixed topologies. Temporal extensions explore discrete snapshots and continuous-time events via TGAT (Xu et al., 2020), TGN (Rossi et al., 2020), DySAT (Sankar et al., 2020), and JODIE-style recurrent embeddings (Kumar et al., 2019). Benchmark datasets frequently used are Reddit, Wikipedia, and MOOC for user–item streams, and ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, and WIKI-Talk for event prediction. Training protocols commonly adopt negative sampling, time-respecting evaluation splits, and metrics such as Hits@k, MRR, and AUC across inductive and transductive settings. Common implementations note memory modules, neighbor caching, mini-batch event processing, and encoders for nodes, edges, and timestamps in codebases and repositories. Despite extensive progress, reported improvements are often confounded by inconsistent temporal splits and varying neighbor sampling budgets. Our study revisits temporal supervision signals and disentangles architectural choices from data ordering effects. We provide a standardized evaluation protocol that aligns message passing depth, memory refresh rates, and batch construction. We analyze latency, peak memory, and throughput to assess practicality in streaming environments. We also examine failure modes under cold-start and partial observability, highlighting where dynamic representations degrade. The resulting insights clarify which components materially drive gains and which ones mainly reflect easier evaluation conditions.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists models, datasets, protocols, and implementation details without connecting them to the paper's objectives or stating a specific gap, thereby lacking synthesis.

spans: Monolingual summarization benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), and Gigaword (Rush et al., 2015) provide abstractive training corpora with varying compression ratios. Multilingual or cross-lingual settings commonly draw on WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Sciaretta et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and VoxSum-style collections for broader language coverage. Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020), often combined with back-translation, pivoting, or pseudo-parallel mining using LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) or LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Evaluation typically reports ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore, with works incorporating COMET, chrF, or ratings for faithfulness and fluency language directions.
Lacks synthesis.document: Cross-lingual summarization seeks to condense information while changing the language of expression. This dual objective complicates supervision, alignment, and evaluation under budget-limited annotation. Prior research varies in how it balances translation quality with content selection. We examine transfer regimes that rely on multilingual pre-training and limited target-language signals. Monolingual summarization benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), and Gigaword (Rush et al., 2015) provide abstractive training corpora with varying compression ratios. Multilingual or cross-lingual settings commonly draw on WikiLingua (Ladhak et al., 2020), MLSum (Sciaretta et al., 2020), XL-Sum (Hasan et al., 2021), and VoxSum-style collections for broader language coverage. Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models include mBART (Liu et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020), often combined with back-translation, pivoting, or pseudo-parallel mining using LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) or LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Evaluation typically reports ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and BERTScore, with works incorporating COMET, chrF, or ratings for faithfulness and fluency language directions. Despite the availability of resources, comparability remains hindered by differing tokenizers and normalization choices. Length mismatch between source and target languages further induces metric sensitivity. We therefore systematize preprocessing and report confidence intervals to contextualize scores. Our analysis isolates the contribution of cross-lingual alignment from summarization-specific inductive biases. We also probe factual consistency with language-agnostic entailment models. Error taxonomy distinguishes lexical hallucinations from structural omissions across language pairs. Finally, we discuss data curation practices that reduce topical skew and duplication. These considerations frame our subsequent modeling and evaluation choices.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates datasets, models, and metrics in a list-like manner without explaining how they relate to the paper’s aims or identifying a concrete research gap.

spans: Fairness formulations include demographic parity, equalized odds, equal opportunity, and calibration, with algorithmic treatments spanning pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies (Kamiran and Calders, 2012; Hardt et al., 2016; Zafar et al., 2017; Narasimhan, 2018). Recommendation backbones frequently use matrix factorization and modern neural variants such as BPR (Rendle et al., 2009), NCF (He et al., 2017), NGCF (Wang et al., 2019), and LightGCN (He et al., 2020). Empirical studies draw on public datasets like MovieLens, LastFM, Yelp, Amazon Books, and KuaiRec, accompanied by sensitive attribute proxies including gender, age, and geography. Evaluation practice typically reports NDCG and Recall at cutoffs, along with fairness measures like exposure disparity, equalized odds gaps, popularity bias indices, and calibration error. Statistical tests include bootstrap intervals and permutation baselines.
Lacks synthesis.document: Algorithmic recommendation systems shape access to information, culture, and opportunity. As deployment expands, concerns about disparate impact across user groups intensify. A growing literature examines how ranking and exposure dynamics intersect with sensitive attributes. We study user-level utility and provider-level exposure under operational constraints. We formalize auditing scenarios that reflect platform feedback loops and budgeted exploration. Fairness formulations include demographic parity, equalized odds, equal opportunity, and calibration, with algorithmic treatments spanning pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies (Kamiran and Calders, 2012; Hardt et al., 2016; Zafar et al., 2017; Narasimhan, 2018). Recommendation backbones frequently use matrix factorization and modern neural variants such as BPR (Rendle et al., 2009), NCF (He et al., 2017), NGCF (Wang et al., 2019), and LightGCN (He et al., 2020). Empirical studies draw on public datasets like MovieLens, LastFM, Yelp, Amazon Books, and KuaiRec, accompanied by sensitive attribute proxies including gender, age, and geography. Evaluation practice typically reports NDCG and Recall at cutoffs, along with fairness measures like exposure disparity, equalized odds gaps, popularity bias indices, and calibration error. Statistical tests include bootstrap intervals and permutation baselines. Yet existing reports vary in how they subsample users and normalize popularity, complicating comparison. We develop a controlled evaluation harness that fixes candidate pools and exposure caps. Our analysis quantifies the trade-off surface between accuracy and exposure regularization. We characterize sensitivity to missing or noisy attributes via synthetic perturbations. We also report cost impacts measured as impressions and latency on real workloads. These results inform practical fairness interventions for large-scale recommenders.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span presents a catalog of definitions, algorithms, datasets, metrics, and tests without tying them to the paper’s perspective or articulating the specific problem being addressed.

spans: Relation extraction has been studied with convolutional and recurrent encoders using position features, including CNN (Zeng et al., 2014), PCNN (Zeng et al., 2015), BiLSTM-ATT (Zhou et al., 2016), and dependency-based Tree-LSTM and GCN variants (Tai et al., 2015; Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017). Pre-trained language models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) dominate recent benchmarks with task-specific classification heads and span pooling. Common datasets include SemEval 2010 Task 8, TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), ChemProt (Krallinger et al., 2017), and DDIExtraction (Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013), with distant supervision variants derived from UMLS and MeSH. Evaluation protocols typically report F1, precision, and recall, with splits and ablations over entity masking or lexical cues.
Lacks synthesis.document: Biomedical relation extraction connects mentions of entities to structured knowledge. Accurate extraction supports curation, search, and hypothesis generation in life sciences. The task is challenging due to long sentences, nested mentions, and ambiguous triggers. Researchers have explored architectures that leverage syntax and domain pre-training. Distant supervision remains attractive yet introduces noisy labels and context drift. Relation extraction has been studied with convolutional and recurrent encoders using position features, including CNN (Zeng et al., 2014), PCNN (Zeng et al., 2015), BiLSTM-ATT (Zhou et al., 2016), and dependency-based Tree-LSTM and GCN variants (Tai et al., 2015; Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017). Pre-trained language models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) dominate recent benchmarks with task-specific classification heads and span pooling. Common datasets include SemEval 2010 Task 8, TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), ChemProt (Krallinger et al., 2017), and DDIExtraction (Herrero-Zazo et al., 2013), with distant supervision variants derived from UMLS and MeSH. Evaluation protocols typically report F1, precision, and recall, with splits and ablations over entity masking or lexical cues. Despite strong benchmarks, reproducibility is hampered by tokenization choices and entity normalization differences. Comparisons are further complicated by inconsistent negative sampling and candidate generation steps. We focus on sentence-level extraction with controlled preprocessing and transparent hyperparameters. Our study examines generalization across out-of-corpus datasets and entity overlap regimes. We also quantify the contribution of syntactic pruning versus pre-training scale. Finally, we discuss annotation artifacts and their interaction with model cues. These considerations guide an evaluation protocol for realistic biomedical text mining.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span compiles models, datasets, and evaluation practices but does not connect them to a guiding argument or specify what gap the current work investigates.

spans: Large-scale vision–language pretraining uses web corpora like Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), SBU Captions (Ordonez et al., 2011), YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016), and LAION-400M (Schuhmann et al., 2021) to learn aligned representations. Representative models include CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), BLIP (Li et al., 2022), and OFA (Wang et al., 2022). Common downstream evaluations cover MSCOCO and Flickr30k for retrieval and captioning, using contrastive fine-tuning, cross-entropy training, and CIDEr optimization with self-critical sequence training (Rennie et al., 2017). Metrics typically include Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10 for retrieval and BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE-L, SPICE, and CIDEr for captioning, with standard Karpathy test splits. Encoders span ResNet backbones and Vision Transformers.
Lacks synthesis.document: Vision–language models enable retrieval, grounding, and captioning by coupling visual and textual representations. Despite remarkable progress, evaluation disparities blur which components truly matter at scale. We revisit pretraining objectives and data curation through a unified lens. Our focus is on image–text retrieval and captioning under constrained compute. We aim to clarify how dataset size, supervision type, and architecture interact in practice. Large-scale vision–language pretraining uses web corpora like Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), SBU Captions (Ordonez et al., 2011), YFCC100M (Thomee et al., 2016), and LAION-400M (Schuhmann et al., 2021) to learn aligned representations. Representative models include CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), BLIP (Li et al., 2022), and OFA (Wang et al., 2022). Common downstream evaluations cover MSCOCO and Flickr30k for retrieval and captioning, using contrastive fine-tuning, cross-entropy training, and CIDEr optimization with self-critical sequence training (Rennie et al., 2017). Metrics typically include Recall@1, Recall@5, Recall@10 for retrieval and BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE-L, SPICE, and CIDEr for captioning, with standard Karpathy test splits. Encoders span ResNet backbones and Vision Transformers. However, reported gains often conflate pretraining scale with evaluation shortcuts and prompt selection. We standardize tokenization, resolution, and text normalization while holding batch size and schedule fixed. Our experiments measure sensitivity to caption noise, duplicate pairs, and near-duplicate images. We analyze compute–performance trade-offs to guide feasible configurations for practitioners. We also study transfer to domain-shifted retrieval scenarios with limited labels. This setup clarifies which ingredients consistently improve performance across tasks and datasets.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates datasets, models, procedures, metrics, and architectures without linking them to the paper’s objectives or articulating a motivating gap, hence it lacks synthesis.

spans: Recent work on aligning large language models has emphasized instruction tuning corpora, supervised fine-tuning procedures, and preference modeling protocols across open and proprietary systems. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions, FLAN, and T0 assemble heterogeneous task templates and input–output pairs drawn from classification, extraction, and generation tasks. Self-Instruct expands seed prompts into synthetic demonstrations, while Alpaca and Dolly provide open replications of instruction-following behaviors using curated or distilled data. Vicuna, OpenAssistant, and RedPajama incorporate conversational traces, multi-turn dialogues, and web-scale corpora to enrich coverage of user intents. Evaluation suites including MMLU, BIG-bench, and HELM report broad performance indicators, while targeted benchmarks like TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, ARC, and GSM8K probe reasoning, commonsense, factuality, and mathematical competence. Traditional reading comprehension corpora such as SQuAD, CoQA, and QuAC continue to be used for instruction reformulations and few-shot prompting templates. Preference optimization methods span reinforcement learning from human feedback with PPO-style objectives, direct preference optimization, and rejection sampling over pairwise comparisons. Human annotation protocols vary from expert raters to crowd workers, with interface designs controlling rubric clarity, calibration examples, and quality assurance checks across multiple rounds. Open evaluations like Chatbot Arena collect head-to-head votes, whereas controlled audits in the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models framework emphasize standardized prompts, transparent setups, and error taxonomies. Multilingual and cross-domain instruction sets appear in BLOOM, XGLM resources, and FLORES-based evaluations targeting translation coverage and typological diversity. Safety-oriented corpora assemble refusal templates, harmlessness criteria, and toxicity filters that have been deployed in RLHF pipelines and guardrail classifiers. Tool-augmented instruction datasets reflect retrieval augmentation with Wikipedia passages, calculator calls for GSM-style arithmetic, code execution for program synthesis, and plugin invocations for browsing or scheduling. Few-shot collections like MetaICL and Natural Instructions define unified input formats, canonical verbalizers, and demonstration sampling heuristics to stabilize cross-task generalization. Data curation practices cite deduplication, contamination screening against test sets, and mixture weighting to balance summarization, translation, reasoning, and dialogue activities. Public recipes mention tokenization normalizers, instruction header formats, Markdown wrapping of exemplars, and stop-sequence control for safer termination behavior during decoding. Cross-evaluation studies report shifts when models are tuned on FLAN v2 versus mixtures that add CoT expositions, with deliberate reasoning traces drawn from GSM8K, StrategyQA, and OpenBookQA. Prompt formatting libraries adopt YAML-like schemas, task taxonomies, and evaluation harnesses that interface with Hugging Face datasets, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local deployment stacks. Curated preference datasets include Anthropic HH, Sparrow-style dialogues, Stanford Human Preferences, and OpenAI WebGPT comparisons assembled through browser-augmented question answering. Reporting practices list win rates, Elo ratings, exact match, ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, and calibration plots using temperature sweeps and reliability diagrams under varied decoding settings. Open baselines cite LLaMA, T5, and GPT-NeoX checkpoints widely used.

Despite the breadth of resources, there remains no consensus on how to isolate domain transfer effects from instruction mixture composition in professional settings. We therefore design controlled ablations and task-centric audits to clarify the specific choices that matter for reliability, fidelity, and maintainability in high-stakes deployments.

Lacks synthesis.document: Tool-augmented large language models promise automation for data analysis, but the surrounding instruction-following literature is fragmented across datasets, training procedures, and evaluations. Recent work on aligning large language models has emphasized instruction tuning corpora, supervised fine-tuning procedures, and preference modeling protocols across open and proprietary systems. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions, FLAN, and T0 assemble heterogeneous task templates and input–output pairs drawn from classification, extraction, and generation tasks. Self-Instruct expands seed prompts into synthetic demonstrations, while Alpaca and Dolly provide open replications of instruction-following behaviors using curated or distilled data. Vicuna, OpenAssistant, and RedPajama incorporate conversational traces, multi-turn dialogues, and web-scale corpora to enrich coverage of user intents. Evaluation suites including MMLU, BIG-bench, and HELM report broad performance indicators, while targeted benchmarks like TruthfulQA, HellaSwag, ARC, and GSM8K probe reasoning, commonsense, factuality, and mathematical competence. Traditional reading comprehension corpora such as SQuAD, CoQA, and QuAC continue to be used for instruction reformulations and few-shot prompting templates. Preference optimization methods span reinforcement learning from human feedback with PPO-style objectives, direct preference optimization, and rejection sampling over pairwise comparisons. Human annotation protocols vary from expert raters to crowd workers, with interface designs controlling rubric clarity, calibration examples, and quality assurance checks across multiple rounds. Open evaluations like Chatbot Arena collect head-to-head votes, whereas controlled audits in the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models framework emphasize standardized prompts, transparent setups, and error taxonomies. Multilingual and cross-domain instruction sets appear in BLOOM, XGLM resources, and FLORES-based evaluations targeting translation coverage and typological diversity. Safety-oriented corpora assemble refusal templates, harmlessness criteria, and toxicity filters that have been deployed in RLHF pipelines and guardrail classifiers. Tool-augmented instruction datasets reflect retrieval augmentation with Wikipedia passages, calculator calls for GSM-style arithmetic, code execution for program synthesis, and plugin invocations for browsing or scheduling. Few-shot collections like MetaICL and Natural Instructions define unified input formats, canonical verbalizers, and demonstration sampling heuristics to stabilize cross-task generalization. Data curation practices cite deduplication, contamination screening against test sets, and mixture weighting to balance summarization, translation, reasoning, and dialogue activities. Public recipes mention tokenization normalizers, instruction header formats, Markdown wrapping of exemplars, and stop-sequence control for safer termination behavior during decoding. Cross-evaluation studies report shifts when models are tuned on FLAN v2 versus mixtures that add CoT expositions, with deliberate reasoning traces drawn from GSM8K, StrategyQA, and OpenBookQA. Prompt formatting libraries adopt YAML-like schemas, task taxonomies, and evaluation harnesses that interface with Hugging Face datasets, OpenAI-compatible APIs, or local deployment stacks. Curated preference datasets include Anthropic HH, Sparrow-style dialogues, Stanford Human Preferences, and OpenAI WebGPT comparisons assembled through browser-augmented question answering. Reporting practices list win rates, Elo ratings, exact match, ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore, and calibration plots using temperature sweeps and reliability diagrams under varied decoding settings. Open baselines cite LLaMA, T5, and GPT-NeoX checkpoints widely used. Yet the landscape does not separate tool-use competence from general following ability or dataset-specific artifacts. We introduce a disentangled assessment that factors tools, prompts, and feedback to identify which elements predict robust analytical performance.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span compiles an extensive list of datasets, benchmarks, and alignment methods without relating them to the paper’s aims or drawing a synthesizing conclusion, hence it lacks synthesis.

spans: Prior studies on abstractive summarization evaluate systems on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, Gigaword, and Multi-News, with baselines such as BART, T5, Pegasus, and ProphetNet. Benchmarks and reports detail setups across these corpora and models (Nallapati et al., 2016; Narayan et al., 2018; Rush, 2015; Fabbri et al., 2019; Koupaee and Wang, 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Abstractive summarization has advanced rapidly with large pretrained sequence-to-sequence models. Despite improved fluency, models often fail to generalize when the source domain diverges from supervised training data. This gap is acute in settings where stylistic, topical, or length distributions shift across sources. Prior domain adaptation strategies examine fine-tuning schedules, vocabulary control, and contrastive alignment. Data selection and filtering have also been explored to reduce spurious correlations. However, systematic assessments of robustness across multiple domains remain inconsistent. Transfer methods may overfit high-resource news corpora and degrade on procedural or scientific texts. Evaluation further conflates factuality with surface overlap, obscuring true generalization behavior. Few studies jointly analyze style, content, and compression across heterogeneous domains. A clearer view of datasets and baselines is necessary to contextualize cross-domain performance. Prior studies on abstractive summarization evaluate systems on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, Gigaword, and Multi-News, with baselines such as BART, T5, Pegasus, and ProphetNet. Benchmarks and reports detail setups across these corpora and models (Nallapati et al., 2016; Narayan et al., 2018; Rush, 2015; Fabbri et al., 2019; Koupaee and Wang, 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). While these resources enable comparison, they vary in article length, summary style, and annotation policy. Differences in reference abstractive density and reporting bias complicate transfer conclusions. Moreover, pretrained checkpoints differ in tokenization and pretraining corpora, affecting adaptation dynamics. Recent measures of factual consistency indicate that domain shift interacts with hallucination tendencies. We focus on characterizing which adaptation levers matter under controlled distributional changes. Our framework isolates data regime, objective, and evaluation factors for fair comparisons. We introduce stratified domain splits to decouple style from topic. We also calibrate decoding to mitigate length and novelty artifacts. Experiments examine generalization on news, how-to, and multi-document domains under matched budgets. The results reveal trade-offs between stability and plasticity that inform future model selection.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists datasets and baseline models with citations but does not connect them to a specific argument, gap, or perspective, thereby lacking synthesis.

spans: Extensive benchmarks cover general and driving scenes, including PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, Cityscapes, BDD100K, and KITTI, alongside detectors such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, YOLOv3–v5, and DETR. Canonical papers describe standard protocols for these settings (Everingham et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Cordts et al., 2016; Geiger et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2017; Carion et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Object detection performance has improved dramatically with convolutional and transformer-based architectures. Despite these gains, robustness in real-world conditions such as rain, fog, and nighttime remains a challenge. Distribution shifts in illumination, contrast, and texture often degrade localization and classification. Data augmentation helps, yet can be insufficient without realistic sensor effects. Synthetic-to-real adaptation has emerged as a complementary direction. However, evaluation under adverse weather is fragmented across studies. Models are frequently compared under varying image resolutions and nonuniform test sets. This complicates attribution of robustness to architectural choices or training recipes. Furthermore, per-class sensitivity to weather differs from aggregate mAP trends. A consolidated view of mainstream datasets and detectors provides necessary context. Standardized metrics and protocols further ground claims about robustness. Extensive benchmarks cover general and driving scenes, including PASCAL VOC, MS COCO, Cityscapes, BDD100K, and KITTI, alongside detectors such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, YOLOv3–v5, and DETR. Canonical papers describe standard protocols for these settings (Everingham et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2014; Cordts et al., 2016; Geiger et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2017; Carion et al., 2020). Yet these benchmarks seldom isolate adverse weather with calibrated sensor models. Public subsets focusing on fog or rain are small and inconsistently annotated. We disentangle robustness sources by fixing data curation and varying augmentation and normalization. Our study quantifies cross-weather transfer while maintaining identical training budgets. We incorporate physics-inspired corruptions to approximate scattering and glare. Additionally, we analyze error taxonomies to pinpoint missed and hallucinated boxes. The protocol reports stratified mAP across weather categories and object scales. This design reduces confounds from resolution and batch-size choices. Findings highlight conditions under which architectural inductive biases confer resilience.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span provides a literature list of datasets and detectors with citations and protocols without linking them to the paper’s aims or articulating a gap, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Work on causal recommendation spans inverse propensity scoring, doubly robust estimators, off-policy bandits, and deconfounding approaches, with evaluations on MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Coat, and KuaiRec. Foundational and benchmark papers detail methods, evaluation metrics, datasets, and baselines used across these settings (Schnabel et al., 2016; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016; Saito, 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Offline evaluation of recommender systems suffers from bias induced by historical logging policies. Click and rating data encode exposure mechanisms that confound counterfactual performance estimates. Naïve replay or random holdout therefore overestimates familiar items and popular users. Causal estimators aim to debias evaluation and learning using propensity information. However, their effectiveness depends on accurate logging and overlap conditions. Practical systems also face high variance and covariate shift across time. Recent work studies regularization and representation learning to stabilize estimators. A clear map of benchmark tasks and estimators assists reproducible comparisons. It also clarifies which assumptions drive observed gains. We first summarize prevailing datasets and methods to situate our study. Work on causal recommendation spans inverse propensity scoring, doubly robust estimators, off-policy bandits, and deconfounding approaches, with evaluations on MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Coat, and KuaiRec. Foundational and benchmark papers detail methods, evaluation metrics, datasets, and baselines used across these settings (Schnabel et al., 2016; Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2016; Saito, 2020). Despite shared terminology, implementations vary in propensity clipping, renormalization, and bootstrapping. These choices materially affect ranking metrics and confidence intervals. We therefore adopt a unified pipeline that logs policies explicitly and enforces overlap checks. The pipeline separates estimator choice from representation learning to expose trade-offs. We evaluate stability under misspecified propensities and temporal drift. Our analysis emphasizes calibration of exposure models and the role of covariate coverage. We report paired-sample intervals to compare learning objectives fairly. We also study estimator sensitivity to user and item cold starts. Taken together, the results caution against purely variance-based model selection. They motivate benchmarks that foreground identifiability alongside accuracy.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates methods, datasets, and citations without connecting them to a motivating gap or the authors’ perspective, hence it lacks synthesis.

spans: Traffic forecasting benchmarks use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS03/04/07/08, with representative architectures including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, and T-GCN. Dataset and method papers document sensor graphs, split protocols, horizons, and metrics in these settings (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Short-term traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation and smart city planning. Spatiotemporal dependencies among sensors challenge classical time-series baselines. Graph neural networks integrate spatial topology with temporal dynamics for improved accuracy. Yet replication across datasets remains difficult due to inconsistent preprocessing. Reported gains often conflate architecture, horizon, and data curation effects. Robust conclusions require careful control of graph construction and normalization. Furthermore, nonstationary demand can invalidate fixed adjacency assumptions. We examine comparability across popular datasets and architectures to clarify reporting. Traffic forecasting benchmarks use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS03/04/07/08, with representative architectures including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, and T-GCN. Dataset and method papers document sensor graphs, split protocols, horizons, and metrics in these settings (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020). Nevertheless, preprocessing pipelines differ in filtering, imputation, and speed aggregation. This leads to measurable discrepancies even for identical models. We standardize sensor selection and temporal alignment to reduce variance. Our evaluation isolates architectural choices from data handling artifacts. We further examine robustness to missing data and sudden regime changes. To probe stationarity assumptions, we test adaptive adjacency schemes. Standard evaluation often overlooks calibration and uncertainty under limited evidence. Negative sampling strategies can distort precision–recall curves in skewed label spaces. Consistent baselines and datasets are needed to contextualize architectural improvements. In this study, we analyze model behavior under schema and surface shift. We compare terminology coverage, entity masking, and sentence length effects. Before detailing our setup, we briefly review commonly used corpora and baselines. We measure calibration error alongside F1 to reflect practical screening needs. Span masking ablations reveal reliance on entity surface forms. spans: Research on cross-lingual entity linking has drawn from multilingual embeddings, alignment resources, and knowledge base signals. Early approaches relied on translation or pivoting through English (McNamee et al., 2011; Ji et al., 2015), while recent methods exploit multilingual transformers mBERT and XLM-R for mention and candidate encoding (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Wu and Dredze, 2019). Lacks synthesis.document: Cross-lingual entity linking remains a core challenge for multilingual information access across the web. The task requires associating surface mentions in one language with canonical entries in a multilingual knowledge base. Practical deployments must cope with noisy text, limited resources, and shifting ontologies. Recent advances in multilingual pretrained models have changed prevailing design assumptions. However, gaps persist in robustness and transfer to truly low-resource domains. Prior work often assumes abundant anchor supervision or high-coverage alias tables. In contrast, many languages lack reliable mention dictionaries or clean cross-lingual signals. This motivates a reexamination of candidate generation and ranking under sparse supervision. We also consider the role of typological features in cross-lingual generalization. Despite impressive averages on public leaderboards, error patterns cluster by script, morphology, and domain. We study these factors by stratifying evaluation and disentangling candidate generation from mention encoding. Our analysis highlights how transliteration and subword coverage interact with retrieval constraints. We further quantify the impact of cross-lingual homographs on disambiguation. Building on these observations, we introduce a training protocol that regularizes retrieval with typology-aware constraints. The approach is designed to be architecture-agnostic and compatible with standard encoders. It also reduces reliance on noisy anchors by leveraging contextual cues in the target language. We complement intrinsic metrics with ablations that isolate each component. To ensure realistic results, we evaluate on held-out domains and unseen scripts. We further report efficiency statistics because candidate generation often dominates runtime. Finally, we release evaluation templates to encourage reporting beyond global accuracy. Together, these elements aim to clarify which cross-lingual signals matter most for reliable entity linking.

spans: Industrial time-series anomaly detection has been explored through statistical baselines, deep representation learning, and hybrid forecasting-error schemes. Classical detectors include ARIMA and STL decomposition with control charts (Box and Jenkins, 1970; Cleveland et al., 1990; Montgomery, 2009), while matrix profile and discord discovery provide subsequence outlier scores (Yeh et al., 2016; Keogh et al., 2005), and spectral density tests capture periodic deviations (Priestley, 1981; Parzen, 1962). Neural approaches use autoencoders, variational models, and networks trained on datasets such as NAB, Yahoo Webscope, Anomaly Archive, and MSL/SMAP from NASA (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Laptev et al., 2015; Dau et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), with attention mechanisms, reconstruction thresholds, and residuals as proxies (Malhotra et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting anomalies in industrial telemetry is critical for safety, cost control, and uptime. Streams are high-dimensional, irregular, and subject to regime shifts. False alarms erode operator trust while misses can be catastrophic. Despite progress, production settings rarely match academic benchmarks. Issues like covariate shift, missingness, and delayed labels complicate deployment. In addition, interpretability is required for root cause analysis and remediation. These constraints motivate a careful reassessment of modeling choices. We focus on methods that remain stable under distribution shift and sparse supervision. We also examine the interface between detection, explanation, and action.
