Neural approaches use autoencoders, variational models, and networks trained on datasets such as NAB, Yahoo Webscope, Anomaly Archive, and MSL/SMAP from NASA (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015; Laptev et al., 2015; Dau et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), with attention mechanisms, reconstruction thresholds, and residuals as proxies (Malhotra et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018; Audibert et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019). Prior evaluations often aggregate across regimes, masking brittle behaviors. We therefore stratify by operational modes and introduce shift-aware validation. Our framework decouples representation learning from threshold selection. We integrate censoring models to handle delayed or partial labels. To improve trust, we pair detectors with counterfactual explanations based on actionable features. The approach reduces spurious alerts while preserving early warnings. We present a recipe for calibrating decisions using limited fault tickets. Extensive ablations isolate the roles of ensembling and covariate normalization. We evaluate on proprietary plants and public datasets under identical protocols. Results highlight consistent benefits under simulated and real shifts. We also report operator feedback from shadow deployments. Collectively, the study maps a path toward dependable anomaly detection in industry.

spans: Research on adaptive traffic signal control spans optimization, control, and reinforcement learning. Classical systems use Webster formulations and max-pressure policies grounded in queueing theory (Webster, 1958; Varaiya, 2013; Wongpiromsarn et al., 2012), while actuated and SCOOT/SCATS deployments rely on heuristic detectors and online tuning (Hunt et al., 1982; Lowrie, 1992). Data-driven approaches apply Q-learning, actor–critic, and graph reinforcement learning with benchmarks such as SUMO, CityFlow, and NGSIM, alongside datasets like PeMS and METR-LA for demand patterns (Watkins and Dayan, 1992; Liang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Krajzewicz et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018), and incorporate reward designs based on delay, queue length, throughput, and emission surrogates (Wei et al., 2018; Chu et al., 2019; Arel et al., 2010; Zang et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban congestion imposes high costs on mobility, equity, and the environment. Traffic signals are a principal actuator for shaping network performance. Fixed-time plans struggle under variable demand and incidents. Adaptive control promises responsiveness but is challenging to tune at scale. Reinforcement learning offers a data-driven alternative with minimal modeling assumptions. However, variability in simulators and metrics hampers comparability. There is also tension between local and network-level objectives. We examine how design choices affect transfer from simulation to field. Our study isolates reward definitions, state encodings, and exploration schedules. Research on adaptive traffic signal control spans optimization, control, and reinforcement learning. Unlike single-intersection studies, we consider corridors with spillback and offsets. We analyze observability limits stemming from partial detection. We further assess robustness to sensor noise and demand shifts. To reduce variance, we adopt consistent random seeds and scenario splits. We incorporate lane-level constraints to prevent unsafe phase sequences. Our agents share parameters across intersections using graph message passing. We calibrate simulators using limited probe trajectory data. Evaluation uses out-of-sample scenarios and peak-period stress tests. We include counterfactual baselines derived from real timing plans. The results emphasize the importance of reward shaping for fairness. We release reproducible scripts and a policy auditing toolkit. These assets aim to foster transparent progress in learning-based traffic control.

spans: Argument mining in legal and regulatory texts has drawn from rhetorical structures, discourse parsing, pretrained encoders, and domain-adapted language models. Prior datasets include the European Court of Human Rights decisions, the IBM Debater corpus, the UK-2019 Statutory Instruments collection, and the CaseHOLD, LEDGAR, and MultiEURLEX resources (Alekseev et al., 2020; Bar-Haim et al., 2014; Chalkidis et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Tuggener et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2021b). Methods span sequence labeling, parsers, and graph neural networks over legal citation graphs, with baselines ranging from CRFs and BiLSTMs to Legal-BERT, Longformer, and DeBERTa variants, often trained with weak supervision from headings or citations (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015; Beltagy et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020; He et al., 2021; Petroni et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Legal practitioners face information overload as caselaw and regulations rapidly expand. Automated argument mining can support retrieval, drafting, and compliance analysis. However, legal discourse exhibits specialized structure and citation practices. Long documents and heterogeneity across jurisdictions pose additional difficulties. Prior methods often neglect domain shifts between courts and statutes. We target robust extraction of claims, evidence, and links in lengthy documents. Our perspective emphasizes transparency and auditability of outputs. We also consider alignment with practitioner workflows and ethical constraints. The discussion situates our approach relative to established corpora and baselines. Despite broad availability of pretrained legal encoders, annotation scarcity remains acute. We therefore combine limited expert labels with distantly supervised signals. A length-aware encoder preserves cross-reference structure without truncation. A hierarchical graph layer propagates constraints across sections. We incorporate citation functions as auxiliary prediction targets. The training objective balances extraction fidelity and structural coherence. Evaluation follows scenario-based splits across courts and time. We report inter-annotator agreement to contextualize upper bounds. Error analysis highlights failure modes around negation and exceptions. Ablations clarify contributions of graph structure and curriculum learning. We release templates and governance checklists to aid deployment. The goal is dependable, explainable argument mining for real legal practice.

spans: Automatic segmentation for radiotherapy has been approached with atlas-based registration, classical machine learning, and deep convolutional architectures. Public datasets include MICCAI Head-Neck Challenge, LiTS for liver and tumors, BraTS for brain gliomas, and MSD tasks covering multiple organs (Raudaschl et al., 2017; Bilic et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Simpson et al., 2019). Architectures span U-Net variants, V-Net, nnU-Net, and transformers for volumetric inputs, trained with losses such as Dice, focal, and boundary losses, using augmentations like elastic deformations and intensity scaling, and evaluated by Dice, HD95, and surface distance metrics (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Milletari et al., 2016; Isensee et al., 2021; Hatamizadeh et al., 2022; Sudre et al., 2017; Karimi et al., 2019; Taha and Hanbury, 2015). Hybrid pipelines integrate organ-at-risk ontologies and statistical shape priors.
Lacks synthesis.document: Radiotherapy planning relies on accurate delineation of organs and targets. Manual contouring is time intensive and prone to interobserver variability. Automation promises consistency and reduced workload for clinicians. Yet clinical adoption depends on robustness, speed, and uncertainty quantification. Imaging heterogeneity across scanners and centers complicates generalization. Protocol differences further impact label definitions and boundaries. We examine scalable segmentation under data shift and tight latency constraints. Our study emphasizes evaluation practices aligned with clinical endpoints. We also analyze calibration and failure detection for safe usage. The following overview positions our work among existing systems. Hybrid pipelines integrate organ-at-risk ontologies and statistical shape priors. Despite strong benchmarks, cross-site performance often drops markedly. We mitigate this by combining self-training and style normalization. A lightweight backbone enables deployment on standard workstation GPUs. We attach an evidential head to surface uncertainties in contour proposals. An efficient patching strategy preserves context without exceeding memory limits. We report time-to-plan and edit-distance metrics alongside Dice. Prospective testing uses fixed protocols and blinded clinician review. Qualitative audits identify common errors at tissue interfaces. Release materials include de-identified models and risk management documentation. The results point to practical routes for safe clinical integration.

spans: Foundational approaches span neural fingerprints and message passing networks across benchmarks such as QM9 and ZINC (Duvenaud et al., 2015; Kearnes et al., 2016; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Sterling and Irwin, 2015). Subsequent extensions include interaction networks, graph attention, and pretraining strategies evaluated on MoleculeNet, Tox21, and PCBA (Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Ying et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). Datasets such as FreeSolv and ESOL provide evaluation protocols.
Lacks synthesis.document: Graph neural networks have become central to computational chemistry for modeling molecules as relational structures. Predicting quantum and biophysical properties from graphs supports drug discovery and materials design at scale. Early formulations emphasized message passing as a way to summarize local neighborhoods efficiently. Despite notable progress, modeling long-range interactions and data scarcity remain open challenges in practice. We focus on the interplay between architectural bias and pretraining for small labeled regimes. Foundational approaches span neural fingerprints and message passing networks across benchmarks such as QM9 and ZINC (Duvenaud et al., 2015; Kearnes et al., 2016; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Sterling and Irwin, 2015). Subsequent extensions include interaction networks, graph attention, and pretraining strategies evaluated on MoleculeNet, Tox21, and PCBA (Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Ying et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). Datasets such as FreeSolv and ESOL provide evaluation protocols. However, these efforts reveal a tension between expressive power and sample efficiency when supervision is scarce. We revisit this trade-off by coupling long-range conditioning with chemically motivated masking during pretraining. Our formulation targets energy-related properties where conventional node-local encoding underperforms. We also quantify how self-supervised objectives interact with topology augmentation in limited data settings. To ensure fairness, we re-evaluate prior splits and control for scaffold leakage in all comparisons. This perspective clarifies when architectural complexity yields gains beyond data curation and augmentation. The resulting analysis motivates principled choices for graph design and pretraining that generalize across assays.

spans: Prior research catalogs fairness definitions and auditing protocols in recommender systems and learning to rank (Burke, 2017; Ekstrand et al., 2018; Diaz et al., 2020). Methodological advances include exposure-aware objectives, constrained optimization for demographic parity, and amortized post-processing of slates (Biega et al., 2018; Singh and Joachims, 2018; Mehrotra et al., 2018). Empirical studies often rely on MovieLens, lastfm-1k, and MSLR-WEB10K to benchmark trade-offs between accuracy and equity (Harper and Konstan, 2015; Celma, 2010; Qin and Liu, 2013). Datasets vary widely.
Lacks synthesis.document: Algorithmic recommendations increasingly mediate access to news, jobs, and entertainment. Fairness concerns arise when exposure and utility are unevenly distributed across users and items. Existing audits emphasize measurement frameworks tied to platform constraints and stakeholder goals. We examine ranking-time interventions that balance utility with constrained fairness budgets. Our formulation accounts for both provider and consumer level objectives. Datasets vary widely. Yet these datasets differ in exposure dynamics and provider granularity, complicating cross-study conclusions. We formalize a budgeted fairness controller that adjusts slate exposure while preserving utility guarantees. The controller integrates seamlessly with differentiable ranking layers used in large-scale systems. We derive generalization bounds that connect budget size to regret in constrained utility. Extensive simulations illustrate robustness to demand shocks and head–tail skew. Finally, we present a policy analysis for stakeholder choice under sensitive attribute uncertainty.

spans: Clinical imaging has benefited from convolutional and transformer backbones alongside domain-adaptive toolkits (Ronneberger et al., 2015; He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Cardoso et al., 2022). Large-scale datasets and benchmarks—MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert, and ChestX-ray14—enable standardized evaluation of thoracic findings and representation learning (Johnson et al., 2019; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017). Pretraining corpora vary in scale, labeling quality, and de-identification procedures across institutions and released subsets, and common splits support reproducible comparison across tasks with anatomical coverage.
Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal learning promises unified representations across medical images and associated text. Radiology report supervision offers rich semantic grounding that complements pixel-level labels. However, modality gaps and limited expert annotations impede generalization across institutions. We investigate cross-modal pretraining that aligns images with structured report cues. Our analysis emphasizes transfer to segmentation and classification under domain shift. This constraint mitigates spurious correspondences between stylistic phrases and image artifacts. We evaluate on segmentation transfer from chest radiographs to computed tomography of the thorax. Our ablations quantify contributions from entity filtering and view-normalized augmentations. We analyze robustness under institution shift using held-out hospitals with distinct acquisition protocols. spans: Recent models demonstrate impressive pass rates on standardized code benchmarks using transformer architectures trained on large repositories (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Rozière et al., 2023; Allal et al., 2023). Evaluation suites such as HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests assess functional correctness and generalization across programming problems (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Common training corpora include filtered snapshots of GitHub, permissively licensed datasets, and mined solutions from online judges and forums archives.
Lacks synthesis.document: Generative models of code increasingly serve as assistive tools for software development. They promise accelerated prototyping while raising questions about reliability and test adequacy. The gap between surface fluency and functional correctness remains a persistent challenge. We study synthesis-time constraints that encourage executable and generalizable programs across tasks. Our approach integrates lightweight verification with uncertainty-aware decoding. Recent models demonstrate impressive pass rates on standardized code benchmarks using transformer architectures trained on large repositories (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Rozière et al., 2023; Allal et al., 2023). Evaluation suites such as HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests assess functional correctness and generalization across programming problems (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Common training corpora include filtered snapshots of GitHub, permissively licensed datasets, and mined solutions from online judges and forums archives. Nonetheless, naive decoding often yields brittle programs that overfit to examples or rely on undefined behavior. We impose resource-aware test synthesis during sampling to penalize spurious shortcuts. The decoding policy coordinates unit tests, type checks, and coverage to filter candidates online. We analyze error modes across categories including control flow, data structure usage, and numerics. Our method complements compiler feedback by steering exploration rather than patching after generation. Experiments show consistent improvements under adversarial test augmentation and reduced reliance on reference solutions. These findings illuminate how lightweight verification can shape practical code generation systems.

spans: Recent models leverage contrastive or masked prediction over raw audio to learn robust features before fine-tuning (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2023). Benchmark corpora for pretraining and evaluation include Librispeech, Multilingual LibriSpeech, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice, spanning diverse languages and recording conditions (Panayotov et al., 2015; Pratap et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Phoneme inventories and grapheme conventions further complicate cross-lingual adaptation for end-to-end systems globally.
Lacks synthesis.document: Building accurate speech recognizers for low-resource languages remains a pressing problem. Scarcity of transcripts and pronunciation lexicons limits conventional supervised pipelines. Self-supervised pretraining and multilingual transfer offer a path to reduce annotation needs. However, domain mismatch and pronunciation variation degrade naive transfer to new languages. We explore data selection and representation adaptation for cross-lingual ASR in extreme low-resource regimes. Our framework jointly optimizes unsupervised objectives and pronunciation-aware fine-tuning. Recent models leverage contrastive or masked prediction over raw audio to learn robust features before fine-tuning (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2023). Phoneme inventories and grapheme conventions further complicate cross-lingual adaptation for end-to-end systems globally. To address these issues, we prioritize source data that matches target prosody and channel characteristics. A lightweight predictor estimates transferability using acoustic and text features without target labels. We incorporate phonotactic constraints during decoding to reduce implausible sequences in low-data settings. Our experiments span three typologically diverse languages with under one hour of supervision each. The analysis decomposes error by pronunciation class and borrowing patterns within code-switched segments. We report gains that persist across speakers and microphones, underscoring stable low-resource adaptation.

spans: Early multimodal detectors coupled convolutional text embeddings with visual backbones such as VGG-16 and ResNet-50, reporting results on FakeNewsNet and Twitter15/16 while relying on handcrafted features for propagation patterns. Subsequent work adopted attention-based fusion with BiLSTM or Transformer encoders, incorporating image regions from Faster R-CNN and language representations from BERT or RoBERTa, and evaluated on Weibo, GossipCop, and PolitiFact subsets. Memory networks and graph neural networks appeared next, linking posts, users, and images through GCN or GAT layers, sometimes augmented with knowledge bases like ConceptNet and VisualGenome to supply entity and relation grounding. Temporal rumor detection leveraged recurrent units and Hawkes process features, whereas contrastive formulations trained SimCLR variants or CLIP-style objectives on MM-COVID, Fakeddit, and PHEME to align semantics across modalities under weak supervision. Data augmentation strategies included back-translation, MixUp for embeddings, CutMix for images, and entity masking, with reported gains on CoAID, MediaEval Verifying Multimedia Use, and the SemEval-2020 Task 11 propaganda benchmark. Calibration and uncertainty estimation appeared via temperature scaling, MC dropout, and deep ensembles, accompanied by robustness tests using ImageNet-C style corruptions adapted for memes and adversarial text paraphrases generated by TextFooler and BAE. Explanations were reported through Grad-CAM, LIME, and Integrated Gradients, and datasets emphasizing cross-lingual phenomena introduced X-Fakeddit, PAN-X rumor corpora, and multilingual extensions of BuzzFace with aligned visual annotations and temporal shifts.

Prior surveys emphasize limitations in robustness and generalization under distribution shift, which informs our problem framing. We therefore examine alignment, calibration, and evidence attribution as levers for building reliable detectors. Early multimodal detectors coupled convolutional text embeddings with visual backbones such as VGG-16 and ResNet-50, reporting results on FakeNewsNet and Twitter15/16 while relying on handcrafted features for propagation patterns. Subsequent work adopted attention-based fusion with BiLSTM or Transformer encoders, incorporating image regions from Faster R-CNN and language representations from BERT or RoBERTa, and evaluated on Weibo, GossipCop, and PolitiFact subsets. We focus on disentangling correlation from causal signals by constraining modality interactions and validating with counterfactual tests. Our analysis targets distribution shift settings curated to reflect realistic platform interventions and content recycling. By aligning modeling choices with diagnostics that measure attribution faithfulness, we aim to clarify what constitutes genuine multimodal verification.

spans: Early neural recommenders combined matrix factorization with multilayer perceptrons, exemplified by NCF and NeuMF on MovieLens and Pinterest, while pairwise formulations adopted BPR losses to optimize top-k ranking metrics such as Recall and NDCG. Graph-based approaches then emerged, with NGCF propagating embeddings along user–item edges, PinSage scaling random walks for web-scale graphs, and LightGCN simplifying message passing by removing transformations and nonlinearities on Gowalla and Yelp. Inductive settings leveraged GraphSAGE and GAT to embed cold-start nodes from sampled neighborhoods, and temporal dynamics were modeled with TGAT, JODIE, and DySAT using time-stamped interactions from Reddit, LastFM, and Taobao. Self-supervised learning introduced contrastive objectives such as InfoNCE, as in SGL, SimGCL, and NCL, preparing augmented graphs through node dropout, edge perturbation, and random walk views on Amazon-Books and Alibaba iFashion. Knowledge-aware recommenders incorporated side information via KGCN, RippleNet, and MetaKG, enriching propagation with TransE-style relation embeddings and entity paths from Freebase, DBpedia, and ConceptNet. Exposure bias and popularity skew were addressed by IPS and SNIPS reweighting, MACR regularization, and causal formulations like CR and DICE, alongside counterfactual estimators trained on KuaiRec and simulated bandit logs. Lacks synthesis.document: Personalized recommendation has shifted toward graph-based formulations in response to the sparsity and heterogeneity of user-item interactions. However, inductive generalization and debiasing remain difficult when exposure and preference are entangled in historical logs. We frame the problem as learning robust relational signals while accounting for selection and popularity biases. To motivate our design, we review representative models and datasets spanning static, temporal, and counterfactual settings. Early neural recommenders combined matrix factorization with multilayer perceptrons, exemplified by NCF and NeuMF on MovieLens and Pinterest, while pairwise formulations adopted BPR losses to optimize top-k ranking metrics such as Recall and NDCG. Graph-based approaches then emerged, with NGCF propagating embeddings along user–item edges, PinSage scaling random walks for web-scale graphs, and LightGCN simplifying message passing by removing transformations and nonlinearities on Gowalla and Yelp. Inductive settings leveraged GraphSAGE and GAT to embed cold-start nodes from sampled neighborhoods, and temporal dynamics were modeled with TGAT, JODIE, and DySAT using time-stamped interactions from Reddit, LastFM, and Taobao. Knowledge-aware recommenders incorporated side information via KGCN, RippleNet, and MetaKG, enriching propagation with TransE-style relation embeddings and entity paths from Freebase, DBpedia, and ConceptNet. Exposure bias and popularity skew were addressed by IPS and SNIPS reweighting, MACR regularization, and causal formulations like CR and DICE, alongside counterfactual estimators trained on KuaiRec and simulated bandit logs. Together, these trends indicate a maturation of architectures but persistent vulnerability to confounding exposures in offline evaluation. We address this by separating preference modeling from exposure modeling and by enforcing invariances across perturbations of the interaction graph. Our framework targets inductive cold-start and counterfactual re-ranking scenarios where deployment constraints preclude full retraining. We further standardize evaluation via unified splits and calibrated metrics that account for popularity skew. The resulting analysis clarifies when graph propagation helps beyond capacity scaling and when it merely amplifies bias.

spans: Back-translation and iterative back-translation have been extensively applied using monolingual data from Common Crawl and Wikipedia, generating synthetic pairs for systems based on Transformer-base and Transformer-big across WMT14 and WMT16 tracks. Unsupervised and weakly supervised approaches rely on cross-lingual embeddings from LASER and multilingual language models such as mBART, XLM, and mT5, initializing encoders and decoders for small bilingual fine-tuning on OPUS subsets. Meta-learning variants adapt MAML, Reptile, and Meta-KD to translation tasks, simulating few-shot episodes with IWSLT and TED Talks corpora and transferring to unseen language pairs including Nepali–English and Sinhala–English. Noise-robust objectives include denoising autoencoding, masked span prediction, and round-trip consistency, often paired with dual learning and minimum risk training to stabilize training under limited supervision. Sparse and efficient models incorporate adapters, LoRA, and prefix tuning to reduce parameter footprints, evaluated with BLEU, chrF, and COMET on Europarl, JW300, and Bible corpora. Data selection heuristics employ cross-entropy difference, bilingual lexical similarity, and temperature-based sampling, curating shards from ParaCrawl, CCAligned, and News Commentary to balance domain and quality. Constraint-based decoding uses lexicon injection, class-based soft constraints, and lexically constrained beam search, and domain adaptation leverages instance weighting, vocabulary re-mapping, and term banks from medical and legal glossaries. Evaluation setups frequently include small dev sets, script normalization, byte-level BPE tokenization, and multilingual joint training schedules across families and regions.

While multilingual pretraining has reduced the gap, adaptation strategies often underperform when lexical and syntactic divergence is high. We investigate how data curation and objective design interact to determine transfer success. To ground our approach, we first catalogue representative techniques and benchmarks used for low-resource evaluation. Evaluation setups frequently include small dev sets, script normalization, byte-level BPE tokenization, and multilingual joint training schedules across families and regions. Despite these innovations, reproducibility varies due to inconsistent preprocessing, data filtering, and evaluation pipelines. We center our study on principled data selection and constrained adaptation under documented domain and grammar shifts. Our objectives disentangle lexical memorization from structural transfer using targeted probes and counterfactual data. We also prioritize cost-aware updates amenable to on-device or nonephemeral deployment. This focus clarifies which ingredients matter for genuine low-resource gains beyond scale and dataset curation artifacts.

spans: Classical methods include ARIMA residual tests, STL decomposition with robust z-scores, and spectral diagnostics, often contrasted with one-class SVMs and Isolation Forest on Yahoo S5 and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark. Deep reconstruction approaches employ denoising autoencoders, variational autoencoders, and contractive autoencoders, with Donut, OmniAnomaly, and USAD reporting F1 and PRAUC on SMAP and MSL from the NASA telemetry corpus. Sequence prediction models utilize LSTM, GRU, and TCN forecasters with error-based detection, and multivariate settings add attention or transformer layers for cross-sensor coupling on SWaT and WADI water treatment logs. Adversarial formulations adopt GAN-based discriminators and sequence critics as in TadGAN and MAD-GAN, balancing reconstruction and discrimination losses to delineate subtle deviations under noisy conditions. Probabilistic techniques introduce normalizing flows, autoregressive likelihoods, and deep state space models, estimating per-timestep densities and tail probabilities for thresholding on Yahoo Webscope and KPI datasets. Graph and relational models represent sensors as nodes connected by topology or learned affinities, using GCN, Graph WaveNet, or Temporal Fusion Transformers to capture long-range structure in power grid and HVAC testbeds. Evaluation protocols standardize point-adjusted scoring, latency-aware metrics, and tolerance windows, with public splits from NAB, UCR anomaly archives, and the SKAB benchmark for pump systems. Labeling practices consider delayed effects, composite alarms, overlapping anomalies, and maintenance interventions recorded in incident tickets and operator shift logs across deployments.
Lacks synthesis.document: Industrial IoT deployments rely on timely anomaly detection to prevent cascading failures and safety incidents. Yet practitioners face distribution drift, missing data, and multimodal correlations that complicate reliable monitoring. We approach the problem by combining predictive uncertainty with structure-aware reconstruction signals. To contextualize design choices, we first summarize common datasets, architectures, and evaluation practices in the literature. Classical methods include ARIMA residual tests, STL decomposition with robust z-scores, and spectral diagnostics, often contrasted with one-class SVMs and Isolation Forest on Yahoo S5 and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark. Deep reconstruction approaches employ denoising autoencoders, variational autoencoders, and contractive autoencoders, with Donut, OmniAnomaly, and USAD reporting F1 and PRAUC on SMAP and MSL from the NASA telemetry corpus. Sequence prediction models utilize LSTM, GRU, and TCN forecasters with error-based detection, and multivariate settings add attention or transformer layers for cross-sensor coupling on SWaT and WADI water treatment logs. Adversarial formulations adopt GAN-based discriminators and sequence critics as in TadGAN and MAD-GAN, balancing reconstruction and discrimination losses to delineate subtle deviations under noisy conditions. Probabilistic techniques introduce normalizing flows, autoregressive likelihoods, and deep state space models, estimating per-timestep densities and tail probabilities for thresholding on Yahoo Webscope and KPI datasets. Graph and relational models represent sensors as nodes connected by topology or learned affinities, using GCN, Graph WaveNet, or Temporal Fusion Transformers to capture long-range structure in power grid and HVAC testbeds. Evaluation protocols standardize point-adjusted scoring, latency-aware metrics, and tolerance windows, with public splits from NAB, UCR anomaly archives, and the SKAB benchmark for pump systems. Labeling practices consider delayed effects, composite alarms, overlapping anomalies, and maintenance interventions recorded in incident tickets and operator shift logs across deployments. Despite this breadth, ground truth ambiguity and post-hoc scoring rules can inflate performance beyond actionable value. We reconcile detection accuracy with operational latency by aligning metrics to intervention costs and change propagation. Our method integrates topology priors and calibrated uncertainty to triage alerts under missing and delayed labels. We further test robustness under simulated sensor dropouts and drift aligned with real maintenance records. This yields a more faithful picture of readiness for sustained industrial monitoring.

spans: Canonical optimization uses FedAvg with periodic client aggregation, while FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedAdam stabilize updates under non-IID data, and q-FedAvg incorporates fairness-aware objectives for skewed loss distributions. Personalization strategies include pFedMe, Per-FedAvg, Ditto, and FedRep, which decompose global and local parameters or add proximal penalties to accommodate client-specific optima on LEAF FEMNIST and Shakespeare. Clustered and multi-task variants such as IFCA, FedFomo, and MOCHA partition clients by relatedness or jointly solve related objectives, with evaluations on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Stack Overflow next-word prediction. Privacy mechanisms build on DP-SGD with moments accountant or Rényi DP, PATE-style teacher aggregation, and secure aggregation protocols following Bonawitz et al., integrated with compression via quantization, sparsification, and sketching. Communication efficiency employs local steps, gradient accumulation, and error feedback, and compression-friendly optimizers like SignSGD, Top-k SGD, and FedCOMGATE are benchmarked under limited uplink budgets. Threat models consider membership inference, property inference, and gradient inversion attacks such as Deep Leakage from Gradients and iDLG, along with model poisoning and backdoor insertion. Public benchmarks include LEAF, TensorFlow Federated simulations, and FedScale, with client availability traces from Reddit, Google Speech Commands, and OpenLoris to model partial participation and churn. Hardware constraints are represented through edge accelerators and mobile CPUs, and heterogeneity is emulated by Dirichlet partitions and temporal sampling masks across client cohorts in repeated rounds of training.
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning enables on-device training without centralizing raw data, but heterogeneity, privacy, and efficiency remain open challenges. In practice, teams must balance test accuracy with communication, fairness, and formal privacy guarantees across diverse clients. We take a principled view that couples personalization with certified privacy under realistic participation patterns. Before presenting our approach, we survey representative optimization, privacy, and personalization techniques and benchmarks. Canonical optimization uses FedAvg with periodic client aggregation, while FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedAdam stabilize updates under non-IID data, and q-FedAvg incorporates fairness-aware objectives for skewed loss distributions. Personalization strategies include pFedMe, Per-FedAvg, Ditto, and FedRep, which decompose global and local parameters or add proximal penalties to accommodate client-specific optima on LEAF FEMNIST and Shakespeare. Clustered and multi-task variants such as IFCA, FedFomo, and MOCHA partition clients by relatedness or jointly solve related objectives, with evaluations on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Stack Overflow next-word prediction. Privacy mechanisms build on DP-SGD with moments accountant or Rényi DP, PATE-style teacher aggregation, and secure aggregation protocols following Bonawitz et al., integrated with compression via quantization, sparsification, and sketching. Communication efficiency employs local steps, gradient accumulation, and error feedback, and compression-friendly optimizers like SignSGD, Top-k SGD, and FedCOMGATE are benchmarked under limited uplink budgets. Threat models consider membership inference, property inference, and gradient inversion attacks such as Deep Leakage from Gradients and iDLG, along with model poisoning and backdoor insertion. Public benchmarks include LEAF, TensorFlow Federated simulations, and FedScale, with client availability traces from Reddit, Google Speech Commands, and OpenLoris to model partial participation and churn. Hardware constraints are represented through edge accelerators and mobile CPUs, and heterogeneity is emulated by Dirichlet partitions and temporal sampling masks across client cohorts in repeated rounds of training. Despite promising results, comparisons are confounded by inconsistent privacy accounting, differing client samplers, and opaque preprocessing. We standardize these factors and examine the trade space between personalization and certified privacy. Our formulation separates population and client-level risks using a bilevel objective with calibrated noise. We further capture real availability by replaying traces during training and evaluation. The resulting insights clarify when private personalization is feasible under realistic participation budgets.

spans: Work on visually rich document QA spans pretraining and datasets, including LayoutLM and LayoutLMv2 for text-layout fusion, Donut and TILT as OCR-free or semi-structured models, DocFormer and LiLT for multimodal transformers, and benchmarks such as DocVQA, InfographicVQA, FUNSD, SROIE, ChartQA, and TextCaps, alongside tools like Detectron2-based detectors and Tesseract OCR pipelines widely adopted in evaluations across common leaderboards and shared tasks alike.
Lacks synthesis.document: Document-level question answering over visually-rich documents requires reasoning over text, layout, and graphics. Recent systems leverage pre-trained vision-language encoders while grappling with domain shift and limited labeled data. We focus on grounding questions to structure that emerges from layout regularities and lexical cues. Prior approaches vary widely in how they encode spatial relations and textual semantics. Work on visually rich document QA spans pretraining and datasets, including LayoutLM and LayoutLMv2 for text-layout fusion, Donut and TILT as OCR-free or semi-structured models, DocFormer and LiLT for multimodal transformers, and benchmarks such as DocVQA, InfographicVQA, FUNSD, SROIE, ChartQA, and TextCaps, alongside tools like Detectron2-based detectors and Tesseract OCR pipelines widely adopted in evaluations across common leaderboards and shared tasks alike. Despite these advances, disparate evaluation protocols obscure which components truly aid generalization across templates. We therefore establish a controlled benchmark that isolates layout invariance from linguistic paraphrase. Building on this setup, we introduce a modular encoder that disentangles reading order, relative position, and semantic similarity. The architecture enables ablations that attribute gains to specific inductive biases rather than parameter scale. Experiments on zero-shot transfer reveal consistent benefits from compositional layout tokens when fonts and page geometry change. We release code, models, and diagnostic splits to encourage reproducible analysis of grounding behaviors.

spans: Representative methods span embedding and reconstruction families, including DeepWalk, node2vec, and GraphSAGE for structural context; GAE, VGAE, and DOMINANT for autoencoding; CoLA and AnomalyDAE for contrastive or denoising objectives; and evaluations commonly report on Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Amazon Computers, and YelpChi, with implementations in PyTorch Geometric and DGL standardizing preprocessing and negative sampling protocols under transductive and inductive graph settings alike.
Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting anomalies in attributed graphs underpins monitoring for fraud, intrusion, and malfunction. Practical deployments must operate with scarce labels while contending with evolving topology. We study inductive detectors that reconcile local structure and attribute sparsity. Prior research offers disparate training signals, from context prediction to self-supervised reconstructions. However, comparative evidence remains confounded by evaluation shortcuts and leakage. Representative methods span embedding and reconstruction families, including DeepWalk, node2vec, and GraphSAGE for structural context; GAE, VGAE, and DOMINANT for autoencoding; CoLA and AnomalyDAE for contrastive or denoising objectives; and evaluations commonly report on Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Amazon Computers, and YelpChi, with implementations in PyTorch Geometric and DGL standardizing preprocessing and negative sampling protocols under transductive and inductive graph settings alike. To address these gaps, we formulate a perturbation-invariant objective that calibrates rarity against neighborhood stability. The design permits principled thresholds via conformal risk control on sliding windows. Our analysis decomposes performance by anomaly archetype and graph stationarity. Extensive experiments on temporal benchmarks demonstrate reliable early detection with bounded false alarms. We discuss limitations in highly clustered regimes and avenues for robust adaptation.

spans: Recent models range from classical ARIMA and Prophet to neural methods such as DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformers, Informer, Autoformer, FEDformer, PatchTST, and DLinear, evaluated on benchmarks including M4, M5, ETT (ETTh1/ETTm2), Electricity, Traffic, Exchange Rate, Weather, and ILI, with toolkits like GluonTS, PyTorch Forecasting, and darts facilitating training pipelines and standardized metrics across long-horizon tasks and multivariate covariate configurations alike.
Lacks synthesis.document: Probabilistic time series forecasting supports planning in energy, retail, and transportation. Nonstationarity and covariate shifts complicate training regimes that rely on fixed windows or calendar features. Recent models range from classical ARIMA and Prophet to neural methods such as DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformers, Informer, Autoformer, FEDformer, PatchTST, and DLinear, evaluated on benchmarks including M4, M5, ETT (ETTh1/ETTm2), Electricity, Traffic, Exchange Rate, Weather, and ILI, with toolkits like GluonTS, PyTorch Forecasting, and darts facilitating training pipelines and standardized metrics across long-horizon tasks and multivariate covariate configurations alike. Despite this variety, reported gains often conflate distributional targets with likelihood choices. We propose a decomposition that unifies scale handling and calendar conditioning across horizons. The approach introduces a calibration layer that normalizes residual dynamics before distributional fitting. Coupled with multi-horizon quantile supervision, it yields interpretable components without sacrificing accuracy. A common loader ensures identical splits, losses, and metrics across baselines. On electricity, traffic, and retail datasets, the method improves MASE and CRPS with fewer parameters. We release templates enabling reproducible ablations across public corpora.

spans: Segmentation research comprises canonical architectures and benchmarks, including U-Net, 3D U-Net, V-Net, nnU-Net, Attention U-Net, DeepLabV3+, TransUNet, and Swin-UNet, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, MSD, KiTS, and ACDC datasets, with pipelines frequently implemented in MONAI and nnU-Net frameworks, and preprocessing relying on SimpleITK spacing normalization, intensity z-scoring, and patch-based tiling strategies for memory-constrained training in three-dimensional acquisitions and cross-vendor clinical cohorts alike.
Lacks synthesis.document: Accurate delineation of lesions in volumetric imaging is crucial for prognosis and treatment planning. Deep networks have improved segmentation but often struggle with inter-scanner variability and limited annotations. We investigate architectural and training choices that stabilize performance under domain shift. Our analysis focuses on decoupling localization sensitivity from boundary precision. We hypothesize that normalization and receptive field control are primary drivers of robustness. Segmentation research comprises canonical architectures and benchmarks, including U-Net, 3D U-Net, V-Net, nnU-Net, Attention U-Net, DeepLabV3+, TransUNet, and Swin-UNet, evaluated on BraTS, ISLES, MSD, KiTS, and ACDC datasets, with pipelines frequently implemented in MONAI and nnU-Net frameworks, and preprocessing relying on SimpleITK spacing normalization, intensity z-scoring, and patch-based tiling strategies for memory-constrained training in three-dimensional acquisitions and cross-vendor clinical cohorts alike. Building on these observations, we introduce a lightweight prior that regularizes feature scales across resolutions. The mechanism complements standard augmentations by constraining intensity statistics without paired adaptation. A calibrated uncertainty head flags slices likely to benefit from clinician review. Experiments across scanners quantify gains under harmonization-free setups. We provide code and cross-site splits to promote transparent comparisons.

spans: Key resources and methods include mBERT, XLM-R, LaBSE, and LASER for multilingual representations; ColBERT-X, mColBERT, and SPLADE for late interaction and sparse expansion; datasets such as MIRACL, Mr.TyDi, mMARCO, and XOR-TyDi QA; and infrastructure like FAISS, Anserini, and Pyserini that underpin dense indexing, approximate nearest neighbor search, and cross-encoder reranking in multilingual retrieval studies spanning scripts and resource levels without translation.
Lacks synthesis.document: Cross-lingual retrieval enables users to search across languages without parallel queries. The challenge lies in aligning semantic spaces while respecting language-specific morphology and scripts. Recent embedding models reduce translation reliance but face robustness gaps in long-tail domains. We examine retrieval architectures that separate multilingual alignment from document relevance modeling. Key resources and methods include mBERT, XLM-R, LaBSE, and LASER for multilingual representations; ColBERT-X, mColBERT, and SPLADE for late interaction and sparse expansion; datasets such as MIRACL, Mr.TyDi, mMARCO, and XOR-TyDi QA; and infrastructure like FAISS, Anserini, and Pyserini that underpin dense indexing, approximate nearest neighbor search, and cross-encoder reranking in multilingual retrieval studies spanning scripts and resource levels without translation. Prior results are difficult to compare due to inconsistent tokenization and per-language tuning. We introduce a standardized pipeline that controls indexing, query expansion, and reranking across typologically diverse corpora. The design decouples lexical and semantic signals through layered retrieval with calibrated score fusion. Our analyses highlight where alignment errors versus term mismatch dominate failure modes. On MIRACL and Mr.TyDi, we report consistent gains in low-resource languages without external translation. We share training scripts and indexes to support reproducible cross-lingual experiments.

spans: Prior work on clinical named entity recognition spans traditional sequence models and modern transformers. Early systems leveraged BiLSTM-CRF decoders (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016) with ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), and were evaluated on CoNLL-2003, i2b2 2010/2012, and BC5CDR (Li et al., 2016). Subsequent studies adopted contextual pretraining, including BioBERT and ClinicalBERT (Lee et al., 2020; Alsentzer et al., 2019) and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), often fine-tuned with CRF layers (Lafferty et al., 2001). Domain adaptation and robustness have been explored with adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), self-training with labels (Ruder and Plank, 2018), multi-source transfer (Guo et al., 2018), and data augmentation using synonym replacement and back-translation (Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Clinical text mining enables downstream applications such as cohort discovery and pharmacovigilance. Extracting entities like medications, diagnoses, and laboratory findings remains challenging due to idiosyncratic language and privacy constraints. Models trained on one institution often degrade when applied to another with different documentation styles. This distribution shift interacts with label scarcity, since annotations are costly and protected. A growing body of research explores representation learning and transfer strategies to address these issues. However, comparative evidence under strict privacy and governance settings is limited.

Prior work on clinical named entity recognition spans traditional sequence models and modern transformers. Early systems leveraged BiLSTM-CRF decoders (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016) with ELMo embeddings (Peters et al., 2018), and were evaluated on CoNLL-2003, i2b2 2010/2012, and BC5CDR (Li et al., 2016). Subsequent studies adopted contextual pretraining, including BioBERT and ClinicalBERT (Lee et al., 2020; Alsentzer et al., 2019) and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), often fine-tuned with CRF layers (Lafferty et al., 2001). Domain adaptation and robustness have been explored with adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), self-training with labels (Ruder and Plank, 2018), multi-source transfer (Guo et al., 2018), and data augmentation using synonym replacement and back-translation (Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016).

Yet little is known about how these design choices behave when institutions cannot share text or identifiers. We investigate settings where only parameter updates or masked statistics may be exchanged across sites. Our study formalizes privacy-constrained transfer and tests practical recipes that respect operational boundaries. We quantify performance, calibration, and drift sensitivity under realistic cross-hospital protocols. The findings aim to guide practitioners seeking robust extraction with minimal data movement. We also outline implications for governance, reproducibility, and compliance in clinical NLP deployments.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists multiple methods, models, and datasets with citations but does not explain their relationships, limitations, or how they connect to the paper’s aims, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Session-based and sequential recommendation has progressed through GRU4Rec (Hidasi et al., 2016), SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018), NextItNet (Yuan et al., 2019), and BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019), often benchmarked on datasets MovieLens-1M, Amazon, Yelp, and Retailrocket. Graph-enhanced models incorporate item and user relations via GCN, PinSage, and LightGCN (Hamilton et al., 2017; Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020), while knowledge-aware approaches such as RippleNet and KGAT connect items to external entities (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). For cold-start personalization, meta-learning and representation transfer have been explored with MAML-style adaptation and MeLU (Finn et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019), alongside self-supervised pretraining objectives exemplified by S3-Rec and CL4SRec (Zhou et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Personalized recommendation under sparse feedback remains a central challenge in modern marketplaces. Production systems must balance accuracy with privacy, efficiency, and fairness constraints. As user behavior fragments across devices and sessions, sequence modeling becomes increasingly important. Moreover, new items appear continuously, creating cold-start scenarios with limited interaction history. Recent attention has turned to methods that leverage structure, side information, and self-supervision. Yet consistent evaluations for extreme cold-start under short sessions are comparatively rare. To contextualize our study, we summarize representative neural and graph-based approaches.

Session-based and sequential recommendation has progressed through GRU4Rec (Hidasi et al., 2016), SASRec (Kang and McAuley, 2018), NextItNet (Yuan et al., 2019), and BERT4Rec (Sun et al., 2019), often benchmarked on datasets MovieLens-1M, Amazon, Yelp, and Retailrocket. Graph-enhanced models incorporate item and user relations via GCN, PinSage, and LightGCN (Hamilton et al., 2017; Ying et al., 2018; He et al., 2020), while knowledge-aware approaches such as RippleNet and KGAT connect items to external entities (Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). For cold-start personalization, meta-learning and representation transfer have been explored with MAML-style adaptation and MeLU (Finn et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019), alongside self-supervised pretraining objectives exemplified by S3-Rec and CL4SRec (Zhou et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020).

Despite these advances, little is known about performance when users provide only a handful of clicks. We study budgeted interaction regimes where models must adapt within five or fewer events. Our experiments compare lightweight adaptation modules under controlled latency budgets. We analyze stability to catalog churn and exposure bias in rapidly evolving inventories. The results highlight failure modes and tradeoffs pertinent to deploying session models in practice. We conclude by discussing implications for evaluation design and responsible recommendation.

spans: Large-scale automatic speech recognition has advanced with self-supervised pretraining and robust multilingual finetuning across diverse corpora in low-resource and high-resource settings alike. Foundational datasets include LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice and Fisher Spanish-English and Switchboard (Panayotov et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Model architectures span CTC and RNN-T decoders over CNN or Transformer encoders, alongside wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and XLSR-style pretraining (Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020). Multilingual and zero-shot transfer have been explored using SpeechStew, MMS, and Whisper training recipes with cross-lingual subword vocabularies and language-agnostic features (Ghodsi et al., 2021; Pratap et al., 2023; Radford et al., 2023).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Automatic speech recognition has seen dramatic gains with self-supervision and large-scale data. However, many languages remain underserved due to limited resources and domain variability. Mismatches in channel, accent, and code-switching complicate deployment for global users. Robust models must generalize across domains while preserving low latency and small memory. We examine data mixing strategies and decoding constraints for multilingual scenarios.

Large-scale automatic speech recognition has advanced with self-supervised pretraining and robust multilingual finetuning across diverse corpora in low-resource and high-resource settings alike. Foundational datasets include LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM, VoxPopuli, and Common Voice and Fisher Spanish-English and Switchboard (Panayotov et al., 2015; Rousseau et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021; Ardila et al., 2020). Model architectures span CTC and RNN-T decoders over CNN or Transformer encoders, alongside wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and XLSR-style pretraining (Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020). Multilingual and zero-shot transfer have been explored using SpeechStew, MMS, and Whisper training recipes with cross-lingual subword vocabularies and language-agnostic features (Ghodsi et al., 2021; Pratap et al., 2023; Radford et al., 2023).

While such ingredients are powerful, recipe interactions remain opaque for mixed-language utterances. We investigate how sampling, lexicon normalization, and language tags impact code-switching accuracy. Our analysis emphasizes deployment-friendly beam pruning and on-device constraints. We present controlled ablations that vary language coverage and acoustic condition. We then quantify robustness to noisy transcripts and domain drift in streaming contexts. The study yields actionable settings for fitting multilingual models under bounded compute. We outline future work on dynamic language routing and rapid language addition.

spans: Estimation of treatment effects from observational data has leveraged a spectrum of statistical and machine learning tools. Classical methods include propensity score matching and inverse probability weighting (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Robins et al., 2000), augmented inverse probability weighting and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). Representation-learning approaches such as TARNet, Dragonnet, and balancing neural networks aim to reduce covariate shift (Shalit et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2019; Johansson et al., 2016). Heterogeneous effects are modeled with Causal Forests and additive regression trees (Wager and Athey, 2018; Chipman et al., 2010), and evaluations use IHDP, JOBS II, ACIC, and MIMIC-III benchmarks (Hill, 2011; LaLonde, 1986; Dorie et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2016).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Related Work

Estimating causal effects from electronic health records is crucial for decision support. Observational data introduce confounding, missingness, and treatment-selection biases. Practical pipelines must balance identification assumptions with model misspecification and variance. Moreover, healthcare interventions evolve over time, complicating evaluation with time-varying confounders. Researchers have proposed a range of adjustment and learning strategies to address these concerns. We review representative approaches and datasets before detailing our study design.

Estimation of treatment effects from observational data has leveraged a spectrum of statistical and machine learning tools. Classical methods include propensity score matching and inverse probability weighting (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Robins et al., 2000), augmented inverse probability weighting and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (Bang and Robins, 2005; van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). Representation-learning approaches such as TARNet, Dragonnet, and balancing neural networks aim to reduce covariate shift (Shalit et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2019; Johansson et al., 2016). Heterogeneous effects are modeled with Causal Forests and additive regression trees (Wager and Athey, 2018; Chipman et al., 2010), and evaluations use IHDP, JOBS II, ACIC, and MIMIC-III benchmarks (Hill, 2011; LaLonde, 1986; Dorie et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2016).

Despite this progress, uncertainty quantification and transportability across hospitals remain underexplored. We investigate estimators that deliver interval estimates while maintaining competitive bias under shift. Our evaluation isolates overlap violations and sensitivity to unobserved confounding proxies. We design semi-synthetic tasks anchored in real covariate distributions to stress-test methods. The results clarify tradeoffs between efficiency, calibration, and robustness for clinical use. We conclude with guidance for linking causal estimands to operational decisions in care pathways.

spans: Molecular property prediction with learned graph representations builds on message passing mechanisms across atom-bond graphs. Foundational architectures include GCN, GAT, and MPNN variants (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Gilmer et al., 2017), along with physics-aware models such as SchNet and DimeNet (Schütt et al., 2017; Klicpera et al., 2020). Recent transformer-style encoders, including Graphormer and GROVER, extend fields and incorporate priors (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks comprise MoleculeNet tasks over QM9, ZINC, PCBA, and FreeSolv with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC or MAE metrics (Wu et al., 2018). Pretraining and transfer leverage masked atom modeling and contrastive objectives in ChemBERTa, MolCLR, and GraphCL (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; You et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Predicting molecular properties underpins discovery in materials science and drug design. Graph-based learning offers a natural interface to encode chemical structure and constraints. However, generalization across scaffolds and assay domains remains a persistent challenge. To situate our contribution, we summarize representative neural architectures and benchmarks.

Molecular property prediction with learned graph representations builds on message passing mechanisms across atom-bond graphs. Foundational architectures include GCN, GAT, and MPNN variants (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Veličković et al., 2018; Gilmer et al., 2017), along with physics-aware models such as SchNet and DimeNet (Schütt et al., 2017; Klicpera et al., 2020). Recent transformer-style encoders, including Graphormer and GROVER, extend fields and incorporate priors (Ying et al., 2021; Rong et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks comprise MoleculeNet tasks over QM9, ZINC, PCBA, and FreeSolv with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC or MAE metrics (Wu et al., 2018). Pretraining and transfer leverage masked atom modeling and contrastive objectives in ChemBERTa, MolCLR, and GraphCL (Chithrananda et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; You et al., 2020).

Yet systematic evaluations of data efficiency and split robustness across assays are limited. We examine low-data regimes with strict scaffold splits and realistic noise injection. Our framework assesses models under controlled augmentation and constrained hyperparameter budgets. We quantify uncertainty and calibration to inform risk-aware screening decisions. The study also reports ablations isolating message depth, positional encodings, and readout choices. Our results expose stability gaps that matter for high-throughput candidate triage. We discuss implications for pretraining strategies and benchmark design in cheminformatics.
