spans: Multimodal sentiment modeling has been explored with Tensor Fusion Network, Low-rank Multimodal Fusion, and MulT, alongside graph-based memory approaches and multimodal transformers (Zadeh et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Tsai et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2020). Common datasets include CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MVSA-Single, MVSA-Multiple, and Twitter15 or Twitter17 for image-text polarity (Zadeh et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Pretraining strategies span image-text contrastive learning, visual entailment pretraining, and masked language modeling with visual tags (Radford et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Evaluation typically reports accuracy and correlation metrics.
Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer user attitudes by integrating text, images, and audio from social platforms. Its promise lies in capturing affective cues that are lost when modalities are processed in isolation. However, practical settings present noisy inputs, colloquialisms, and rapidly drifting visual memes. This section reviews progress while identifying limits that inform our study. Early work fused handcrafted visual features with bag of words representations to predict polarity labels. With deep learning, joint encoders emerged that align representations across modalities. Temporal models further captured conversational context and user specific baselines. Despite gains, models still struggle with distribution shift and spurious correlations between modality specific artifacts and labels. Benchmarks commonly emphasize in domain accuracy rather than stable generalization. Moreover, annotation guidelines rarely calibrate frames like sarcasm or multimodal incongruity. These gaps motivate architectures that disentangle modality evidence and expose uncertainty. They also call for evaluation that stresses robustness and accountability. We therefore revisit cross modal attention and causal regularization under a unified training schedule. Multimodal sentiment modeling has been explored with Tensor Fusion Network, Low-rank Multimodal Fusion, and MulT, alongside graph-based memory approaches and multimodal transformers (Zadeh et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Tsai et al., 2019; Hazarika et al., 2020). Common datasets include CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MVSA-Single, MVSA-Multiple, and Twitter15 or Twitter17 for image-text polarity (Zadeh et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Pretraining strategies span image-text contrastive learning, visual entailment pretraining, and masked language modeling with visual tags (Radford et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Evaluation typically reports accuracy and correlation metrics. While these resources catalyzed rapid progress, they expose biases toward Western, English speaking users. Visual memes and filters also change faster than benchmark updates. Cross modality misalignment confounds attention mechanisms that conflate salience with sentiment. Inconsistent annotation guidelines further entrench dataset specific shortcuts. Several studies explore debiasing, but many rely on metadata that is unavailable at test time. Others introduce adversarial objectives that destabilize training and sacrifice calibration. There remains a need to quantify how much each modality actually contributes to final predictions. We address this by structuring attention as a set of causal queries over modality specific interventions. The design separates content evidence from stylistic confounds using counterfactual masks. It also encourages sparse attributions that are traceable to tokens and regions. To probe robustness, we create stress tests that perturb incongruent channels. We further assess whether models maintain sign consistency under controlled flips of individual modalities. Our experiments cover both sentence level and utterance level settings. We evaluate out of distribution performance through temporal slices and creator splits. We report predictive accuracy, calibration error, and stability under controlled corruption. The results indicate strong gains on robustness without sacrificing in domain accuracy. We conclude by discussing implications for fair moderation and human centered deployment.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists numerous methods, datasets, and pretraining strategies with citations and metrics, but it does not articulate a gap, connect these works to the paper’s aims, or present any author perspective, thus exemplifying a literature list that lacks synthesis.

spans: Recent graph-based predictors include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, GMAN, and AGCRN, which variously employ diffusion convolution, temporal convolutions, spatiotemporal attention, and adaptive adjacency learning (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). Benchmarks commonly use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08 with MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Data augmentation strategies include noise injection, time warping, and mixup for sequences (Um et al., 2017; DeVries and Taylor, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). External features include weather, events, and holidays.
Lacks synthesis.document: Traffic forecasting underpins routing, congestion mitigation, and proactive control in urban networks. Spatiotemporal dependencies and irregular sensor layouts make the task challenging. Graph neural networks provide a natural inductive bias for modeling road topology. Nevertheless, temporal heterogeneity and missing data cause brittle predictions during incidents. This section surveys advances and frames the limitations addressed in our approach. Early pipelines decomposed flow into seasonal and residual components with ARIMA variants. Neural models later replaced manual seasonality with gated recurrence on lagged signals. Convolutional architectures projected sensors onto grids to reuse image operators. Graph convolutions improved locality by diffusing information along directed edges. Attention mechanisms further captured long range dependencies across corridors. Yet most models assume fixed graphs that ignore dynamic control and lane closures. Transfer across cities also remains difficult due to scale and sensor heterogeneity. Robust forecasting requires principled treatment of topology changes and covariate shift. We pursue adaptive graphs and causal regularizers within a unified training routine. We also revisit imputation strategies that respect conservation laws. Recent graph-based predictors include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, GMAN, and AGCRN, which variously employ diffusion convolution, temporal convolutions, spatiotemporal attention, and adaptive adjacency learning (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). Benchmarks commonly use METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08 with MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Data augmentation strategies include noise injection, time warping, and mixup for sequences (Um et al., 2017; DeVries and Taylor, 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). External features include weather, events, and holidays. Despite strong scores, most evaluations consider routine conditions with limited incident coverage. Predictors frequently extrapolate poorly when topology or demand patterns shift. Static graphs cannot reflect closures, reversible lanes, or signal retiming. Similarly, missing sensors trigger propagation errors that cascade across horizons. The interaction of control policies with learned dynamics remains understudied. We target these limitations with a model that adapts edges using context. The approach injects control signals as priors over connectivity. Temporal modules separate recurrent dynamics from event triggered changes. To counter missing data, we leverage physics informed constraints on flows. We also introduce invariance penalties that stabilize predictions across day types. A curriculum schedules disturbances to avoid collapse during early training. We evaluate transfer across cities using aligned subnetworks and covariate matching. Our ablations examine the tradeoffs between adaptivity and stability. Results show improved robustness without degrading peak hour accuracy. The analysis highlights implications for resilient urban operations.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span compiles models, benchmarks, augmentation strategies, and external features with citations but does not connect them to a specific gap or the paper’s approach, thereby lacking synthesis.

spans: Approaches include FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, Per-FedAvg, and pFedMe, alongside meta-learning and multi-task formulations for personalization (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Fallah et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020). Public datasets for language modeling and typing research often reference WikiText-103, Penn Treebank, Reddit comments, and the StackOverflow federated corpus (Merity et al., 2016; Marcus et al., 1993; Baumgartner et al., 2020; TensorFlow Federated, 2019). Privacy accounting typically relies on Rényi differential privacy and subsampled Gaussian mechanisms (Mironov, 2017; Abadi et al., 2016). Evaluation emphasizes perplexity, top-k accuracy, communication rounds, and client participation rates.
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning enables on device training without centralizing raw user data. Mobile text entry is a canonical application with strong privacy constraints. Personalization is vital because typing styles, dictionaries, and contexts vary widely. However, heterogeneity across users and devices complicates optimization and evaluation. Communication limits and partial participation further stress training dynamics. This section reviews related methods and situates our contributions. Early federated algorithms focused on weighted averaging under non IID data. Successive work introduced proximal regularization to control client drift. Others explored clustered aggregation to partition users into latent communities. Personalization layers and multi task formulations became popular for keyboards. Yet a principled decomposition between shared knowledge and private traits remains elusive. Moreover, existing metrics seldom reflect downstream user experience beyond accuracy. Approaches include FedAvg, FedProx, Scaffold, Per-FedAvg, and pFedMe, alongside meta-learning and multi-task formulations for personalization (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Fallah et al., 2020; Dinh et al., 2020). Public datasets for language modeling and typing research often reference WikiText-103, Penn Treebank, Reddit comments, and the StackOverflow federated corpus (Merity et al., 2016; Marcus et al., 1993; Baumgartner et al., 2020; TensorFlow Federated, 2019). Privacy accounting typically relies on Rényi differential privacy and subsampled Gaussian mechanisms (Mironov, 2017; Abadi et al., 2016). Evaluation emphasizes perplexity, top-k accuracy, communication rounds, and client participation rates. While these ingredients provide a toolbox, real keyboards display skewed vocabularies and rapid drift. Domain shifts arise from new slang, trending names, and multilingual mixing. Heavy tail phenomena complicate averaging because rare words dominate perceived quality. Strict privacy budgets restrict gradient signal and slow convergence. Communication budgets further limit the number of effective updates per user. These realities demand mechanisms that adapt quickly yet remain stable under constraints. We introduce a decomposition that isolates transferable patterns from idiosyncratic habits. The server maintains a compact shared representation learned across tasks. Clients attach lightweight adapters that steer predictions toward personal distributions. Confidence calibrated routing decides when to trust private or shared paths. We integrate privacy accounting into the routing to bound information leakage. Our curriculum targets frequent tokens first and gradually expands coverage. We measure user centric benefits through completion rate and correction rate. We further track latency, energy, and stall events during interactive typing. Experiments simulate participation skew and overnight vocabulary shifts. We compare against strong personalized baselines under matched privacy budgets. The approach reduces corrections while respecting strict privacy and communication limits. We discuss deployment considerations for production keyboard systems.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists algorithms, datasets, privacy tools, and metrics with citations but does not explain their relationships or the gap addressed, thus lacking synthesis and author perspective.

spans: Prominent self-supervised encoders include wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM, and BEST-RQ, trained on large corpora like Libri-Light, VoxPopuli, and GigaSpeech with contrastive or masked prediction objectives (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Kahn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Low-resource benchmarks use Common Voice, BABEL, MLS low-resource splits, and TED-LIUM protocols (Ardila et al., 2020; Gales et al., 2014; Pratap et al., 2020; Rousseau et al., 2012). Adaptation techniques span layer-wise finetuning, adapters, multi-task learning, and shallow fusion with language models (Houlsby et al., 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016; Gulati et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Automatic speech recognition has advanced rapidly with self supervised pretraining and large transformers. Yet many languages lack labeled audio and present severe domain shift. Phonetic diversity and code switching further complicate training. This section reviews prior work and motivates our method for low resource settings. Early systems relied on HMM GMM pipelines with pronunciation lexicons. End to end models later replaced components with connectionist temporal classification and attention. Self supervised encoders now learn from unlabeled audio at scale. Despite gains, adaptation to new domains still incurs high sample complexity. Long tail speakers and accents remain poorly covered in common corpora. Evaluation practice often ignores robustness to background noise and channel variation. We focus on sample efficient adaptation with explicit control over domain mismatch. Our approach builds on hierarchical adapters for speech encoders. We also align learned units with articulatory priors to improve transfer. To contextualize our design, we summarize notable resources and methods. Prominent self-supervised encoders include wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, WavLM, and BEST-RQ, trained on large corpora like Libri-Light, VoxPopuli, and GigaSpeech with contrastive or masked prediction objectives (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022; Kahn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Low-resource benchmarks use Common Voice, BABEL, MLS low-resource splits, and TED-LIUM protocols (Ardila et al., 2020; Gales et al., 2014; Pratap et al., 2020; Rousseau et al., 2012). Adaptation techniques span layer-wise finetuning, adapters, multi-task learning, and shallow fusion with language models (Houlsby et al., 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016; Gulati et al., 2020). Despite broad coverage, these resources do not reflect conversational speech in many communities. Domain shift appears in background noise, telephony channels, and code switching. Annotation scarcity further limits effective lexicon and language model adaptation. Unlabeled audio from the target domain is often available at scale. We therefore seek methods that exploit such audio while preserving privacy. Our model introduces adapters that gate pretrained features by domain attributes. The gating reduces interference between mismatched phonotactics and prosody. We incorporate lightly supervised articulatory priors as auxiliary targets. A curriculum schedules adapter capacity based on validation stability. We add a contrastive objective that separates nuisance factors from content. To quantify robustness, we vary channel conditions and noise mixtures. We also examine transfer under rapid speaker turnover and code switching. Evaluation uses word error rate alongside accent and domain stratification. We compare to strong baselines with matched computational budgets. Results reveal faster adaptation and better stability under scarce labels. Ablations confirm the role of domain gates and auxiliary targets. We conclude with implications for inclusive speech technologies.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span catalogs encoders, datasets, and techniques with citations and objective types but offers no linkage to the paper’s goals or identification of a research gap, demonstrating a lack of synthesis.

spans: Representative models include PCNN with selective attention, MultiR, Mintz labeling, and the CONVATT family, and reinforcement learning instance selectors (Zeng et al., 2015; Hoffmann et al., 2011; Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2018). Datasets derive from NYT-Freebase alignments, TACRED, SemEval Task 8, and DocRED for document relations (Riedel et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2017; Hendrickx et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders often include BERT, RoBERTa, SpanBERT, and LUKE, paired with distant supervision losses (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Yamada et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Distant supervision aligns knowledge base facts with text to create training data for relation extraction. The approach scales labeling but introduces substantial noise from sentence mismatches. Multi instance learning and noise modeling aim to mitigate these issues. Recent pretraining has further improved sentence encoders for long contexts. Despite progress, robustness and factual consistency remain problematic in open domains. This section discusses key strands and clarifies the space our method targets. Early models pooled sentences per entity pair with attention to highlight informative mentions. Subsequent work introduced bag level classifiers with relaxed selection assumptions. Recent approaches model sentence truth via latent variables or reinforcement learning. Evidence aggregation across documents has also become an active topic. Nevertheless, noise persists when bags contain only non expressing sentences. Representative models include PCNN with selective attention, MultiR, Mintz labeling, and the CONVATT family, and reinforcement learning instance selectors (Zeng et al., 2015; Hoffmann et al., 2011; Mintz et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2018). Datasets derive from NYT-Freebase alignments, TACRED, SemEval Task 8, and DocRED for document relations (Riedel et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2017; Hendrickx et al., 2010; Yao et al., 2019). Pretrained encoders often include BERT, RoBERTa, SpanBERT, and LUKE, paired with distant supervision losses (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Yamada et al., 2020). Despite broad adoption, these datasets exhibit reporting bias and limited entity diversity. Heuristics like at least one assumption break down for sparse relations. Models often overfit to surface patterns that correlate weakly with facts. Bag construction also obscures mention level uncertainty within documents. Document level aggregation can amplify label noise when evidence is ambiguous. Prior calibration methods rarely estimate instance probabilities in a principled manner. We pursue a generative labeling model that disentangles bag and mention truth. The model introduces a prior over mention expressiveness grounded in discourse cues. It then marginalizes mention assignments to obtain bag likelihoods for training. Structured variational inference yields tractable updates compatible with pretrained encoders. We incorporate counterfactual data augmentation to test sensitivity to spurious triggers. A new evaluation protocol isolates partial evidence scenarios with negative control mentions. We report precision, recall, and calibration metrics at the mention and bag levels. Comparative baselines include robust MIL, RL selectors, and confidence regularization. Our approach improves extraction accuracy while maintaining calibrated probabilities. It also reduces contradictions with external knowledge base constraints. Case studies illustrate finer granularity error analysis for ambiguous sentences. Ablations reveal contributions of the prior, variational family, and augmentations. We provide code and data preprocessing scripts for reproducibility. Broader impacts concern responsible deployment on noisy open web corpora.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates models, datasets, and encoders with citations and no connection to the paper’s objectives or an articulated gap, thus exhibiting a literature list without synthesis.

spans: Early graph neural models emphasize message passing on fixed graphs, including GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), while molecular benchmarks such as QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) and MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018) standardize evaluation. Subsequent architectures integrate edge updates and directed messages, D-MPNN (Yang et al., 2019) and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020). Pretraining on chemical corpora appears in GROVER (Rong et al., 2020) and ChemBERTa. Property prediction reports commonly cover ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC metrics reported.
Lacks synthesis.document: Molecular property prediction is central to virtual screening, toxicity analysis, and materials discovery. Recent years have seen the maturation of deep graph learning as a dominant paradigm for learning from chemical structures. Unlike fixed fingerprints, learned representations can adapt to task-specific inductive biases and multi-task objectives. Nonetheless, dataset size, label noise, and scaffold distribution shift still challenge robust generalization. Moreover, practical workflows require calibrated uncertainty and principled molecular splits to avoid overestimation. To contextualize our approach, we summarize representative lines of work and commonly used benchmarks. Early graph neural models emphasize message passing on fixed graphs, including GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GAT (Veličković et al., 2018), while molecular benchmarks such as QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014) and MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018) standardize evaluation. Subsequent architectures integrate edge updates and directed messages, D-MPNN (Yang et al., 2019) and AttentiveFP (Xiong et al., 2020). Pretraining on chemical corpora appears in GROVER (Rong et al., 2020) and ChemBERTa. Property prediction reports commonly cover ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 with scaffold splits and ROC-AUC metrics reported. Despite the diversity of architectures, comparisons are often confounded by inconsistent preprocessing and hyperparameter budgets. Furthermore, improvements on small molecular datasets can stem from ensembling and data leakage rather than genuine inductive advances. In safety-critical applications, the ability to abstain under uncertainty can be as important as raw accuracy. We therefore adopt rigorous scaffold-balanced splits and assess calibration alongside predictive metrics. We also analyze representation transfer from pretraining regimes under controlled data ablations. Our study aims to clarify performance drivers and provide reproducible guidance for robust molecular modeling.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists multiple models, datasets, and metrics (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, QM9, MoleculeNet, D-MPNN, AttentiveFP, GROVER, ChemBERTa, ESOL, FreeSolv, Tox21) without explaining their relationships or how they connect to the paper’s argument, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Transformer-based code models span general-purpose LLMs and specialized encoders, including GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022), trained on web-scale repositories. Benchmarking commonly uses HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), and CodeXGLUE (Lu et al., 2021), with pass@k or exact-match metrics. Techniques such as prompt engineering, few-shot learning, and self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) appear alongside unit-test filtering and execution-based reranking in practical pipelines for competitive coding tasks and benchmarks.
Lacks synthesis.document: Neural code generation has progressed rapidly with the advent of large language models trained on vast software corpora. The task entails producing compilable, functional programs that meet a specified natural language intent. Practical systems must negotiate syntax, semantics, external libraries, and problem constraints under limited context windows. At the same time, evaluation practices and data curation strongly influence the perceived capabilities of these models. Prior work has also examined the impact of execution feedback and unit tests on search and decoding. We first recap representative models, datasets, and commonly used techniques. Transformer-based code models span general-purpose LLMs and specialized encoders, including GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), Codex (Chen et al., 2021), CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022), trained on web-scale repositories. Benchmarking commonly uses HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), and CodeXGLUE (Lu et al., 2021), with pass@k or exact-match metrics. Techniques such as prompt engineering, few-shot learning, and self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) appear alongside unit-test filtering and execution-based reranking in practical pipelines for competitive coding tasks and benchmarks. These ingredients form the backdrop for our examination of context management and tool integration. We focus on retrieval-augmented generation to inject relevant API usage and project-specific conventions. Our analysis highlights brittleness under small prompt perturbations and unstable estimates of pass@k across seeds. To mitigate overfitting to unit tests, we decouple solution synthesis from verification and compare constrained decoders. We also quantify the tradeoff between temperature-based sampling and execution-guided pruning. Finally, we release standardized prompts, seeds, and scripts to enable apples-to-apples evaluation. This framework clarifies where performance originates and where it remains fragile.

Lacks synthesis.document: Recommender systems increasingly mediate access to goods, information, and cultural opportunities. As such, concerns about fairness, accountability, and transparency have moved to the foreground. In ranking scenarios, exposure and calibration interact with utility in complex, feedback-driven ways. Regulation and platform policy create additional constraints that shape algorithm design and evaluation. We scope our discussion to fairness in top-k recommendation with implicit feedback. Before detailing our modeling strategy, we review common metrics, datasets, and algorithmic baselines. Work on fairness-aware recommendation surveys metrics like demographic parity (Calders and Verwer, 2010), equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), and exposure disparity (Singh and Joachims, 2018), with re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010) and FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017). Datasets frequently include MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2016), Last.fm (Cantador et al., 2011), and RecSys Challenge collections, and evaluation emphasizes NDCG, MAP, and calibrated exposure. Policy-gradient schemes based on REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) and constrained optimization via dual ascent appear as implementation choices across studies in offline and online settings and competitions. While these ingredients are widely used, there remains ambiguity about appropriate risk budgets for group-level constraints. Our interest lies in the stability of exposure constraints under distribution shift induced by user adaptation. We characterize this shift using counterfactual propensity estimators derived from randomized interventions. We then instantiate constrained learners with identical fairness targets but varied surrogate losses. The resulting comparisons illuminate sensitivity to propensity misspecification and surrogate curvature. We complement offline evaluation with a pre-registered A/B test measuring long-term exposure balance. Together, the study clarifies practical levers for robustly satisfying fairness targets in recommender systems.

Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer opinions or affect from synchronized language, vision, and audio streams. The field has benefited from standardized datasets and the availability of reliable feature extractors. Nonetheless, cross-domain robustness and speaker generalization remain persistent challenges. Additionally, label noise and annotation subjectivity complicate model comparison across benchmarks. The interaction among modalities often exhibits redundancy and conflict that naïve fusion cannot resolve. We outline prominent architectures, datasets, and features that shape current practice. Multimodal sentiment and emotion research spans early concatenation baselines and structured fusion, including TFN (Zadeh et al., 2017), MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), alongside transformers like LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019) and ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019). Evaluation uses CMU-MOSI (Zadeh et al., 2016), CMU-MOSEI (Zadeh et al., 2018), and MELD (Poria et al., 2019), reporting accuracy, F1, and mean absolute error. Additional modalities and cues appear through acoustic features, facial action units, with toolkits like OpenFace (Baltrušaitis et al., 2018) and librosa (McFee et al., 2015). Despite these resources, disagreement in label granularity and segment alignment hinders cumulative progress. Moreover, many reported gains are fragile to speaker split protocols and leakage through pretraining. We therefore prioritize controlled ablations that quantify complementarity rather than aggregate accuracy alone. Our modeling emphasizes causal probes that isolate pathways of cross-modal influence. We design interventions that suppress modality-specific evidence to test reliance and redundancy. Finally, we standardize training seeds and provide scripts for faithful replication across toolchains. The resulting analysis clarifies when and how multimodal fusion delivers reliable improvements.

spans: Augmentation for sequence labeling includes synonym substitution and word deletion heuristics like EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), contextual token replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and contextualized masking with CBERT (Wu et al., 2019). Paraphrastic generation appears through back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016) and round-trip translation pipelines, while mixup-style interpolation (Zhang et al., 2018) has been adapted to token spans. Evaluations frequently report CoNLL-2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), and OntoNotes 5.0 (Pradhan et al., 2013), with span-level F1 and entity-wise calibration metrics under low-resource transfer and cross-lingual settings.
Lacks synthesis.document: Named entity recognition in low-resource settings suffers from limited annotations and domain shift. Data augmentation offers a practical path to increase sample diversity without costly labeling. However, naive perturbations can distort entity boundaries and degrade calibration. The multilingual regime adds tokenization and morphological variability that complicate reuse of monolingual recipes. We focus on augmentation schemes that preserve span structure while encouraging lexical variety. To situate our study, we briefly review widely used techniques and benchmarks for NER augmentation. Augmentation for sequence labeling includes synonym substitution and word deletion heuristics like EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), contextual token replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and contextualized masking with CBERT (Wu et al., 2019). Paraphrastic generation appears through back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016) and round-trip translation pipelines, while mixup-style interpolation (Zhang et al., 2018) has been adapted to token spans. Evaluations frequently report CoNLL-2003 (Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), and OntoNotes 5.0 (Pradhan et al., 2013), with span-level F1 and entity-wise calibration metrics under low-resource transfer and cross-lingual settings. While these ingredients are popular, few studies quantify the interaction between augmentation and constrained decoding. We compare span-aware perturbations with entity-consistent sampling under identical budgets and seeds. Our experiments examine robustness to annotation noise by simulating boundary flips and label swaps. We further analyze calibration and boundary uncertainty using abstention curves and ECE metrics. The results highlight the importance of preserving morphological agreement in multilingual contexts. We release recipes that integrate with existing training loops and pretokenizers for reproducible adoption. This evaluation delineates effective augmentation choices for realistic low-resource NER.

Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). These considerations motivate a comprehensive study of grounded, efficient, and reliable multimodal systems.

spans: Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used.

Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages.
Lacks synthesis.document: Evaluating multimodal systems requires coordinated metrics for perception, grounding, and language quality. Simple accuracy is insufficient when tasks demand spatial reasoning or temporal consistency. Recent work emphasizes auditability and interpretability alongside raw performance numbers. We outline bodies of work that inform the construction of comprehensive evaluation suites. Foundational studies on pretrained language models and transfer learning survey a wide range of techniques across tasks and domains, including contextual encoders and retrieval components. Early transformer architectures are represented by BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), while subsequent works emphasize scaling and prompting in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and instruction tuning (Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). For sentence representation and retrieval, DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely used. Benchmarks frequently referenced include GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), and MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016). Other datasets are also commonly used. Text metrics such as exact match and BLEU are complemented by entity-level and relation-level scores. For images, detection and segmentation quality influence downstream captioning and referring expressions. Human studies can assess preference and faithfulness but introduce cost and variance. Diagnostic datasets target contrastive phenomena to reduce shortcut learning. Perturbation tests probe stability under paraphrasing and minor visual changes. Cost-aware evaluation tracks compute, memory, and energy to reflect deployment constraints. Multi-language settings expose hidden biases in tokenization and normalization. Open-source leaderboards encourage competition but risk overfitting to public test sets. Research on object detection and vision backbones spans region-based detectors, one-stage models, and transformer-based designs. Canonical detectors include Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015), RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017), and YOLOv3/v5 (Redmon and Farhadi, 2018; Jocher et al., 2020), while DETR (Carion et al., 2020) introduces set prediction with bipartite matching. Backbones frequently considered are ResNet (He et al., 2016), EfficientNet (Tan and Le, 2019), and Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021). Common evaluation corpora include COCO (Lin et al., 2014), PASCAL VOC (Everingham et al., 2010), and OpenImages (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). Training heuristics reference focal loss, assignment, multi-scale augmentation, and moving averages. Cross-modal retrieval tasks serve as proxies for alignment but may not reflect reasoning depth. Task graphs can compose atomic skills into end-to-end benchmarks. Logging standardized traces supports reproducibility and failure analysis. Secure sandboxes are valuable for privacy-sensitive assessments with proprietary assets. Weighted aggregations reduce dominance of any single category in composite scores. Calibration and abstention metrics inform safe fallback mechanisms. Agreement between automatic metrics and human judgments remains a moving target. The synthesis of these aspects guides principled evaluation of grounded models.

spans: Research on multimodal hate speech detection draws on image-text fusion architectures and annotated corpora created across platforms such as Twitter and Instagram. Early convolutional encoders combine visual region features with word embeddings for binary labels (Gomez et al., 2019; Kiela et al., 2020). Transformer-based models leverage cross-attention and contrastive pretraining to align memes and captions (Hossain et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Prasad et al., 2022). Public datasets include MMHS150K, Hateful Memes, and FB-Hateful-Meme, with accompanying baseline implementations (Kiela et al., 2020; Fersini et al., 2022; Mandl et al., 2021). Auxiliary signals such as OCR tokens, tags, attributes, and features have been incorporated through late fusion or adapter layers (Velioglu et al., 2020; Sabat et al., 2019; Das et al., 2021; Muennighoff, 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Multimodal abuse and hate speech on social platforms presents a persistent challenge for automated moderation. Images, text, and layout often interact to convey meaning that is not apparent from any single channel. Advances in deep representation learning have created opportunities to detect harmful content at scale. Yet, the multimodal setting raises issues of alignment, robustness, and cultural context. Governance requirements further demand transparency about model behavior under distribution shift. These pressures motivate a careful examination of what is known about multimodal detection pipelines. This section reviews prior findings and situates the scope of our study. Text encoders alone can misclassify benign terms that acquire derogatory meaning through imagery. Visual features alone can miss targeted slurs embedded in memes and screenshots. Multimodal models therefore require mechanisms to focus on salient cross-channel evidence. Training such models is complicated by noisy annotations and adversarial editing of posts. Researchers have explored robustness against text overlays, occlusions, and stylistic perturbations. Evaluation practices must consider subgroup performance and temporal drift across platform communities. Together these considerations shape the design space for responsible classifiers. Research on multimodal hate speech detection draws on image-text fusion architectures and annotated corpora created across platforms such as Twitter and Instagram. Early convolutional encoders combine visual region features with word embeddings for binary labels (Gomez et al., 2019; Kiela et al., 2020). Transformer-based models leverage cross-attention and contrastive pretraining to align memes and captions (Hossain et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Prasad et al., 2022). Public datasets include MMHS150K, Hateful Memes, and FB-Hateful-Meme, with accompanying baseline implementations (Kiela et al., 2020; Fersini et al., 2022; Mandl et al., 2021). Auxiliary signals such as OCR tokens, tags, attributes, and features have been incorporated through late fusion or adapter layers (Velioglu et al., 2020; Sabat et al., 2019; Das et al., 2021; Muennighoff, 2022). Despite these resources, key uncertainties persist around the role of pretraining objectives for rare multimodal slurs. Prior reports suggest that noisy OCR can either help disambiguate sarcasm or amplify false positives. Cross-lingual settings introduce additional drift when visual metaphors differ across languages. There is also limited clarity about data curation choices that balance fairness and efficacy. To address these issues, we examine architectures under controlled interventions on tokenization, region proposals, and matching strategies. Our analysis distinguishes lexical leakage from genuine cross-modal grounding. We further quantify calibration under content remix patterns that mirror realistic platform behavior. The resulting findings are intended to inform dataset updates and feature prioritization. We provide stratified evaluations that expose brittle correlations in common benchmarks. A focus on shift-resilient evidence aggregation yields practical implications for deployment policies. We also discuss annotation guidelines that reduce ambiguity for borderline cases. Finally, we outline open problems in multimodal moderation that require community coordination. These include shared testbeds for adversarial editing and standardized reporting on subgroup harms. The remainder of the paper details methodology, experiments, and limitations.

spans: Forecasting baseload and renewable generation has drawn on autoregressive models, recurrent neural networks, and probabilistic decomposition. Classical ARIMA and SARIMA provide baselines for hourly series (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hyndman and Athanasopoulos, 2018). Sequence models capture nonlinear dynamics through LSTM and GRU encoders with exogenous covariates such as temperature and calendar effects (Smyl, 2020; Salinas et al., 2020). Global forecasting strategies train across many meters using DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer, and Informer variants with quantile losses (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). Public benchmarks include M4, M5, GEFCom2014, and ETT, which support deterministic and probabilistic evaluation protocols (Makridakis et al., 2020; Makridakis et al., 2022; Hong et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Reliable energy forecasts underpin grid stability and market operations. The increasing penetration of renewables heightens variability across spatial and temporal scales. Forecasting systems must balance accuracy, interpretability, and computational budget. Domain knowledge about weather and demand patterns remains central to modeling choices. Probabilistic outputs are necessary for risk aware planning and reserve sizing. However, methodological proliferation complicates fair comparison among approaches. We structure the discussion around modeling paradigms, data regimes, and evaluation criteria. Short term horizons demand high resolution exogenous inputs and careful feature engineering. Medium term horizons benefit from hierarchical reconciliation across regions and customer classes. Long term horizons encounter structural changes that violate stationarity assumptions. Forecast errors can cascade into operational constraints with nonlinear costs. Institutions also require transparent audits of models under extreme weather scenarios. Benchmarks must reflect real world constraints like data latency and sensor outages. These themes motivate a closer look at representative modeling strategies. Forecasting baseload and renewable generation has drawn on autoregressive models, recurrent neural networks, and probabilistic decomposition. Classical ARIMA and SARIMA provide baselines for hourly series (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hyndman and Athanasopoulos, 2018). Sequence models capture nonlinear dynamics through LSTM and GRU encoders with exogenous covariates such as temperature and calendar effects (Smyl, 2020; Salinas et al., 2020). Global forecasting strategies train across many meters using DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer, and Informer variants with quantile losses (Oreshkin et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). Public benchmarks include M4, M5, GEFCom2014, and ETT, which support deterministic and probabilistic evaluation protocols (Makridakis et al., 2020; Makridakis et al., 2022; Hong et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021). Despite diverse techniques, the community lacks clarity about the determinants of calibrated uncertainty. Choices about target normalization and seasonal decomposition can drive unexpected biases. Exogenous features introduce leakage risks when derived from future weather assimilation. We systematically vary covariate availability to quantify sensitivity across models. Our analysis separates the benefits of global training from those of architecture capacity. We also examine robustness to holidays, regime shifts, and data gaps. Results inform guidelines for probabilistic scoring and production deployment. We release reproducible pipelines for common datasets with standardized backtesting. The study highlights interactions between loss functions and temporal aggregation. It further examines the role of cross learning between load and generation series. We include ablations that probe feature importance under distribution shift. Remaining challenges include rare event modeling and equity in model performance. Future work can explore multitask formulations that unite dispatch and forecasting. The following sections detail methodology, results, and implications.

spans: Biomedical relation extraction has utilized dependency parsing, supervision, and contextualized encoders alongside specialized corpora. Early pipelines relied on feature engineering with parse paths and kernels for protein–protein and drug–drug interactions (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Tikk et al., 2010). Neural methods adopt CNNs and BiLSTMs over sentence and document scopes (Zeng et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017; Verga et al., 2018). Pretrained language models fine-tuned on BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT report gains across benchmarks such as DDI, BC5CDR, and DocRED (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Additional supervision arises from knowledge bases CTD, UMLS, and Hetionet and from silver labels derived via co-mention heuristics (Davis et al., 2019; Bodenreider, 2004; Himmelstein et al., 2017).
Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Extracting biomedical relations from the literature supports hypothesis generation and knowledge base curation. The volume of new articles outpaces the capacity of manual annotation. Automated systems must resolve long context, synonymy, and nested entities. Errors can propagate into downstream discovery and clinical decision support. Domain specific pretraining and structured resources offer complementary advantages. Evaluation protocols remain heterogeneous across tasks and datasets. We outline the methodological landscape relevant to relation extraction. Sentence level extraction can miss cross sentence evidence that appears in abstracts and full text. Document level models introduce additional resolution for co referenced entities across sections. Distant supervision scales labeling at the cost of noisy alignments to knowledge bases. Bootstrapping strategies attempt to control confirmation bias through pattern diversification. Span boundary errors complicate downstream normalization to standardized vocabularies. Annotation interfaces increasingly capture rationales in addition to labels. These trends frame the context for reviewing representative techniques. Biomedical relation extraction has utilized dependency parsing, supervision, and contextualized encoders alongside specialized corpora. Early pipelines relied on feature engineering with parse paths and kernels for protein–protein and drug–drug interactions (Bunescu and Mooney, 2005; Tikk et al., 2010). Neural methods adopt CNNs and BiLSTMs over sentence and document scopes (Zeng et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017; Verga et al., 2018). Pretrained language models fine-tuned on BioBERT, SciBERT, and PubMedBERT report gains across benchmarks such as DDI, BC5CDR, and DocRED (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Gu et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Additional supervision arises from knowledge bases CTD, UMLS, and Hetionet and from silver labels derived via co-mention heuristics (Davis et al., 2019; Bodenreider, 2004; Himmelstein et al., 2017). Despite broad progress, ambiguity persists about how context windows influence precision at high recall. The interaction between entity disambiguation and relation cues remains under quantified. We analyze the role of distant supervision filtering and curriculum schedules. Experiments compare sentence, paragraph, and document scopes under matched training budgets. Our study measures calibration, evidence localization, and robustness to spurious lexical triggers. We additionally probe negative sampling strategies against overlapping entity pairs. Findings inform practical guidance for building reliable extraction pipelines. We introduce data splits that limit article leakage across train and test. The benchmark emphasizes clinically salient relations aligned to curated ontologies. We also include ablations on entity masking and marker schemes. Error analysis underscores coreference resolution and discourse cues as open challenges. The work concludes with recommendations for evaluation and reporting standards. Future extensions could integrate structured prompting and interactive labeling. The next sections describe datasets, models, and analyses.

spans: Navigation in crowds has been studied with social force models, obstacle formulations, reinforcement learning, and imitation from demonstrations. Classical methods encode interaction through ORCA to generate collision-free trajectories under kinematic constraints (Helbing and Molnár, 1995; Van den Berg et al., 2011). Learning-based approaches include deep RL with LSTM policies and model predictive control hybrids trained in simulators (Chen et al., 2017; Everett et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks span ETH, UCY, and Stanford Drone with standardized metrics for collision rate (Pellegrini et al., 2009; Lerner et al., 2007; Robicquet et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019). Sensing modalities integrate RGB-D, LiDAR, and tracking, while priors derive from SLAM datasets such as KITTI and TUM (Geiger et al., 2013; Sturm et al., 2012).
Lacks synthesis.document: Related Work Navigation among humans is a hallmark challenge for autonomous robots operating in public spaces. Agents must respect social norms while ensuring safety and task efficiency. Perception latency and actuation limits further complicate interaction under uncertainty. Modeling multiple agents introduces combinatorial complexity in prediction and planning. Data driven policies can exploit structure but risk overfitting to specific environments. Design choices must balance interpretability, performance, and deployment constraints. We overview prior methods and empirical settings that anchor this literature. Motion forecasting accuracy is tightly coupled to short horizon control feasibility. Crowd density distributions vary substantially across campuses, parks, and sidewalks. Scene geometry and bottlenecks introduce occlusions that degrade tracking quality. Evaluation must encompass collision rate, smoothness, and human comfort. Simulators offer repeatability but can poorly approximate real world multi agent dynamics. Transfer from synthetic to embodied platforms remains a persistent source of brittleness. These considerations inform how we interpret algorithmic benchmarks. Navigation in crowds has been studied with social force models, obstacle formulations, reinforcement learning, and imitation from demonstrations. Classical methods encode interaction through ORCA to generate collision-free trajectories under kinematic constraints (Helbing and Molnár, 1995; Van den Berg et al., 2011). Learning-based approaches include deep RL with LSTM policies and model predictive control hybrids trained in simulators (Chen et al., 2017; Everett et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2020). Benchmarks span ETH, UCY, and Stanford Drone with standardized metrics for collision rate (Pellegrini et al., 2009; Lerner et al., 2007; Robicquet et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2019). Sensing modalities integrate RGB-D, LiDAR, and tracking, while priors derive from SLAM datasets such as KITTI and TUM (Geiger et al., 2013; Sturm et al., 2012). Despite steady progress, questions remain about generalization across densities and camera placements. Policy smoothness can trade off with responsiveness in crowded scenarios. We analyze control horizons, perception rates, and social context features under matched budgets. Experiments compare training on synthetic scenes to fine tuning with real trajectories. Our metrics extend beyond collision counts to capture comfort proxies. We conduct ablations on prediction uncertainty and risk sensitive objectives. The study delineates failure modes tied to sensing and forecasting interactions. We summarize implementation details to facilitate reproducibility in future evaluations. The dataset splits avoid spatial and temporal leakage across environments. Results highlight limits of purely reactive policies in dense flows. Hybrid planners show promise when aligned with calibrated forecasts. Safety validation requires scenario coverage and conservative fallback strategies. We close with open problems in sim to real transfer and human factors. Subsequent sections describe models, data, and analyses.

spans: Neural code generation has advanced through large language models and execution-guided decoding across benchmarks. Encoders and decoders fine-tuned on CodeSearchNet and CodeXGLUE corpora support translation and summarization tasks (Husain et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Panthaplackel et al., 2020). Evaluation uses HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS with metrics and unit-test feedback (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cassano et al., 2022). Prominent models include Codex, CodeT5, and AlphaCode, often employing copy or deobfuscation objectives (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Fried et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Auxiliary techniques leverage retrieval of code snippets and documentation, as well as test-time self-consistency and filtering (Parvez et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2022; Le et al., 2023).
Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Program synthesis with neural models aims to translate intent into executable code. The task spans natural language to code, code to code, and multimodal conditioning. Practical systems must handle library usage, types, and runtime constraints. Safety concerns arise from insecure patterns and data exfiltration risks. Benchmarks increasingly emphasize robustness and generalization across languages. Execution feedback provides a unique learning signal unavailable in ordinary text generation. We survey representative approaches and datasets supporting this area. Tokenization choices influence copy behavior and identifier handling. Decoding strategies trade off diversity and exactness in function signatures. Retrieval can bring context that narrows search over valid solutions. Multilingual models must reconcile syntax and idioms across ecosystems. Data contamination complicates attribution of performance to model capacity. Evaluation protocols vary in test coverage and determinism. These considerations frame how we interpret empirical results. Neural code generation has advanced through large language models and execution-guided decoding across benchmarks. Encoders and decoders fine-tuned on CodeSearchNet and CodeXGLUE corpora support translation and summarization tasks (Husain et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Panthaplackel et al., 2020). Evaluation uses HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS with metrics and unit-test feedback (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cassano et al., 2022). Prominent models include Codex, CodeT5, and AlphaCode, often employing copy or deobfuscation objectives (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Fried et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). Auxiliary techniques leverage retrieval of code snippets and documentation, as well as test-time self-consistency and filtering (Parvez et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2022; Le et al., 2023). Despite rapid progress, fundamental questions remain about semantic understanding versus pattern matching. Execution signals can create reward hacking if tests are weak or unrepresentative. We probe sensitivity to prompt phrasing, input-output formatting, and canonicalization. Experiments compare constrained decoding with repair loops that invoke static analyzers. Our study measures pass rates, runtime, and failure taxonomy under ablations. We also quantify contamination by cross checking overlaps with training corpora. Findings characterize where retrieval and execution feedback most reduce search. We release scripts for deterministic evaluation and reproducibility. The corpus is partitioned to prevent scaffold leakage between train and test. Results motivate more challenging tests emphasizing semantics over templates. We propose guidelines for prompt formatting that reduce ambiguity. Remaining challenges include tooling integration and safe code policies. Future research can unify synthesis with refactoring and repair in loops. The following sections describe setup, models, and analyses.

spans: Unsupervised domain adaptation for medical image segmentation has been studied through adversarial alignment, style transfer, and self-training across diverse datasets, including BraTS, MSD, and LiTS. Adversarial feature alignment methods employ discriminators to reduce distribution shift between source and target features (Tzeng et al., 2017; Hoffman et al., 2018), while image-to-image translation approaches leverage mappings to match appearances before segmentation (Zhu et al., 2017; Huo et al., 2018). Pseudo-labeling and entropy minimization techniques refine target predictions to stabilize training (French et al., 2018; Vu et al., 2019), and consistency regularization encourages invariant predictions under perturbations (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017). Architectural backbones commonly include U-Net and its variants (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Isensee et al., 2021) and attention-based decoders (Oktay et al., 2018) for volumetric scans and cross-plane consistency. Evaluation protocols frequently report Dice and Hausdorff metrics on multi-center cohorts, with intensity normalization, bias field correction, histogram matching, and test-time augmentation as routine preprocessing steps (Nyúl and Udupa, 1999; Shinohara et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Automatic segmentation under domain shift remains a central obstacle for deploying clinical deep learning across sites and scanners. Variations in acquisition protocols and patient populations produce distributional discrepancies that degrade generalization, particularly for rare pathologies. Prior work highlights the need for methods that adapt without extensive target annotation while preserving anatomical fidelity. We focus on cross-modality and cross-center transfer for volumetric neuroimaging where alignment errors have high clinical cost. Unsupervised domain adaptation for medical image segmentation has been studied through adversarial alignment, style transfer, and self-training across diverse datasets, including BraTS, MSD, and LiTS. Adversarial feature alignment methods employ discriminators to reduce distribution shift between source and target features (Tzeng et al., 2017; Hoffman et al., 2018), while image-to-image translation approaches leverage mappings to match appearances before segmentation (Zhu et al., 2017; Huo et al., 2018). Pseudo-labeling and entropy minimization techniques refine target predictions to stabilize training (French et al., 2018; Vu et al., 2019), and consistency regularization encourages invariant predictions under perturbations (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017). Architectural backbones commonly include U-Net and its variants (Ronneberger et al., 2015; Isensee et al., 2021) and attention-based decoders (Oktay et al., 2018) for volumetric scans and cross-plane consistency. Evaluation protocols frequently report Dice and Hausdorff metrics on multi-center cohorts, with intensity normalization, bias field correction, histogram matching, and test-time augmentation as routine preprocessing steps (Nyúl and Udupa, 1999; Shinohara et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Despite progress on alignment, reported improvements often conflate gains from preprocessing with representation learning, leaving the relative contribution of each component unclear. Moreover, robustness to intensity outliers and thin structures is rarely stress-tested under realistic perturbations, masking failure modes that matter for triage. We therefore disentangle augmentation, normalization, and adaptation by designing controlled interventions that isolate their effects on boundary precision and small-lesion recall. Our study consolidates evaluation across harmonized splits and ablation protocols to expose trade-offs between stability and sensitivity under shift. The resulting analysis surfaces principled design choices for resource-constrained deployment and establishes a transparent baseline for future adaptive segmentation research.

spans: Recent progress in neural code generation and repair has been driven by large-scale pretraining on source repositories and curated benchmarks such as CodeSearchNet, HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS. Autoregressive transformers adapted for programming languages learn next-token distributions over code tokens and docstrings (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), while encoder-decoder models leverage span infilling and denoising objectives to synthesize functions and patches (Raffel et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented generators condition decoding on nearest neighbor snippets or API exemplars to improve adherence to library idioms (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2023), and prompt-engineering strategies tailor task specifications with few-shot demonstrations, docstring templates, or unit tests (Liu et al., 2021; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021; Ye et al., 2022). Execution-based evaluation using pass@k on HumanEval and MBPP, static analysis to check type or linter conformity, and compiler success rates widely serve as primary metrics across studies (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document: Recent deployments of code assistants expose both productivity gains and fragility when models confront unseen libraries, ambiguous specifications, or security-sensitive contexts. Teams increasingly expect tools that can transform natural language intent into safe, maintainable code with verifiable behavior. However, brittle prompt handling and limited grounding in project context often lead to syntactic fixes that miss semantic requirements. To clarify where current systems succeed and fail, we examine the interaction between evaluation design and controllability mechanisms. Our analysis centers on generalization under constrained context windows and the reliability of self-verification. Recent progress in neural code generation and repair has been driven by large-scale pretraining on source repositories and curated benchmarks such as CodeSearchNet, HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS. Autoregressive transformers adapted for programming languages learn next-token distributions over code tokens and docstrings (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), while encoder-decoder models leverage span infilling and denoising objectives to synthesize functions and patches (Raffel et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented generators condition decoding on nearest neighbor snippets or API exemplars to improve adherence to library idioms (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2023), and prompt-engineering strategies tailor task specifications with few-shot demonstrations, docstring templates, or unit tests (Liu et al., 2021; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021; Ye et al., 2022). Execution-based evaluation using pass@k on HumanEval and MBPP, static analysis to check type or linter conformity, and compiler success rates widely serve as primary metrics across studies (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). While benchmarks emphasize functional correctness, industrial adoption hinges on predictability under minor edits and adherence to organizational style guides. Prior studies suggest that post-hoc testing can recover some failures, yet it remains unclear how verification signals should be injected to steer generation without mode collapse. We therefore compare lightweight retrieval, structured prompts, and iterative repair under a unified protocol that decouples search, synthesis, and checking. By instrumenting traces with fine-grained failure taxonomies, we reveal dependence on brittle lexical cues and inconsistent use of API preconditions. The results motivate interface designs that surface uncertainty, encourage specification refinement, and support safe fallback behaviors in continuous integration.

spans: Temporal knowledge graph reasoning has been explored with extensions of static embedding models and recurrent or attention-based dynamics across benchmarks including ICEWS14, ICEWS05-15, GDELT, and WIKIDATA12k. Translation-style approaches incorporate timestamp parameters or decay functions to modulate entity-relation interactions over time (TransE: Bordes et al., 2013; RotatE: Sun et al., 2019; DE-SimplE: Goel et al., 2020), while complex-valued and tensor factorization models capture periodicity and phase shifts (TComplEx: Lacroix et al., 2018; TNTComplEx: Xu and Li, 2019). Sequence encoders introduce temporal context with GRUs or self-attention to model evolving neighborhoods and event intensities (Trivedi et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021). Training regimes commonly rely on time-aware negative sampling, contrastive objectives, and regularization over temporal smoothness or sparsity (Ruffinelli et al., 2020; Sutskever et al., 2013; Li et al., 2021). Evaluation typically reports filtered MRR and Hits@k with interpolation and extrapolation protocols defined per dataset split, alongside ablations on window size and history length (Zhu et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2016; García-Durán et al., 2018).
Lacks synthesis.document: Temporal reasoning over knowledge graphs is essential for forecasting events, tracing entity evolution, and answering time-sensitive queries. Unlike static link prediction, temporal settings must contend with distribution drift and nonstationary sparsity. Further, realistic deployments require extrapolation beyond the observed time horizon with calibrated uncertainty. Sequence encoders introduce temporal context with GRUs or self-attention to model evolving neighborhoods and event intensities (Trivedi et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021). Training regimes commonly rely on time-aware negative sampling, contrastive objectives, and regularization over temporal smoothness or sparsity (Ruffinelli et al., 2020; Sutskever et al., 2013; Li et al., 2021). Evaluation typically reports filtered MRR and Hits@k with interpolation and extrapolation protocols defined per dataset split, alongside ablations on window size and history length (Zhu et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2016; García-Durán et al., 2018). Despite broad algorithmic coverage, experimental setups often conflate temporal granularity with supervision density, making cross-benchmark comparison tenuous. Many models also assume globally shared dynamics, which conflicts with domain-specific recurrence patterns present in political and biomedical streams. We revisit representation choices by decoupling structural encoding from temporal parameterization while holding sampling strategies fixed. Our framework tests whether inductive biases favor interpolation at the expense of forecasting rare events under long gaps. By integrating unified preprocessing and a consistent validation protocol, we clarify when temporal history truly helps and when it introduces leakage. The study provides guidance for selecting model classes under constraints on memory, latency, and update frequency.

spans: Federated learning on edge devices has adopted communication-efficient optimization, personalization, and privacy mechanisms across standard benchmarks such as EMNIST, Shakespeare, StackOverflow, and SpeechCommands. Baseline aggregation relies on local SGD with weighted averaging of client updates (FedAvg: McMahan et al., 2017), with modifications to address client drift and heterogeneity including proximal regularization (FedProx: Li et al., 2020), control variates (SCAFFOLD: Karimireddy et al., 2020), and normalization of update directions (FedNova: Wang et al., 2020). Adaptive server optimizers and momentum schemes further accelerate convergence under partial participation (FedOPT: Reddi et al., 2021; FedAdam; FedYogi) for large, nonconvex objectives. Personalization layers and mixture strategies enable client-specific adapters or clustered models to capture non-iid behavior (Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Dinh et al., 2020; Mansour et al., 2020). Privacy tooling spans secure aggregation for transport-layer confidentiality (Bonawitz et al., 2017), record-level differential privacy using gradient clipping and noise (Abadi et al., 2016; Kairouz et al., 2021), and audits of memorization or membership inference on held-out users (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document: Personalizing models directly on devices promises better user experience without centralizing sensitive data. Yet heterogeneous hardware, connectivity constraints, and skewed usage patterns complicate both optimization and evaluation. Production systems must trade off accuracy, fairness, and privacy budgets while meeting latency targets. We study these tensions through the lens of cross-device federated training for text and speech inputs. Baseline aggregation relies on local SGD with weighted averaging of client updates (FedAvg: McMahan et al., 2017), with modifications to address client drift and heterogeneity including proximal regularization (FedProx: Li et al., 2020), control variates (SCAFFOLD: Karimireddy et al., 2020), and normalization of update directions (FedNova: Wang et al., 2020). Despite an abundance of algorithms, reported efficiency often mixes parallelism gains with algorithmic progress, obscuring practical choices. Furthermore, privacy accounting is inconsistently tied to participation rates, making comparisons across workloads unreliable. We therefore factor computation, communication, and privacy cost into a unified budget that reflects real deployment limits. Our experiments contrast aggregation and personalization options under controlled straggler models and quota constraints. The findings inform principled defaults for sustainable, private personalization in mobile applications.

spans: Causal representation learning for decision-making intersects discovery, invariance, and model-based planning across environments such as Atari, Procgen, DeepMind Control Suite, and grid-world simulators with controllable interventions and confounders across tasks. Structure learning approaches estimate directed graphs from observations using constraint-based tests, score search, or differentiable relaxations (PC: Spirtes et al., 2000; GES: Chickering, 2002; NOTEARS: Zheng et al., 2018), while latent-variable models aim to disentangle factors aligned with intervention targets (Locatello et al., 2019; Khemakhem et al., 2020). Invariance-based objectives reduce spurious correlations through risk extrapolation and group robustness criteria (Peters et al., 2016; Arjovsky et al., 2019; Rosenfeld et al., 2021). Model-based reinforcement learning integrates causal structure into world models or transition predictors to support counterfactual rollouts and policy evaluation (Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018; Levine, 2020; Guez et al., 2020). Evaluation relies on intervention generalization, counterfactual policy regret, and recovery of ground-truth graphs on synthetic generators, with additional metrics tracking sample efficiency and OOD performance over held-out domains (Pearl, 2009; Bareinboim and Pearl, 2016; Schölkopf et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Robust control in nonstationary environments depends on understanding which aspects of observations change under intervention and which remain stable. Standard deep reinforcement learning often entangles nuisance variation with causal factors, leading to brittle policies. When rewards, dynamics, or sensor viewpoints shift, agents frequently overfit to superficial statistics rather than actionable structure. We examine representation choices that enable intervention-aware planning without sacrificing sample efficiency. Moreover, alignment between discovered components and actionable variables is seldom verified beyond qualitative plots. We formalize test-time interventions and confounding processes to separate invariance from mere invariability. Our study compares discovery and planning interfaces under fixed compute and rollout budgets, emphasizing policy stability under shift. The findings clarify which objectives transfer across domains and which rely on dataset-specific shortcuts.

Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Multimodal affect analysis integrates acoustic, visual, and textual cues to infer attitudes and emotions from human communication. Despite steady progress, the field continues to grapple with inconsistent generalization across speakers, languages, and recording conditions. Multimodal sentiment research reports extensive benchmarks on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, IEMOCAP, and MELD, alongside method families such as Tensor Fusion Network (Zadeh et al., 2017), Low-rank Multimodal Fusion (Liu et al., 2018), Multimodal Transformer (Tsai et al., 2019), and MulT variants (Tsai et al., 2019). Further developments include memory-augmented fusion (Hazarika et al., 2018), graph-based affect reasoning (Ghosal et al., 2019), cross-modal distillation (Hazarika et al., 2020), and pretrained audiovisual encoders like AV-HuBERT, wav2vec 2.0, and CLIP used in robust multimodal pipelines (Baevski et al., 2020; Shamma et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021). These trends illustrate the breadth of modeling choices and the centrality of standardized corpora in reporting progress. Yet reported gains can hinge on pipeline details, raising questions about stability under domain shift. Prior evaluations often emphasize average scores, leaving practical reliability underexplored. Annotation protocol differences further complicate comparisons across datasets and tasks. Modeling strategies also vary in how they encode cross-modal timing and missing modality scenarios. Recent work on robustness highlights augmentation, calibration, and causal views of confounding factors. In multimodal alignment, datasets such as LRS3, LRS2, GRID, CREMA-D, and RAVDESS are paired with techniques including CTC-based synchronization, dynamic time warping, AVSR, and contrastive alignment losses reported by Afouras et al., Chung et al., and Ma et al. These alignment resources and techniques are commonly repurposed to enhance token-level synchronization for downstream recognition and understanding. However, systematic studies on error propagation from alignment into affect prediction remain scarce. We outline an evaluation setting that stresses cross-corpus shift, noisy modalities, and label imbalance. The setup emphasizes reproducibility and transparent ablations over bespoke feature engineering. By foregrounding robustness, the discussion aims to contextualize accuracy gains alongside failure modes.
