spans: Road traffic forecasting has accumulated benchmarks on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with widely used spatiotemporal models including DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Subsequent advances explore adaptive adjacency learning (Guo et al., 2019), attention-based diffusion (Zhang et al., 2020), multi-resolution temporal kernels (Cini et al., 2022), neural controlled differential equations for streams (Kidger et al., 2020), and topology-aware dynamic graphs such as MTGNN and STG-NCDE for improved sequence modeling under complex road networks and cities globally.

Exogenous signals appear in studies combining weather from NOAA, event schedules, and holiday calendars with baselines like ARIMA and Prophet and graph models including DCRNN, Graph WaveNet, and GMAN, additionally as gradient boosting ensembles such as XGBoost and LightGBM.
Lacks synthesis.document: Related Work Urban mobility systems depend on accurate short-term forecasting to manage congestion and safety. Learning on sensor networks must integrate spatial structure with temporal dynamics under limited latency. Road traffic forecasting has accumulated benchmarks on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with widely used spatiotemporal models including DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020). Subsequent advances explore adaptive adjacency learning (Guo et al., 2019), attention-based diffusion (Zhang et al., 2020), multi-resolution temporal kernels (Cini et al., 2022), neural controlled differential equations for streams (Kidger et al., 2020), and topology-aware dynamic graphs such as MTGNN and STG-NCDE for improved sequence modeling under complex road networks and cities globally. These models illustrate the evolution from fixed graphs toward adaptive, data-driven topologies. Yet training stability and interpretability remain open concerns in high-variance traffic regimes. Data irregularity, missing streams, and sensor faults complicate routine deployment. Demand-side signals introduce feedback that undermines naive extrapolation in peak periods. Evaluation practices differ in how they partition nodes and time to avoid leakage. Incorporating uncertainty estimates can help operators quantify operational risk. Public datasets increasingly include weather and incident logs, inviting multimodal studies of causality. However, real-time constraints require careful distillation of auxiliary features for inference budgets. We consider practical settings emphasizing sparse sensors and evolving routes. Our setup stresses cold-start intersections and topology expansion events. The objective is to frame forecasting quality jointly with reliability under operational shifts.

Lacks synthesis.document: Introduction Low-resource named entity recognition challenges conventional assumptions about labeled data and domain stability. Sequence models must handle noisy capitalization, code-switching, and morphological variability across languages. Named entity recognition research surveys augmentations on CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes, WNUT17, and WikiAnn, with widely adopted techniques including Easy Data Augmentation (Wei and Zou, 2019), back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), span substitution with dictionaries (Zhang et al., 2020), and contextual synonym replacement (Kumar et al., 2019). Recent studies further leverage mBERT and XLM-R encoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), MixUp for spans (Guo et al., 2020), paraphrase generators with PEGASUS and BART (Zhang et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020), and lexicon-driven slot sampling for entity diversity (Fang et al., 2021). These augmentation strategies reflect diverse attempts to synthesize realistic variability from limited corpora. However, the interaction between augmentation and entity boundary detection is still poorly documented. Label noise and schema drift often surface during cross-dataset transfer. Evaluation protocols vary in tokenization alignment, which affects span-level metrics. Multilingual settings add language identification and script normalization complexities. Resource catalogs and annotation guidelines differ in coverage for nested and discontinuous entities. Cross-lingual transfer lines report results on WikiAnn, MasakhaNER, PAN-X, and LORELEI using mBERT, XLM-R, infoXLM, and mT5, alongside translation-based bootstrapping, multilingual dictionaries, and label projection from corpora documented by Rahimi et al., Pan et al., and Adelani et al. Such resources underpin reproducible comparisons but also magnify domain gaps in open-world entities. Practical pipelines must also account for privacy constraints in data acquisition. We outline an approach that prioritizes stability under heterogeneous label sets. The experiments target shift-resistant training signals and measurable generalization. The resulting discussion centers on data processes as much as architectures.

Despite the breadth of tooling, empirical comparisons often rely on overlapping datasets and inconsistent labeling. Tooling heterogeneity complicates attribution of findings to algorithmic choices versus implementation details. Execution semantics in the EVM expose corner cases that defeat naive abstract interpretations. Gas costs and environmental constraints further shape exploit feasibility in deployed systems. Benchmarking practices differ in how they handle compiler versions and library-linked code. Real deployments add upgradeability patterns, proxies, and governance modules that affect flow analysis. The goal is to clarify benchmarking artifacts while maintaining practical relevance for auditors.

spans: Large-scale instruction tuning has been explored across models and benchmarks, including T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 with prompts (Brown et al., 2020), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), and InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022). Lacks synthesis.document: Large language models exhibit emergent capabilities when trained with diverse objectives and data. Instruction tuning provides a mechanism to align pretrained models with user goals through curated tasks. Multilinguality adds challenges due to cross-lingual transfer, tokenization variance, and evaluation scarcity. Prior research has demonstrated that instruction mixtures can improve generalization beyond seen tasks. However, differences across languages and scripts complicate the design of robust instruction sets. In this work we study the structure of instruction granularity and coverage for cross-lingual transfer. We examine measurement protocols that disentangle instruction following from underlying knowledge. Our analysis emphasizes consistent scoring and prompt formatting across languages. We also consider the role of small adapters for controlling per-language behaviors. This section reviews developments that form the backdrop for our study. Early sequence-to-sequence models established that textual instructions can condition model behavior. Subsequent studies extended the approach to large decoder-only models and mixed-task training. Multilingual extensions explored translation-based augmentation and shared subword vocabularies. Large-scale instruction tuning has been explored across models and benchmarks, including T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 with prompts (Brown et al., 2020), FLAN (Wei et al., 2022), and InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022). Datasets such as SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), Natural Instructions (Mishra et al., 2022), and BIG-bench (Srivastava et al., 2022) are commonly used, alongside evaluation on SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) and MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Methods like parameter-efficient tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021) and self-instruction (Wang et al., 2023) are frequently reported too. These efforts collectively motivate a closer look at how instruction composition interacts with multilingual transfer. Unlike prior surveys, we focus on the coupling between prompt templates and evaluation artifacts. We also track the effect of tokenization schemes on per-language calibration. We define a three-part framework covering data creation, parameterization, and measurement. The data component isolates instruction phrasing, exemplars, and negative constraints. The parameterization component compares full fine-tuning, adapters, and prompt-tuning under compute limits. The measurement component separates automatic metrics from human judgments with bilingual adjudication. We adopt resource bands to reflect realistic deployment scenarios in multilingual settings. Our experiments analyze stability, sample efficiency, and sensitivity to instruction perturbations. We further investigate error modes unique to low-resource scripts and mixed-direction scripts. The findings highlight the necessity of consistent evaluation across heterogeneous task families. We conclude with recommendations for reproducible multilingual instruction curation. Limitations include reliance on public corpora and moderate-scale models. Ethical considerations address annotation equity and potential harms from culturally insensitive prompts.

spans: Multivariate time series anomaly detection has been widely studied with approaches such as LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MAD-GAN (Li et al., 2019), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020). Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor streams is essential for reliability in industrial systems. Models must localize faults quickly while maintaining low false alarm rates. Real deployments face nonstationarity, missing data, and asynchronous sensors. Reconstruction-based detectors dominate, but they often overfit regular variations. Probabilistic formulations improve calibration but can be brittle under distribution shift. We study a representation that balances temporal context with cross-channel structure. Our analysis isolates the roles of lag window, seasonality, and dependency priors. We also formalize an evaluation protocol that penalizes fragmented detections. This section summarizes prior work and datasets relevant to our study. Early methods relied on autoregressive residual thresholds under Gaussian assumptions. Neural forecasters extended this idea by learning nonlinear predictors for normal patterns. Graph-based models exploit process topology to share statistical strength across sensors. Multivariate time series anomaly detection has been widely studied with approaches such as LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MAD-GAN (Li et al., 2019), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020). Recent evaluations also reference KDD Cup 2018 EEG, SMD (Su et al., 2019), and the UCR archive. These references highlight both the diversity of architectures and the variety of benchmarks. In contrast with existing evaluations, we emphasize latency-aware scoring and root-cause hints. We instantiate a family of detectors with shared embeddings and lightweight decoders. The approach encourages sparse saliency that maps to actionable channels. We design ablations to separate forecasting accuracy from detection robustness. We also investigate how densification of labels affects training dynamics. Our metrics report event-level precision and recall alongside pointwise indicators. We quantify stability using bootstrap confidence intervals over sliding windows. We examine the sensitivity of thresholds to class imbalance and drift. We compare hyperparameter heuristics that control lag and dilation rates. Complexity is assessed by wall-clock latency and memory per time step. Results indicate gains under synthetic and real distribution shifts. Case studies on industrial logs demonstrate interpretable failures and earlier alarms. Limitations include reliance on public topology proxies and moderate horizon lengths. We discuss ethical risks from surveillance misuse and propose mitigation guidelines.

spans: Fairness in recommendation has been studied using datasets such as MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2015), Yelp (Asghar et al., 2016), and Amazon Books (McAuley et al., 2015), with additional logs from Last.fm and KuaiRec. Methods span re-ranking like FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), diversity-inspired xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010), pairwise regularization for equity (Beutel et al., 2019), and provider-aware objectives for multi-stakeholder settings (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019) across offline and online evaluations.

Stakeholders include consumers, producers, and the platform mediating trade-offs. Traditional accuracy objectives can exacerbate historical biases. Our framework models supply constraints and position bias jointly. We separate allocative fairness from predictive parity to clarify goals. We also measure opportunity costs for affected providers. This section reviews empirical settings and algorithmic tools relevant to our study. Prior work spans user- and item-side fairness with varying definitions and proxies. Fairness in recommendation has been studied using datasets such as MovieLens-1M (Harper and Konstan, 2015), Yelp (Asghar et al., 2016), and Amazon Books (McAuley et al., 2015), with additional logs from Last.fm and KuaiRec. Common metrics include demographic parity, equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016), calibration (Steck, 2018), and exposure-based measures (Singh and Joachims, 2018). Methods span re-ranking like FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), diversity-inspired xQuAD (Santos et al., 2010), pairwise regularization for equity (Beutel et al., 2019), and provider-aware objectives for multi-stakeholder settings (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019) across offline and online evaluations. These studies define the landscape of metrics and interventions considered in practice. Our contribution is a policy-aware objective that internalizes supplier constraints. We derive a convex surrogate for exposure allocation with guarantees. The derivation incorporates estimate uncertainty via distributional robustness. We provide a plug-in estimator for position-dependent click propensities. Calibration is enforced through a constrained optimization that respects coverage. We propose a stochastic re-ranking algorithm compatible with large catalogs. The method adapts to budget shifts without retraining predictors. We analyze regret and constraint violation bounds under partial feedback. Simulations illustrate trade-offs between welfare, fairness, and efficiency. Large-scale experiments use counterfactual evaluation with learned propensities. We report both item-level and group-level metrics with confidence intervals. Results indicate improved provider equity at modest accuracy cost. Limitations include reliance on logged policy support and group labels. We discuss transparency and consent considerations for affected creators. We release code to facilitate reproducible assessment of fairness policies.

spans: Vision–language retrieval has been benchmarked on Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2015), MS COCO (Lin et al., 2014), Conceptual Captions (Sharma et al., 2018), and Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2017). Representative models include dual encoders such as CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), and multi-stream or fusion models like ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), ALBEF (Li et al., 2021), and BLIP (Li et al., 2022). Training corpora aggregate WebImageText, LAION-400M, and image–text pairs from news and social media.

Our analysis decomposes performance by caption style and visual diversity. We also consider cross-lingual captions and noisy alt-text. This section reviews benchmarks, models, and training corpora. Early systems relied on CCA projections of hand-crafted features. Deep encoders replaced features with learned representations and shared spaces. Joint training introduced in-batch contrastive losses for efficiency. Training corpora aggregate WebImageText, LAION-400M, and image–text pairs from news and social media. These resources set the context for our experimental design. Unlike prior work, we emphasize calibration and abstention under retrieval. We design a temperature-aware scoring rule for long-tail queries. The rule adjusts similarity thresholds by density estimates. A compact encoder distills knowledge from a larger teacher. Distillation matches neighborhood structure while preserving compactness. We introduce perturbation tests for robustness to caption paraphrases. The tests include synonym swaps and word order changes. We quantify sensitivity using retrieval consistency across augmentations. Evaluation considers memory, latency, and energy at inference. We report metrics under closed and open vocabulary conditions. Results show improved robustness with minimal compute overhead. Failure analysis reveals gaps on compositional and counting queries. Limitations include lack of video and multilingual pretraining. We discuss dataset licensing and potential bias amplification.

spans: Research on cross-domain sentiment spans datasets such as the Amazon Reviews corpus (Blitzer et al., 2007), the Multi-Domain Sentiment Dataset (Pang and Lee, 2004), Yelp polarity reviews (Zhang et al., 2015), and IMDB movie reviews (Maas et al., 2011), alongside Twitter sentiment collections and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank (Socher et al., 2013) used for auxiliary supervision and vocabulary coverage. Methodologically, studies apply domain-adversarial training (Ganin et al., 2016), correlation alignment (Sun and Saenko, 2016), virtual adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2017), pseudo-labeling (Lee, 2013), and fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019).

To situate our study, we briefly outline representative datasets and methods that shape the space. Despite these advances, existing protocols understate domain drift in review style and topic shifts. We therefore design splits that delay label availability and stress semantic sparsity. Experiments compare against strong adversarial and contrastive baselines. We report metrics beyond accuracy, including expected calibration error and abstention utility. spans: Benchmark datasets used for molecular learning include MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, and BACE, the QM9 quantum chemistry corpus, ZINC subsets, and Tox21 toxicity panels (Wu et al., 2018; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Huang et al., 2016). Data scarcity and distribution shift across chemical series hinder generalization in practical settings. We study how inductive biases over local functional groups interact with scaffold splits. Our method integrates substructure pooling with uncertainty-aware training. We aim to improve out-of-domain performance under realistic medicinal chemistry constraints. Before detailing our approach, we review representative datasets and architectures that dominate benchmarks. Benchmark datasets used for molecular learning include MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, and BACE, the QM9 quantum chemistry corpus, ZINC subsets, and Tox21 toxicity panels (Wu et al., 2018; Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Irwin and Shoichet, 2005; Huang et al., 2016). These resources standardize tasks yet may obscure scaffold-level disparities relevant to deployment. Accordingly, we prioritize evaluation on stringent scaffold and temporal splits drawn from proprietary assays. Our formulation decomposes predictions into additive group contributions with learned interaction terms. This encourages interpretability aligned with chemists’ intuition about substituent effects. We calibrate uncertainties to flag unreliable extrapolations for active learning. Comparative experiments show gains on solubility, toxicity, and binding affinity tasks. Ablations analyze contributions from pooling granularity and calibration choices. We also discuss implications for data acquisition in early discovery cycles.

spans: Common benchmarks span the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge, the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, LUNA16 for pulmonary nodules, ACDC for cardiac MRI, ISLES for stroke lesions, and KiTS for renal tumors (Menze et al., 2015; Antonelli et al., 2022; Setio et al., 2017; Bernard et al., 2018; Maier et al., 2017; Heller et al., 2019). Lacks synthesis.document: Accurate segmentation of medical imagery remains challenging in regimes with limited labels. Scanner variability and pathology heterogeneity further complicate supervised training. Self-supervised pretraining offers a promising way to leverage abundant unlabeled scans. However, most studies tune objectives without considering clinical deployment constraints. We investigate representation learning that preserves anatomical topology under sparse annotations. Our framework introduces structure-aware pretext tasks tied to organ geometry. We couple these with uncertainty regularization to reduce overconfident errors. Before detailing our design, we summarize prevalent datasets and pretraining methods used in the field. Common benchmarks span the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge, the Medical Segmentation Decathlon, LUNA16 for pulmonary nodules, ACDC for cardiac MRI, ISLES for stroke lesions, and KiTS for renal tumors (Menze et al., 2015; Antonelli et al., 2022; Setio et al., 2017; Bernard et al., 2018; Maier et al., 2017; Heller et al., 2019). These resources enable controlled comparisons but often neglect cross-site generalization and label noise. We therefore curate federated splits reflecting hospital-specific protocols and demographics. Our loss enforces consistency across views aligned by deformable registration. We pair this with a lightweight decoder compatible with standard clinical GPUs. Experiments demonstrate improvements on tumor and organ delineation with fewer labels. We release scripts and federation guidelines to support reproducible assessment.

spans: Public corpora often used in fairness studies include MovieLens 1M and 20M, Amazon product reviews, Last.fm listening logs, and RecSys Challenge datasets from Yelp and Trivago (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Celma, 2010; Quadrana et al., 2018). Methodological directions feature FA*IR for fair ranking (Zehlike et al., 2017), exposure-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibrated recommendation (Steck, 2018), adversarial debiasing (Beutel et al., 2019), popularity reweighting for long-tail balance (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019), fairness regularization (Yao and Huang, 2017), and fairness-aware online bandits (Patro et al., 2020).

Yet fairness objectives can conflict with accuracy and business constraints. We formulate a framework that reconciles exposure guarantees with user satisfaction. Our perspective treats fairness as a constrained optimization over ranking policies. We validate with user studies and large-scale logs. Before presenting the framework, we survey datasets and algorithmic strands commonly referenced in fairness-aware recommendation. Public corpora often used in fairness studies include MovieLens 1M and 20M, Amazon product reviews, Last.fm listening logs, and RecSys Challenge datasets from Yelp and Trivago (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Celma, 2010; Quadrana et al., 2018). Methodological directions feature FA*IR for fair ranking (Zehlike et al., 2017), exposure-aware re-ranking (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibrated recommendation (Steck, 2018), adversarial debiasing (Beutel et al., 2019), popularity reweighting for long-tail balance (Abdollahpouri et al., 2019), fairness regularization (Yao and Huang, 2017), and fairness-aware online bandits (Patro et al., 2020). These strands establish useful baselines but rarely address multi-stakeholder trade-offs dynamically. Accordingly, we model feedback loops that couple provider exposure and user engagement. Our method integrates counterfactual estimators with constrained policy gradients. We derive bounds that connect fairness constraints to long-term value variance. Experiments compare against re-ranking, adversarial, and calibration baselines under identical budgets. We analyze impacts on minority providers and cold-start users. spans: Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid approaches further integrated content planning and discourse ordering using entity grids, content selectors, or latent plan decoders, as shown in Puduppully et al. (2019), Narayan et al. (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Evaluation practices relied on automatic metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR (Lin, 2004; Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), with learned metrics like BERTScore and MoverScore later applied to mitigate lexical mismatch penalties (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Common corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), Gigaword (Graff et al., 2003), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021), alongside domain-specific collections such as arXiv, PubMed, and BigPatent (Cohan et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019). Control mechanisms for length and style have been explored through constrained decoding, prefix tuning, and prompt-based steering (Fan et al., 2018; Keskar et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), while faithfulness concerns motivated factuality-oriented training using question-answering critics, entailment objectives, and entity-level constraints (Eyal et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets.
Lacks synthesis.document: Abstractive summarization has become a cornerstone for condensing long texts into concise narratives across news, scientific literature, and policy documents. Despite rapid progress, practitioners continue to report tensions between informativeness, faithfulness, and controllability in real deployments. Models that succeed on curated benchmarks often face robustness issues under distribution shift and domain diversity. Deployment constraints add additional pressures around latency, memory, and privacy, particularly for edge and on-premise scenarios. Curating appropriate training data remains difficult, with annotation cost, copyright, and domain mismatch complicating scale. These practical challenges motivate a careful review of modeling trends and evaluation settings that dominate the literature. A systematic view also clarifies how datasets, metrics, and architectures interact to shape reported progress. We therefore contextualize recent advances and common practices that frame the current state of the field. Early neural architectures for document summarization emphasized hierarchical encoders and pointer mechanisms, with representative systems including the pointer-generator network of See et al. (2017), the reinforcement learning objective of Paulus et al. (2018), and the coverage penalty explored by Tu et al. (2016). Abstractive sequence-to-sequence models subsequently incorporated copy and coverage refinements, as in Gehrmann et al. (2018), while extractive formulations adopted neural ranking over sentence candidates using contextual encoders similar to Nallapati et al. (2017) and Narayan et al. (2018). Large-scale pretraining shifted performance trends, with BERT-based summarizers leveraging encoder-only objectives for sentence scoring (Liu, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020) and encoder-decoder T5 or BART variants producing fluent abstracts from news datasets such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid approaches further integrated content planning and discourse ordering using entity grids, content selectors, or latent plan decoders, as shown in Puduppully et al. (2019), Narayan et al. (2021), Fan et al. (2019), and Dou et al. (2021). Evaluation practices relied on automatic metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR (Lin, 2004; Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005), with learned metrics like BERTScore and MoverScore later applied to mitigate lexical mismatch penalties (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). Common corpora include CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015), XSum (Narayan et al., 2018), Newsroom (Grusky et al., 2018), Gigaword (Graff et al., 2003), Multi-News (Fabbri et al., 2019), and GovReport (Huang et al., 2021), alongside domain-specific collections such as arXiv, PubMed, and BigPatent (Cohan et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019). Control mechanisms for length and style have been explored through constrained decoding, prefix tuning, and prompt-based steering (Fan et al., 2018; Keskar et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021), while faithfulness concerns motivated factuality-oriented training using question-answering critics, entailment objectives, and entity-level constraints (Eyal et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Multi-document summarization adopted graph-based aggregation and retrieval-augmented generation, combining centrality measures, cluster attention, and evidence reranking across Multi-News, WikiSum, and WCEP (Yasunaga et al., 2017; Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019; Perez-Beltrachini et al., 2019). Cross-lingual and multilingual settings leveraged mBART and mT5 with sentencepiece vocabularies over CC-100 and mC4, utilized bilingual constraints or alignment losses to preserve content across translation, and evaluated on MLSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua benchmarks (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Scialom et al., 2020; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Recent compression-oriented studies consider knowledge distillation, layer pruning, and quantization-aware training for summarizers on-device, with evaluations on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit TIFU reporting latency and memory reductions under tight budgets. The breadth of techniques reveals numerous design choices that interact with task assumptions and resource availability. However, persistent discrepancies between automatic scores and human judgments highlight limitations in conventional evaluation regimens. Domain-specific requirements, such as citation consistency in scientific briefs or sensitivity controls in clinical notes, further complicate model selection. Practical workflows increasingly favor modular toolchains that combine retrieval, content planning, and constrained decoding with lightweight oversight. Emerging governance guidelines emphasize transparency around training data provenance and risk assessments for factuality-sensitive applications. Opportunities arise in bridging dataset biases with targeted objectives that better reflect user-facing criteria. Scalable methods that maintain faithfulness under tight budgets are particularly pressing for organizations operating outside large compute centers. Against this backdrop, we outline the problem setting and empirical strategy adopted in the remainder of the paper.

spans: Early graph-based forecasters adopt diffusion or spectral operators to encode road networks, with DCRNN using diffusion convolution on directed graphs and STGCN applying Chebyshev filters and temporal gating. Graph WaveNet introduces an adaptive adjacency alongside dilated convolutions for long-range dependencies, while ASTGCN integrates spatial and temporal attention within stacked convolutional blocks. Subsequent transformer-flavored architectures such as GMAN, STTN, and ST-Transformer emphasize multi-head attention across nodes and timesteps and often embed positional encodings tied to periodicity. Evaluation commonly relies on the METR-LA and PEMS-BAY sensor datasets, with additional benchmarks including PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS08 for California freeways, and TaxiBJ for citywide flows. Training protocols frequently adopt horizon-specific losses, masked MAE for handling missing sensors, and curriculum learning to stabilize rollout length during multi-step prediction. External signals include weather, holidays, and incidents, added via embeddings, concatenation, auxiliary encoders, and temporal markers and calendars.

We frame the task as multi-step forecasting on sensor graphs under realistic missing data. Graph WaveNet introduces an adaptive adjacency alongside dilated convolutions for long-range dependencies, while ASTGCN integrates spatial and temporal attention within stacked convolutional blocks. Subsequent transformer-flavored architectures such as GMAN, STTN, and ST-Transformer emphasize multi-head attention across nodes and timesteps and often embed positional encodings tied to periodicity. External signals include weather, holidays, and incidents, added via embeddings, concatenation, auxiliary encoders, and temporal markers and calendars. Despite progress, performance drops sharply under distribution shift caused by accidents, construction, or extreme weather. Existing adaptive adjacency schemes often overfit to stationary correlations and ignore causal interventions. Furthermore, error compounding across horizons leads to instability, especially for sparsely observed nodes. We address these challenges by distinguishing structural persistence from episodic disruptions within a probabilistic spatio-temporal model. The approach decouples long-term topology from event-conditioned dynamics through hierarchical latent factors. This design targets calibrated forecasts when sensor coverage is intermittent or when covariates are partially missing. Our learning objective aligns one-step and multi-step risks using a consistency regularizer that preserves uncertainty propagation. We complement standard sensor benchmarks with targeted shift suites that simulate lane closures and rerouting. The evaluation protocol stresses robustness by reporting tail-aware metrics and coverage under conformal predictors. Ablations quantify the contribution of hierarchical factors, covariate gates, and adaptive message passing. We report improvements across horizons with reduced negative log-likelihood and better calibration slopes. Qualitative analyses illustrate resilient behavior during congestion waves and incident clearance phases. Code, data splits, and configuration files will be released to support reproducibility and follow-up research.

spans: Bias in implicit-feedback recommenders has been addressed with inverse propensity scoring, where propensity-weighted estimators correct exposure imbalance in datasets such as MovieLens-1M and Yahoo! R3. Pairwise debiasing methods like CausE and SVD-IPS modify the training objective to discount popularity, while user-stratified negative sampling schemes aim to equilibrate click propensities. Exposure-aware re-ranking approaches, including xQuAD, FA*IR, and DELTR, allocate positions under constraints on demographic parity or minimum exposure for protected item groups. Fairness metrics commonly report exposure disparity, calibration within groups, equal opportunity at fixed recall, and user-level risk differences across Amazon, Yelp, and Last.fm benchmarks. Causal graphical analyses model selection bias with logged bandit feedback, using techniques such as doubly robust estimation, self-normalized IPS, and marginal sensitivity models. Recent datasets, including Bias-in-Rec, Kiva microloans, and BookCrossing demographics, support evaluations that separate provider fairness, consumer fairness, and popularity confounding in offline simulators.

Concerns about fairness arise when exposure is systematically skewed toward already advantaged users or items. Ranking positions translate into economic value, so disparities can amplify feedback loops. Platforms also contend with selection bias because observed clicks reflect previous ranking policies. These dynamics complicate evaluation and learning from logged data. Prior work proposes algorithmic interventions to mitigate documented harms, yet evidence remains fragmented across tasks and datasets. We review approaches that address bias in both model training and result presentation. Despite the breadth of methods, few studies analyze trade-offs between provider exposure and user relevance under consistent protocols. Moreover, interventions optimized for demographic parity can unintentionally degrade minority consumer utility if calibration is ignored. We formalize these tensions through a bi-criteria objective that internalizes exposure constraints during training. The formulation links counterfactual exposures with risk-sensitive ranking losses using a shared set of sufficient statistics. This perspective clarifies when re-ranking alone suffices and when representation learning must absorb the constraint. Our experiments leverage logged bandit feedback while maintaining unbiased estimators through careful propensity clipping. We adopt a uniform evaluation suite with consistent splits and cross-dataset demographic mappings. Analyses examine Pareto frontiers and fairness regret to quantify policy choices. We release diagnostic tools that report both exposure and welfare decomposition at user and item subgroup levels. spans: Early multimodal studies used the Multi30K dataset, extending Flickr30K with German and French captions, and explored attention over global CNN features extracted by VGG or ResNet encoders. The WMT16 and WMT17 shared tasks spurred text-plus-image systems such as Imagination, Doubly-Attentive NMT, and Fusion-Conv, alongside retrieval-based rerankers that exploit visual neighborhood similarity. Speech-to-text resources including How2 and MuST-C enabled audio-conditioned translation, where models consume log-mel spectrograms with convolutional front-ends or pre-extracted wav2vec features. Recent transformer baselines integrate visual tokens from Faster R-CNN region proposals or CLIP embeddings, feeding them through cross-modal attention layers or gated fusion modules. Data augmentation techniques include back-translation with synthetic images, masked region modeling, and contrastive alignment that ties source phrases to detected objects. Benchmarks report BLEU and METEOR on Multi30K, Ambiguous Captions, and IKEA assembly instructions, with human assessments targeting pronoun resolution and visually dependent lexical choices.

While neural translation models have advanced rapidly, improvements from auxiliary modalities remain inconsistent. A key challenge is aligning heterogeneous representations without drowning the signal in strong language models. Another difficulty lies in evaluation, where standard metrics may ignore visually grounded adequacy. This section summarizes benchmarks and architectures relevant to grounded translation. Prior surveys emphasize the tension between end-to-end learning and feature-level fusion strategies. We group related work by resource type and integration mechanism. We then describe recent findings on robustness to occlusion and domain shift. Our review informs a design that tests modality contributions under controlled ablations. Yet gains from images or audio often vanish when text-only encoders are scaled or pretrained on massive corpora. This pattern suggests a need for protocols that hold language strength constant while varying auxiliary inputs. We therefore evaluate controlled capacity budgets and ablate fusion points across comparable parameter counts. The study also considers annotation noise in object detectors and automatic speech transcripts. We analyze when modality signals act as regularizers versus primary information sources. A causal framing separates associative cues from truly necessary sensory evidence. Experiments quantify improvements on ambiguity-targeted subsets rather than overall averages alone. spans: Classical baselines include ARIMA residual monitoring, Hotelling’s T-squared on PCA scores, and seasonal-trend decomposition with robust z-scores. Deep learning methods feature reconstruction and probabilistic objectives, such as LSTM-VAE, Donut, DAGMM, and OmniAnomaly modeling latent dynamics with stochastic recurrent layers. Temporal convolutional detectors like DeepAnt and MSCRED capture short-term motifs and cross-channel signatures through dilated convolutions and attention over multiscale residual maps. Hybrid graph-temporal models, including GDN, MTAD-GAT, and THOC, leverage learned dependency structures to propagate anomaly evidence across sensors. Evaluations commonly rely on NAB, Yahoo S5, SMAP, and MSL, with later testbeds such as SWaT and WADI introducing multistage process faults and strict delay penalties. Scoring protocols report point-level F1, range-aware precision via tolerances, and early-detection area metrics under sliding windows and exclusion zones around injected events, with label noise handled by smoothing heuristics and delayed confirmation periods during evaluation phases.

Our evaluation simulates operator feedback loops and delayed adjudication. We report coverage, time-to-detect, and net utility alongside traditional range-based metrics. Robustness analyses consider missing sensors, clock drift, and correlated disturbances. We also examine the effect of retraining cadence on stability and false positives. spans: Benchmark suites such as Defects4J, Bugs.jar, QuixBugs, and IntroClass provide failing tests and reference fixes across Java, Python, and C programs. Classical baselines include GenProg, RSRepair, and Nopol, which search patch spaces through genetic operators, random restarts, and constraint solving. Neural techniques such as SequenceR, CoCoNuT, and Tufano et al.’s Transformer model edits code by learning copy mechanisms, subtokenization, and context windows from large repositories. Recent approaches like TBar, PraPR, and AlphaRepair integrate template-based fix patterns, bytecode-level mutations, and retrieval-augmented prompts for patch generation. Evaluation typically measures test-suite adequacy, plausibility versus correctness labels via manual inspection, and generalization to unseen projects under cross-project splits. Complementary resources include ManySStuBs4J for small bug patterns, CodeRepairBench for unified pipelines, and Defects4J-Additions that expand tasks with flakiness annotations and multi-hunk changes alongside patch minimization and independent test replication for stronger claims of semantic preservation robustly.

Traditional search-based repair explores mutation operators guided by test outcomes. Learning-based systems leverage neural models to propose edits conditioned on buggy context. We then discuss gaps in reliability measurement under realistic developer workflows. spans: Early extractive approaches model content selection using graph ranking (Erkan and Radev, 2004) and maximal marginal relevance (Carbonell and Goldstein, 1998), while neural encoder-decoder models leverage attention (Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017). However, existing evaluations often emphasize single-domain news or instructional content and overlook conversational variability. We define a benchmark that stresses robustness by varying acoustic fidelity, camera motion, and discourse cohesion. Our approach separates selection from fusion by training a retrieval module before an abstractive generator. The design enables controllable trade-offs between coverage and fluency using lightweight knobs at inference. We additionally analyze failure cases to reveal modality-specific hallucinations and alignment drifts. Experiments compare constrained decoding with evidence dropout to estimate calibration under distribution shifts. Human studies measure factuality, focus, and cross-modal grounding using fine-grained rubrics. spans: Forecasting with spatial temporal graphs employs diffusion convolution and sequence models, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Li et al., 2018), as well as PEMS04 and PEMS08 (Zheng et al., 2020), with variants exploring temporal convolutions (Bai et al., 2018), attention based transformers such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) and Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and spatio temporal regularization through graph learning modules.

Road networks exhibit strong spatial dependencies and heterogeneous temporal dynamics that motivate graph-based models. Yet deployments must cope with missing sensors, atypical events, and shifting travel demand across cities. Robust forecasting therefore requires architectures that generalize beyond fixed periodicity and narrow calibration windows. Existing comparisons often conflate model capacity with horizon-specific optimization and ignore structural uncertainty. We revisit generalization by decoupling spatiotemporal encoding from horizon-wise losses and by evaluating across unseen networks. Nevertheless, most pipelines still assume a static adjacency or rely on time-of-day encodings that fail under incidents. We study regime shifts created by weather, sporting events, and road closures to test adaptive reasoning. Our model couples a learned graph prior with a probabilistic state-space forecaster for multi-horizon predictions. Training uses multi-objective criteria that balance point accuracy, calibration, and change-point detection. We derive diagnostics for spillover effects that identify when topological errors dominate temporal noise. Cross-city splits and sensor cold-start experiments expose the brittleness of common design choices. The findings highlight where structural learning contributes to resilience without incurring excessive latency.

spans: Clinical language processing leverages electronic health records corpora such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016) and i2b2 challenges (Uzuner et al., 2008), alongside task datasets like MedNLI (Romanov and Shivade, 2018) and CheXpert labels (Irvin et al., 2019). Demographic and socioeconomic attributes often correlate with documentation practices, label availability, and health outcomes. Auditing methods must therefore assess subgroup disparities alongside overall accuracy and calibration. Despite these resources, subgroup reliability remains poorly characterized due to label noise and site-specific charting styles. We formalize fairness as equalized calibration while allowing cost-sensitive operational thresholds across clinical pathways. Our estimator learns instance weights through counterfactual augmentation that balances observable covariates within strata. We release an auditing toolkit that reports bias metrics with uncertainty intervals and subgroup shift indicators. The empirical study spans intensive care, emergency department, and radiology note collections from multiple hospitals. We provide stratified evaluations by age, sex, and race proxies derived from structured metadata. Results reveal trade-offs between calibration parity and sensitivity that depend on triage prevalence and label delay. We discuss deployment safeguards including monitoring triggers and fallback policies for high-risk contexts. An ethics statement details governance, data protections, and limitations of the observational setting.

spans: Low resource translation has adopted data augmentation techniques such as back translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), self training (He et al., 2020), and dual learning with reinforcement signals (He et al., 2016), often combined with multilingual pretraining. In low-resource scenarios, practitioners must amplify signal from monolingual data and related languages. However, the interaction between augmentation quality and multilingual transfer is still not well understood. Typical evaluations emphasize single language families and mask fragility under domain shift and script variation. This paper examines curriculum design, noise shaping, and calibration to clarify trade-offs in low-resource pipelines. We analyze morphologically rich and distant language pairs to expose where transfer helps and where it hinders. Our study also considers lexicon entropy and morphology-aware tokenization to tune generation under limited data. We propose a quality-aware curriculum that schedules pseudo-parallel data by uncertainty and coverage. spans: Research on text to code has examined semantic parsing and program synthesis across benchmarks including Django (Oda et al., 2015), CoNaLa (Yin et al., 2018), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), and APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Modeling approaches span sequence to sequence learners like PLBART (Ahmad et al., 2021) and CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), constrained decoding with grammars, retrieval augmented generation over code corpora, and large language models fine tuned on repositories such as GitHub using masked span objectives and causal continuation.
Lacks synthesis.document: Converting natural language specifications into executable programs has matured quickly with advances in pretrained code models. Practical adoption nonetheless requires faithful execution, interpretable constraints, and robust generalization to unseen tasks. Benchmarks now test both synthesis from signatures and repair of buggy code under limited feedback. At the same time, prompt design, retrieval, and constrained decoding have become central levers for deployment. We focus on verifiable text to code generation with minimal unit tests and partial type annotations. Our study disentangles the roles of retrieval, constraint learning, and execution feedback across diverse tasks. We also analyze evaluation instability stemming from nondeterminism, test leakage, and multiple correct solutions. To situate our approach, we summarize prominent corpora and modeling directions in recent work. Research on text to code has examined semantic parsing and program synthesis across benchmarks including Django (Oda et al., 2015), CoNaLa (Yin et al., 2018), MBPP (Austin et al., 2021), HumanEval (Chen et al., 2021), and APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Modeling approaches span sequence to sequence learners like PLBART (Ahmad et al., 2021) and CodeT5 (Wang et al., 2021), constrained decoding with grammars, retrieval augmented generation over code corpora, and large language models fine tuned on repositories such as GitHub using masked span objectives and causal continuation. Despite strong aggregate scores, many systems overfit to surface forms and brittle prompt patterns. We present a modular framework that pairs retrieval with type-guided decoding and counterexample-driven repair. The framework supports plug-in verifiers and unit test synthesis to tighten the execution loop. Our experiments evaluate compositional generalization, perturbation robustness, and test-time adaptation across languages. Findings indicate efficiency gains and fewer silent failures under constrained resources.

spans: Prior studies on traffic forecasting span classical time-series filters, convolutional models, and graph learning approaches. ARIMA and VAR baselines are standard (Smith and Brown, 2003; Box and Jenkins, 2015). Early deep models include ST-ResNet and DeepST for grid-based city data (Zhang et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2016). Graph-based methods such as DCRNN, STGCN, ASTGCN, and Graph WaveNet capture road network dependencies using diffusion processes, spectral filters, attention, or dilated temporal convolutions (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). Public datasets widely used for evaluation include METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and TaxiBJ (Jagdish et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). Evaluation typically reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across standard horizons.
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban traffic forecasting underpins mobility planning, congestion mitigation, and real-time routing. Despite notable progress in spatiotemporal modeling, robustness under distribution shift remains limited. In practice, incidents, weather, and special events perturb demand patterns in ways that static models rarely anticipate. Moreover, sensor outages and topology changes introduce structural drift that complicates long-horizon prediction. We investigate how to represent exogenous disruptions while preserving graph inductive biases. Our goal is to learn transferable dynamics that adapt quickly to atypical conditions without extensive retraining. Prior studies on traffic forecasting span classical time-series filters, convolutional models, and graph learning approaches. ARIMA and VAR baselines are standard (Smith and Brown, 2003; Box and Jenkins, 2015). Early deep models include ST-ResNet and DeepST for grid-based city data (Zhang et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2016). Graph-based methods such as DCRNN, STGCN, ASTGCN, and Graph WaveNet capture road network dependencies using diffusion processes, spectral filters, attention, or dilated temporal convolutions (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019). Public datasets widely used for evaluation include METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03/04/07/08, and TaxiBJ (Jagdish et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2017). Evaluation typically reports MAE, RMSE, and MAPE across standard horizons. These advances motivate a formulation that unifies graph diffusion with event-driven context adaptation. We operationalize this idea through a modular architecture that conditions temporal kernels on learned exogenous codes. The approach incorporates uncertainty by aligning probabilistic forecasts with decision-oriented loss functions. To assess generalization under shift, we design perturbation regimes that simulate closures, storms, and demand spikes. Our empirical study quantifies resilience using counterfactual rollouts and recovery-time metrics beyond standard errors. The results highlight when and how context-aware graph models mitigate degradation while preserving efficiency.

spans: Named entity recognition has been studied across general and clinical domains with pretraining regimes and models. CRF and BiLSTM-CRF architectures remain standard baselines for span tagging (Lafferty et al., 2001; Huang et al., 2015). Contextual encoders such as ELMo, BERT, ClinicalBERT, and BioBERT provide strong lexical and syntactic signals for entities (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Alsentzer et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Common benchmarks include CoNLL03 for news, OntoNotes for mixed genres, BC5CDR and NCBI-Disease for biomedical text, and the i2b2 2010/2012 shared tasks for clinical notes (Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Pradhan et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016; Uzuner et al., 2011). Adaptation techniques leverage adversarial training, self-training, vocabulary alignment, and pseudo-labeling across unlabeled corpora.
Lacks synthesis.document: Clinical named entity recognition is essential for structuring information from free-text notes and guidelines. However, privacy constraints and domain mismatch limit the utility of generic annotations. Models trained on newswire often overfit stylistic cues that do not transfer to clinical phrasing. Additionally, label scarcity and abbreviation drift hinder robust boundary detection. We study representation transfer that respects clinical terminology while maintaining generalization. Our approach views adaptation as a balance between lexical memorization and consistency under perturbations. We frame evaluation to reflect realistic deployment conditions with limited supervision. Adaptation techniques leverage adversarial training, self-training, vocabulary alignment, and pseudo-labeling across unlabeled corpora. Building on these observations, we design a cross-domain training objective that decouples entity semantics from surface form. A constrained vocabulary interface mediates updates to reduce catastrophic forgetting. Beyond token-level F1, we quantify calibration and shift-robustness under controlled domain gaps. We further analyze abbreviation expansion and ontology linking as auxiliary tasks. An ablation study isolates the effects of representation tuning, data selection, and regularization. The resulting insights inform practical recipes for adapting NER systems in clinical settings.

spans: Recommender systems ingest multimodal signals to capture item semantics and user intent. Matrix factorization and Bayesian personalized ranking constitute collaborative filtering baselines (Koren et al., 2009; Rendle et al., 2009). Visual and acoustic features from images and trailers are exploited by VBPR, DeepStyle, and MMGCN to enrich sparse interactions via joint embeddings or graph propagation (He and McAuley, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2019). Knowledge-aware models integrate side graphs using TransE-style link predictors and meta-path guided attention (Bordes et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019). Public benchmarks include MovieLens, Amazon Product Data, Ali-CCP, and Pinterest with leave-one-out protocols (Harper and Konstan, 2015; McAuley et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Geng et al., 2015). Common metrics comprise HR@K, NDCG@K.
Lacks synthesis.document: Personalization at scale requires models that leverage scarce interactions without overfitting spurious regularities. Multimodal content is abundant but heterogeneously informative across users and items. Balancing collaborative signals with content requires careful inductive biases and training curricula. Moreover, exposure confounding and popularity skew complicate both optimization and evaluation. We study how modality-specific representations contribute to ranking gains under distribution shift. Our perspective is to disentangle user intent from item affordances via causal regularization. We also examine data efficiency when only a subset of items have rich modalities. We instantiate this with a multi-view encoder and a slate-aware loss that corrects sampling bias. We derive training objectives that align counterfactual estimators with practical negative sampling. A suite of interventions probes robustness to missing or corrupted modalities. The analysis clarifies where multimodal signals help and when they can safely be ignored.

spans: Automatic speech recognition systems have progressed through hybrid, end-to-end, and self-supervised paradigms with noise robustness. HMM-DNN hybrids with fMLLR and i-vector adaptation remain on corpora (Gales, 1998; Saon et al., 2013). End-to-end CTC, attention encoder-decoder, and RNN-T architectures reduce decoding complexity leveraging units and objectives (Graves et al., 2006; Chan et al., 2016; He et al., 2019). Self-supervised front-ends such as wav2vec 2.0, HuBERT, and W2v-BERT improve performance (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021). Robustness techniques include beamforming, SpecAugment, and dereverberation (Vesperini et al., 2016; Park et al., 2019). Widely used datasets include LibriSpeech, WSJ, CHiME-4/6, and Aurora-4 with splits and tools (Panayotov et al., 2015; Lippmann, 1997; Vincent et al., 2017; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000).
Lacks synthesis.document: Robust speech recognition in everyday environments remains a central goal for interactive systems. Background noise, reverberation, and device mismatch degrade both acoustic modeling and decoding. While data augmentation helps, its gains vary widely across domains and utterance lengths. Moreover, on-device constraints push models to trade accuracy for latency and memory. We focus on the interplay between self-supervised features and noise-aware training objectives. Our design targets stability under unseen noise types without sacrificing streaming constraints. To that end, we analyze how feature normalization and curriculum schedules affect convergence. Widely used datasets include LibriSpeech, WSJ, CHiME-4/6, and Aurora-4 with splits and tools (Panayotov et al., 2015; Lippmann, 1997; Vincent et al., 2017; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000). The resulting insights motivate an architecture that couples conformer encoders with adaptive denoising modules. A dual-path loss balances token alignment with segment-level consistency under perturbations. We evaluate streaming and offline variants to examine latency-accuracy trade-offs. Beyond word error rate, we report robustness profiles stratified by SNR and device class. This yields actionable guidance for deploying speech models in uncontrolled acoustic conditions.

spans: Estimation of treatment effects under interference builds on foundations in causal inference and experimental design. The potential outcomes framework and SUTVA provide baselines without spillovers (Neyman, 1923; Rubin, 1974). Network and cluster interference have been modeled via exposure mappings, partial interference, and two-stage randomization (Aronow and Samii, 2017; Hudgens and Halloran, 2008; Tchetgen Tchetgen and VanderWeele, 2012). Randomization-based estimators, inverse probability weighting, and augmented estimators extend to graph settings with uncertainty quantification (Rosenbaum, 2007; Aronow et al., 2013). Observational strategies leverage propensity scores on networks, synthetic controls, and difference-in-differences with interference adjustments (Imbens and Rubin, 2015; Abadie et al., 2010; Forastiere et al., 2021). Common applications include vaccination campaigns, marketplaces, and information diffusion studies with metrics defined over ego networks and clusters.
Lacks synthesis.document: Online experiments increasingly operate on dense social graphs where spillovers are unavoidable. Standard estimators that assume isolation can be biased even when randomization is used. Product rollouts often combine user notifications, content feeds, and ad auctions that propagate exposure. Decision makers need estimates that separate direct effects from network-mediated effects. We study experimental designs and estimators that remain interpretable under realistic interference. Our perspective emphasizes transparency of estimands and the costs of simplifying assumptions. We also prioritize practicality for large-scale industrial deployments. We implement assignment mechanisms compatible with platform constraints and derive unbiased estimators. Scalable variance estimators enable sequential monitoring without inflating false positives. Simulations on replay logs complement field tests to assess sensitivity to graph misspecification. The framework clarifies trade-offs between precision, runtime, and policy relevance.

spans: Prior studies frequently evaluate on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, PEMS08, and TaxiBJ, using architectures such as DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, MTGNN, GMAN, STSGCN, and ASTGCN, with implementations following standard published benchmarks and splits.
Lacks synthesis.document: Traffic forecasting underpins intelligent transportation and urban mobility planning. Accurate predictions help reduce congestion, emissions, and travel time variability. Recent advances in graph neural networks have improved spatiotemporal modeling capacities. Yet the interplay between road topology and demand heterogeneity remains difficult to capture. Classical statistical models rely on stationarity assumptions that rarely hold in practice. Pure sequence models overlook structural correlations among sensors and links. Hybrid approaches attempt to balance temporal recurrence with graph convolutions. Evaluation protocols vary widely across regions and data resolutions. Public datasets differ in sampling rates, sensor coverage, and missing data patterns. Forecasting horizons impose distinct biases on model design and training objectives. Robustness to distribution shifts is especially important during atypical events. Weather, incidents, and policy changes can all perturb baseline traffic dynamics. Transferability across cities also poses unresolved challenges for practitioners. Many works introduce adaptive adjacency or attention to encode dynamic connectivity. Others emphasize physics-inspired constraints to stabilize long-horizon predictions. Data augmentation and imputation strategies mitigate sparsity but may amplify bias. Benchmarks still lack standardized protocols for cross-city generalization. Realistic ablations should reflect lane closures, sensor outages, and seasonal shifts. We revisit modeling choices with a focus on controllable inductive biases. We also examine how uncertainty quantification interacts with routing objectives. We consolidate observations into practical recommendations for deployment. We compare models under consistent preprocessing and common training budgets. We analyze error decomposition to distinguish topology from demand contributions. We stress transparent reporting of evaluation metrics and hyperparameters. Despite reported gains, comparative fairness remains unclear across these settings. Prior studies frequently evaluate on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, PEMS08, and TaxiBJ, using architectures such as DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, MTGNN, GMAN, STSGCN, and ASTGCN, with implementations following standard published benchmarks and splits. Such diversity complicates claims about universal superiority of any single method. Our study isolates topology priors under controlled transfer protocols. We quantify benefits of dynamic adjacency relative to fixed graphs. We integrate exogenous features through calibrated temporal encoders. We release code, preprocessing scripts, and reproducible configurations. Ethical considerations include potential misuse in surveillance and inequitable routing. Limitations involve constrained sensor availability and unmodeled behavioral responses. We outline opportunities for integrating simulation and causal inference in future work.

spans: Prominent datasets and baselines include BraTS, ISLES, MSD, and KiTS, with models such as U-Net, nnU-Net, DeepLabv3+, and SegFormer, plus MONAI augmentations, evaluated via Dice, Hausdorff distance, metrics, and stratified cross-validation protocols.

Prominent datasets and baselines include BraTS, ISLES, MSD, and KiTS, with models such as U-Net, nnU-Net, DeepLabv3+, and SegFormer, plus MONAI augmentations, evaluated via Dice, Hausdorff distance, metrics, and stratified cross-validation protocols. Such diversity complicates fair comparison and clear attribution of performance gains. Prior work shows that data curation can rival architectural innovations in impact. Cross-site harmonization is essential when label noise correlates with scanner vendors. Modality dropout strategies seek to stabilize inference when sequences are unavailable. Attention mechanisms help the model balance global context with fine boundaries. Calibrated uncertainty is valuable for triaging manual review in clinical workflows. Test-time augmentation often improves stability but can inflate computation. Mixed precision accelerates training while preserving numerical stability on modern GPUs. We investigate how modality-aware normalization influences boundary adherence. We assess whether curriculum schedules mitigate optimization instabilities. We explore segmentation consistency under controlled modality ablations. We report calibration, robustness, and fairness metrics alongside Dice and Hausdorff. We analyze failure modes using uncertainty clusters and anatomy-aware stratification. We seek clinically interpretable error patterns for actionable model improvements. We unify preprocessing to minimize confounding in cross-dataset comparisons. We benchmark parameter counts against throughput to assess efficiency tradeoffs. We quantify sensitivity to annotation sparsity using progressive mask thinning. We study effects of scanner drift through time-aware evaluation splits. We release scripts enabling reproducible cross-site experiments and audits. Our ablations cover normalization, augmentation, and loss functions comprehensively. We discuss governance for dataset usage and patient privacy constraints. Limitations include access to only a subset of modalities in some cohorts. We summarize implications for deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings. Finally, we outline promising avenues for federated learning and causal data augmentation.

We analyze error propagation between retrieval and reasoning modules. We incorporate abstention to handle insufficient evidence gracefully. Limitations include incomplete coverage of commonsense relations in graphs. Ethical considerations involve bias propagation through curated knowledge bases. spans: Multimodal sarcasm research has introduced datasets and models spanning text, image, and user context. MUStARD (Castro et al., 2019) compiles audiovisual clips with dialogue transcripts, while MMSD (Cai et al., 2019) collects paired tweets and images annotated for sarcasm. Later efforts include the multimodal Twitter corpus of SARCOS (Schifanella et al., 2016), multimodal Reddit sarcasm subsets derived from SARC (Khodak et al., 2018), and the multimodal extension of SemEval Task 7 on humor and irony (Hossain et al., 2020). Modeling approaches range from late-fusion baselines using VGG-16 and GloVe embeddings, to cross-modal attention with ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), to contrastive alignment with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and to graph-based user modeling that leverages interaction networks on conversation threads across platforms.
Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting sarcasm online is essential for robust content moderation and sentiment-aware applications. Sarcasm often arises from incongruity between literal text and contextual cues, including imagery and user history. Prior computational work demonstrates that unimodal text models struggle when cues are distributed across modalities. To address this challenge, we investigate representations that tie local visual evidence to pragmatic textual markers under realistic data imbalance. Related research on textual sarcasm has traced signals in lexical polarity, intensifiers, and discourse structures learned by transformers. Studies also show that conversational turn-taking and author style provide discriminative features when aggregated over histories. Multimodal sarcasm research has introduced datasets and models spanning text, image, and user context. MUStARD (Castro et al., 2019) compiles audiovisual clips with dialogue transcripts, while MMSD (Cai et al., 2019) collects paired tweets and images annotated for sarcasm. Later efforts include the multimodal Twitter corpus of SARCOS (Schifanella et al., 2016), multimodal Reddit sarcasm subsets derived from SARC (Khodak et al., 2018), and the multimodal extension of SemEval Task 7 on humor and irony (Hossain et al., 2020). Modeling approaches range from late-fusion baselines using VGG-16 and GloVe embeddings, to cross-modal attention with ViLBERT (Lu et al., 2019), to contrastive alignment with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), and to graph-based user modeling that leverages interaction networks on conversation threads across platforms. Despite these resources, disagreement in annotation guidelines and varying definitions of sarcasm complicate cross-dataset evaluation. Further, image-text pairs frequently encode world knowledge that standard pretrained encoders underrepresent. We therefore focus on grounding mechanisms that adjudicate clashes between depicted actions and captioned claims. Our approach links region-level objects and attributes to sentiment shifts using constrained cross-attention and sparse supervision. We evaluate on three benchmarks with consistent splits and report ablations on fusion depth and calibration. Finally, we discuss failure cases involving culture-specific jokes and the limits of visual commonsense.

spans: Benchmarking for time-series anomaly detection commonly relies on public corpora and a mix of classical and neural models. The Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015) and Yahoo Webscope S5 (Laptev et al., 2015) offer labeled point and contextual anomalies, while the UCR Anomaly Archive (Tatbul et al., 2018) aggregates diverse univariate series. Model families include statistical baselines such as ARIMA and STL-ESD, reconstruction-based methods like LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), Donut variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2018), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), as well as density models like DeepSVDD (Ruff et al., 2018). Recent proposals extend to forecasting with DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), GAN methods like TadGAN (Geiger et al., 2020), and models e.g., OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document: Monitoring IoT deployments requires reliable detection of anomalous behavior in multivariate streams. False alarms and latency directly translate into operational costs in cyber-physical systems. Existing detectors vary in their modeling assumptions and training regimes, complicating downstream selection. We analyze the trade-off between sample efficiency, calibration, and interpretability under streaming constraints. Prior work highlights the brittleness of reconstruction losses under distribution shift and concept drift. Moreover, unsupervised scores can be difficult for operators to action without localized explanations. We therefore examine detectors through the lens of attribution faithfulness and retraining cost per drift event. Benchmarking for time-series anomaly detection commonly relies on public corpora and a mix of classical and neural models. The Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015) and Yahoo Webscope S5 (Laptev et al., 2015) offer labeled point and contextual anomalies, while the UCR Anomaly Archive (Tatbul et al., 2018) aggregates diverse univariate series. Model families include statistical baselines such as ARIMA and STL-ESD, reconstruction-based methods like LSTM-AD (Malhotra et al., 2015), Donut variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2018), and USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), as well as density models like DeepSVDD (Ruff et al., 2018). Recent proposals extend to forecasting with DeepAR (Salinas et al., 2020), GAN methods like TadGAN (Geiger et al., 2020), and models e.g., OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019). While these benchmarks provide useful common ground, label quality and windowing conventions often differ substantially. This variability obscures meaningful comparisons of thresholds, decision delays, and maintenance overheads. We propose an evaluation protocol that decouples detection quality from forecast skill using fixed-lag scoring. spans: Foundational datasets and architectures for molecular graphs have shaped recent benchmarks in property prediction. Subsequent variants incorporate physics-motivated features, with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2018), DimeNet and DimeNet++ (Klicpera et al., 2020), and equivariant transformers like SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020) and E3NN (Geiger and Smidt, 2020) emphasizing rotationally consistent interactions and GemNet (Gasteiger et al., 2021) for directionality.

We therefore revisit architectural inductive biases with controlled training protocols and uncertainty estimates. spans: Cross-lingual NER has been explored through standard corpora and multilingual encoders with varying supervision regimes. CoNLL 2002/2003 (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003) provides early benchmarks in Spanish, Dutch, English, and German, while WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017) offers weakly supervised coverage for 282 languages. spans: A broad literature explores textual data augmentation across supervised and semi-supervised regimes. EDA with synonym replacement, insertion, swap, and deletion (Wei and Zou, 2019) offers simple baselines on SST-2 and TREC, while back-translation via neural machine translation systems (Sennrich et al., 2016) produces paraphrases for IMDB and Yelp. Contextual augmentation replaces tokens using bidirectional language models (Kobayashi, 2018), and UDA employs back-translation plus consistency training for semi-supervised tasks (Xie et al., 2020). Additional strategies include TF-IDF word dropout (Fadaee et al., 2017), paraphrase generation with PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and mixup-style interpolation in embedding space for text classification (Guo et al., 2019), with evaluations reported on AG News and other public benchmarks and multiple multilingual variants on XNLI and MLDoc datasets.

spans: Early approaches to networked traffic forecasting employed spatiotemporal convolutions and diffusion processes to model sensor interactions, exemplified by DCRNN (Li et al., 2018) and STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), with evaluations commonly conducted on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY benchmark datasets publicly. Subsequent studies introduced temporal convolutions and adaptive graph learning to capture long-range dependencies, including Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), MixHop variants (Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019), and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020), tests on PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS07. Attention-based architectures expanded this line with temporal self-attention and receptive fields, as seen in ASTGCN (Guo et al., 2019), GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020), and STTN (Xu et al., 2020), frequently paired with positional encodings and residual connections. Parallel efforts explored physics-aware formulations, including variational graph autoencoders for uncertainty quantification (Kipf and Welling, 2016), physics-informed recurrent models (Raissi et al., 2019), and diffusion-based forecasters (Rangapuram et al., 2018), supported by ancillary sensor metadata and weather covariates. Transformer variants generalized sequence modeling with sparse attention, such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), LogTrans (Li et al., 2019), Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022), preprocessing with normalization of historical traffic signals.

In addition, many pipelines underutilize contextual signals such as event calendars, lane closures, and topology metadata available to agencies. We consolidate insights from prior work to motivate a systematic benchmark that aligns metrics with deployment constraints.
