Our analysis highlights how teacher forcing ratios and scheduled sampling influence stability during peak congestion periods. We introduce a stratified evaluation that disaggregates metrics by road class, speed regime, and missingness level to reflect operational priorities. To support reproducibility, we release data loaders, split scripts, and validation protocols compatible with common GNN and Transformer libraries. The resulting framework facilitates apples-to-apples comparisons and reveals when architectural complexity yields diminishing returns. Finally, we discuss implications for procurement processes in agencies that must balance accuracy, latency, and interpretability.

spans: Automated essay scoring has evolved from handcrafted feature bundles toward neural encoders, with classic systems like e-rater (Attali and Burstein, 2006) and newer CNN/LSTM encoders (Taghipour and Ng, 2016) evaluated on ASAP, TOEFL11, and FCE corpora under prompt-specific cross-prompt protocols. Subsequent transformer-based methods leveraged pretrained language models for features and prompt conditioning, including BERT fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa adaptations (Liu et al., 2019), and DeBERTa variants (He et al., 2021), integrating rubric keywords or discourse cues. Research on explainability emphasized feature importance and rationales, employing SHAP analyses (Lundberg and Lee, 2017), attention visualizations (Jain and Wallace, 2019), and example-based explanations with influence functions (Koh and Liang, 2017), compared against rater criteria or rubric annotations. Dataset development likewise broadened coverage and labels, with ICNALE essays and proficiency metadata (Ishikawa, 2013), FCE error-coded corpus (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011), the ASAP set, and BEA shared tasks (Yannakoudakis et al., 2019) standardizing splits and scoring scales. Related strands investigated grammatical error correction and quality estimation as signals, drawing on JFLEG and CoNLL datasets (Napoles et al., 2017; Ng et al., 2014), tagging architectures (Rei and Yannakoudakis, 2016), and objectives aligning rubric dimensions and proficiency.

Automated essay scoring systems promise cost-effective assistance but risk reinforcing biases without careful validation. Moreover, the pedagogical utility of scores depends on transparent links to criteria and actionable suggestions. Existing benchmarks vary widely in genre, proficiency distribution, and annotation granularity, complicating fair comparisons. These inconsistencies motivate a closer examination of data splits, calibration, and generalization regimes for practical deployment. We contextualize current advances with attention to evaluation protocols that reflect educational priorities. Despite strong leaderboard results, many models degrade when prompts change or when essays include code-switching and nonstandard orthography. Our analysis treats these phenomena as structured distribution shifts rather than mere noise. We design controlled train-test configurations to separate prompt-specific memorization from robust language proficiency modeling. In addition, we examine rater disagreement and employ uncertainty-aware metrics that penalize overconfident predictions on ambiguous responses. We compare calibration strategies, including temperature scaling and isotonic regression, for maintaining actionable score thresholds. To support instructional use, we couple global scores with criterion-aligned evidence extracted from model internals and shallow discourse features. We provide ablations that disentangle the contributions of pretraining, prompt tokens, and training objectives across datasets. The resulting picture clarifies which design choices carry across domains and which require prompt-specific adaptation. Finally, we discuss implications for formative assessment settings where fairness and transparency are paramount.

spans: Core algorithms for federated optimization address data heterogeneity and communication constraints, beginning with FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017) and extending to FedProx (Li et al., 2020) and SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2019), evaluated on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and CIFAR-10 in non-IID. Optimization advances also include adaptive server optimizers and normalization, such as FedOpt (Reddi et al., 2021), FedAdam, FedYogi, and FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), which stabilize training under variable client participation, partial local epochs, and straggler effects significantly. Communication reduction has been pursued through gradient quantization and sparsification, leveraging QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017), SignSGD with error-feedback (Bernstein et al., 2018; Karimireddy et al., 2019), and top-k selection (Stich et al., 2018) for bandwidth-limited links scenarios. Fairness and personalization have been explored via q-FFL (Li et al., 2020), clustered federated learning (Sattler et al., 2020), meta-learning initializations like Per-FedAvg (Fallah et al., 2020), and personalization layers, frequently measured with metrics and group disparity indicators. Benchmarking suites such as LEAF (Caldas et al., 2019) and FLUTE (Diao et al., 2021) provide standardized preprocessing, client partitions, and simulators, while production logs from mobile keyboard prediction and speech domains offer participation patterns and privacy constraints.

Moreover, privacy mechanisms like secure aggregation and differential privacy interact with communication schedules and update sparsity. Prior studies sometimes report throughput or accuracy in isolation, obscuring trade-offs relevant to edge deployment. We examine these trade-offs by aligning algorithmic choices with measured device costs and variability in participation. Our perspective emphasizes end-to-end efficiency metrics that reflect application service-level objectives. Although the literature offers many ingredients, their combined impact on battery drain and responsiveness remains undercharacterized. We build a measurement harness that records energy, CPU cycles, memory, and wireless traffic during representative training rounds. Using controlled client pools and configurable network traces, we compare optimizers, compression, and aggregation schedules under equal-accuracy targets. We quantify the energy-accuracy frontier and identify regimes where additional local computation is cheaper than extra uplink communication. We further explore how adaptive client sampling improves both fairness and tail latency for slow devices. Our experiments integrate secure aggregation to assess overheads and feasibility within realistic duty cycles. spans: Classical time-series anomaly detection employs statistical and distance-based techniques such as ARIMA residual analysis (Box and Jenkins, 1970), seasonal decomposition with thresholds, Local Outlier Factor (Breunig et al., 2000), and Isolation Forest (Liu et al., 2008) across industrial settings globally. Neural reconstruction methods replace manual features with learned embeddings, exemplified by LSTM autoencoders (Malhotra et al., 2016), variational autoencoders for probabilistic scoring (Kingma and Welling, 2014), and temporal convolutional denoisers, trained with sliding windows and stopping heuristics criteria. Generative approaches model normal behavior distributions and flag deviations, including Donut with missing-data imputation (Xu et al., 2018), OmniAnomaly using stochastic recurrent flows (Su et al., 2019), and MAD-GAN ensembles (Li et al., 2019) for high-dimensional sensor streams. Multivariate correlation is captured by attention mechanisms and residual correlation maps, as in MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), DAGMM-style hybrids (Zong et al., 2018), graph temporal networks, and transformers adapted for irregular sampling with masking and positional encodings. Furthermore, ground-truth labels are scarce, delayed, and sometimes ambiguous due to multi-causal incidents. Traditional detectors can be brittle to distribution shifts, while overparameterized models may overfit subtle artifacts. A practical evaluation must integrate detection quality with diagnostic latency and actionability. We overview prominent paradigms before introducing an assessment protocol tailored to operational constraints. We adopt consistent windowing, scoring, and delay penalties to avoid optimistic inflation from retrospective smoothing. In addition, we present cross-facility transfer tests that challenge robustness under diverse sensor layouts and processes. Our study analyzes alert aggregation and cooldown strategies that balance sensitivity and operator workload. We examine calibration under varying anomaly prevalence and propose thresholding methods that maintain interpretable risk categories. To support diagnostics, we pair probabilistic scores with ranked attributions over channels and time segments. We provide ablations quantifying the influence of reconstruction loss, context length, and sampling normalization. The resulting insights clarify how modeling choices map to maintenance objectives and downtime costs. Finally, we release scripts and baselines to facilitate reproducible comparisons across public and semi-public corpora.

Prior work demonstrates that modeling choices in retrieval and aggregation can dominate end-to-end quality. Yet it remains difficult to compare methods when training data, evidence granularity, and evaluation metrics vary widely across studies. We outline these challenges to motivate a careful examination of evidence selection and uncertainty handling in cross-domain settings. Our overview emphasizes the link between answerability and calibrated confidence in realistic deployments. We investigate evaluation schemes that couple answer correctness with evidence sufficiency and coverage. Concretely, we compare fixed-k retrieval against confidence-adaptive retrieval under identical compute budgets. We analyze how retriever recall interacts with reader calibration to influence abstention quality. We further test adversarial robustness using paraphrases and distractor passages that preserve answerability while degrading lexical overlap. Our experiments highlight conditions under which selective prediction reduces harmful errors without excessive deferral. We release a standardized evaluation harness with aligned splits, metrics, and cost accounting for retrieval. Finally, we discuss practical implications for product teams integrating question answering into search and assistance applications.

However, little is known about how these models behave when sensors drop out for extended periods. We formalize this as a stochastic masking process aligned with observed maintenance logs. The proposed benchmarks quantify both short-term accuracy and multi-horizon degradation during outages. We report calibration metrics and uncertainty intervals to assess decision risk. spans: Representative traffic forecasting approaches include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, GMAN, DSTAGNN, AGCRN, and MTGNN, as well as transformer baselines adapted to graphs and temporal convolutions. Common benchmarks span METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08, with evaluation typically reported via MAE, RMSE, and MAPE under fixed sensor splits, and with auxiliary metadata such as road distances, adjacency construction by k-nearest neighbors, and time-of-day encodings documented across public implementations and leaderboards from academic repositories and widely referenced benchmark papers and reports.
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban mobility systems rely on accurate traffic forecasting to manage congestion and deliver reliable travel times. Recent advances in deep learning have shifted the focus from univariate temporal models to spatiotemporal architectures that exploit road network structure. Graph neural networks provide a natural parameterization for sensor interactions mediated by the topology of transportation networks. Despite notable progress, performance varies widely across cities, sensor densities, and deployment constraints. Furthermore, the reproducibility of reported gains is challenged by heterogeneous preprocessing and unstandardized evaluation protocols. A clear view of data regimes and inductive biases is essential for making models dependable in practice. Representative traffic forecasting approaches include DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, ASTGCN, GMAN, DSTAGNN, AGCRN, and MTGNN, as well as transformer baselines adapted to graphs and temporal convolutions. Common benchmarks span METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08, with evaluation typically reported via MAE, RMSE, and MAPE under fixed sensor splits, and with auxiliary metadata such as road distances, adjacency construction by k-nearest neighbors, and time-of-day encodings documented across public implementations and leaderboards from academic repositories and widely referenced benchmark papers and reports. However, the field lacks a principled analysis of how graph construction choices interact with horizon length and data sparsity. We investigate the sensitivity of forecasting accuracy to topology definitions, temporal aggregation, and exogenous features under a unified protocol. Our study quantifies the contribution of spatial and temporal components by ablations that hold evaluation metrics and data splits constant. We further examine robustness to missing sensors, corrupted streams, and shift between weekdays and weekends. To support transparent comparisons, we release preprocessing scripts that standardize scaling, masking, and horizon offsets. We also provide lightweight baselines that isolate recurrent, convolutional, and attention mechanisms without architecture-specific heuristics. The resulting insights inform deployment guidelines for agencies operating under compute, latency, and maintenance constraints. Together, these findings illuminate design tradeoffs that matter more than architectural novelty for reliable traffic prediction.

spans: Prominent clinical IE systems include BiLSTM-CRF pipelines, SciSpaCy entity recognizers, and transformer encoders such as BioBERT, BlueBERT, ClinicalBERT, and PubMedBERT trained on biomedical corpora. Additional benchmarks include MedNLI, BC5CDR, NCBI Disease, and CHEMPROT collections, with standard train dev splits.
Lacks synthesis.document: Extracting entities and relations from clinical narratives is vital for cohort identification, pharmacovigilance, and outcome studies. Compared with general-domain text, clinical notes exhibit heavy abbreviation, telegraphic style, and institution-specific terminology. Privacy regulations and annotation burden constrain dataset size and availability, complicating model development. Domain-adaptive pretraining and distant supervision have partially mitigated data scarcity but often degrade calibration and portability. In particular, span boundary ambiguity and nested mentions remain challenging in production pipelines. A systematic comparison of modeling choices under realistic deployment constraints is therefore needed. Additional benchmarks include MedNLI, BC5CDR, NCBI Disease, and CHEMPROT collections, with standard train dev splits. We focus on de-identified discharge summaries and problem lists to quantify tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and maintainability. Our framework separates lexical normalization, section detection, and entity recognition to improve modularity and error analysis. We evaluate models under cross-site validation to expose brittleness to institution shifts and note formatting differences. To address label scarcity, we assess selective annotation strategies guided by uncertainty and section-specific sampling. We report calibration curves and threshold-independent metrics to clarify deployment risks beyond micro-averaged F1. We release reproducible preprocessing and inference scripts with unit tests that document assumptions. The results provide operational guidance for clinical IE pipelines in settings where retraining is infrequent and auditing is mandatory.

spans: Canonical collaborative and content-aware recommenders encompass matrix factorization, BPR, NCF, NeuMF, NGCF, LightGCN, GATNE, and xDeepFM, along with post-hoc explainers based on attention weights and gradient attribution. Standard evaluation draws on MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, Amazon Product Reviews, Yelp, and LastFM, reporting Hit@K, NDCG@K, MAP, and AUC over leave-one-out splits, while side information such as item descriptions, user reviews, and knowledge graph links is included in several widely used public baselines and benchmark leaderboards track explainability outputs with template-based rationales metrics.
Lacks synthesis.document: Recommendation platforms increasingly seek explanations that justify suggested items to end users and auditors. Explanations can improve trust, enable debugging, and satisfy transparency regulations in high-stakes domains. Yet the link between recommendation accuracy and explanation usefulness remains underexplored. Prior work often evaluates with offline proxies that may not reflect user understanding or business goals. Moreover, explanation templates risk leaking recommendation signals and inflating perceived faithfulness. Canonical collaborative and content-aware recommenders encompass matrix factorization, BPR, NCF, NeuMF, NGCF, LightGCN, GATNE, and xDeepFM, along with post-hoc explainers based on attention weights and gradient attribution. Standard evaluation draws on MovieLens 1M, MovieLens 20M, Amazon Product Reviews, Yelp, and LastFM, reporting Hit@K, NDCG@K, MAP, and AUC over leave-one-out splits, while side information such as item descriptions, user reviews, and knowledge graph links is included in several widely used public baselines and benchmark leaderboards track explainability outputs with template-based rationales metrics. Despite the breadth of datasets and algorithms, there is little agreement on how to measure explanation faithfulness and plausibility jointly. We formulate a protocol that disentangles ranking performance from explanation evaluation through counterfactual masking and simulator studies. The protocol defines perturbations that target tokens or entities referenced by the explanation while preserving relevance features. We pair human judgments with automatic robustness metrics to triangulate faithfulness under controlled ablations. To reduce annotation burden, we design a small set of prompts and adversarial candidates that reveal inconsistency quickly. We also analyze calibration of explanation confidence and its correlation with downstream satisfaction proxies. Our experiments compare extractive, template, and generative explainers under identical ranking backbones. The findings surface tradeoffs between user-perceived helpfulness and causal impact on recommendations. We conclude with guidelines for reporting that align explanation quality with practical deployment constraints.

Lacks synthesis.document: On-device speech recognition enables private, low-latency voice interfaces without continuous connectivity. Federated learning offers a means to train acoustic and language models across many devices without centralizing raw audio. However, user heterogeneity, intermittent connectivity, and hardware diversity complicate optimization stability and evaluation. Distribution shift across accents, background noise, and speaking styles further degrades aggregate performance. Standard centralized recipes do not readily translate to federated protocols due to communication and privacy constraints. There is a need to separate algorithmic advances from confounding factors introduced by data partitioning and deployment choices. We therefore examine benchmarks and formulations that isolate the dominant sources of variance in reported gains. Additional resources include AISHELL, Switchboard, Fisher, and VoxPopuli, with non-iid partitions, participation dropouts, and on-device quantization constraints documented in public baselines and open-source simulation toolkits widely. We introduce a simulator that reproduces client sampling, local computation limits, and drift patterns measured from real applications. The study compares partial fine-tuning strategies that adapt front ends or language models while freezing acoustic encoders. We analyze update clipping, adaptive optimizers, and client weighting schemes under identical privacy budgets. Our protocol emphasizes robust baselines with transparent hyperparameters and reproducible preprocessing. Results reveal tradeoffs between accuracy, uplink cost, and fairness across demographic groups. These insights inform practitioners deploying federated ASR under tight privacy and latency requirements.

Lacks synthesis.document: Models trained on one dataset often fail when deployed in environments with different textures, viewpoints, or devices. Domain generalization studies aim to learn features that transfer to unseen domains without access to target data. Beyond average accuracy, practitioners require reliability across sites and operating conditions. However, heterogeneous protocols and backbones make it difficult to attribute improvements to specific ideas. Research on domain generalization has explored invariance penalties such as IRM and VREx, distribution alignment via MMD and CORAL, augmentation strategies including Mixup and RandAugment, and meta-learning approaches like MLDG, MetaReg, and SWAD. Benchmarks commonly include PACS, VLCS, Office-Home, TerraIncognita, and DomainNet, with evaluation averaging accuracy across held-out domains under fixed backbones and training seeds documented by public codebases. Additional datasets feature Camelyon17, FMoW, and WILDS collections, while robustness metrics consider worst-domain accuracy, calibration error, and shift-induced degradation curves in practice. We present a controlled evaluation that isolates architectural, data, and optimization factors using a common training pipeline. The design ensures identical backbones, augmentations, and early stopping rules for all methods. We further probe the role of domain count, sample size imbalance, and label shift on generalization behavior. To assess reliability, we measure performance stratified by object frequency and scene attributes derived from interpretable tags. Our analysis highlights when invariance penalties help and when they conflict with class separation. We complement accuracy with calibration and abstention metrics relevant for risk-sensitive applications. Finally, we report compute-normalized results to contextualize gains against resource budgets. All code, logs, and random seeds are released to support independent verification. The study clarifies which components matter most for robust cross-domain recognition.

spans: Recent work on federated learning surveys privacy-preserving optimization, communication-efficient updates, and personalization across heterogeneous clients (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020), plus robust aggregation and adversarial resilience (Blanchard et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018) and benchmark suites such as LEAF and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; He et al., 2020).

In healthcare-focused deployments, data modalities and evaluation setups follow prior datasets and pipelines, drawing on brain tumor segmentation and pathology cohorts, sensing corpora, and cross-silo EMR benchmarks (Menze et al., 2015; Sheller et al., 2020; Rieke et al., 2020; Saeed et al., 2011). Methodologically, client-drift mitigation, proximal regularization, adaptive client weighting, and meta-learning variants recur across studies (Sahu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020; Reddi et al., 2021; Fallah et al., 2020), while privacy techniques span secure aggregation, differential privacy, and homomorphic encryption (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016; Aono et al., 2017). Systems work references aggregation, asynchronous updates, and scheduling on benchmarks such as LEAF, OARF, and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; Nishio and Yonetani, 2019; He et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, an appealing property for sensitive domains such as healthcare. Despite extensive benchmarking, questions remain about evaluation fidelity when clinical constraints distort participation and labeling. Prior results also reveal brittle behavior when client data distributions drift over time due to practice changes or sensor aging. We focus on cross-silo settings where institutions exhibit persistent statistical heterogeneity and intermittent connectivity. Considerations extend from optimization stability to governance, given strict audit requirements and shifting consent policies. In healthcare-focused deployments, data modalities and evaluation setups follow prior datasets and pipelines, drawing on brain tumor segmentation and pathology cohorts, sensing corpora, and cross-silo EMR benchmarks (Menze et al., 2015; Sheller et al., 2020; Rieke et al., 2020; Saeed et al., 2011). Systems work references aggregation, asynchronous updates, and scheduling on benchmarks such as LEAF, OARF, and FedML (Caldas et al., 2018; Nishio and Yonetani, 2019; He et al., 2020). These references demonstrate the breadth of tooling, but the interplay between methodological and operational axes remains underexplored in practice. Consequently, design choices should be examined under workload models that capture clinical cadence and resource envelopes. We thus formulate a study to stress-test training and privacy budgets under realistic participation regimes.

spans: Graph neural networks include spectral and spatial message-passing variants for node, edge, and graph prediction (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Gilmer et al., 2017; Veličković et al., 2018), with pooling approaches (Ying et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2019) and evaluation on citation graphs including Cora, PubMed, Reddit, and OGB (Sen et al., 2008; Hu et al., 2020).

Temporal and relational reasoning has been approached through dynamic graph embeddings, temporal point process models, and knowledge graph completion objectives, evaluated on benchmarks and datasets such as ICEWS, GDELT, WIKIDATA5M, and FB15k-237 (Trivedi et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2020; Toutanova and Chen, 2015). Architectures include recurrent or attention-based message passing with time encodings, decay mechanisms, and snapshot aggregations (Rossi et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019; Kazemi and Goel, 2018), while negative sampling, adversarial training, and calibration strategies are common training heuristics (Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; He et al., 2018). Evaluation protocols vary across inductive versus transductive settings and micro- versus macro-temporal granularity, with metrics spanning MRR, Hits@k, and AUC and temporal coverage analysis.

We center the discussion on temporal link prediction with inductive generalization across entities. Prior findings highlight sensitivity to negative sampling choices and windowing policies during training. Reproducibility further depends on standardizing timestamp normalization and edge de-duplication procedures. Consequently, comparisons should be drawn under consistent filtering rules and time-aware baselines. The remainder of this paper outlines datasets, models, and protocols used in our study.

spans: Research at the intersection of speech, vision, and translation explores speech-to-text translation without transcripts, image-guided machine translation, and alignments (Berard et al., 2016; Weiss et al., 2017; Elliott et al., 2017; Caglayan et al., 2019), with datasets including MuST-C, How2, Multi30K, and Flickr30k Entities (Di Gangi et al., 2019; Sanabria et al., 2018; Elliott et al., 2016; Plummer et al., 2015).

Multimodal pre-training for translation leverages contrastive objectives, masked prediction, and alignment losses over speech, text, and images, building on frameworks such as CLIP, wav2vec, and M3P, and corpora like LAION, VoxPopuli, and CoVoST (Radford et al., 2021; Baevski et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Schuhmann et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Methods span cross-attention fusion, gated adapters, and modality-specific encoders with shared multilingual subword vocabularies (Lu et al., 2019; Tsai et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2021), while training recipes report curriculum schedules, temperature scaling, and multilingual balancing heuristics (Conneau et al., 2020; Arivazhagan et al., 2019; Bapna and Firat, 2019). Evaluations use BLEU, chrF, and COMET on MuST-C, How2, and Multi30K in zero-shot conditions across languages globally.

spans: Neural code understanding spans sequence-to-sequence models, graph-based encoders, and pretrained models over source code, with applications to summarization, code search, and vulnerability detection (Alon et al., 2019; Hellendoorn et al., 2020; Ahmad et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2020), and evaluation on datasets such as CodeSearchNet, CodeXGLUE, and Defects4J (Husain et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021; Just et al., 2014).

Classical program analysis contributes static taint tracking, abstract interpretation, and symbolic execution, supported by tools and frameworks including CodeQL, Infer, Frama-C, KLEE, and angr (Sui et al., 2016; Calcagno et al., 2015; Cousot and Cousot, 1977; Cadar et al., 2008; Shoshitaishvili et al., 2016). Empirical studies report benchmarks and datasets such as Juliet, SARD, Devign, and BigCloneBench with tasks covering vulnerability identification, clone detection, and bug localization (NIST, 2014; Zhou et al., 2019; Svajlenko et al., 2014). Hybrid approaches integrate mined patterns, program dependence graphs, and API usage models for richer features (Li et al., 2016; Krinke, 2001; LeClair et al., 2019), while evaluation protocols emphasize precision-recall curves, ROC-AUC, F1, and cost-effectiveness metrics over labeled functions and commits, and detailed repository-level change histories.

Yet training data for security-oriented tasks often suffers from skewed labeling and duplication. Differences in build systems and language dialects further complicate preprocessing and normalization. This paper examines cross-project generalization for vulnerability detection and summary generation. We consider settings where only limited metadata is available for supervision and filtering. These resources and techniques motivate a systematic look at evaluation design and artifact curation. We discuss protocols for temporal splits aligned to release cycles and dependency changes. Finally, we outline the experimental setup, baselines, and ablation criteria.

spans: Interactive recommendation draws on contextual bandits, slate decision models, and reinforcement learning with feedback loops (Li et al., 2010; Swaminathan et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019), coupled with training on public benchmark datasets like MovieLens, Criteo, RecSys Challenge, and KuaiRec (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Juan et al., 2016; Jannach et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2022).

Off-policy evaluation in recommendation relies on estimators that correct for logging bias using propensity scores, importance sampling variants, and model-based value functions (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983; Horvitz and Thompson, 1952; Dudík et al., 2014; Jiang and Li, 2016). Methods include IPS, SNIPS, DR, DML, and fitted Q evaluation with doubly robust extensions, shrinkage, and self-normalization (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Dudík et al., 2011; Feng et al., 2021; Le et al., 2019), with slate-specific corrections and position bias (Joachims et al., 2017; Ai et al., 2018). Experimental practice reports replay and simulators on datasets such as MovieLens, Yahoo! R6A, Criteo, and KuaiRec, using regret and off-policy accuracy as metrics (Li et al., 2011; Harper and Konstan, 2016; Chapelle et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document: Recommendation platforms interact with users over time, turning ranking into a sequential decision process. Data shifts and exposure biases complicate learning and evaluation under bandit feedback. Interactive recommendation draws on contextual bandits, slate decision models, and reinforcement learning with feedback loops (Li et al., 2010; Swaminathan et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019; Ie et al., 2019), coupled with training on public benchmark datasets like MovieLens, Criteo, RecSys Challenge, and KuaiRec (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Juan et al., 2016; Jannach et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2022). Real-world systems must contend with delayed rewards and confounded behavior logs. Reward shaping and diversity constraints introduce additional trade-offs between exploration and satisfaction. We study learning and evaluation protocols that avoid logging-policy overfitting. Environments also require safety mechanisms limiting harmful or low-quality exposures during learning. R6A, Criteo, and KuaiRec, using regret and off-policy accuracy as metrics (Li et al., 2011; Harper and Konstan, 2016; Chapelle et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2022). spans: Early optimization strategies emphasize simple weight averaging and client sampling (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016), while stabilization under heterogeneous devices is addressed by proximal regularization and adaptive aggregation (Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy and security components include secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2006), and evaluations rely on imaging benchmarks such as CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, BraTS, and UK Biobank (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Sudlow et al., 2015).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Federated learning has emerged as a compelling paradigm for training models across institutions without centralizing raw data. In clinical imaging, this promise intersects with strict privacy laws and fragmented infrastructures that complicate data sharing. Hospitals maintain heterogeneous scanners, labeling practices, and software stacks, which collectively challenge optimization and evaluation. Moreover, outcome shifts and demographic skews can bias site-specific models in ways that undermine equitable performance. These pressures motivate algorithms that can learn robustly from decentralized data while honoring regulatory and operational constraints. Yet methodological choices around communication, personalization, and privacy budgeting remain underexplored in domain-specific settings. Our study focuses on chest radiography classification across multiple health systems subject to diverse governance policies. We investigate how aggregation noise, client dropout, and label prevalence affect convergence and calibration.

Prior work has documented that non identically distributed data leads to slower training and degraded generalization. Instability often arises when gradient directions across sites diverge, amplifying drift under limited communication budgets. Personalization layers and mixture-of-experts style adapters have been proposed to bridge site idiosyncrasies while retaining a shared backbone. However, clinical deployments also confront auditing needs that require interpretable summaries of contribution and risk. Communication-efficient updates must also coexist with encryption protocols and intermittent network availability. In this context, careful benchmarking is essential to disentangle algorithmic benefits from infrastructure artifacts. We therefore align protocol design with measurement principles that emphasize fairness, uncertainty, and label provenance. We outline ablations that expose when reweighting, momentum schedules, or adaptive client selection drive improvements.

Early optimization strategies emphasize simple weight averaging and client sampling (McMahan et al., 2017; Konečný et al., 2016), while stabilization under heterogeneous devices is addressed by proximal regularization and adaptive aggregation (Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy and security components include secure aggregation and differential privacy mechanisms (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2006), and evaluations rely on imaging benchmarks such as CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, BraTS, and UK Biobank (Irvin et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2019; Menze et al., 2015; Sudlow et al., 2015). 

While these ingredients provide building blocks, healthcare settings complicate assumptions about participation and stability. Many hospitals enforce maintenance windows that yield predictable but prolonged client unavailability. Labeling operations frequently lag imaging acquisition by weeks, introducing temporal feedback loops into training data. These practicalities motivate protocols that adaptively schedule clients while preserving privacy budgets. They also require calibration strategies that withstand sporadic updates and evolving case mixes. We evaluate sensitivity to client churn and site size through controlled drop patterns and synthetic resampling. For interpretability, we integrate counterfactual attention maps subject to site-specific constraints on gradient access. Our metrics include subgroup AUC gaps, expected calibration error, and privacy cost profiles.

The study design reflects three goals tied to external validity in clinical operations. First, we aim to decouple statistical heterogeneity from communication volatility in estimates of model improvement. Second, we seek to characterize how privacy mechanisms interact with calibration under shift. Third, we probe whether lightweight personalization can reduce disparities without widening performance variance. The resulting insights inform policy choices about participation thresholds and reporting obligations. They also suggest how to align engineering roadmaps with governance expectations for safety monitoring. We release code to encourage reproducibility under constrained networking assumptions. Institutional review approvals and data use agreements governed all data handling and experiment execution.

spans: Pretrained sequence models and instruction-tuned variants underpin many approaches to automated feedback, including BERT, T5, and GPT-3 style decoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022). Evaluation and benchmarking draw on question answering and educational platforms such as SQuAD, GLUE, EdNet, and ASSISTments, alongside rubric-annotated corpora for short answers and essays (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020; Heffernan and Heffernan, 2014). ASAP and BEA shared tasks are additionally used in several studies.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Effective feedback accelerates learning by pinpointing misconceptions and highlighting actionable next steps. In large courses, providing timely individualized guidance strains instructor bandwidth and delays remediation. Natural language generation offers a scalable alternative but must balance accuracy, tone, and pedagogical value. Models that draft suggestions from student work risk hallucinations that can mislead or demotivate. Educational settings further demand transparency about evidence and alignment with rubrics and standards. These challenges motivate systems that are helpful yet cautious under real classroom constraints. We investigate feedback generation for short factual responses in introductory science courses. Our framework emphasizes faithfulness to source evidence and calibrated uncertainty communication.

Prior approaches fall into retrieval-augmented generation, classification with templated text, and edit-based rewriting. Retrieval locates relevant passages or exemplars that anchor claims to trusted content. Classification techniques map responses to rubric categories that drive structured messages. Edit models transform student answers toward reference solutions while preserving student voice. Despite progress, limited domain transfer and brittle error detection hinder adoption. Human raters also report that tone consistency and politeness remain uneven across cohorts. We accordingly adopt evaluation protocols that foreground both correctness and perceived helpfulness. Our data curation captures diverse phrasings, partial credit cases, and adversarial paraphrases.

For deployment realism, we restrict access to course materials and disallow internet retrieval. Uncertainty estimates drive abstention when evidence falls below calibrated thresholds. We benchmark ablations to quantify contributions from retrieval, calibration, and style conditioning. Pretrained sequence models and instruction-tuned variants underpin many approaches to automated feedback, including BERT, T5, and GPT-3 style decoders (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022). Evaluation and benchmarking draw on question answering and educational platforms such as SQuAD, GLUE, EdNet, and ASSISTments, alongside rubric-annotated corpora for short answers and essays (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Choi et al., 2020; Heffernan and Heffernan, 2014). ASAP and BEA shared tasks are additionally used in several studies. 

Beyond task framing, feedback systems must navigate privacy obligations and student consent. Log retention policies and model updates should avoid leaking identifiable information. We implement privacy-preserving analytics that aggregate error types without storing raw answers. To ground claims, we integrate retrieval over instructor-authored notes with citation snippets. A style controller moderates verbosity and formality to respect course norms. We instruct models to defer to uncertainty rather than guess when knowledge is missing. The evaluation protocol blends automatic metrics with double-blind human judgments. We report inter-rater reliability and annotate disagreements to surface ambiguous cases. Error analyses categorize hallucination, omission, and misinterpretation across knowledge types. The findings inform rubrics and heuristics for better prompt designs and safeguards. These practices aim to strengthen trustworthiness and reduce instructor overhead without overpromising capability. All experiments complied with institutional policies on student data and informed consent.

spans: Cross-lingual summarization draws on multilingual pretrained encoders and decoders such as mBART, mT5, and PEGASUS (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). Public resources for training and evaluation include CNN/Daily Mail, XSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua, sometimes paired with machine-translated proxies for low-resource targets (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Additional baselines also include extractive heuristics and pointer-generator models with ranking components (Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Summarization enables readers to digest events quickly without scanning full articles. For international newsrooms, the need extends across languages with divergent discourse structures. Multilingual summarization systems must compress information while preserving salience and factual grounding. Low-resource languages amplify the challenge due to scarce high-quality training pairs. Additionally, stylistic norms about attribution and hedging vary across outlets and regions. These factors complicate evaluation because reference summaries capture only a slice of acceptable renderings. Our work studies cross-lingual abstractive summarization for global news feeds. We target scenarios where source articles and target summaries occupy different languages.

A central question is how to leverage translation signals without overfitting to literal phrasing. Naively translating sources and fine-tuning monolingual models often yields brittle paraphrases. We therefore examine architectures and data curation strategies that encourage language-agnostic content planning. The approach integrates contrastive supervision to penalize spurious copying across languages. Cross-lingual summarization draws on multilingual pretrained encoders and decoders such as mBART, mT5, and PEGASUS (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). Public resources for training and evaluation include CNN/Daily Mail, XSum, XL-Sum, and WikiLingua, sometimes paired with machine-translated proxies for low-resource targets (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Hasan et al., 2021; Ladhak et al., 2020). Additional baselines also include extractive heuristics and pointer-generator models with ranking components (Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2019). 

Beyond modeling, we focus on calibration and verifiability under domain shift. We incorporate retrieval from multilingual wire services to ground claims. Evidence pointers are surfaced in the target language alongside segment-level uncertainty. Evaluators can click citations to inspect support for each salient statement. We train with a mixture of supervised and contrastive losses to balance fluency and faithfulness. A length controller allows editors to select headline, brief, or digest formats. We report performance on languages spanning three families and two scripts. Expert evaluation uses calibrated scales for accuracy, style, and geopolitical neutrality.

Automatic metrics include ROUGE variants and semantic similarity measures aligned with translation quality. We supplement these with entity-level consistency checks across languages. To stress test robustness, we introduce noisy machine-translated sources and domain-shifted topics. Ablations probe the contributions of retrieval, contrastive learning, and decoding constraints. We also audit for hallucinated entities and unsupported attributions. Results highlight trade-offs between compression rate and factual precision in low-resource settings. Case studies illustrate successes and failure modes across named entities and events. The analysis connects modeling choices with observed calibration behaviors. We discuss implications for news production workflows and editorial oversight. Resources and code will be released to facilitate reproducibility and shared evaluation. Ethical considerations cover anonymity of sources and amplification risks in sensitive coverage.

spans: Spatiotemporal forecasting models often build on graph recurrent and convolutional architectures, including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, and ASTGCN (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019). Common evaluation datasets encompass METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with error metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE standardized across studies (Jagadish et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Benchmarks compare temporal attention, diffusion operators, and spectral filters across graphs and horizons (Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Urban mobility systems hinge on accurate forecasts of traffic speeds and volumes. Operators adjust signals and dispatch resources based on near-term predictions of flow. Long-range planning also depends on structural understanding of demand and bottlenecks. Graph neural networks encode roadway topology to capture spatial dependencies among sensors. Temporal dynamics are modeled through recurrence, convolution, or attention mechanisms. Nevertheless, deployments confront missing data, sensor outages, and distribution shift from events. In many cities, seasonal patterns interact with infrastructure projects in complex ways. Our work addresses reliability under perturbations in network connectivity and observation density.

We consider a scenario where sensors intermittently fail due to maintenance and weather. The model must interpolate missing streams while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. We propose training objectives that tie uncertainty to reconstruction difficulty. To emulate outages, we mask segments according to empirically observed failure durations. We additionally test robustness to topology errors from outdated maps. Performance is summarized at corridor and network levels to reflect operational needs. We provide a framework for stress testing different degradation regimes. This aligns evaluation with real constraints faced by transport agencies.

Prior work has investigated data imputation and robust training, but often in isolation. We connect these strands through joint objectives that penalize brittle feature use. Ablations reveal how each component contributes to accuracy and calibration under shift. Comparisons span model capacity, training budgets, and outage severity to permit fair assessment. Spatiotemporal forecasting models often build on graph recurrent and convolutional architectures, including DCRNN, STGCN, Graph WaveNet, and ASTGCN (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2019). Common evaluation datasets encompass METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, with error metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE standardized across studies (Jagadish et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020). Benchmarks compare temporal attention, diffusion operators, and spectral filters across graphs and horizons (Zheng et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2020). 

Building on this context, we emphasize stress-tested estimates under partial observability. We calibrate interval forecasts to maintain coverage as sensors drop out. The training loss balances point accuracy with uncertainty sharpness and validity. We design metrics that expose overconfidence under bursty missingness patterns. Interpretability modules trace which neighbors drive predictions during outages. We analyze whether learned dependencies align with domain expectations about corridors. Case studies illustrate responses to stadium events and inclement weather. Findings inform maintenance scheduling and sensor redundancy decisions. The codebase supports reproducible ablations and standardized stress scenarios. Partnerships with agencies enable validation on archived incident logs. Ethical considerations address equity in service impacts from adaptive control.

spans: Face recognition benchmarks commonly involve LFW, IJB-A/B/C, VGGFace2, and MegaFace, with protocol variations across verification and identification tasks (Huang et al., 2007; Klare et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2018; Kemelmacher-Shlizerman et al., 2016). Prominent loss functions and backbones include SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace trained on large-scale web scrapes (Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2019). Fairness assessments frequently report subgroup false positive and true positive rates alongside demographic parity and equalized odds style summaries (Hardt et al., 2016; Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Face recognition systems increasingly mediate access control, device unlocking, and forensic triage. Their societal impact has sparked scrutiny of accuracy, bias, and accountability. Error rates vary across demographic groups due to data imbalance, imaging conditions, and modeling choices. Vendors face evolving regulations that demand transparency about performance and training data. Technical work therefore intersects with policy requirements and community expectations. Our study investigates fairness interventions under domain shift in unconstrained imagery. We focus on open-set verification where thresholds affect false match risks. The goal is to reduce disparities while preserving overall security posture.

In operational settings, faces appear under varied lighting, pose, and occlusion. Surveillance cameras also introduce compression and motion blur that distort features. These conditions interact with demographic attributes such as skin tone and age. Methods that perform well on curated web photos can degrade substantially in practice. We adopt a pipeline that disentangles representation, thresholding, and calibration. Each component offers opportunities to trade off accuracy and equity. Our evaluation emphasizes coverage of hard operational cases and transparent reporting. We use stratified splits that control for confounders in background and environment. This design isolates the effect of interventions on subgroup metrics.

Face recognition benchmarks commonly involve LFW, IJB-A/B/C, VGGFace2, and MegaFace, with protocol variations across verification and identification tasks (Huang et al., 2007; Klare et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2018; Kemelmacher-Shlizerman et al., 2016). Prominent loss functions and backbones include SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace trained on large-scale web scrapes (Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2019). Fairness assessments frequently report subgroup false positive and true positive rates alongside demographic parity and equalized odds style summaries (Hardt et al., 2016; Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). 

We complement these metrics with cost-sensitive analyses for access control scenarios. Calibrated likelihood ratios provide interpretable scores under varying priors. To improve equity, we study debiasing pretext tasks and balanced hard mining. A temperature scaling step aligns scores across subgroups before thresholding. We also explore mixture thresholds conditioned on quality estimators to mitigate disparities. Robustness is examined under simulated compression and motion blur perturbations. We quantify trade-offs between security loss and fairness gain across settings. Ablations compare representation training, sampling, and calibration components. A qualitative audit inspects failure modes by pose, occlusion, and illumination. We document any accuracy regressions on the most represented groups. The results inform procurement guidelines and disclosure templates for agencies. Our codebase includes tools to compute group metrics with confidence intervals. All datasets were processed under consent and applicable data protection laws. We discuss limitations related to annotation consistency and demographic taxonomies.

spans: Early multimodal translation used global CNN features combined with attention over the source sentence to condition decoding models (Specia et al., 2016; Elliott et al., 2017). Subsequent work replaced global vectors with region-level features from Faster R-CNN, enabling bottom-up attention and grounding of choices (Anderson et al., 2018; Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). Pretrained vision-language encoders such as ViLBERT, LXMERT, and UNITER align modalities for translation tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Benchmarks include Multi30K, COCO Captions, and WMT multimodal tasks; studies examine transformer backbones, visual adapters, and contrastive pretraining (Elliott et al., 2016; Bugliarello et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal machine translation seeks to ground target language generation in both linguistic and visual context. Despite recent progress, systems still struggle when text alone is underspecified or pragmatically ambiguous. Visual cues can disambiguate referents, resolve ellipsis, and guide lexical choice, yet integrating them reliably remains challenging. Empirical gains are sensitive to data scale, domain shift, and the granularity of visual representations. Moreover, evaluation has largely emphasized BLEU and METEOR, which may understate improvements from grounded reasoning. This motivates a closer examination of modeling choices that determine how and when images influence token prediction. We focus on encoder design, fusion strategies, and the interaction between pretraining and fine-tuning across domains. To situate our study, we summarize representative datasets and architectures used in prior multimodal translation research. Early multimodal translation used global CNN features combined with attention over the source sentence to condition decoding models (Specia et al., 2016; Elliott et al., 2017). Subsequent work replaced global vectors with region-level features from Faster R-CNN, enabling bottom-up attention and grounding of choices (Anderson et al., 2018; Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). Pretrained vision-language encoders such as ViLBERT, LXMERT, and UNITER align modalities for translation tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Benchmarks include Multi30K, COCO Captions, and WMT multimodal tasks; studies examine transformer backbones, visual adapters, and contrastive pretraining (Elliott et al., 2016; Bugliarello et al., 2021). Collectively, these lines of work highlight the breadth of available supervision but leave open questions about the timing and selectivity of visual conditioning. In particular, global features can overwhelm textual cues in low-resource settings, whereas region signals may be too sparse for idiomatic expressions. Pretrained cross-modal encoders offer stronger alignments, yet their objectives can conflict with autoregressive decoding. We therefore isolate fusion depth and gating as primary levers for controlling visual influence. Our design contrasts shallow prefix fusion with mid-layer cross-attention under matched parameter budgets. We also examine whether contrastive objectives at pretraining time translate into consistent BLEU and COMET gains under domain shift. To reduce confounds, we hold textual encoders and tokenization fixed while varying only the visual pathway. We augment standard metrics with targeted contrast sets that probe referent resolution and color adjective grounding. These tests allow us to separate fluency from grounding, emphasizing cases where visuals should matter. Finally, we analyze failure modes using counterfactual image swaps and masked-region ablations. This analysis clarifies when image features serve as priors versus evidence in the decoder. The resulting perspective connects architectural decisions to measurable gains in grounded translation.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists prior models, datasets, and methods with citations but does not connect them to the authors’ aims, gaps, or argument; it reads as a literature catalog without synthesis.

spans: Early prompting relied on manual templates and few-shot in-context exemplars, as shown by GPT-3 on GLUE and SuperGLUE (Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Pattern-exploiting training and verbalizer design stabilize performance with small labeled sets, including PET and LM-BFF on classification tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Soft prompting methods optimize continuous vectors or prefixes while freezing backbones, covering P-tuning, prefix-tuning, and prompt tuning (Liu et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021) schemes. Instruction finetuning extends prompting with multitask collections such as T0, FLAN, and Super-NaturalInstructions, with gains on ANLI and MNLI (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document: Prompt-based learning reframes task instructions as part of the model input rather than external supervision. This shift emphasizes leveraging pretrained knowledge with minimal task-specific updates. However, prompt design, stability under distributional shifts, and data efficiency remain open challenges. We review approaches that operationalize prompts across discrete and continuous parameterizations. Early prompting relied on manual templates and few-shot in-context exemplars, as shown by GPT-3 on GLUE and SuperGLUE (Brown et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Pattern-exploiting training and verbalizer design stabilize performance with small labeled sets, including PET and LM-BFF on classification tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Soft prompting methods optimize continuous vectors or prefixes while freezing backbones, covering P-tuning, prefix-tuning, and prompt tuning (Liu et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021) schemes. Instruction finetuning extends prompting with multitask collections such as T0, FLAN, and Super-NaturalInstructions, with gains on ANLI and MNLI (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). These developments trace a movement from handcrafted cues toward learned interfaces between instructions and representations. Yet the relative benefits of discrete versus continuous prompts are confounded by backbone size and optimization protocols. For small models, prompt tuning can underfit semantics, while for large models, manual templates can underutilize capacity. Likewise, instruction finetuning intermingles data size and format, blurring the role of verbalization. We unify evaluation by controlling backbone, batch size, and token budget across methods. Our analysis isolates the interaction between prompt locality and layer-wise adaptation. We disentangle template lexicalization from label mapping to clarify when verbalizers dominate. We further measure calibration under prompt perturbations to assess robustness. To avoid cherry-picking, we fix search spaces and reuse identical seeds. We complement aggregate scores with targeted diagnostics on compositional generalization. These diagnostics track consistency across paraphrases and unseen label names. We also examine negative transfer when instructions span heterogeneous tasks. The study highlights sensitivity to initialization and the importance of curvature-aware optimizers. Finally, we discuss compute trade-offs relative to full finetuning under tight latency budgets. Together, these findings ground practical recommendations for selecting prompt mechanisms. They also illuminate remaining gaps that motivate structured prompting and hybrid adaptation.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates multiple methods and datasets with citations but does not articulate relationships to the authors’ problem or identify a specific gap; it lacks synthesis beyond listing prior work.

spans: Temporal graph models began with time-respecting random walks and embeddings for links, including CTDNE and DynGEM on networks (Nguyen et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2018). Event-driven representations introduced point processes and temporal attention, as in DyRep and JODIE for user–item interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2019) on platforms. Temporal graph attention networks modeled continuous-time messages with sampling, exemplified by TGAT and TGN on Wikipedia and Reddit (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020). Benchmarks evaluate link prediction and node classification with temporal splits on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and Wikipedia, using negative sampling (Boschee et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document: Dynamic graphs capture interactions that evolve over time, requiring models to reason about temporal context as well as structure. Static encoders miss causal ordering and recency effects that drive link formation and diffusion. Incorporating time complicates neighborhood definition, batching, and negative sampling for training. Moreover, evaluation must avoid leakage by respecting temporal order during splits. Recent architectures integrate time into message passing using kernels, memory, or attention. Each design balances expressivity against scalability for streaming updates. Memory modules retain long-range history but incur write amplification. Attention offers flexible conditioning yet can be costly on dense event streams. Our study compares temporal parameterizations under matched sampling budgets. We isolate how encoding absolute timestamps versus inter-event intervals affects prediction. We further examine the role of feature refresh in stabilizing representations. Before detailing our approach, we outline representative temporal graph methods and datasets. Temporal graph models began with time-respecting random walks and embeddings for links, including CTDNE and DynGEM on networks (Nguyen et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2018). Event-driven representations introduced point processes and temporal attention, as in DyRep and JODIE for user–item interactions (Trivedi et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2019) on platforms. Temporal graph attention networks modeled continuous-time messages with sampling, exemplified by TGAT and TGN on Wikipedia and Reddit (Xu et al., 2020; Rossi et al., 2020). Benchmarks evaluate link prediction and node classification with temporal splits on ICEWS14, ICEWS18, and Wikipedia, using negative sampling (Boschee et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2019). Together, these lines chart a progression from handcrafted walks to learned event encoders. However, fairness questions persist around hindsight features and batch-dependent context windows. We therefore propose a leakage-aware protocol that fixes observation horizons during training. Under this protocol, memory write frequency and attention fanout become comparable across models. We also quantify calibration drift when event rates spike or lull. Our analysis highlights the need for adaptive time encoding that tracks nonstationarity. Finally, we release lightweight diagnostics that detect timestamp leakage using controlled shuffles. These tools establish a reliable basis for future temporal graph evaluations.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists models and benchmarks for temporal graphs without connecting them to a specific research gap or the authors’ approach, thus lacking synthesis and author perspective.

spans: Federated optimization adopts FedAvg and variants that address client heterogeneity, notably FedProx and SCAFFOLD (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy accounting often relies on DP-SGD with subsampling and noise schedules tracked by the moments accountant or Rényi DP tools (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Secure aggregation and compression mitigate leakage and cost, via Bonawitz protocols, SignSGD, and QSGD (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bernstein et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2017). Benchmarks use LEAF, FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and StackOverflow to assess accuracy, communication, and privacy under heterogeneous client sampling (Caldas et al., 2019; McMahan et al., 2017) conditions.
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning distributes training across edge devices while keeping raw data local. This paradigm promises personalization and privacy but complicates optimization and accounting. Heterogeneous hardware and data skew produce stragglers and update bias. Communication budgets further constrain gradient exchange and synchronization. Privacy guarantees interact with these systems constraints in subtle ways. In particular, noise addition changes the effective learning rate and gradient sparsity. Moreover, clipping per-client updates introduces bias that can accumulate over rounds. Robust evaluation therefore requires joint control of optimization, communication, and privacy parameters. We focus on regimes with thousands of devices and intermittent connectivity. Our analysis characterizes trade-offs between convergence and end-user privacy loss. We build on standardized data partitions to compare algorithms consistently. We also model participation as a stochastic process tied to device availability. Under this model, privacy spending depends on cohort size and sampling variance. We calibrate clipping and noise to equalize effective privacy across populations. To contextualize our choices, we review representative algorithms and benchmarks. The following summary highlights core optimization, privacy, and systems components studied in the literature. We then turn to practical datasets and accounting tools used in empirical studies. Federated optimization adopts FedAvg and variants that address client heterogeneity, notably FedProx and SCAFFOLD (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020). Privacy accounting often relies on DP-SGD with subsampling and noise schedules tracked by the moments accountant or Rényi DP tools (Abadi et al., 2016; Mironov, 2017). Secure aggregation and compression mitigate leakage and cost, via Bonawitz protocols, SignSGD, and QSGD (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bernstein et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2017). Benchmarks use LEAF, FEMNIST, Shakespeare, and StackOverflow to assess accuracy, communication, and privacy under heterogeneous client sampling (Caldas et al., 2019; McMahan et al., 2017) conditions. Collectively, these ingredients define the operating envelope for private federated learning at scale. However, published baselines rarely align budgets across methods, obscuring comparisons. Our study provides matched-budget baselines and sensitivity analyses to clarify these interactions.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates algorithms, privacy accounting techniques, and benchmarks without drawing connections to the authors’ objectives or identifying a gap; it functions as a list rather than a synthesized argument.

spans: Regularization-based approaches constrain updates to preserve prior tasks, including EWC, SI, and LwF (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017; Li and Hoiem, 2017) regularizers. Replay-based strategies interleave new data with exemplars or generative memories, exemplified by GEM, A-GEM, and Experience Replay (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019) methods. Architectural expansion preserves capacity via task-specific modules, including Progressive Networks and adapter-based finetuning with BERT backbones for NLP (Rusu et al., 2016; Houlsby et al., 2019). Benchmarks include CLINC150, MultiWOZ, Amazon Reviews, and continual GLUE, with metrics for forgetting and transfer (Larson et al., 2019; Budzianowski et al., 2018) across task sequences.
Lacks synthesis.document: Continual learning studies models that acquire new skills without catastrophically forgetting previous tasks. In NLP, this challenge arises naturally as user intents, domains, and label spaces evolve. Sequential training naively overwrites parameters, leading to rapid degradation on earlier tasks. Mitigating this interference requires balancing plasticity and stability during updates. Approaches vary along algorithmic constraints, memory usage, and architectural choices. We briefly survey representative strategies and benchmarks used to study this setting. Regularization-based approaches constrain updates to preserve prior tasks, including EWC, SI, and LwF (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Zenke et al., 2017; Li and Hoiem, 2017) regularizers. Replay-based strategies interleave new data with exemplars or generative memories, exemplified by GEM, A-GEM, and Experience Replay (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019) methods. Architectural expansion preserves capacity via task-specific modules, including Progressive Networks and adapter-based finetuning with BERT backbones for NLP (Rusu et al., 2016; Houlsby et al., 2019). Benchmarks include CLINC150, MultiWOZ, Amazon Reviews, and continual GLUE, with metrics for forgetting and transfer (Larson et al., 2019; Budzianowski et al., 2018) across task sequences. These strands offer complementary mechanisms but differ in compute and storage requirements. For language models, replay can be dominated by token-level redundancy without careful sampling. Regularization methods depend strongly on curvature estimates that drift during finetuning. Architectural expansion reduces interference but may impede zero-shot transfer across tasks. We evaluate methods under a unified protocol that fixes budget, order, and model size. Our design decouples task definition from gradient accumulation to control exposure. We incorporate rehearsal buffers that respect privacy constraints through deduplication. Additionally, we test adapter isolation against soft parameter sharing at distinct layers. Metrics include forward transfer, backward transfer, and intransigence to capture different behaviors. We report both average accuracy and area under the accuracy–time curve. Ablations assess the impact of orderings that cluster semantically similar tasks. Sensitivity analyses probe how forgetting scales with buffer size and update frequency. Finally, we release scripts that reproduce sequences and evaluation across corpora. These resources aim to standardize comparisons and reveal robust continual learning practices.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span is a catalog of methods and benchmarks that does not articulate a connecting argument, perspective, or gap; it simply lists prior approaches and datasets without synthesis.

spans: Neural CRS models leveraging persona or knowledge graphs include ReDial (Li et al., 2018), OpenDialKG (Moon et al., 2019), KBRD (Chen et al., 2019), KGSF (Zhou et al., 2020), and KECRS (Ma et al., 2021). Common evaluation resources comprise INSPIRED (Hayati et al., 2020), DuRecDial (Liu et al., 2020), TG-ReDial (Zhou et al., 2020b), GoRecDial (Kang et al., 2019), and MovieLens-based splits, with auxiliary supervision from DailyDialog, ConvAI2, MultiWOZ, and Reddit-derived conversational corpora.
Lacks synthesis.document: Conversational recommendation aims to elicit preferences through dialog while producing item suggestions. This paradigm promises transparency and engagement compared with static recommenders. Yet practical deployments still struggle with sparse signals and shifting intents. Prior surveys emphasize the interplay between language understanding and decision making. In particular, integrating long-term user models remains challenging when conversations are brief. System design must reconcile dialog policies with item ranking under limited feedback. Recent work explores retrieval-augmented generation for justification and grounding. Neural CRS models leveraging persona or knowledge graphs include ReDial (Li et al., 2018), OpenDialKG (Moon et al., 2019), KBRD (Chen et al., 2019), KGSF (Zhou et al., 2020), and KECRS (Ma et al., 2021). Common evaluation resources comprise INSPIRED (Hayati et al., 2020), DuRecDial (Liu et al., 2020), TG-ReDial (Zhou et al., 2020b), GoRecDial (Kang et al., 2019), and MovieLens-based splits, with auxiliary supervision from DailyDialog, ConvAI2, MultiWOZ, and Reddit-derived conversational corpora. Despite resource growth, evaluation practices remain inconsistent across domains. Metrics often mix turn-level appropriateness with session-level success without clear weighting. Cold-start users exacerbate the mismatch between offline metrics and interactive outcomes. Moreover, catalog churn quickly invalidates static popularity priors. Safety considerations introduce constraints on persuasion and disclosure. Language models can hallucinate attributes that alter user trust and choices. Personalization creates feedback loops that entrench narrow item exposure. Cross-lingual and cross-market transfer is underexplored for dialog-centric recommenders. Robustness to adversarial or sarcastic queries is similarly underinvestigated. Reproducibility also suffers from opaque preprocessing and private data dependencies. To address these challenges, we reframe CRS evaluation around goal-conditioned trajectories. We introduce a simulator that couples preference drift with inventory dynamics. The simulator enables counterfactual analysis of policy updates under distribution shift. We complement simulation with user studies that trace satisfaction beyond click signals.

spans: Representative fusion architectures include MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), MFM (Tsai et al., 2019a), MulT (Tsai et al., 2019b), MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), and SLSTM variants for cross-modal alignment. Evaluation commonly uses IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, with modalities spanning speech prosody, facial action units, gaze, and lexical features, and metrics such as accuracy, F1, MAE, and concordance correlation, alongside robustness checks on noise, speaker imbalance, and temporal desynchrony during evaluation.
Lacks synthesis.document: Multimodal emotion recognition seeks to infer affect from coordinated signals. The task underpins applications in wellbeing, education, and media analysis. However, real-world deployment faces subtle shifts in speakers, devices, and cultures. A central question is how to fuse asynchronous modalities reliably. Early concatenation methods underutilized cross-modal structure. Attention mechanisms improved selectivity but introduced new overfitting risks. Temporal alignment remains difficult when cues are sparse or contradictory. Calibration drift also complicates threshold selection across datasets. Label ambiguity further limits statistical efficiency at scale. Semi-supervised learning offers leverage when annotations are scarce. Self-training and contrastive objectives have shown promise under covariate shift. Representative fusion architectures include MFN (Zadeh et al., 2018), MFM (Tsai et al., 2019a), MulT (Tsai et al., 2019b), MAG-BERT (Rahman et al., 2020), and SLSTM variants for cross-modal alignment. Evaluation commonly uses IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, with modalities spanning speech prosody, facial action units, gaze, and lexical features, and metrics such as accuracy, F1, MAE, and concordance correlation, alongside robustness checks on noise, speaker imbalance, and temporal desynchrony during evaluation. Despite significant progress, generalization to unseen speakers is inconsistent. Domain adaptation techniques reduce gap but can degrade specificity. Privacy constraints often prevent release of raw audio or video streams. Federated training raises questions about aggregation under heterogeneity. Interpretability is required for clinical and educational stakeholders. Local explanations may not reflect global model behavior. We consider how causal structure can guide robust fusion. Our framework decouples representation alignment from task calibration. It quantifies uncertainty propagation across modalities and time. Comprehensive experiments measure benefits under realistic distribution shifts.

Applications include fraud surveillance, intrusion monitoring, and integrity auditing. Unlike i.i.d. settings, dependencies propagate irregularities across neighborhoods. This complicates both modeling and evaluation. A core difficulty is disentangling structural rarity from semantic novelty. Simple thresholds on degree or attributes can be brittle. Representation learning offers alternatives through neighborhood aggregation. However, oversmoothing may suppress discriminative variation. Heterophily further challenges standard homophily-based encoders. Temporal dynamics introduce nonstationarity that mimics anomalies. Scalability becomes acute on multi-million node graphs. Memory footprints constrain negative sampling and calibration. Label scarcity is common due to review costs. Synthetic labels risk biasing learned detectors. Robust protocols must separate contamination from ground truth. Methods range from proximity scores and embeddings to GNNs, including DeepWalk (Perozzi et al., 2014), node2vec (Grover and Leskovec, 2016), GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GAT (Velickovic et al., 2018), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), DOMINANT (Ding et al., 2019), and AnomalyDAE (Fan et al., 2020). spans: Optimization variants address heterogeneity and drift, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), and q-FedAvg (Li et al., 2020b).
