Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning enables on-device training with privacy advantages over centralized pipelines. However, mobile platforms impose energy and bandwidth constraints that shape algorithm design. Non-i.i.d. data and intermittent connectivity further complicate convergence guarantees. Clients vary in compute, memory, and thermal budgets across hardware generations. Communication rounds dominate energy consumption under dense models. Compression reduces payload but can degrade final accuracy. Stragglers and dropouts perturb aggregation and fairness. Resource adaptation must consider energy limits and user experience. Scheduling strategies can opportunistically exploit charging or Wi-Fi availability. Optimization variants address heterogeneity and drift, including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), FedProx (Li et al., 2020), SCAFFOLD (Karimireddy et al., 2020), FedNova (Wang et al., 2020), and q-FedAvg (Li et al., 2020b). Communication efficiency is pursued via SignSGD (Bernstein et al., 2018), STC (Sattler et al., 2019), Deep Gradient Compression (Lin et al., 2018), dropout, and aggregation schemes like FedPAQ (Reisizadeh et al., 2020), with evaluations on FEMNIST, Shakespeare, StackOverflow, and CIFAR-10. Despite impressive results, system-level evaluations often omit energy accounting. Measurements frequently use wall-power proxies that misestimate battery impacts. Idle state transitions and radio wakes introduce hidden costs. Hybrid digital-analog links alter per-bit energy in practice. Co-design across optimizer, model, and runtime is essential on phones. Energy-aware objectives can stabilize learning under aggressive compression. Heterogeneous participation also raises fairness considerations. We operationalize fairness using budget-weighted contributions. Our scheduler predicts marginal accuracy per joule for each client. It allocates rounds under stochastic availability and drift. Off-policy evaluation is sensitive to positivity violations and overlap. Propensity estimation degrades in high-dimensional dynamic settings. Balancing approaches struggle when treatments evolve continuously. Instrumental variables are rare and weak in practice. Recent work explores invariance as an organizing principle. Temporal invariance requires careful handling of lags and aggregation. Synthetic data may not reflect clinical workload or noise. Evaluation protocols often ignore deployment feedback loops. spans: Recent approaches to speech domain adaptation draw on adversarial feature alignment (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017), self-training with pseudo labels (Lee, 2013; Sohn et al., 2020), consistency regularization (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), and representation learning on large audio corpora such as Librispeech, VoxCeleb, and Common Voice. Contrastive objectives appear in several variants (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), while transformer acoustic encoders follow wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Benchmarks report on CHiME.
Lacks synthesis.document: Automatic speech recognition in noisy target domains remains challenging despite advances in self-supervised pretraining. Models trained on curated corpora often fail when confronted with channel shifts, accents, and background interference. Prior work explores both feature and label space adaptation, yet a consistent treatment of temporally structured noise is limited. We focus on cross-domain robustness where only untranscribed target audio is available during adaptation. This setting amplifies the risk of confirmation bias in self-training and the drift of posterior confidence estimation. A central question is how to stabilize adaptation without access to ground-truth labels or parallel recordings. In this section we review techniques commonly used for domain adaptation in speech and outline remaining challenges. We then motivate a strategy that integrates uncertainty estimation with curriculum-aligned augmentation. Recent approaches to speech domain adaptation draw on adversarial feature alignment (Ganin et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017), self-training with pseudo labels (Lee, 2013; Sohn et al., 2020), consistency regularization (Laine and Aila, 2017; Tarvainen and Valpola, 2017), and representation learning on large audio corpora such as Librispeech, VoxCeleb, and Common Voice. Contrastive objectives appear in several variants (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), while transformer acoustic encoders follow wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Benchmarks report on CHiME. While these ingredients recur across studies, few works examine how uncertainty estimates interact with pseudo-label schedules in nonstationary noise. Empirical analyses rarely separate acoustic distortions from lexical shift, making it difficult to attribute improvements to specific mechanisms. Moreover, contrastive pretext tasks can destabilize when augmentations violate phonetic consistency in reverberant scenes. We therefore investigate adaptation curricula that modulate augmentation intensity according to model confidence. Our hypothesis is that well-calibrated confidence can gate pseudo-label generation and mitigate error amplification. To test this, we couple confidence-aware filtering with a noise-conditional augmentation pipeline. The approach allows separation of channel, speaker, and environment factors through targeted perturbations. We also incorporate a small teacher ensemble to regularize early adaptation steps when confidence is unreliable. Evaluation spans synthetic mixtures and real recordings to assess transfer under controlled and wild conditions. Metrics cover word error, calibration, and stability under perturbation sweeps. We further analyze per-phoneme substitution patterns to trace where the curriculum exerts the most benefit. Finally, we situate the results in a broader view of semi-supervised learning for audio understanding. The following section details datasets, augmentation recipes, and training schedules used in our experiments.

Datasets used for evaluation include Multi-News, WikiSum, Multi-XScience, and GovReport (Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Content planning and entity-centric selection appear as auxiliary strategies (Shen et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020).
Lacks synthesis.document: Multi-document summarization must reconcile overlap, contradiction, and source-specific terminology. Scientific literature complicates this because evidence appears in heterogeneous formats and rhetorical zones. Extractive pipelines preserve factual grounding but struggle with discourse flow across sections. Abstractive models permit fusion, yet hallucination and citation drift remain persistent risks. Our study targets citation-faithful summaries that trace claims to supporting passages. We formalize the task as jointly selecting evidence and generating discourse-coherent statements. The framework emphasizes document-level grounding signals derived from section titles and citation markers. We also consider lexical priors from domain ontologies to constrain generation. Below, we briefly survey representative techniques and datasets used in multi-document settings. Datasets used for evaluation include Multi-News, WikiSum, Multi-XScience, and GovReport (Fabbri et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Content planning and entity-centric selection appear as auxiliary strategies (Shen et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020). These resources do not directly encode citation-to-claim alignment, which motivates additional supervision. We introduce a rationale extraction layer that conditions decoding on verified spans. The layer treats each citation as a soft constraint enforced through coverage and copy penalties. To mitigate hallucination, a spectral regularizer penalizes divergence between generated attributions and evidence indices. Our decoding objective therefore balances informativeness with groundedness at sentence level. We adapt long-context attention to ensure scalability without sacrificing token-level alignment. The training protocol combines contrastive evidence selection with self-denoising on shuffled source sets. This yields robustness to missing or redundant documents in the input cluster. We evaluate on biomedical and public policy corpora with curated citation links. Metrics include factual consistency, evidence precision, and citation-level calibration. Human assessment focuses on support traceability and discourse coherence across sources. We report ablations to quantify the contribution of rationale conditioning and spectral regularization.

spans: Robotic manipulation has used PPO, DDPG, and SAC (Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). We hypothesize that contact-centric supervision can bridge this gap without additional teleoperation burden. To that end, we derive pseudo-contacts from tactile proxies and motion discontinuities. These signals shape both the encoder and the action-value critic during pretraining. During fine-tuning, the agent is regularized to preserve contact cues while adapting to task specifics. The approach encourages consistent grasp geometry and reduces grasp-release oscillations. We evaluate across tool use, container manipulation, and articulated object tasks. spans: Work on fairness in recommendation spans pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies, including re-ranking for fair exposure (Zehlike et al., 2017; Biega et al., 2018), matrix factorization and adversarial objectives (Kamishima et al., 2012; Beutel et al., 2019), and learning of demographic parity and equal opportunity (Calders et al., 2009; Hardt et al., 2016). Evaluations use MovieLens, Last.fm, and Goodreads with group annotations (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Celma, 2010; Wan and McAuley, 2018). Multi-sided metrics consider provider fairness and consumer utility (Singh and Joachims, 2018; Sapiezynski et al., 2019).
Lacks synthesis.document: Recommender systems distribute attention and opportunity across users and items at scale. This allocation can amplify historical inequities unless objectives incorporate fairness constraints. Exposure disparities arise when ranking optimizes click prediction without provider-side considerations. Consumer harms surface when relevance models encode demographic signals from interaction histories. Recent work formalizes these issues through distributional metrics and constrained optimization. We study fairness in two-sided marketplaces where both consumers and providers receive guarantees. Our focus is ranking-time interventions compatible with large-scale retrieval pipelines. Work on fairness in recommendation spans pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies, including re-ranking for fair exposure (Zehlike et al., 2017; Biega et al., 2018), matrix factorization and adversarial objectives (Kamishima et al., 2012; Beutel et al., 2019), and learning of demographic parity and equal opportunity (Calders et al., 2009; Hardt et al., 2016). Evaluations use MovieLens, Last.fm, and Goodreads with group annotations (Harper and Konstan, 2016; Celma, 2010; Wan and McAuley, 2018). Multi-sided metrics consider provider fairness and consumer utility (Singh and Joachims, 2018; Sapiezynski et al., 2019). While influential, these strategies rarely account for retrieval-stage biases that precede re-ranking. In practice, candidate generators filter the space in ways that shape feasible fairness trade-offs. We introduce a calibration layer that controls exposure budgets before downstream ranking. The layer matches demand forecasts while constraining demographic parity at slice level. It interfaces with production search through lightweight score transformations. Our constraints are differentiable surrogates that upper bound disparity under monotone ranking. We derive regret bounds connecting exposure slack to utility loss. The analysis motivates per-bucket budget setting based on historical supply elasticity. We demonstrate scalability in a high-volume marketplace with strict latency targets. Metrics include exposure Gini, group-normalized DCG, and counterfactual equality of opportunity. We also report supply-side spillovers measured via provider satisfaction surveys. Ablations isolate the contribution of retrieval calibration relative to standard re-ranking baselines. Ethical considerations address data provenance, user consent, and transparency of budget policies.

spans: Molecular property prediction has adopted message passing neural networks and their variants, including GCN, GraphSAGE, and GIN (Gilmer et al., 2017; Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019). spans: CLIP pretrains a dual-encoder on 400M image–text pairs from diverse web sources, while ALIGN scales a similar contrastive objective with larger noisy alt-text data. BLIP integrates caption bootstrapping with vision encoders and language decoders to improve image–text understanding on COCO, Flickr30k, and Conceptual Captions. Flamingo introduces cross-attention between a frozen visual backbone and a large language model for few-shot multimodal tasks across VQAv2, VizWiz, TextCaps, and OK-VQA. LiT explores locked-image tuning with a frozen image tower and trains only the text encoder using ImageNet and JFT labels for strong zero-shot transfer. Data curation has expanded with LAION-400M and LAION-5B, and filtering strategies such as CLIP-score and aesthetic scoring; benchmarks include ImageNet, ImageNet-V2, ObjectNet, and ImageNet-R for robustness evaluation. Adaptation techniques feature linear probing on frozen features, prompt tuning and textual inversion for category conditioning, and adapter modules inserted into ViT or Transformer layers, with reported gains on domain-specific sets like iNaturalist, Places, and EuroSAT. Additional evaluations cover CLIP-B LAION variants, Winoground, Hateful Memes, and NLVR2, and pretraining mixes such as CC12M, YFCC100M, and RedCaps under comparable compute budgets in recent releases.

Flamingo introduces cross-attention between a frozen visual backbone and a large language model for few-shot multimodal tasks across VQAv2, VizWiz, TextCaps, and OK-VQA. LiT explores locked-image tuning with a frozen image tower and trains only the text encoder using ImageNet and JFT labels for strong zero-shot transfer. Data curation has expanded with LAION-400M and LAION-5B, and filtering strategies such as CLIP-score and aesthetic scoring; benchmarks include ImageNet, ImageNet-V2, ObjectNet, and ImageNet-R for robustness evaluation. Adaptation techniques feature linear probing on frozen features, prompt tuning and textual inversion for category conditioning, and adapter modules inserted into ViT or Transformer layers, with reported gains on domain-specific sets like iNaturalist, Places, and EuroSAT. Additional evaluations cover CLIP-B LAION variants, Winoground, Hateful Memes, and NLVR2, and pretraining mixes such as CC12M, YFCC100M, and RedCaps under comparable compute budgets in recent releases. While these ingredients demonstrate impressive breadth, their interactions with shift-aware tuning remain under-explored. We study how label scarcity and feature drift alter the calibration of text prompts and adapters. Specifically, we compare frozen, partially frozen, and fully trainable configurations under equalized compute and data budgets. Our analysis highlights that naive prompting can overfit spurious lexical cues when the retrieval distribution narrows. Conversely, lightweight adapters tend to preserve global alignment at the cost of local discrimination without targeted regularization. We therefore examine temperature scaling and entropy margin losses to rebalance confidence under domain shift. We also revisit dataset filtering, measuring how duplicate captions inflate apparent generalization. To ground the evaluation, we adopt a protocol that separates category shift from style shift using controlled subsamples. This design clarifies whether failures arise from vocabulary mismatch or visual covariates. We supplement accuracy with retrieval calibration error and conditional coverage metrics. These metrics expose systematic undercoverage on rare classes despite stable top-1 accuracy. Diagnostic ablations further reveal that mixing a small number of in-domain pairs can outperform extensive generic pretraining. The finding aligns with theoretical expectations for representation smoothing under covariate shift. Finally, we report cost curves to relate performance to wall-clock and memory footprints. This perspective informs practical choices for adapting foundation models in constrained settings. We close with implications for dataset construction and open challenges in safe deployment.

spans: Rainbow combines multiple DQN improvements, including distributional value estimation, prioritized replay, multi-step targets, and noisy nets, and is a standard baseline on Atari 2600. Dreamer and DreamerV2 learn latent dynamics with world models and optimize imagined trajectories on DeepMind Control Suite and Atari. CURL leverages contrastive learning over augmentations to improve sample efficiency on pixel-based control, while SPR and DrQv2 use strong data augmentation and target networks to stabilize learning. Dyna-style planning integrates learned models with on-policy updates, and MuZero unifies tree search with value and policy learning across Atari, Go, and chess. Procgen and DMControl Generalization Benchmark introduce procedural generation and held-out visual variations to assess robustness, and CAUSALWORLD offers factorized robotic tasks with known generative structure. Methods such as Invariant Risk Minimization, Domain Adversarial Training, and AugMix-inspired perturbations have been applied to RL pretraining, alongside datasets like Atari-100k, URLB, and RL Unplugged for offline evaluation. Representation learning approaches based on bisimulation metrics, contrastive predictive coding, and prototypical RL have also been reported, with evaluations on Meta-World, DMControl tasks with color jitter, and Atari Human Starts protocols in standard leaderboards.
Lacks synthesis.document: Reinforcement learning in high-dimensional environments requires sample-efficient representations. A central question is how to disentangle controllable and spurious factors to enable robust policy transfer. Prior work suggests that causal structure can regularize learning, yet concrete practices vary across domains. We consider the intersection of model-based rollouts, invariance penalties, and data augmentation. Our scope includes visual control tasks and discrete-action benchmarks where dynamics and confounding differ. We define transfer as maintaining performance under interventions on textures, camera poses, or reward mappings. This section reviews key baselines that motivate our experimental design. We emphasize the gap between proxy metrics and downstream returns. To keep comparisons faithful, all algorithms are trained under matched compute and replay budgets. The following literature summarizes common paradigms and datasets used to evaluate representation quality. Rainbow combines multiple DQN improvements, including distributional value estimation, prioritized replay, multi-step targets, and noisy nets, and is a standard baseline on Atari 2600. Dreamer and DreamerV2 learn latent dynamics with world models and optimize imagined trajectories on DeepMind Control Suite and Atari. CURL leverages contrastive learning over augmentations to improve sample efficiency on pixel-based control, while SPR and DrQv2 use strong data augmentation and target networks to stabilize learning. Dyna-style planning integrates learned models with on-policy updates, and MuZero unifies tree search with value and policy learning across Atari, Go, and chess. Procgen and DMControl Generalization Benchmark introduce procedural generation and held-out visual variations to assess robustness, and CAUSALWORLD offers factorized robotic tasks with known generative structure. Methods such as Invariant Risk Minimization, Domain Adversarial Training, and AugMix-inspired perturbations have been applied to RL pretraining, alongside datasets like Atari-100k, URLB, and RL Unplugged for offline evaluation. Representation learning approaches based on bisimulation metrics, contrastive predictive coding, and prototypical RL have also been reported, with evaluations on Meta-World, DMControl tasks with color jitter, and Atari Human Starts protocols in standard leaderboards. Despite broad coverage, these systems vary in how they respond to interventions that break spurious pixel correlations. We frame this sensitivity through a causal lens, distinguishing invariant dynamics from context-specific appearance. To that end, we estimate environment-specific nuisance variables and regularize policies toward agreement across interventions. Our objective couples a latent transition model with a contrastive invariance penalty derived from counterfactual rollouts. The design complements data augmentation by requiring consistency across synthetic interventions that preserve controllable factors. We compare against strong baselines under fixed wall-clock and parameter budgets. The analysis decomposes gains into representation alignment, model bias, and credit assignment. We evaluate on Atari-100k, Procgen Hard, and DMControlGB to probe distinct forms of shift. For fairness, we re-tune key hyperparameters and report interquartile performance across seeds. We also examine failure cases where invariance conflicts with exploration incentives. These cases reveal that naive penalties can stall discovery of reward-relevant features. A targeted schedule that defers invariance until after value shaping mitigates the stall. Further ablations test memory capacity, model horizon, and augmentation diversity. We summarize computational costs to contextualize potential deployment trade-offs. Together, the results articulate when structure helps and when it must yield to exploration. We conclude with limitations and opportunities for integrating causal discovery with scalable RL.

spans: wav2vec 2.0 introduced self-supervised pretraining with contrastive predictive coding on unlabeled audio, followed by CTC fine-tuning on Librispeech and other corpora. HuBERT extended masked prediction with clustered targets and reported gains on Libri-Light and VoxCeleb, while XLS-R scaled multilingual pretraining across 128 languages using CommonVoice, BABEL, and VoxPopuli. Whisper trained an encoder–decoder on large web-scale weakly labeled data and reports robust zero-shot transcription and translation on diverse benchmarks. Classic supervised architectures include LAS for attention-based sequence-to-sequence, RNN-T for streaming recognition, and hybrid HMM–DNN CTC systems with SpecAugment regularization. Multilingual benchmarks commonly include FLEURS, MLS, and CommonVoice, and low-resource settings often use IARPA BABEL subsets and OpenSLR corpora for targeted evaluation. Teacher–student pipelines appear with frame-level CTC posteriors, encoder hidden-state regression, and decoupled language model distillation for shallow fusion decoding, with reported improvements on VoxPopuli ASR, TED-LIUM, and multilingual Librispeech. Parameter-efficient training approaches including adapters, low-rank factorization, and multilingual bottlenecks have been explored across wav2vec-style encoders, and evaluation typically reports WER on Librispeech, CoVoST, and FLEURS subsets with per-language breakdowns. Cross-lingual sharing is often measured using phoneme error trends and transfer ratios.
Lacks synthesis.document: Multilingual automatic speech recognition seeks to balance cross-lingual transfer with language-specific nuance. Scaling encoders and pretraining on diverse audio has improved average accuracy but masks low-resource disparities. Distillation promises efficiency and personalization by transferring knowledge from strong teachers to compact students. However, choices about targets, alignments, and multilingual capacity interact in nontrivial ways. We examine prior approaches spanning CTC, sequence-to-sequence, and transducer formulations. Our interest centers on how supervision granularity and language conditioning affect generalization. We also consider data selection and augmentation for balancing typological coverage. This section outlines representative models, datasets, and training heuristics used in the literature. The survey organizes references by pretraining, architectures, and evaluation suites. We first review recent large-scale systems that motivate distillation strategies. wav2vec 2.0 introduced self-supervised pretraining with contrastive predictive coding on unlabeled audio, followed by CTC fine-tuning on Librispeech and other corpora. HuBERT extended masked prediction with clustered targets and reported gains on Libri-Light and VoxCeleb, while XLS-R scaled multilingual pretraining across 128 languages using CommonVoice, BABEL, and VoxPopuli. Whisper trained an encoder–decoder on large web-scale weakly labeled data and reports robust zero-shot transcription and translation on diverse benchmarks. Classic supervised architectures include LAS for attention-based sequence-to-sequence, RNN-T for streaming recognition, and hybrid HMM–DNN CTC systems with SpecAugment regularization. Multilingual benchmarks commonly include FLEURS, MLS, and CommonVoice, and low-resource settings often use IARPA BABEL subsets and OpenSLR corpora for targeted evaluation. Teacher–student pipelines appear with frame-level CTC posteriors, encoder hidden-state regression, and decoupled language model distillation for shallow fusion decoding, with reported improvements on VoxPopuli ASR, TED-LIUM, and multilingual Librispeech. Parameter-efficient training approaches including adapters, low-rank factorization, and multilingual bottlenecks have been explored across wav2vec-style encoders, and evaluation typically reports WER on Librispeech, CoVoST, and FLEURS subsets with per-language breakdowns. Cross-lingual sharing is often measured using phoneme error trends and transfer ratios. Despite these advances, cross-lingual distillation can amplify teacher biases when phonotactics diverge. We investigate whether soft targets should be conditioned on language-specific adapters to avoid over-regularization. Our formulation aligns teacher posteriors with student emissions through a calibrated temperature per language. We couple this with pronunciation-aware masking that emphasizes rare grapheme–phoneme correspondences. The training schedule alternates multilingual and monolingual batches to stabilize normalization. We benchmark capacity-controlled students to isolate the effect of supervision granularity. Evaluation considers WER, CER, and coverage of named entities across resource tiers. We include fairness diagnostics by reporting per-gender and per-accent gaps when metadata permits. Ablations assess the role of teacher capacity, data scale, and adapter placement. The results indicate that naive temperature sharing degrades transfer in agglutinative languages. Language-conditioned calibration reduces this degradation without sacrificing streaming latency. We also find that modest multilingual bottlenecks outperform heavy adapters at constant FLOPs. Error analyses show improvements on code-switch boundaries and diacritics restoration. We release scripts to reproduce all settings within standard open-source toolkits. Finally, we discuss remaining pitfalls and opportunities for robust multilingual deployment.

spans: DCRNN models traffic flow with diffusion convolution and sequence-to-sequence GRUs, and reports gains on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY under multi-step horizons. STGCN employs localized graph convolutions with temporal gated convolutions to capture spatiotemporal patterns, evaluated on METR-LA and PEMS datasets. Graph WaveNet combines dilated temporal convolutions with adaptive adjacency matrices and achieves strong results on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS-03/04/07/08. MTGNN integrates graph learning with multi-scale temporal convolutions and shows improvements on several PEMS benchmarks and Electricity. GMAN applies multi-step attention with learnable embeddings for nodes and time indices, validated on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. ASTGCN introduces spatial and temporal attention along with multi-component time-of-day modules and presents results on TaxiBJ and METR-LA. Additional baselines include LSTNet, Temporal Convolutional Networks, and Transformer variants with positional encodings, and common datasets extend to BikeNYC and Shenzhen Taxi, with metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE. Dynamic graph approaches leverage attention over recent readings, Chebyshev polynomials, or latent space kernels, and training tricks include scheduled sampling, curriculum learning, and layer normalization across horizons on unified preprocessing. Public leaderboards typically report 12, 24, and 48 step forecasts metrics.
Lacks synthesis.document: Traffic forecasting relies on modeling spatial and temporal dependencies over sensor networks. Road graphs are dynamic, noisy, and partially observed, creating challenges for stable prediction. Graph neural networks have emerged as strong backbones for these settings. However, architectural choices and training regimes complicate fair comparison. We revisit spatiotemporal modules with an eye toward inductive generalization. This section reviews representative methods and datasets that inform our study design. We separate works by how they encode spatial topology, temporal recurrence, and attention. We also note evaluation practices and typical preprocessing pipelines. The review highlights common baselines and canonical benchmarks. We begin with sequence models and progress to adaptive graphs. DCRNN models traffic flow with diffusion convolution and sequence-to-sequence GRUs, and reports gains on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY under multi-step horizons. STGCN employs localized graph convolutions with temporal gated convolutions to capture spatiotemporal patterns, evaluated on METR-LA and PEMS datasets. Graph WaveNet combines dilated temporal convolutions with adaptive adjacency matrices and achieves strong results on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, and PEMS-03/04/07/08. MTGNN integrates graph learning with multi-scale temporal convolutions and shows improvements on several PEMS benchmarks and Electricity. GMAN applies multi-step attention with learnable embeddings for nodes and time indices, validated on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY. ASTGCN introduces spatial and temporal attention along with multi-component time-of-day modules and presents results on TaxiBJ and METR-LA. Additional baselines include LSTNet, Temporal Convolutional Networks, and Transformer variants with positional encodings, and common datasets extend to BikeNYC and Shenzhen Taxi, with metrics such as MAE, RMSE, and MAPE. Dynamic graph approaches leverage attention over recent readings, Chebyshev polynomials, or latent space kernels, and training tricks include scheduled sampling, curriculum learning, and layer normalization across horizons on unified preprocessing. Public leaderboards typically report 12, 24, and 48 step forecasts metrics. Despite architectural diversity, most models assume fixed stationarity within sampling windows. We test whether adaptive topology and horizon-specific normalization reduce brittleness under regime changes. Our design factors node degree, road capacity, and incident alerts into a unified conditioning signal. We contrast learned adjacency with exogenous priors from distance and speed limits. The evaluation isolates improvements attributable to topology updates versus temporal ensembling. We adopt a consistent pipeline for imputation, scaling, and edge pruning to avoid confounds. We measure error profiles by congestion level to capture heteroskedastic behavior. Experiments indicate that horizon-aware normalization stabilizes multi-step rollouts during peaks. Conversely, naive dynamic graphs can overreact to sensor noise without temporal smoothing. An ablation over incident alerts shows gains concentrate on disruptive events. We report compute, memory, and latency to contextualize deployment in urban centers. The framework complements existing architectures rather than replacing them. We discuss implications for city planning and resilient transport operations. Limitations and future directions close the section.

spans: Codex demonstrates strong few-shot code generation across Python and other languages and is commonly evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP. AlphaCode trains on competitive programming data and reports success on Codeforces-style problems and APPS subsets. CodeT5 integrates span corruption and identifier-aware pretraining, while CodeBERT and GraphCodeBERT provide bimodal encoders for code–text retrieval and understanding, with results on CodeXGLUE tasks. PolyCoder and SantaCoder offer open-source baselines trained on permissive corpora, and StarCoder extends coverage with filtered GitHub data. For program repair, DeepFix targets C programs with compiler errors, DrRepair leverages feedback-directed search with neural guidance, and TFix applies sequence-to-sequence learning to real-world fixes from Git histories. Defects4J, QuixBugs, Bears, and ManySStuBs4J supply curated bug–fix pairs, while IntroClass and Codeflaws cover student code. Execution-based evaluation frequently uses pass@k, exact match, and coverage metrics, and harnesses rely on unit tests or fuzzing to validate outputs. Repair benchmarks often include synthetic mutation suites and real-world bugs, and compile-and-run pipelines compare generation, beam search, and self-consistency sampling across time and memory budgets. Training data sources span BigQuery GitHub, CodeSearchNet, and Stack Overflow dumps with license filtering policies.
Lacks synthesis.document: Program synthesis with pretrained language models has advanced rapidly across generation, repair, and explanation tasks. Despite strong average performance, failure modes persist under distribution shift and weak specifications. We study test-time strategies that align sampling with executable semantics. Our focus is distinct from scaling alone and targets controllable improvements. This section reviews representative systems and benchmarks that shape current practice. We organize prior work by pretraining data, objective functions, and evaluation protocols. We also summarize common repair datasets and metrics. The aim is to clarify assumptions and typical baselines. We restrict attention to open and widely cited resources to facilitate replication. We begin with large-scale code models and then cover repair systems. Codex demonstrates strong few-shot code generation across Python and other languages and is commonly evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP. AlphaCode trains on competitive programming data and reports success on Codeforces-style problems and APPS subsets. CodeT5 integrates span corruption and identifier-aware pretraining, while CodeBERT and GraphCodeBERT provide bimodal encoders for code–text retrieval and understanding, with results on CodeXGLUE tasks. PolyCoder and SantaCoder offer open-source baselines trained on permissive corpora, and StarCoder extends coverage with filtered GitHub data. For program repair, DeepFix targets C programs with compiler errors, DrRepair leverages feedback-directed search with neural guidance, and TFix applies sequence-to-sequence learning to real-world fixes from Git histories. Defects4J, QuixBugs, Bears, and ManySStuBs4J supply curated bug–fix pairs, while IntroClass and Codeflaws cover student code. Execution-based evaluation frequently uses pass@k, exact match, and coverage metrics, and harnesses rely on unit tests or fuzzing to validate outputs. Repair benchmarks often include synthetic mutation suites and real-world bugs, and compile-and-run pipelines compare generation, beam search, and self-consistency sampling across time and memory budgets. Training data sources span BigQuery GitHub, CodeSearchNet, and Stack Overflow dumps with license filtering policies. While strong results accrue on standardized tasks, many successes rely on permissive or redundant test suites. We address this gap by conditioning sampling on specification signals available at test time. The method integrates execution traces into the decoding objective through lightweight reranking. Unlike retraining, the approach leaves model parameters untouched and adds negligible overhead. We analyze effects on pass@k, coverage, and unique solution counts across multiple seeds. The study includes controlled ablations over prompt format, timeouts, and compiler feedback. We examine generalization under adversarial unit tests that target spurious shortcuts. Results show consistent gains when trace feedback is informative and neutral changes otherwise. Error analysis highlights shifts from brittle heuristics to semantically grounded edits. We also compare search strategies to characterize diversity and redundancy in candidate sets. Practical considerations include sandboxing, caching, and deterministic toolchains. Cost profiles indicate improved throughput per successful solution at fixed budgets. Our findings inform deployment strategies for code generation in continuous integration pipelines. We end with limitations and directions for specification-aware generation.

spans: Early graph convolutional models framed node aggregation with spectral filters and approximate convolutions (Bruna et al., 2014; Kipf and Welling, 2017). Inductive neighborhood sampling in GraphSAGE enabled scalable training on evolving graphs (Hamilton et al., 2017), while attention-based propagation in GAT emphasized feature-dependent weighting of neighbors (Veličković et al., 2018). Message Passing Neural Networks unified update and readout operations for molecular property prediction and popularized sum/mean pooling variants (Gilmer et al., 2017), and later extensions explored edge features, virtual nodes, and hierarchical pooling (Xu et al., 2019; Ying et al., 2018; Cangea et al., 2018). Benchmarking commonly relies on MoleculeNet suites such as QM9, ESOL, and FreeSolv, as well as ZINC, Open Graph Benchmark molecules (Hu et al., 2020), and classification tasks like Tox21 and HIV (Wu et al., 2018; Irwin et al., 2012). Standard evaluation includes MAE, RMSE, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC metrics scores.
Lacks synthesis.document: Predicting molecular properties from graphs underpins discovery in chemistry and drug design. Graph neural networks have become the de facto paradigm due to their ability to exploit relational inductive biases. Despite strong results, performance varies widely across datasets and evaluation protocols. Recent work highlights sensitivity to architecture choices, pooling strategies, and feature engineering. Moreover, discrepancies between scaffold and random splits complicate fair comparison. A clear account of baseline behavior under controlled settings remains incomplete. We situate our study in the landscape of graph representation learning for molecules. Early graph convolutional models framed node aggregation with spectral filters and approximate convolutions (Bruna et al., 2014; Kipf and Welling, 2017). Inductive neighborhood sampling in GraphSAGE enabled scalable training on evolving graphs (Hamilton et al., 2017), while attention-based propagation in GAT emphasized feature-dependent weighting of neighbors (Veličković et al., 2018). Message Passing Neural Networks unified update and readout operations for molecular property prediction and popularized sum/mean pooling variants (Gilmer et al., 2017), and later extensions explored edge features, virtual nodes, and hierarchical pooling (Xu et al., 2019; Ying et al., 2018; Cangea et al., 2018). Benchmarking commonly relies on MoleculeNet suites such as QM9, ESOL, and FreeSolv, as well as ZINC, Open Graph Benchmark molecules (Hu et al., 2020), and classification tasks like Tox21 and HIV (Wu et al., 2018; Irwin et al., 2012). Standard evaluation includes MAE, RMSE, ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC metrics scores. These developments set the stage for examining how architectural and data-centric factors interact in standardized pipelines. However, current benchmarks often conflate hyperparameter search with model capacity, obscuring the sources of gains. We therefore disentangle featurization, message aggregation, and readout under unified training budgets. Our analysis emphasizes cross-dataset stability and sensitivity to scaffold shift. We also assess the effect of explicit 3D information versus learned positional encodings. To ensure comparability, we harmonize preprocessing, early stopping, and metric reporting. The resulting measurements illuminate when added complexity yields reproducible benefits. Ultimately, our protocol aims to support reliable selection of molecular GNNs for real-world screening.

spans: Domain-adapted language models trained on biomedical corpora have reported gains across multiple tasks, including BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), ClinicalBERT (Alsentzer et al., 2019), and PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021). Benchmarks commonly evaluate on datasets such as i2b2/VA shared tasks for de-identification and concept extraction, the n2c2 challenges for medication extraction and smoking status, MedNLI for inference over clinical text, and the ShARe/CLEF eHealth corpus for disorder mentions. Additional resources include MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV for critical care notes, PhysioNet challenges for mortality prediction, and MedQA and PubMedQA for question answering in clinical and biomedical domains. Prompting and instruction-tuning with medical question sets, exam-style problems, and synthetic dialogues have also been explored, including MultiMedQA, MedMCQA, and USMLE-style evaluations. Shared baselines often pair cased tokenizers with UMLS-based concept linking, conditional random fields for sequence labeling, and span-level micro-averaged F1 for reporting.
Lacks synthesis.document: Clinical text analysis must reconcile domain-specific terminology with privacy, sparsity, and annotation costs. Transfer learning with large pre-trained encoders has amplified downstream accuracy while reducing task-specific engineering. Yet clinical narratives remain heterogeneous across institutions and writing styles. Differences in note templates, coding practices, and temporal coverage challenge robustness. Furthermore, risk-sensitive deployment requires transparent evaluation protocols beyond single metrics. Prior studies emphasize that reported improvements can hinge on preprocessing choices and label definition. We review representative resources and models that frame the current evaluation landscape. To contextualize existing results, we summarize datasets and modeling trends. Domain-adapted language models trained on biomedical corpora have reported gains across multiple tasks, including BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), ClinicalBERT (Alsentzer et al., 2019), and PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021). Benchmarks commonly evaluate on datasets such as i2b2/VA shared tasks for de-identification and concept extraction, the n2c2 challenges for medication extraction and smoking status, MedNLI for inference over clinical text, and the ShARe/CLEF eHealth corpus for disorder mentions. Additional resources include MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV for critical care notes, PhysioNet challenges for mortality prediction, and MedQA and PubMedQA for question answering in clinical and biomedical domains. Prompting and instruction-tuning with medical question sets, exam-style problems, and synthetic dialogues have also been explored, including MultiMedQA, MedMCQA, and USMLE-style evaluations. Shared baselines often pair cased tokenizers with UMLS-based concept linking, conditional random fields for sequence labeling, and span-level micro-averaged F1 for reporting. Despite the breadth of resources, comparability is limited by inconsistent splits, leakage risks, and varying de-identification levels. We focus on harmonized evaluation that separates lexical memorization from clinical reasoning skills. Our protocol aligns training budgets, hyperparameter ranges, and early stopping across tasks. We further analyze calibration and out-of-distribution behavior for high-stakes predictions. In doing so, we trace which capabilities transfer across tasks and which require domain adaptation. The resulting insights clarify where modeling effort should prioritize efficiency or supervision. This study aims to provide practical guidance for reliable clinical NLP evaluation.

spans: Model-free deep reinforcement learning has produced canonical algorithms including DQN for discrete control, actor–critic variants such as A3C and PPO, and off-policy continuous control methods like DDPG, TD3, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Fujimoto et al., 2018; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Standardized evaluation often uses OpenAI Gym and MuJoCo locomotion tasks, DeepMind Control Suite, and robot manipulation benchmarks in Robosuite and Meta-World (Brockman et al., 2016; Todorov et al., 2012; Tassa et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Sim-to-real transfer has leveraged domain randomization, dynamics randomization, and privileged learning in simulation before deployment on hardware (Tobin et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2018; Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Offline RL and imitation learning leverage D4RL datasets and logs, with BC, DAgger, and GAIL baselines (Fu et al., 2020; Ho and Ermon, 2016).
Lacks synthesis.document: Robots must learn robust control policies that handle rich perception and dynamics. Data-driven reinforcement learning promises general-purpose behaviors without meticulous system identification. However, instability, sample inefficiency, and evaluation variance impede practical deployment. Benchmarks often lack standardized reporting across seeds and time limits. Moreover, success in simulation does not guarantee safe real-world transfer. We outline representative algorithms and evaluation settings relevant to this study. Model-free deep reinforcement learning has produced canonical algorithms including DQN for discrete control, actor–critic variants such as A3C and PPO, and off-policy continuous control methods like DDPG, TD3, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Schulman et al., 2017; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Fujimoto et al., 2018; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Standardized evaluation often uses OpenAI Gym and MuJoCo locomotion tasks, DeepMind Control Suite, and robot manipulation benchmarks in Robosuite and Meta-World (Brockman et al., 2016; Todorov et al., 2012; Tassa et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Sim-to-real transfer has leveraged domain randomization, dynamics randomization, and privileged learning in simulation before deployment on hardware (Tobin et al., 2017; Peng et al., 2018; Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Offline RL and imitation learning leverage D4RL datasets and logs, with BC, DAgger, and GAIL baselines (Fu et al., 2020; Ho and Ermon, 2016). While these ingredients are widely used, their interactions with runtime constraints remain under-explored. In particular, the trade-offs between data reuse and stability vary across tasks and reward scales. We examine how update-to-data ratios influence learning under fixed wall-clock budgets. Our protocol enforces identical replay sizes, network widths, and gradient steps for comparability. We include sensitivity analyses to initialization and stochasticity in simulators. For transfer, we assess policy robustness under randomized contacts and sensor noise. We also evaluate episode return, success rates, and safety thresholds under unified timeouts. The resulting picture clarifies which design choices matter most when resources are limited. Based on these observations, we provide guidelines for efficient controller training on new robots. Our findings target reliable, compute-aware reinforcement learning for embodied systems.

spans: Classical statistical machine translation leveraged phrase-based models and lexical reordering with strong language models (Koehn et al., 2003; Och and Ney, 2004). Neural machine translation introduced encoder–decoder architectures with attention and later Transformers that improved fluency and long-range dependencies (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Data augmentation approaches such as back-translation, dual learning, and self-training enabled gains in low-resource regimes (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020). Multilingual pretraining and transfer explored mBART, mT5, and massively multilingual models trained on OPUS and CCAligned (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; El-Kishky et al., 2020; Tiedemann, 2012). Evaluation typically reports BLEU, chrF, and COMET on WMT and IWSLT test sets, with coverage checks on FLORES-101 and Bible-derived corpora. Recent datasets also include JW300, ParaCrawl, and TED Talks collections with varying quality filters and deduplication pipelines and releases.
Lacks synthesis.document: Low-resource machine translation remains challenging due to data scarcity, domain mismatch, and orthographic variation. Practical deployments must also contend with noisy web text and limited compute. While scaling improves average quality, small languages often lag behind widely spoken ones. Understanding what contributes most to gains is complicated by heterogeneous training recipes. Classical statistical machine translation leveraged phrase-based models and lexical reordering with strong language models (Koehn et al., 2003; Och and Ney, 2004). Neural machine translation introduced encoder–decoder architectures with attention and later Transformers that improved fluency and long-range dependencies (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Data augmentation approaches such as back-translation, dual learning, and self-training enabled gains in low-resource regimes (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020). Multilingual pretraining and transfer explored mBART, mT5, and massively multilingual models trained on OPUS and CCAligned (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; El-Kishky et al., 2020; Tiedemann, 2012). Evaluation typically reports BLEU, chrF, and COMET on WMT and IWSLT test sets, with coverage checks on FLORES-101 and Bible-derived corpora. Recent datasets also include JW300, ParaCrawl, and TED Talks collections with varying quality filters and deduplication pipelines and releases. Despite these advances, systematic comparisons under consistent tokenization and filtering are still rare. Reported improvements sometimes reflect corpus composition rather than modeling differences. We therefore examine preprocessing decisions and parameter budgets as primary variables. Our study isolates effects of back-translation amounts across morphologically diverse families. We analyze transfer patterns as a function of lexical overlap and script sharing. To ensure fairness, we control for subword vocabulary size and source-target balance. We report statistical significance and robustness to alternate test sets. We further investigate calibration and uncertainty for downstream interactive settings. These results distill practical recommendations for low-resource training pipelines. The analysis aims to inform cost-aware translation for underserved languages.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists families of methods, models, datasets, and metrics without linking them to a specific research question or identified gap, satisfying the lack-of-synthesis criterion.

spans: Object detection frameworks have evolved from two-stage region-based models to single-stage detectors with real-time performance, including Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN, RetinaNet, SSD, and YOLO variants (Ren et al., 2015; He et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2016; Redmon et al., 2016; Bochkovskiy et al., 2020). Aerial and remote sensing benchmarks provide diverse targets and resolutions, such as DOTA for oriented objects, xView for large-scale satellite scenes, VisDrone for UAV imagery, and DIOR for multi-class optical remote sensing (Xia et al., 2018; Lam et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Training commonly begins with ImageNet or COCO pretraining and applies multi-scale augmentation, mosaic strategies, and anchor optimization. Evaluation typically reports AP, AP50, AP75, and mAP across scales, with oriented IoU variants for rotated boxes. Public toolkits include MMDetection, Detectron2, and mmdet-rotated baselines for reproducible experiments and comparisons.
Lacks synthesis.document: Detecting small, densely packed objects in aerial imagery remains a fundamental challenge. High viewing angles, scale variation, and clutter complicate feature sharing across levels. Rotations and extreme aspect ratios further stress anchor design and matching. Data imbalance and label noise also degrade learning in operational settings. Prior work has explored specialized heads and oriented anchors, yet generalization remains uneven. Understanding how common design choices interact with aerial data regimes is necessary. We summarize prevalent architectures and benchmarks that define the current landscape. This overview provides context for the evaluation protocol adopted in our study. We first recall representative detectors and datasets in use. Object detection frameworks have evolved from two-stage region-based models to single-stage detectors with real-time performance, including Faster R-CNN, Mask R-CNN, RetinaNet, SSD, and YOLO variants (Ren et al., 2015; He et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2016; Redmon et al., 2016; Bochkovskiy et al., 2020). Aerial and remote sensing benchmarks provide diverse targets and resolutions, such as DOTA for oriented objects, xView for large-scale satellite scenes, VisDrone for UAV imagery, and DIOR for multi-class optical remote sensing (Xia et al., 2018; Lam et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Training commonly begins with ImageNet or COCO pretraining and applies multi-scale augmentation, mosaic strategies, and anchor optimization. Evaluation typically reports AP, AP50, AP75, and mAP across scales, with oriented IoU variants for rotated boxes. Public toolkits include MMDetection, Detectron2, and mmdet-rotated baselines for reproducible experiments and comparisons. Despite broad adoption, existing reports differ in image tiling, NMS for rotated boxes, and test-time scaling. These choices yield large swings in accuracy that obscure architectural effects. We therefore fix preprocessing, label formats, and augmentation when comparing variants. Additionally, we analyze sensitivity to resolution, stride, and training duration. The consolidated findings clarify robust defaults for aerial detection at varied budgets. Our goal is to support reliable, reproducible deployment in time-critical applications.

spans: Recent spatiotemporal forecasting models have explored graph structures and temporal convolutions across benchmark highway sensor datasets. STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020) report strong results on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY, while GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020) and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020) incorporate attention and adaptive adjacency. Temporal models such as LSTNet (Lai et al., 2018) and Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) emphasize long-range dependencies, and continuous-time architectures like Neural ODEs (Chen et al., 2018) have also been applied. Data augmentation and robustness have been examined through traffic incident corpora and synthetic perturbations (Zhang et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2020), and external signals from weather APIs and calendar features are frequently used (Hoang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). Standard evaluation compares multi-step horizons using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, often with masked loss for missing sensors and fixed train/validation/test splits under common random seeds and protocols.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Urban traffic forecasting is a core problem in intelligent transportation and city-scale planning. Accurate predictions inform congestion mitigation, signal control, and traveler information systems. Advances in sensing have produced dense freeway and arterial datasets with complex spatial dependencies. Modeling these dependencies alongside nonlinear temporal dynamics remains challenging under missing data and incidents. Recent progress in deep learning highlights the importance of structure-aware temporal modeling for multi-horizon forecasts.

Recent spatiotemporal forecasting models have explored graph structures and temporal convolutions across benchmark highway sensor datasets. STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), DCRNN (Li et al., 2018), Graph WaveNet (Wu et al., 2019), and MTGNN (Wu et al., 2020) report strong results on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY, while GMAN (Zheng et al., 2020) and AGCRN (Bai et al., 2020) incorporate attention and adaptive adjacency. Temporal models such as LSTNet (Lai et al., 2018) and Informer (Zhou et al., 2021) emphasize long-range dependencies, and continuous-time architectures like Neural ODEs (Chen et al., 2018) have also been applied. Data augmentation and robustness have been examined through traffic incident corpora and synthetic perturbations (Zhang et al., 2020; Cui et al., 2020), and external signals from weather APIs and calendar features are frequently used (Hoang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). Standard evaluation compares multi-step horizons using MAE, RMSE, and MAPE on METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS04, and PEMS08, often with masked loss for missing sensors and fixed train/validation/test splits under common random seeds and protocols.

Despite substantial benchmarks and tooling, robustness under distribution shift is still under-specified in many studies. Sensor outages, regime changes from weather or events, and network reconfigurations degrade performance in deployment. Furthermore, latency constraints and streaming inference limit the feasibility of heavyweight architectures in practice. These considerations motivate approaches that quantify uncertainty and adapt dynamically to structural perturbations.

We investigate spatiotemporal forecasting with an emphasis on transferable graph structure and efficient temporal operators. Our study isolates the effects of graph construction, temporal receptive field, and exogenous variable integration. We also provide a standardized protocol for cross-dataset transfer and ablation under realistic outage scenarios. The results demonstrate consistent gains while clarifying trade-offs relevant to deployment at metropolitan scale.

spans: Instruction-following and alignment of large language models have been pursued through supervised finetuning on heterogeneous task collections and through preference-based optimization. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022), FLAN and FLAN-T5 (Wei et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2022), T0 (Sanh et al., 2022), Dolly (Conover et al., 2023), and Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023) compile templated prompts and demonstrations. Self-Instruct (Wang et al., 2023) and related bootstrapping recipes generate synthetic instruction–response pairs, while Vicuna (Chiang et al., 2023) and OpenAssistant (Köpf et al., 2023) rely on community-contributed conversations. Reinforcement learning from human feedback adopts preference datasets and reward modeling (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022; Stiennon et al., 2020), and direct preference optimization and related methods remove explicit reward models (Rafailov et al., 2023; Rafailov et al., 2024). Evaluation also reports MT-Bench results (Zheng et al., 2023), HELM and BIG-bench aggregates (Liang et al., 2022; Srivastava et al., 2022), and red-teaming benchmarks (Ganguli et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2024), with hallucination and toxicity audits.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Instruction-tuned language models have rapidly advanced general-purpose NLP and conversational assistance. Aligning model behavior with human intent requires data curation, scalable training, and principled evaluation. Practical deployments must balance capability, safety, and efficiency under limited supervision. Open benchmarks and training corpora vary widely in composition, licensing, and quality. This diversity complicates comparisons across methods and reported outcomes.

Instruction-following and alignment of large language models have been pursued through supervised finetuning on heterogeneous task collections and through preference-based optimization. Datasets such as Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022), FLAN and FLAN-T5 (Wei et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2022), T0 (Sanh et al., 2022), Dolly (Conover et al., 2023), and Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023) compile templated prompts and demonstrations. Self-Instruct (Wang et al., 2023) and related bootstrapping recipes generate synthetic instruction–response pairs, while Vicuna (Chiang et al., 2023) and OpenAssistant (Köpf et al., 2023) rely on community-contributed conversations. Reinforcement learning from human feedback adopts preference datasets and reward modeling (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022; Stiennon et al., 2020), and direct preference optimization and related methods remove explicit reward models (Rafailov et al., 2023; Rafailov et al., 2024). Evaluation also reports MT-Bench results (Zheng et al., 2023), HELM and BIG-bench aggregates (Liang et al., 2022; Srivastava et al., 2022), and red-teaming benchmarks (Ganguli et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2024), with hallucination and toxicity audits.

Despite the breadth of datasets and protocols, measurement remains fragile across prompt formats and reference sets. Small implementation differences can overshadow algorithmic contributions when scaling laws are not controlled. Moreover, safety evaluations often lack coverage for non-English contexts and emerging jailbreak tactics. Addressing these issues requires rigorous ablations, transparent releases, and cross-lab replication.

We study instruction finetuning under matched compute, data size, and prompt templates. Our analysis decomposes gains from supervision, preference optimization, and refusal heuristics. We provide open training scripts, artifact cards, and evaluation seeds for reproducibility. The resulting insights guide robust alignment choices for medium-scale models.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates datasets, methods, and evaluations like FLAN, T0, RLHF, and MT-Bench without connecting them to a specific gap or the authors' perspective, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Temporal action recognition and detection in videos leverage convolutional and transformer backbones together with proposal or anchor-free localization. 3D CNNs such as I3D (Carreira and Zisserman, 2017), SlowFast (Feichtenhofer et al., 2019), and TSM (Lin et al., 2019) provide spatiotemporal features, while TimeSformer (Bertasius et al., 2021) and VideoSwin (Liu et al., 2022) supply tokenized attention. For temporal localization, methods include SSN (Zhao et al., 2017), BMN (Lin et al., 2019b), P-GCN (Zeng et al., 2019), G-TAD (Xu et al., 2020), and ActionFormer (Zhang et al., 2022). Benchmark datasets such as THUMOS14 (Jiang et al., 2014), ActivityNet v1.3 (Caba Heilbron et al., 2015), HACS (Zhao et al., 2019), and Charades (Sigurdsson et al., 2016) define standard splits, while Kinetics-400/600 (Kay et al., 2017) often supports pretraining. Common evaluation reports mean Average Precision at multiple tIoU thresholds and frame-level recall under proposal budgets, using official codebases and curated feature zips. End-to-end approaches emphasize efficiency with annotations and pseudo-label mining on untrimmed streams (Zhang et al., 2021; Ahmed et al., 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Related Work

Understanding actions in untrimmed videos requires recognizing categories and localizing temporal boundaries. Realistic media streams exhibit long-range dependencies, background clutter, and overlapping events. Fully supervised labels are costly, motivating scalable architectures and data-efficient regimes. Meanwhile, pretraining and feature sharing influence downstream detection accuracy. Prior art spans proposal generation, classification, and end-to-end optimization.

Temporal action recognition and detection in videos leverage convolutional and transformer backbones together with proposal or anchor-free localization. 3D CNNs such as I3D (Carreira and Zisserman, 2017), SlowFast (Feichtenhofer et al., 2019), and TSM (Lin et al., 2019) provide spatiotemporal features, while TimeSformer (Bertasius et al., 2021) and VideoSwin (Liu et al., 2022) supply tokenized attention. For temporal localization, methods include SSN (Zhao et al., 2017), BMN (Lin et al., 2019b), P-GCN (Zeng et al., 2019), G-TAD (Xu et al., 2020), and ActionFormer (Zhang et al., 2022). Benchmark datasets such as THUMOS14 (Jiang et al., 2014), ActivityNet v1.3 (Caba Heilbron et al., 2015), HACS (Zhao et al., 2019), and Charades (Sigurdsson et al., 2016) define standard splits, while Kinetics-400/600 (Kay et al., 2017) often supports pretraining. Common evaluation reports mean Average Precision at multiple tIoU thresholds and frame-level recall under proposal budgets, using official codebases and curated feature zips. End-to-end approaches emphasize efficiency with annotations and pseudo-label mining on untrimmed streams (Zhang et al., 2021; Ahmed et al., 2022).

Nevertheless, existing pipelines often assume stationary event priors and dense training coverage. Domain shifts across content genres and camera motion degrade proposal quality and calibration. Moreover, real-time constraints favor single-stage models with adaptive computation budgets. These pressures call for representations that preserve temporal precision under imperfect supervision.

We develop a framework that unifies tokenized attention with boundary-aware objectives. Our experiments examine label sparsity, pretraining choices, and decoding strategies. The study clarifies where capacity and annotation interact to shape localization trade-offs.
