spans: Fairness-aware recommendation has considered exposure, calibration, and utility trade-offs under user- and item-side constraints. Post-processing and re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Vargas and Castells, 2011), FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), DELTR (Zehlike and Castillo, 2020), and FairCo (Biega et al., 2020) adjust rankings to meet demographic parity or equalized exposure. In-processing techniques include adversarial debiasing and regularization for collaborative filtering (Yao and Huang, 2017; Beutel et al., 2019; Mansoury et al., 2020), while counterfactual and causal objectives address selection bias (Liang et al., 2016; Schnabel et al., 2016; Bonner and Vasile, 2018). Public datasets like MovieLens-1M, Last.fm, Amazon Reviews, and Yelp are often annotated with sensitive attributes via heuristics or external sources (Ekstrand et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). Metrics commonly include disparity of exposure (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibration error (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018), precision/recall, nDCG, and user coverage, with simulation studies on synthetic slates to probe long-term dynamics (Bower et al., 2021; Mehrotra et al., 2018), plus standardized leaderboards to significantly enhance reproducibility across comparable baselines.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Related Work

Recommender systems influence user exposure and provider opportunities at scale. Imbalances can harm disadvantaged groups and distort platform ecosystems. Research has explored fairness constraints alongside utility objectives in ranking. Operational constraints and feedback loops complicate interventions beyond offline optimization. As a result, evaluation design is as critical as algorithmic changes.

Fairness-aware recommendation has considered exposure, calibration, and utility trade-offs under user- and item-side constraints. Post-processing and re-ranking methods such as xQuAD (Vargas and Castells, 2011), FA*IR (Zehlike et al., 2017), DELTR (Zehlike and Castillo, 2020), and FairCo (Biega et al., 2020) adjust rankings to meet demographic parity or equalized exposure. In-processing techniques include adversarial debiasing and regularization for collaborative filtering (Yao and Huang, 2017; Beutel et al., 2019; Mansoury et al., 2020), while counterfactual and causal objectives address selection bias (Liang et al., 2016; Schnabel et al., 2016; Bonner and Vasile, 2018). Public datasets like MovieLens-1M, Last.fm, Amazon Reviews, and Yelp are often annotated with sensitive attributes via heuristics or external sources (Ekstrand et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). Metrics commonly include disparity of exposure (Singh and Joachims, 2018), calibration error (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018), precision/recall, nDCG, and user coverage, with simulation studies on synthetic slates to probe long-term dynamics (Bower et al., 2021; Mehrotra et al., 2018), plus standardized leaderboards to significantly enhance reproducibility across comparable baselines.

Despite active progress, audits frequently rely on proxies for sensitive attributes with unknown error. Counterfactual reasoning is hindered by sparse logging and shifting user populations. Realistic simulations require behavior models calibrated to longitudinal interaction data. Practical deployments must consider incentive compatibility and legal compliance.

We investigate ranking policies that trade exposure guarantees against utility under click-feedback drift. Our approach incorporates uncertainty in attribute inference and couponing effects on supply. We provide open-source tooling for stress tests under simulated marketplace shocks. The findings inform robust fairness allocations for platform-scale recommenders.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists methods, datasets, and metrics without connecting them or articulating a gap or perspective, satisfying the lack of synthesis criterion.

spans: Automated program repair has evolved from generate-and-validate pipelines to learning-based patch synthesis over standardized benchmarks. Classical methods include GenProg (Le Goues et al., 2012), AE (Weimer et al., 2009), PAR (Kim et al., 2013), Prophet (Long and Rinard, 2016), and Nopol (Xuan et al., 2017), which search fix spaces guided by tests or probabilistic models. Neural approaches such as DeepFix (Gupta et al., 2017), SequenceR (Chen et al., 2019), TBar (Liu et al., 2019), AlphaRepair (Xia et al., 2022), and Codex-based patching explore token, AST, and edit representations. Benchmarks commonly include Defects4J (Just et al., 2014), Bugs.jar (Saha et al., 2018), ManySStuBs4J (Karampatsis et al., 2020), QuixBugs (Lin et al., 2017), and IntroClass (Le Goues et al., 2012), with additional Python and C corpora. Evaluation reports plausible and correct patch counts, overfitting rates, and timeouts under fixed seeds and resource budgets, often using the Bears and RepairThemAll harnesses (Madeiral et al., 2019; Durieux et al., 2019). Recent studies examine generalization audits (Liu et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2022).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Introduction

Software defects impose significant costs in productivity, reliability, and security. Automated program repair aims to generate patches that satisfy a specification, often via test suites. Benchmark-driven evaluation has standardized comparisons but also incentivized narrow optimizations. Understanding the landscape of techniques and datasets is essential for realistic progress. We overview canonical systems and measurements before describing our study.

Automated program repair has evolved from generate-and-validate pipelines to learning-based patch synthesis over standardized benchmarks. Classical methods include GenProg (Le Goues et al., 2012), AE (Weimer et al., 2009), PAR (Kim et al., 2013), Prophet (Long and Rinard, 2016), and Nopol (Xuan et al., 2017), which search fix spaces guided by tests or probabilistic models. Neural approaches such as DeepFix (Gupta et al., 2017), SequenceR (Chen et al., 2019), TBar (Liu et al., 2019), AlphaRepair (Xia et al., 2022), and Codex-based patching explore token, AST, and edit representations. Benchmarks commonly include Defects4J (Just et al., 2014), Bugs.jar (Saha et al., 2018), ManySStuBs4J (Karampatsis et al., 2020), QuixBugs (Lin et al., 2017), and IntroClass (Le Goues et al., 2012), with additional Python and C corpora. Evaluation reports plausible and correct patch counts, overfitting rates, and timeouts under fixed seeds and resource budgets, often using the Bears and RepairThemAll harnesses (Madeiral et al., 2019; Durieux et al., 2019). Recent studies examine generalization audits (Liu et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2022).

Despite standardized harnesses, many plausible patches remain incorrect under semantically diverse tests. Generation strategies can overfit to project idiosyncrasies and shallow patterns in test oracles. Moreover, dataset leakage and duplicated bugs can inflate estimates of generalization. Stronger baselines and controlled evaluation are necessary to validate improvements.

We examine synthesis and search trade-offs under cost constraints and partial specifications. Our experiments consider cross-project transfer, compiler feedback, and dynamic test augmentation. The analysis provides guidance on scalable, trustworthy repair in continuous integration settings.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span catalogs methods, datasets, and metrics without explaining their relationships or the gap they leave, and it avoids author perspective, so it lacks synthesis.

spans: Earlier studies investigate spatio-temporal forecasting using graph convolutional networks and recurrent units (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), evaluate on benchmarks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Jain et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018), explore adaptive adjacency or attention mechanisms to model dynamic connectivity (Guo et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020), and compare against baselines including ARIMA, VAR, and SVR, as well as models like LSTM and Temporal Convolutional Networks (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Bai et al., 2018).
Lacks synthesis.document: Urban mobility systems increasingly rely on accurate short term traffic forecasting to manage congestion and safety. Data are generated by heterogeneous sensors, ride sharing platforms, and connected vehicles, yielding complex spatio temporal patterns. Graph neural networks provide a natural formalism because road segments and intersections can be represented as nodes and edges. However, nonstationarity, topology changes, and missing data persist as central obstacles to reliable deployment. Classical time series models can be interpretable but fail to capture nonlocal dependencies under sparse observations. Recent research therefore emphasizes learning dynamic spatial relations while preserving temporal continuity in noisy settings. Approaches differ in how they parameterize adjacency, fuse exogenous variables, and handle multi horizon prediction. Earlier studies investigate spatio-temporal forecasting using graph convolutional networks and recurrent units (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), evaluate on benchmarks such as METR-LA and PEMS-BAY (Jain et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018), explore adaptive adjacency or attention mechanisms to model dynamic connectivity (Guo et al., 2019; Pan et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020), and compare against baselines including ARIMA, VAR, and SVR, as well as models like LSTM and Temporal Convolutional Networks (Box and Jenkins, 1976; Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Bai et al., 2018). Beyond accuracy, practitioners increasingly value reliability measures that quantify predictive dispersion across the network. Probabilistic forecasting with calibrated uncertainty is particularly important for ramp metering and emergency response. Existing evaluations are often limited to fixed graphs and closed set sensors, obscuring generalization to planned expansions or outages. Data shift arising from weather, events, and infrastructure maintenance requires models that adapt without catastrophic forgetting. Edge directionality and turn restrictions add constraints that complicate straightforward message passing formulations. Multi task settings couple speed, volume, and occupancy, inviting shared representations that respect measurement units and delays. Privacy considerations further restrict the use of high frequency GPS traces, encouraging methods that leverage aggregated counts. Interpretability is also a concern when transportation planners must justify signal timing changes to public stakeholders. Benchmarks rarely report cost sensitive metrics that reflect penalties for late or early congestion warnings. There is a need for protocols that evaluate resilience to sensor failures and topology perturbations in truly open world conditions. Standardizing horizons, loss functions, and error decomposition would facilitate more meaningful comparisons across deployments. We conclude by outlining desiderata for models that remain accurate, calibrated, and robust under structural evolution of urban networks.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span is a literature list naming multiple methods, datasets, and baselines with citations but provides no connection to a gap, motivation, or the authors' perspective, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: In medical federated learning, optimization baselines include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), with privacy mechanisms such as secure aggregation and differential privacy appearing in practical deployments (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2014). Empirical studies evaluate convolutional and transformer models on cohorts or public surrogates like BraTS, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, and MIMIC-CXR (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2019), reporting metrics for classification and segmentation and detection performance.
Lacks synthesis.document: Federated learning promises collaboration across hospitals without centralizing protected health information. Imaging workflows are attractive targets because labeling is costly and institutions vary in scanners and protocols. However, optimizing across clinics faces heterogeneity in patient populations, device vendors, and acquisition parameters. Communication budgets and straggler effects further complicate training at scale on real hospital networks. Clinical operations demand auditability and resilience against disconnected clients and partial participation. Secure aggregation reduces leakage but interacts with compression and personalization mechanisms. Domain shift across sites can dominate generalization error despite apparent improvements in local validation. Meaningful evaluation therefore requires stratification by site, modality, and pathology prevalence. The role of self supervised pretraining and feature normalization remains unsettled in privacy constrained learning. Medical governance also imposes requirements for traceability and reproducibility of all model updates. Architectures must accommodate high resolution inputs while maintaining tractable memory footprints at the client. In medical federated learning, optimization baselines include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, and FedNova (McMahan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020; Karimireddy et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020), with privacy mechanisms such as secure aggregation and differential privacy appearing in practical deployments (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Dwork et al., 2014). Empirical studies evaluate convolutional and transformer models on cohorts or public surrogates like BraTS, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, and MIMIC-CXR (Menze et al., 2015; Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2019), reporting metrics for classification and segmentation and detection performance. Practical deployments must also address partial labeling, weak supervision, and drift in annotation policies. Personalization strategies aim to balance global sharing with local adaptation under strict privacy constraints. Weighted aggregation and proximal regularization can mitigate client imbalance and nonconvexity to a degree. Nevertheless, diagnosing failures requires standardized telemetry and cross site diagnostics that respect governance. Benchmark suites should encompass realistic communication limits and intermittent connectivity patterns. We emphasize that stakeholder incentives and compliance obligations shape the feasible design space as much as algorithms. Finally, anticipated regulatory guidance will likely favor transparent pipelines and rigorous monitoring of fairness and safety.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span catalogs methods, privacy mechanisms, and datasets with citations but does not articulate how they relate to the current work or identify a specific gap, thereby lacking synthesis.

spans: Prior cross-lingual entity linking studies consider candidate generation and ranking with a variety of resources and encoders, including Wikipedia anchors, cross-lingual dictionaries, and multilingual embeddings such as MUSE, mBERT, and XLM-R (Raiman and Raiman, 2018; Conneau et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Evaluations frequently draw on KBP and Wikipedia-based benchmarks datasets like TAC-KBP 2015, AIDA-CoNLL, and multilingual hyperlinks, while comparing sparse retrieval with BM25 against dense bi-encoders and dual encoders using FAISS (Ji et al., 2015; Hoffart et al., 2011; Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Johnson et al., 2017).
Lacks synthesis.document: Cross-lingual entity linking aims to map mentions in a source language to canonical entries in a shared knowledge base. The task is difficult because surface forms vary across languages and scripts while context can be scarce. Realistic settings must handle limited training data and domain shift between news, Wikipedia, and social media. Candidate generation often dominates overall accuracy and latency in production pipelines. Recent neural architectures exploit multilingual pretraining but still rely on careful negative sampling to avoid collapse. Prior cross-lingual entity linking studies consider candidate generation and ranking with a variety of resources and encoders, including Wikipedia anchors, cross-lingual dictionaries, and multilingual embeddings such as MUSE, mBERT, and XLM-R (Raiman and Raiman, 2018; Conneau et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Evaluations frequently draw on KBP and Wikipedia-based benchmarks datasets like TAC-KBP 2015, AIDA-CoNLL, and multilingual hyperlinks, while comparing sparse retrieval with BM25 against dense bi-encoders and dual encoders using FAISS (Ji et al., 2015; Hoffart et al., 2011; Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Johnson et al., 2017). Practical applications must further address ambiguous names and culturally specific disambiguation cues. Document level aggregation and coherence modeling can reduce spurious local matches in noisy settings. Nil detection remains challenging where the target knowledge base is incomplete or out of date. Low resource languages benefit from lexicon expansion and transliteration but coverage gaps persist. Alignment of mention spans and tokenization schemes also impacts both candidate recall and ranking stability. Efficient indexing is necessary for frequent updates and low latency across global traffic patterns. Calibration of confidence estimates is needed for downstream human-in-the-loop curation. Error analysis often reveals biases toward high-resource entities with abundant linking histories. More diversified evaluation is essential to assess robustness across typologically distant language pairs. Governance considerations arise when linking personal names under varying legal frameworks. Scalable triaging should route uncertain cases to human experts with appropriate context. Ultimately, deployment requires attention to audit trails, privacy, and content moderation policies. We frame these practical aspects alongside the modeling considerations to ground evaluation in real constraints.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists datasets, resources, and methods with citations but offers no explicit connection to a research gap or to the authors' aims, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Summarization research encompasses extractive and abstractive paradigms with widely used datasets such as CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Gigaword (Hermann et al., 2015; Narayan et al., 2018; Graff et al., 2003). Prominent model families include pointer-generator networks, transformer encoder-decoders like BART and PEGASUS, and reinforcement learning approaches that directly optimize ROUGE or mixed objectives through policy gradient and actor-critic updates (See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), often reported alongside extractive baselines like TextRank and neural sentence selectors (Mihalcea and Tarau, 2004; Nallapati et al., 2017).
Lacks synthesis.document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense source texts while generating fluent novel expressions. Reinforcement learning has been introduced to shift training signals toward sequence level quality objectives. However, instability and credit assignment limit consistent gains across domains and lengths. Exposure bias and mismatch between training and evaluation metrics complicate fair comparisons. Reward definitions also struggle to reflect factuality, salience, and readability simultaneously. Recent work examines constrained decoding and calibration to mitigate hallucination without excessive verbosity. spans: Robust speech recognition has been evaluated across corpora including CHiME, Aurora-4, WSJ, and LibriSpeech with synthetic and real noise conditions (Vincent et al., 2013; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000; Paul and Baker, 1992; Panayotov et al., 2015). Methods span acoustic front-end and end-to-end modeling, incorporating multi-microphone beamforming with BeamformIt, data augmentation with MUSAN and simulated RIRs, architectural choices like CTC and RNN-T, and pretraining learning approaches such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Anguera et al., 2007; Snyder et al., 2015; Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document: Automatic speech recognition systems suffer significant degradation under additive noise and reverberation. Far-field microphones, moving speakers, and nonstationary backgrounds exacerbate the mismatch between training and deployment. Robustness therefore depends on both acoustic modeling and front-end signal processing choices. Data augmentation often improves performance but can introduce harmful shifts if not carefully tuned. Multi-condition training interacts with language model constraints for decoding in surprising ways. Evaluation setups differ in microphone arrays, room geometry, and noise profiles, complicating cross-paper comparisons. The rise of self-supervised encoders has also changed how small labeled datasets can be exploited. Nevertheless, end-to-end training remains sensitive to batch size, optimizer schedules, and label smoothing. Real-world deployments must consider on-device constraints and streaming latency budgets. Adaptation to new acoustic domains is critical when environments change frequently. Online unsupervised methods can drift without periodic anchors and human oversight. Uncertainty estimation assists in deferring unreliable transcriptions during safety critical operations. Robust speech recognition has been evaluated across corpora including CHiME, Aurora-4, WSJ, and LibriSpeech with synthetic and real noise conditions (Vincent et al., 2013; Pearce and Hirsch, 2000; Paul and Baker, 1992; Panayotov et al., 2015). Methods span acoustic front-end and end-to-end modeling, incorporating multi-microphone beamforming with BeamformIt, data augmentation with MUSAN and simulated RIRs, architectural choices like CTC and RNN-T, and pretraining learning approaches such as wav2vec 2.0 and HuBERT (Anguera et al., 2007; Snyder et al., 2015; Graves et al., 2006; Graves, 2012; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). Scoring transcripts solely by word error rate may mask harmful substitutions in rare keywords. Confidence metrics and semantic error rate can complement lexical measures for task oriented scenarios. Personalized language modeling promises accuracy gains but must respect privacy and memory limits. Robustness should also reflect wake word false activations and tail latency in streaming decoders. Open benchmarks with shared noise profiles and room impulse responses would facilitate reproducible progress. Future studies should align metrics with downstream utility while acknowledging compute and device constraints.

Temporal forecasting has leveraged classic sequence models and recent attention-based architectures across public benchmarks. Datasets such as MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016), eICU (Pollard et al., 2018), PhysioNet 2012 (Silva et al., 2012), and COVID-19 hospital data (Kissler et al., 2020) are frequently used. Methods include autoregressive baselines, LSTM and GRU encoders (Cho et al., 2014), Temporal Convolutional Networks (Bai et al., 2018), Informer (Zhou et al., 2021), Autoformer (Wu et al., 2021), and FEDformer (Zhou et al., 2022). Evaluation commonly reports MAE, RMSE, AUROC, and PR-AUC with horizon-based splits and rolling-origin validation and calibration.

Efficient deployment threads through these areas as models move from offline training to interactive environments. Quantization, distillation, and sparsity can reduce footprint while preserving core behaviors over target distributions. spans: Early benchmarks and models for visual question answering established common evaluation practices across datasets and architectures, including VQA v1.0, VQA v2.0, GQA, and CLEVR. Baseline systems ranged from LSTM-question plus CNN-image fusion to attention-based bilinear pooling, with notable variants such as SAN, BAN, MUTAN, and bottom-up top-down attention. Later transformer approaches adopted multimodal pretraining on Conceptual Captions, Visual Genome, and COCO, exemplified by LXMERT, ViLBERT, UNITER, VinVL, and OSCAR. Methods leveraging scene graphs, neural module networks, and neuro-symbolic reasoning targeted compositional generalization, as seen in MAC, NS-VQA, and Neural-Symbolic VQA. Recent work explored prompt-based alignment and contrastive objectives using CLIP, ALIGN, BLIP, and Flamingo, alongside evaluation protocols emphasizing robustness, distribution shift, and textual bias diagnostics. Additional resources include TextCaps, VQA-CP, TDIUC, and VizWiz, which broaden task coverage and stress real-world accessibility challenges too.

Despite steady gains on benchmarks, models still rely on superficial correlations and struggle with compositional queries and distribution shift. Prior analyses highlight that evaluation protocols, annotation artifacts, and pretraining data choices can mask true reasoning capabilities. We revisit these issues through the lens of calibration, causality, and robustness under systematically perturbed inputs. To situate our study, we summarize representative datasets and architectures that have shaped the field. While informative, this landscape leaves open how training objectives and architectural choices translate into calibrated reasoning under shift. Our protocol measures counterfactual consistency and answer stability across semantically equivalent paraphrases and visual edits. We develop diagnostics that decompose correctness by reasoning step and quantify uncertainty allocation across modalities. Through these analyses, we outline design principles for evaluation and modeling that better align with robust multimodal reasoning.

spans: Clinical time-series forecasting has drawn on varied datasets and modeling paradigms, including MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, eICU, and the PhysioNet Challenge cohorts for mortality, decompensation, and length-of-stay prediction. Classical baselines such as ARIMA, VAR, and Kalman filtering are commonly contrasted with neural approaches like LSTM, GRU, TCN, and seq2seq attention models. More recent architectures include Transformer variants for long horizons, such as LogSparse Transformer, Informer, Reformer, and FEDformer, alongside N-BEATS, DeepAR, and Temporal Fusion Transformer for mixed covariates. Handling missingness and irregular sampling typically leverages GRU-D, BRITS, and Latent ODE methods, with imputation benchmarks on UCI HAR, PhysioNet 2012, and MIMIC subsets. Evaluation protocols span AUROC, AUPRC, calibration metrics, horizon-specific MAE, and dynamic time warping, with public leaderboards tracking reproducibility and robustness. Common splits use patient-level partitioning to mitigate leakage across temporal windows and units.

Practical deployment is complicated by irregular sampling, missingness, censoring, and nonstationary care practices. Moreover, naive evaluation can inflate performance through leakage and population imbalance, obscuring clinical utility. We study how representation, uncertainty, and data partitioning interact to influence reliable forecasting in the ICU. Before detailing our approach, we outline common datasets, baselines, and evaluation conventions in this area. spans: Benchmarking molecular representation learning has centered on MoleculeNet tasks such as ESOL, FreeSolv, Lipophilicity, BBBP, BACE, Tox21, and ToxCast, with extensions to OGB-MolHIV, PCBA, and the OGB-LSC PCQM4M challenge. Message-passing architectures include GCN, GAT, MPNN, D-MPNN, and Weave, while 3D-aware models such as SchNet, PhysNet, DimeNet, DimeNet++, and SE(3)-Transformer incorporate geometric information. Transformer-style encoders like Graphormer, GROVER, and MAT operate over graphs or SMILES with learned attention biases. Self-supervised approaches apply contrastive or generative objectives, including GraphCL, InfoGraph, MolCLR, AttrMasking, and GPT-GNN, sometimes paired with RDKit augmentations. Datasets often provide SMILES strings, SDF conformers, and computed descriptors, with data splits by scaffold, random, or time. Standard metrics include ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, RMSE, and MAE across classification and regression endpoints. Leaderboards track progress using curated featurizations, hyperparameter ranges, and reproducible scaffold splits across multiple benchmarks.

spans: Fairness-aware recommendation has been studied across benchmarks such as MovieLens, Last.fm, Amazon Product Data, Yelp, KuaiRec, and MIND, with simulated environments derived from the RecSys Challenge datasets. Evaluation commonly reports demographic parity, equal opportunity, disparate exposure, calibration, and user-level NDCG alongside provider-side exposure parity. Group definitions span gender, age, and geography inferred from profiles, as well as provider attributes like business category, ownership, and popularity tiers. spans: Neural program synthesis from natural language has been evaluated on CoNaLa, Django, and WikiSQL for semantic parsing, with broader coding tasks on CodeSearchNet, MBPP, HumanEval, and APPS. Early sequence-to-sequence baselines used LSTMs with attention and copy mechanisms, followed by grammar-guided decoders such as TranX and Seq2Struct that enforce AST constraints. Pretrained encoder-decoder models including CodeT5, PLBART, and BART adapt to code-text pairs, while large language models like Codex, PaLM-Coder, and AlphaCode demonstrate strong few-shot performance. Auxiliary signals incorporate type signatures, unit tests, and executor feedback, with constrained decoding, beam search with verification, and guided search over DSLs. Common metrics include exact match, BLEU, and pass@k, with robustness studies using adversarial paraphrases and perturbations to identifiers, formatting, and comments. Benchmarks sometimes supply unit tests, reference ASTs, or hidden evaluators to standardize comparison and reproducibility.

spans: Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3.

Knowledge distillation has been explored through response-based transfer, feature imitation, and relation preservation across diverse NLP tasks, with methods such as DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019), TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020) reducing parameter counts while retaining accuracy on GLUE, MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), and QQP. Extensions incorporate task-adaptive teachers (Turc et al., 2019), intermediate-layer matching (Romero et al., 2015), patient knowledge transfer (Sun et al., 2019), data augmentation via back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), and self-training with pseudo-labels (He et al., 2020). Additional strategies align attention distributions (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Sun et al., 2019), logits under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015), and sample-level relations using contrastive objectives (Tian et al., 2020; Khosla et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence distillation for generative models employs sequence-level training (Kim and Rush, 2016), teacher re-ranking for abstractive summarization (Chan et al., 2021), and offline data filtering with CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) and XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) to stabilize decoding. Cross-lingual and multilingual distillation leverage parallel corpora (Conneau and Lample, 2019), multilingual teachers and students on mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020), and supervision from resources such as TED Talks (Cettolo et al., 2012), Europarl (Koehn, 2005), CCAligned (El-Kishky et al., 2020), and Wikipedia dumps.

Despite their strength, the computational demands of these systems exceed budgets in many real-time applications. Distillation transfers behavior from a high-capacity teacher to a smaller student through soft targets or auxiliary feature objectives. Students trained in this manner can approximate the teacher while allowing faster, cheaper inference on commodity hardware. Open questions persist regarding stability, calibration, and generalization under domain shift when training compressed students. Beyond accuracy, practitioners monitor robustness to perturbations, calibration reliability, fairness metrics, and memory footprints. For on-device inference, streaming scenarios impose tight end-to-end latency budgets that interact with batching and quantization artifacts. Data curricula and sample selection may further reduce the cost of student training by prioritizing informative or diverse examples. Teacher choice influences outcomes, including ensembles, intermediate checkpoints, and multi-task supervision for richer guidance. These considerations motivate a systematic comparison of capacity, training cost, and downstream trade-offs in constrained regimes. We therefore design experiments that isolate compute, data availability, and domain shift to provide actionable guidance.

Lacks synthesis.document: Neural information retrieval has shifted from sparse lexical matching to learned dense and hybrid representations. Applications in web search, question answering, and enterprise retrieval require both effectiveness and strict latency bounds. Indexing, re-ranking, and caching strategies interact with model size and training objectives in complex ways. Early transformer-based models established strong baselines across language understanding benchmarks including GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), with architectures such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019) driving progress, alongside large-scale generative systems like T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), notably GPT-3. In retrieval pipelines, bi-encoders enable approximate nearest neighbor search, whereas cross-encoders provide precise re-ranking. Batching and vector quantization reduce serving costs but can degrade ranking consistency across tail queries. Knowledge distillation offers an appealing path to deploy accurate yet efficient rankers without full cross-encoder complexity. Previous work studies distillation from cross-encoders to bi-encoders using contrastive, margin-based, or listwise objectives. However, retrieval-oriented distillation must reconcile binary labels, graded relevance, and long-document contexts. The choice of teacher data, negatives, and document truncation can alter the learned similarity landscape. Latency budgets span turn-taking thresholds, barge-in responsiveness, and on-device wake word detection. Distillation naturally suits modular and end-to-end settings by transferring guidance from resource-rich teachers. We investigate dialog domains with diverse slot schemas, code-switching, and noisy far-field audio. Metrics cover word error rate, intent accuracy, end-to-end task success, and energy per utterance. We examine schedulers that adapt decoder steps to user patience and bandwidth conditions. Our analysis considers teacher choice, alignment losses, and semi-supervised data for constrained training. Results are summarized to provide guidance on building efficient assistants under privacy and compute constraints.

spans: Recent advances in medical image reconstruction and analysis leverage supervised architectures and contrastive pretraining across diverse datasets. U-Net remains a strong baseline for segmentation and reconstruction post-processing (Ronneberger et al., 2015), while residual encoders and attention variants report gains on structural fidelity (He et al., 2016; Oktay et al., 2018; Woo et al., 2018). Unsupervised and self-supervised objectives have been explored with variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Goodfellow et al., 2014), and with instance discrimination methods such as MoCo, SimCLR, and BYOL (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Benchmarks include fastMRI for MRI undersampling (Zbontar et al., 2018), BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), and the IXI and ACDC datasets collections (IXI, 2006; Bernard et al., 2018).
Lacks synthesis.document: Magnetic resonance image reconstruction increasingly relies on learned priors that balance artifact suppression with structural fidelity. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks clarity on how pretraining regimes transfer to clinical undersampling patterns. Prior work has explored both k-space models and image-domain refinement, yet the interaction between supervision levels remains uncertain. To contextualize our approach, we outline representative directions in modeling and evaluation. We focus on architectures, objectives, and datasets that define the current landscape for accelerated MRI. Within this landscape, supervised and self-supervised methods coexist with varied training signals. Recent advances in medical image reconstruction and analysis leverage supervised architectures and contrastive pretraining across diverse datasets. U-Net remains a strong baseline for segmentation and reconstruction post-processing (Ronneberger et al., 2015), while residual encoders and attention variants report gains on structural fidelity (He et al., 2016; Oktay et al., 2018; Woo et al., 2018). Unsupervised and self-supervised objectives have been explored with variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Goodfellow et al., 2014), and with instance discrimination methods such as MoCo, SimCLR, and BYOL (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Grill et al., 2020). Benchmarks include fastMRI for MRI undersampling (Zbontar et al., 2018), BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), and the IXI and ACDC datasets collections (IXI, 2006; Bernard et al., 2018). These resources and methods motivate examining how representation quality interacts with sampling masks and coil configurations. We target the scenario where limited annotations coexist with abundant raw k-space, reflecting realistic hospital constraints. Our framework decomposes the problem into encoding, reconstruction, and calibration, each aligned with data availability. The evaluation emphasizes sensitivity to rare pathologies and stability under variable acceleration rates. We also report ablation tests isolating augmentation strength from contrastive temperature and queue size. Taken together, the study clarifies the role of pretraining signals in accelerating MRI without degrading clinically critical detail.

spans: Conversational recommendation has drawn on reinforcement learning and knowledge-aware modeling across open-domain and task-specific settings. Early systems optimize slot-filling or dialog policies with REINFORCE and policy gradient methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 2000), later extended with actor–critic and off-policy variants such as DQN, DDPG, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Knowledge graphs and graph neural networks provide structured signals through neighborhood propagation or attention (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Velickovic et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Evaluation commonly relies on ReDial conversations for recommendation dialogs (Li et al., 2018), MovieLens ratings for offline metrics (Harper and Konstan, 2015), and large-scale review corpora from Amazon and Yelp to pretrain or distill user preferences (McAuley et al., 2015; Yelp, 2019) in various domains today.
Lacks synthesis.document: Conversational recommenders aim to elicit preferences interactively while minimizing user burden. Designing such agents requires balancing exploration, personalization, and natural language grounding. Static evaluation alone can obscure policy failures that emerge during multi-turn interactions. Moreover, sparse rewards and delayed feedback complicate credit assignment in dialog states. This section reviews directions relevant to learning policies and exploiting structured knowledge. We distinguish between policy learning, representation building, and evaluation protocols. Conversational recommendation has drawn on reinforcement learning and knowledge-aware modeling across open-domain and task-specific settings. Early systems optimize slot-filling or dialog policies with REINFORCE and policy gradient methods (Williams, 1992; Sutton et al., 2000), later extended with actor–critic and off-policy variants such as DQN, DDPG, and SAC (Mnih et al., 2015; Lillicrap et al., 2016; Haarnoja et al., 2018). Knowledge graphs and graph neural networks provide structured signals through neighborhood propagation or attention (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Velickovic et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Evaluation commonly relies on ReDial conversations for recommendation dialogs (Li et al., 2018), MovieLens ratings for offline metrics (Harper and Konstan, 2015), and large-scale review corpora from Amazon and Yelp to pretrain or distill user preferences (McAuley et al., 2015; Yelp, 2019) in various domains today. These ingredients shape how systems manage cold-start users and evolving intents over long sessions. In particular, reward design and off-policy correction must account for biased logging and exposure effects. We study policy objectives that align information gain with recommendation utility over dialog turns. The approach integrates uncertainty estimates to modulate question asking versus item suggesting. Our experiments examine robustness to annotation noise and popularity skew. Beyond offline metrics, we simulate adaptive feedback to measure conversation efficiency and user satisfaction.

spans: Cross-lingual transfer and low-resource translation have been studied through pretraining, data augmentation, and multilingual evaluation suites. Back-translation and dual learning establish strong baselines for exploiting monolingual text (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016), while unsupervised and pivot-based approaches reduce reliance on parallel data (Lample et al., 2018; Firat et al., 2016). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models such as mBART and mT5 provide multilingual priors for fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), and sentence encoders like LASER enable corpus filtering and mining (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Common benchmarks include IWSLT for speech transcripts, WMT news tasks, OPUS collections, and FLORES for many-to-many evaluation (Cettolo et al., 2014; Bojar et al., 2018; Tiedemann, 2012; Goyal et al., 2021) covering typologically diverse pairs and varying data scales worldwide over multiple domains.
Lacks synthesis.document: Building machine translation for low-resource languages remains a central challenge in multilingual NLP. Data scarcity, domain drift, and script variation conspire to degrade generalization. Recent progress has come from scaling pretraining and refining data selection pipelines. However, the interplay between augmentation and domain adaptation is not fully understood. We summarize representative approaches and resources before outlining our study design. Our focus spans objectives for leveraging monolingual text and protocols for evaluating transfer. Cross-lingual transfer and low-resource translation have been studied through pretraining, data augmentation, and multilingual evaluation suites. Back-translation and dual learning establish strong baselines for exploiting monolingual text (Sennrich et al., 2016; He et al., 2016), while unsupervised and pivot-based approaches reduce reliance on parallel data (Lample et al., 2018; Firat et al., 2016). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models such as mBART and mT5 provide multilingual priors for fine-tuning (Liu et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021), and sentence encoders like LASER enable corpus filtering and mining (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Common benchmarks include IWSLT for speech transcripts, WMT news tasks, OPUS collections, and FLORES for many-to-many evaluation (Cettolo et al., 2014; Bojar et al., 2018; Tiedemann, 2012; Goyal et al., 2021) covering typologically diverse pairs and varying data scales worldwide over multiple domains. These threads motivate a careful examination of how tokenization, sampling, and objective choice affect domain shift. We consider practical constraints such as noisy web text, limited dictionaries, and hardware budgets. The methodology factors in target-side fluency, adequacy, and calibration for downstream tasks. We explore curriculum strategies that couple bitext scarcity with synthetic data schedules. Experiments analyze sensitivity to script normalization and vocabulary granularity. The results highlight when cross-lingual pretraining helps and when tailored adaptation is required.

spans: Spatiotemporal traffic forecasting has been advanced by graph-based sequence models and dynamic connectivity mechanisms. Diffusion and convolutional operators on road networks underpin DCRNN, STGCN, and Graph WaveNet (Li et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019), while adaptive adjacency and node-specific embeddings characterize AGCRN and related variants (Bai et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2021). Attention over spatial and temporal neighborhoods further refines long-range dependencies (Veličković et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2020). Standard benchmarks include METR-LA and PEMS-BAY for speed and occupancy prediction, along with PEMS03, PEMS04, and PEMS08 for larger sensor grids (Li et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2020) with consistent data splits and horizon settings enabling comparable metrics across horizons and sensors under recurring congestion and weather perturbation conditions nationwide.

Temporal dynamics are influenced by recurrent patterns and rare disruptions, both challenging for sequence models. Graph formulations capture topology, but stationarity assumptions can fail under dynamic routing. Recent architectures inject spatial priors into recurrent or convolutional modules to handle propagation effects. We review representative modeling choices and public datasets used in the literature. Our discussion distinguishes operator design, adaptivity, and benchmark protocol. Together, these ingredients create a testbed where architectural differences translate into measurable trade-offs. We focus on flexible operators that accommodate changing connectivity without expensive retraining. The training objective balances pointwise accuracy with calibration across forecast horizons. We assess efficiency under constrained latency budgets consistent with real-time deployment. Stress tests evaluate sensitivity to sensor dropouts and distributional shifts. The analysis informs when static graphs suffice and when dynamic relations are necessary.

spans: Automated short-answer assessment has been explored with feature-based scoring, neural similarity models, and pretrained language encoders. Early systems relied on lexical overlap and handcrafted discourse features similar to e-rater components (Attali and Burstein, 2006; Yannakoudakis et al., 2011), while Siamese architectures and attention mechanisms modeled paraphrase-level correspondences (Mueller and Thyagarajan, 2016; Rocktäschel et al., 2016). Contextual encoders such as BERT, RoBERTa, and SBERT improved semantic matching for rubric-aligned predictions (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Widely used datasets include ASAP-SAS from the Hewlett Foundation, SciEntsBank and BEETLE for student response analysis, and newer domain-specific sets in biology and physics classrooms (Kaggle, 2012; Dzikovska et al., 2013; Nielsen et al., 2019) covering multiple question types, varied prompts, and scoring schemes across grade levels and subjects worldwide.
Lacks synthesis.document: Automated assessment promises timely feedback in classrooms where instructor time is limited. Short answers are particularly challenging due to lexical variability and sparse supervision. Practical deployment also requires transparency and robust domain transfer. Prior work spans feature engineering, metric learning, and pretrained encoders. We outline datasets and modeling patterns commonly adopted in this area. The review emphasizes input representations and supervision signals. Automated short-answer assessment has been explored with feature-based scoring, neural similarity models, and pretrained language encoders. Early systems relied on lexical overlap and handcrafted discourse features similar to e-rater components (Attali and Burstein, 2006; Yannakoudakis et al., 2011), while Siamese architectures and attention mechanisms modeled paraphrase-level correspondences (Mueller and Thyagarajan, 2016; Rocktäschel et al., 2016). Contextual encoders such as BERT, RoBERTa, and SBERT improved semantic matching for rubric-aligned predictions (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). These references motivate disentangling semantic similarity from rubric-specific evidence aggregation. We investigate calibration strategies that align score distributions across prompts. The training setup balances pairwise contrastive learning with prompt-conditioned regression. We analyze bias with respect to demographic terms and content length. Our evaluation includes prompt transfer and unseen course generalization. We discuss implications for formative feedback and reliability in classroom settings.

spans: Early prompt-based strategies for few-shot relation extraction emphasize pattern verbalization and label mapping with masked language models, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), and ADAPET (Tam et al., 2021). Subsequent approaches introduce continuous prompts and lightweight adapters for task conditioning, such as P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and Prompt Tuning for T5 (Lester et al., 2021). Data resources commonly used to benchmark these methods include FewRel 1.0 and 2.0 (Han et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019), TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), and SemEval-2010 Task 8 (Hendrickx et al., 2010). Recent instruction-driven methods leverage larger language models via in-context learning and templated rationales, drawing on GPT-3 style setups (Brown et al., 2020), T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022), and LAMA-style probing suites (Petroni et al., 2019) across standard evaluation protocols.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Few-shot relation extraction seeks to identify semantic relations between entities when only a handful of labeled examples are available. The rise of pretrained language models has reframed the problem from metric learning to prompt-conditioned inference with constrained supervision. Despite accuracy gains, current systems remain sensitive to prompt wording, label verbalizers, and domain shifts. This section reviews the landscape and clarifies the technical assumptions that shape the evaluation regimes in this area.

Early metric-based methods adapted prototypical representations and episodic training to approximate class structure under data scarcity. Subsequent transformer encoders introduced stronger contextualization but still depended on brittle hand-crafted templates. Task design choices around entity markers, sentence cropping, and negative sampling often dominate reported improvements. Moreover, inconsistent train–test splits and cross-domain generalization protocols complicate direct comparison across papers. These observations motivate a sharper look at the roles of prompts, representations, and data curation in few-shot settings.

Early prompt-based strategies for few-shot relation extraction emphasize pattern verbalization and label mapping with masked language models, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), and ADAPET (Tam et al., 2021). Subsequent approaches introduce continuous prompts and lightweight adapters for task conditioning, such as P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and Prompt Tuning for T5 (Lester et al., 2021). Data resources commonly used to benchmark these methods include FewRel 1.0 and 2.0 (Han et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019), TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), and SemEval-2010 Task 8 (Hendrickx et al., 2010). Recent instruction-driven methods leverage larger language models via in-context learning and templated rationales, drawing on GPT-3 style setups (Brown et al., 2020), T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022), and LAMA-style probing suites (Petroni et al., 2019) across standard evaluation protocols.

Together, these components reveal a field where modeling choices are intertwined with annotation granularity and label semantics. Prompt engineering implicitly encodes task definitions, blurring the line between representation learning and dataset specification. Robust evaluation should disentangle improvements due to scaling from those attributable to calibration or better verbalizers. Furthermore, label space entropy and relation argument structure can confound in-context learners if weak priors dominate. We therefore examine how template design, demonstration selection, and entity marking interact under controlled ablations.

Our analysis emphasizes reproducible splits, standardized verbalizers, and calibrated decoding under constrained resources. We revisit cross-domain transfer from newswire to Wikipedia, enforcing constant budgets for demonstrations and gradient steps. The study isolates prompt sensitivity from pretraining advantages by aligning tokenization and marker conventions across baselines. Finally, we outline a measurement model that attributes variance in accuracy to specific prompt and data factors.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span lists methods, datasets, and large models with citations but offers no connection to a specific argument, gap, or perspective, thus lacking synthesis.

spans: Foundational graph encoders for molecular property prediction include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GIN (Xu et al., 2019), with benchmarks curated in MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018). Architectures exploiting geometric information extend this line with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020). Widely used datasets span QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC and ZINC-12k (Irwin et al., 2012), PCBA and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018), and the OGB molecule suite including ogbg-molhiv and ogbg-molpcba (Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining and feature engineering practices commonly rely on masked attribute modeling, context prediction, and RDKit-derived descriptors, drawing on GraphCL (You et al., 2020), InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020), GROVER (Rong et al., 2020), and attr-masking setups in OGB leaderboards under standard splits and evaluation metrics across benchmarks, commonly reported in literature.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Predicting molecular properties from structure is central to drug discovery and materials design. Learning directly on graphs mitigates feature engineering and offers end-to-end adaptability to new endpoints. Yet performance often hinges on the alignment between molecular geometry, featurization choices, and the inductive biases of the encoder. We review techniques and evaluation protocols before presenting our study.

Early work emphasized two-dimensional graphs with handcrafted atom and bond attributes derived from cheminformatics toolkits. The emergence of message passing neural networks unified many variants under a shared computational template. Recent models incorporate distance-aware filters and angular terms to better capture local electronic effects. Data leakage due to scaffold overlap and salt-removal rules complicates fair assessment across public benchmarks. These considerations motivate principled data splits and careful normalization of input chemistry.

Foundational graph encoders for molecular property prediction include GCN (Kipf and Welling, 2017), GraphSAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GIN (Xu et al., 2019), with benchmarks curated in MoleculeNet (Wu et al., 2018). Architectures exploiting geometric information extend this line with SchNet (Schütt et al., 2017), DimeNet (Klicpera et al., 2020), and SE(3)-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020). Widely used datasets span QM9 (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014), ZINC and ZINC-12k (Irwin et al., 2012), PCBA and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018), and the OGB molecule suite including ogbg-molhiv and ogbg-molpcba (Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining and feature engineering practices commonly rely on masked attribute modeling, context prediction, and RDKit-derived descriptors, drawing on GraphCL (You et al., 2020), InfoGraph (Sun et al., 2020), GROVER (Rong et al., 2020), and attr-masking setups in OGB leaderboards under standard splits and evaluation metrics across benchmarks, commonly reported in literature.

Despite the breadth of models, reported gains can reflect differences in stereochemistry handling rather than representational capacity. Three-dimensional methods are particularly sensitive to conformer generation quality and partial charge assignment. Transfer learning across datasets benefits from consistent valence models and standardized canonicalization. We emphasize evaluation under scaffold and temporal splits to quantify generalization to novel chemotypes. Our analysis disentangles architectural effects from data preprocessing through controlled ablations.

The study presents a reference pipeline with reproducible featurization, geometry settings, and hyperparameter budgets. We benchmark multiple encoders under identical splits and revisit conclusions about the value of 3D cues. The results reveal how target noise and descriptor redundancy interact with model capacity in realistic settings. Finally, we discuss implications for active learning and prospective validation in medicinal chemistry campaigns.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span is a catalog of models, datasets, and pretraining approaches with citations, but it neither relates them to a problem statement nor articulates how they connect to the paper’s aims.

spans: Prominent anomaly detection models for multivariate time series include LSTM-VAE (Park et al., 2018), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), GDN (Deng and Hooi, 2021), THOC (Shen et al., 2020), and Anomaly Transformer (Xu et al., 2021). Standard benchmarks encompass SMD and MSL/SMAP (Su et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), SWaT and WADI (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016; Ahmed et al., 2017), Yahoo A1 (Laptev et al., 2015), TSB-UB (Montero-Manso et al., 2020), and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015). Evaluation practices frequently vary in windowing rules, point-level versus range-level scoring, and delay penalties, with implementations often derived from the Merlion toolkit (Bhatnagar et al., 2021), PyOD (Zhao et al., 2019), and Darts (Herzen et al., 2022). Reporting conventions also differ in anomaly window inflation and tolerance parameters.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Detecting anomalies in multivariate telemetry is essential for safe and reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Industrial sensors exhibit nonstationarity, missing data, and asynchronous sampling that complicate modeling. Supervised labels are scarce and often delayed, motivating semi-supervised and unsupervised formulations. We survey representative approaches and identify evaluation pitfalls that mask true generalization.

Classical baselines rely on robust statistics, autoregressive filters, and isolation-based schemes that provide interpretable alerts. Neural methods leverage representation learning to capture cross-channel dependencies without manual crafting. However, heavy smoothing and windowing decisions can suppress short-lived but critical transients. Threshold calibration is further complicated by nonstationary operating modes and maintenance interventions. These characteristics motivate principled scoring and uncertainty quantification for deployment.

Prominent anomaly detection models for multivariate time series include LSTM-VAE (Park et al., 2018), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), OmniAnomaly (Su et al., 2019), MSCRED (Zhang et al., 2019), USAD (Audibert et al., 2020), GDN (Deng and Hooi, 2021), THOC (Shen et al., 2020), and Anomaly Transformer (Xu et al., 2021). Standard benchmarks encompass SMD and MSL/SMAP (Su et al., 2019; Hundman et al., 2018), SWaT and WADI (Mathur and Tippenhauer, 2016; Ahmed et al., 2017), Yahoo A1 (Laptev et al., 2015), TSB-UB (Montero-Manso et al., 2020), and the Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (Lavin and Ahmad, 2015). Evaluation practices frequently vary in windowing rules, point-level versus range-level scoring, and delay penalties, with implementations often derived from the Merlion toolkit (Bhatnagar et al., 2021), PyOD (Zhao et al., 2019), and Darts (Herzen et al., 2022). Reporting conventions also differ in anomaly window inflation and tolerance parameters.

Despite extensive benchmarking, reproducibility remains fragile due to hidden preprocessing and inconsistent resampling. Alarm economics also matter because false positives drive operator fatigue and costly rollbacks. We therefore frame anomaly detection as risk-sensitive estimation with explicit costs for delay and escalation. The analysis emphasizes calibration curves and post-hoc thresholding trained on frozen model scores. We examine shift-robustness under seasonal drift, missing blocks, and actuator saturations in controlled ablations.

Our empirical study standardizes window definitions, overlap rules, and look-ahead leakage prevention. We report range-aware metrics alongside point-wise precision and recall to balance localization and detection. The results clarify how dependence structure, window size, and training objectives trade off across regimes. Finally, we offer practical guidelines for operations teams adopting learning-based detectors in production.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates models, datasets, and toolkits with no integration or explanation of how they inform the authors’ approach, satisfying the lack of synthesis criterion.

spans: Standard continuous-control baselines include DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018), and SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), alongside behavior cloning and DAGGER for imitation (Ross et al., 2011). Offline and imitation-flavored algorithms widely used for manipulation comprise CQL (Kumar et al., 2020), AWAC (Nair et al., 2020), IQL (Kostrikov et al., 2021), BRAC (Wu et al., 2019), and DAPG for demonstrations (Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Benchmark suites frequently referenced include Meta-World (Yu et al., 2020), D4RL Locomotion and Adroit (Fu et al., 2020), Robomimic (Mandlekar et al., 2021), RLBench (James et al., 2020), and OpenAI Gym Robotics (Brockman et al., 2016). Pretraining and representation resources appear in R3M (Nair et al., 2022), VC-1 (Majumdar et al., 2023), and MVP (Xiao et al., 2022), with Franka Kitchen (Gupta et al., 2019) and ManiSkill (Mu et al., 2021).
Lacks synthesis.document:
Learning dexterous manipulation remains challenging due to contact-rich dynamics and partial observability. Reinforcement learning offers a unified objective but suffers from sample inefficiency and instability on real hardware. Imitation and offline learning mitigate risky exploration by exploiting demonstrations and logged experience. We consolidate relevant algorithms and datasets before detailing our methodology.

Policy learning with expressive function approximators requires careful regularization to avoid exploitation of simulator artifacts. Domain randomization and system identification reduce the gap between simulation and real robots but are rarely standardized. Visual observations introduce representation issues that entangle perception with control. Evaluation protocols also vary in success thresholds, resets, and action rate constraints across platforms. These factors complicate apples-to-apples comparisons of learning signals and convergence behavior.

Standard continuous-control baselines include DDPG (Lillicrap et al., 2016), PPO (Schulman et al., 2017), TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018), and SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), alongside behavior cloning and DAGGER for imitation (Ross et al., 2011). Offline and imitation-flavored algorithms widely used for manipulation comprise CQL (Kumar et al., 2020), AWAC (Nair et al., 2020), IQL (Kostrikov et al., 2021), BRAC (Wu et al., 2019), and DAPG for demonstrations (Rajeswaran et al., 2017). Benchmark suites frequently referenced include Meta-World (Yu et al., 2020), D4RL Locomotion and Adroit (Fu et al., 2020), Robomimic (Mandlekar et al., 2021), RLBench (James et al., 2020), and OpenAI Gym Robotics (Brockman et al., 2016). Pretraining and representation resources appear in R3M (Nair et al., 2022), VC-1 (Majumdar et al., 2023), and MVP (Xiao et al., 2022), with Franka Kitchen (Gupta et al., 2019) and ManiSkill (Mu et al., 2021).

Despite algorithmic diversity, improvements often stem from better initialization and data quality rather than new objectives. We highlight the importance of demonstrations that match hardware idiosyncrasies and reward shaping choices. A consistent benchmarking harness with fixed seeds and identical termination rules narrows variance significantly. Our study measures performance under limited resets and actuator delays that mimic deployment conditions. We also analyze safety filters and recovery behaviors that bound exploration risk.

The experimental design emphasizes reproducibility with shared assets and open logs for audit. We ablate representation, objective, and data coverage to attribute gains to specific components. Results clarify data efficiencies and robustness trends across visual and state-based policies. We conclude with recommendations for hardware-aware evaluation and reliable sim-to-real transfer.

spans: Canonical segmentation backbones in medical imaging include U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), U-Net++ (Zhou et al., 2018), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021), often paired with Dice and cross-entropy losses. Augmentation strategies widely reported comprise MixUp (Zhang et al., 2018), CutMix (Yun et al., 2019), RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020), and intensity- or geometry-based transforms offered by MONAI and TorchIO (Cardoso et al., 2022; Pérez-García et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining alternatives adopt contrastive and clustering objectives such as MoCo (He et al., 2020), SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), and SwAV (Caron et al., 2020). Representative benchmarks include BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), KiTS (Heller et al., 2019), MSD tasks (Simpson et al., 2019), CheXpert and ChestX-ray14 (Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017), and ISIC (Codella et al., 2019) across modalities and clinical contexts.
Lacks synthesis.document:
Data augmentation is a critical component for robust medical image segmentation under limited annotation budgets. Cross-institutional variability in scanners and protocols challenges models trained on single-site data. Augmentations can simulate plausible acquisition shifts but also risk violating anatomical realism. We review augmentation and pretraining practices in light of evaluation design.

Simple geometric transforms improve data coverage yet may distort organ topologies at high magnitudes. Intensity perturbations must respect modality-specific physics, particularly in MR sequences and CT Hounsfield units. Incorporating spatial priors through shape models can mitigate implausible deformations during training. Consistent preprocessing pipelines are necessary to isolate augmentation effects from resampling artifacts. These observations motivate principled ablations that separate regularization from genuine generalization.

Canonical segmentation backbones in medical imaging include U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), U-Net++ (Zhou et al., 2018), and nnU-Net (Isensee et al., 2021), often paired with Dice and cross-entropy losses. Augmentation strategies widely reported comprise MixUp (Zhang et al., 2018), CutMix (Yun et al., 2019), RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020), and intensity- or geometry-based transforms offered by MONAI and TorchIO (Cardoso et al., 2022; Pérez-García et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining alternatives adopt contrastive and clustering objectives such as MoCo (He et al., 2020), SimCLR (Chen et al., 2020), BYOL (Grill et al., 2020), and SwAV (Caron et al., 2020). Representative benchmarks include BraTS (Menze et al., 2015; Bakas et al., 2018), KiTS (Heller et al., 2019), MSD tasks (Simpson et al., 2019), CheXpert and ChestX-ray14 (Irvin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017), and ISIC (Codella et al., 2019) across modalities and clinical contexts.

Despite broad adoption, augmentation policies are often tuned on validation sets that leak task structure. Heterogeneous metrics and label conventions further obscure incremental gains across public leaderboards. We recommend reporting calibration curves and uncertainty maps to assess clinical safety. Our experiments vary augmentation strength while holding sampling and optimizer settings fixed. The analysis disentangles benefits from distribution coverage versus improved invariance.

We also examine transfer from self-supervised encoders to small labeled cohorts under strict splits. The study highlights trade-offs between photometric robustness and boundary accuracy for different organs. Results support practical settings that avoid extreme transforms and prioritize anatomy-aware constraints. Finally, we synthesize guidelines for reproducible, clinically grounded augmentation protocols.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates architectures, augmentations, SSL methods, and datasets without articulating their relationships or the gap being addressed, meeting the lack of synthesis definition.

spans: Research on molecular property prediction has reported results on QM9, ZINC, MoleculeNet, PCBA, and curated ChEMBL subsets using canonical scaffold splits and standardized atom and bond features. Architectures commonly include graph convolutional networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), message passing neural networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), graph attention networks (Velickovic et al., 2018), SchNet (Schutt et al., 2018), DimeNet and DimeNet++ (Klicpera et al., 2020), and equivariant transformers (Fuchs et al., 2020). Training and evaluation practices span RDKit featurization baselines, self-supervised pretraining on ZINC15 or PubChem, multi-task objectives on MoleculeNet panels such as BBBP, Tox21, and HIV, and leaderboard protocols adopted by Open Graph Benchmark PCQM4M and GEOM datasets. Benchmarking suites include ESOL, FreeSolv, Lipophilicity, QM7, and QM9-Torsion, while data augmentation strategies cover SMILES enumeration and conformer ensembles from ETKDG, CREST, or ANI, alongside hyperparameter sweeps with early stopping and decoy-aware splitting via DeepChem utilities.
Lacks synthesis.document: Predicting quantum and pharmacological properties of small molecules is a central task in cheminformatics and drug discovery. Early supervised pipelines relied on expert descriptors and kernel methods, but scalability and transfer remained limited. Graph neural networks now dominate because they can exploit molecular topology and 3D cues with fewer manual features. However, reproducibility across datasets and the comparability of reported numbers are still open concerns in the community. Differences in featurization, splitting, and target curation can overshadow algorithmic advances and complicate evaluation. Consequently, a careful review of benchmarks, architectures, and training protocols is needed to contextualize existing results. Research on molecular property prediction has reported results on QM9, ZINC, MoleculeNet, PCBA, and curated ChEMBL subsets using canonical scaffold splits and standardized atom and bond features. Architectures commonly include graph convolutional networks (Kipf and Welling, 2017), message passing neural networks (Gilmer et al., 2017), graph attention networks (Velickovic et al., 2018), SchNet (Schutt et al., 2018), DimeNet and DimeNet++ (Klicpera et al., 2020), and equivariant transformers (Fuchs et al., 2020). Training and evaluation practices span RDKit featurization baselines, self-supervised pretraining on ZINC15 or PubChem, multi-task objectives on MoleculeNet panels such as BBBP, Tox21, and HIV, and leaderboard protocols adopted by Open Graph Benchmark PCQM4M and GEOM datasets. Benchmarking suites include ESOL, FreeSolv, Lipophilicity, QM7, and QM9-Torsion, while data augmentation strategies cover SMILES enumeration and conformer ensembles from ETKDG, CREST, or ANI, alongside hyperparameter sweeps with early stopping and decoy-aware splitting via DeepChem utilities. Despite the breadth of options, experimental setups rarely control for conformer generation, label imbalance, and leakage simultaneously. As a result, performance trends can invert across tasks when evaluation practice is harmonized under common preprocessing. Recent calls for standardized baselines underscore the importance of transparent hyperparameters and fixed public scripts. Addressing these concerns requires disentangling modeling capacity from data handling choices through controlled ablations. We frame our study within this agenda by aligning data pipelines and reporting statistically principled comparisons across families of GNNs.

spans: Benchmark datasets for multimodal emotion recognition widely include IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, covering acted and in-the-wild speech, video, and text modalities. Methodological families span early fusion of handcrafted prosody and facial action units, late fusion of unimodal classifiers, and aligned intermediate fusion using attention over time-synchronized streams. Recent architectures report gains with transformers and pre-trained encoders, such as MulT, MISA, MMIM, and MAG-BERT, alongside audio CNN front-ends on log-mel spectrograms, 3D CNNs for visual features, and contextualized language embeddings from BERT or RoBERTa. Evaluation is typically conducted with speaker-independent splits, cross-session protocols, and metrics like accuracy, F1, CCC, and MAE, with standard preprocessing including voice activity detection, face tracking via OpenFace, and forced alignment from Montreal Forced Aligner. Reported baselines often include SVMs on eGeMAPS, LSTMs over word sequences, HMMs for temporal smoothing, and decision-level stacking with calibration on development folds only.
Lacks synthesis.document: Emotion recognition across speech, vision, and text has accelerated with advances in deep learning and multimodal fusion. Applications span mental health monitoring, human-computer interaction, and social robotics where robustness is essential. Yet inconsistencies in annotation schemes, label granularity, and protocol design hinder comparability. The literature reflects broad choices in features, temporal alignment, and uncertainty handling. To structure this landscape, we first summarize prominent datasets, method families, and evaluation standards. Benchmark datasets for multimodal emotion recognition widely include IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, MELD, and RAVDESS, covering acted and in-the-wild speech, video, and text modalities. Methodological families span early fusion of handcrafted prosody and facial action units, late fusion of unimodal classifiers, and aligned intermediate fusion using attention over time-synchronized streams. Recent architectures report gains with transformers and pre-trained encoders, such as MulT, MISA, MMIM, and MAG-BERT, alongside audio CNN front-ends on log-mel spectrograms, 3D CNNs for visual features, and contextualized language embeddings from BERT or RoBERTa. Evaluation is typically conducted with speaker-independent splits, cross-session protocols, and metrics like accuracy, F1, CCC, and MAE, with standard preprocessing including voice activity detection, face tracking via OpenFace, and forced alignment from Montreal Forced Aligner. Reported baselines often include SVMs on eGeMAPS, LSTMs over word sequences, HMMs for temporal smoothing, and decision-level stacking with calibration on development folds only. Despite common benchmarks, cross-corpus generalization and demographic coverage remain underexplored. The coupling of annotation noise and temporal segmentation further complicates fair assessment. Adopting principled splits and calibration-aware metrics can clarify model behavior under shift. Recent studies have highlighted the need for open protocols and robust statistical testing. We therefore focus on settings that decouple modality contributions while controlling confounds in training and evaluation.
Lacks synthesis.reason: The span enumerates datasets, model classes, and evaluation practices as a literature list without linking them to a specific argument or gap, and it avoids any author stance, hence it lacks synthesis.

spans: Federated learning for medical imaging has been studied across datasets such as BRATS, ISIC, CheXpert, ChestX-ray14, MIMIC-CXR, and HAM10000, using hospital-wise non-IID partitions and privacy-preserving protocols. Optimization algorithms frequently include FedAvg, FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedNova, and MOON, with clients training CNNs or U-Nets under limited communication rounds and periodic model averaging. Security and privacy components typically adopt secure aggregation, differential privacy with Gaussian mechanisms, homomorphic encryption for gradient updates, and audit logging through blockchain-inspired append-only ledgers. Evaluation reports highlight Dice and Hausdorff for segmentation, AUROC and AUPRC for classification, communication cost per round, and convergence under stragglers, often implemented in Flower, TensorFlow Federated, PySyft, or FedML with simulated or real hospital nodes. Despite extensive experimentation, the impact of site-level covariates and annotation sparsity is not fully characterized. Moreover, existing protocols rarely quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in a harmonized manner. Reproducibility is further hampered by heterogeneous codebases and opaque preprocessing recipes. Establishing transparent baselines and stratified partitions can clarify the limits of current algorithms. We therefore present a study that standardizes clients, privacy budgets, and compute settings for fair comparison.

Evaluation protocols typically report average travel time, throughput, queue length, and delay, drawing data from TSC benchmarks, Hangzhou and Jinan datasets, Peachtree corridor logs, and CityFlow-Benchmark configurations with public seeds. Baseline implementations often rely on Replay Buffer tuning, target-network updates, reward normalization, fixed yellow times, and demand scaling, and comparisons include actuated signals and coordinated fixed-time plans.

The problem spans heterogeneous sections, technical nomenclature, and cross-referenced contributions that challenge standard sequence models. Prior work has studied data scaling, auxiliary objectives, and content selection under varying domain constraints.
